review.text: Lovis Corinth: Self Portrait as Howling Bacchant, 1905, Insel Hombroich There is a haunted dread in the eyes of this bacchant. That howl - more distress than joy. Mania, frenzy, delirium; a Dionysian letting go. This is the mental picture that furnished my mind as I read of Gustav von Aschenbach. Aschenbach is the eminent artist of disciplined control, he has based his whole career on fame, he has achieved recognition through hard graft, a hundred little inspirations that have accrued, that have been beaten out and formed into a burnished oeuvre of classicist grace and clarity. But the machine begins to whir out of control, that motus animi continuus, the very heart of eloquence, continues to spin its wheels, refuses to be regulated by the measured rhythm of Aschenbach's daily routine afternoon snooze, spins, spins, whirs on, he must out to quiet the beast. Very swiftly the stage seems to darken into a dream-like state. The tram stop deserted, the streets bare, a stranger suddenly, inexplicably, at the portico of the mortuary chapel - and Aschenbach experiences an opening of his soul, a sudden unrest. Wanderlust, he calls it. Really? This nightmare vision of a primordial wilderness of the dank and decaying, this jungle of greenery with the glowering eyes of a tiger? A vision so stark that he has to shake his head to rid himself of it? But Aschenbach has always been aware of the need for travel as a hygienic necessity, the needed (earned?) escape from work, that "rigorous, frigid, and ardent duty." Aschenbach lives his life with a tightly clenched fist - but on his journey to Trieste, then Pula, then Venice, he gradually begins to relax that grip, the fist opens, the arm hangs loose. He is carried on the water, he is rowed by a menacing gondolier who takes him further than he wants to go, warning him that he will pay, he will pay. And pay he will. But he is delivered safely to the very edge of the civilised world, and there, at the next table in his hotel, waiting for dinner, is a family of young Poles, amongst them a boy of around fourteen. "With astonishment Aschenbach noticed that the boy was perfectly beautiful." Vollkommen schön. Beauty. Kant calls it "interesseloses Wohlgefallen" - disinterested pleasure, an echo of Plato's ideas. According to Plato there is a realm of ultimate ideals, where goodness, wisdom, truth exist in changeless perfection. A vision of these ideals is planted into our souls, but man is a forgetful creature, and loses the sharp focus of that vision. Reason, Justice, Virtue cannot be perceived by our senses, but there is one perfect ideal which is visible, and that is Beauty. An earthly image of Beauty reminds us with a frisson of shock of that Absolute. Thus physical beauty here on earth points to a higher reality, a purely spiritual abstract concept. Ah but. There has to be a but. Beauty is a sensuous pleasure, and can provoke a purely sensual response. To have, to hold, to possess, to enjoy. That is not the entrance to a higher sphere. Disinterested pleasure, remember. Aschenbach has to be given the opportunity to enjoy this perfection as a high ideal: surely that is why Tadzio is a prepubescent male, the incarnation of the unspoilt perfect body, no unsightly sproutings or eruptions, smooth, slender, harmonious. It ought to be, has to be possible to leave carnal desire out of the account, to admire this creature as divine ideal. And indeed Aschenbach starts quite well, with transcendental thoughts over dinner about the relationship between the general and the particular in human beauty "from there to think about the general problems of form and art and eventually found his thoughts and findings to resemble certain apparently fortuitous ideas in a dream, that on closer inspection reveal themselves to be completely stale and unworkable." And the next morning there is a putrid smell in the lagoon. Ah but Aschenbach battles. He tries to see the Greek statue, the perfection, himself as a fatherly figure: "And a fatherly awe, the complete devotion of the one who tries to create beauty to the one who is endowed with it filled and moved his heart." He strains to remain the disinterested artist, sees the analogy between his work and this example of youthful perfection, his hewing from the marble of language and this perfect form, are they not similar? "His eyes embraced that noble figure at the bounds of the blue, and in enthusiastic rapture he believed to embrace beauty itself, form as a thought in the mind of God, the one and pure perfection living in the human spirit and of which a human image and analog was erected here for worship." But this is intoxication, 'Rausch', the frenzy of self-deceit. He believes his own poetic musings - but wanton desire is the worm in the rose. Tadzio is no ideal, he is all too human, and it is no longer beauty as such that Aschenbach worships from afar. He wants, he desires human contact. A look, a smile. Recognition. Well, we know how it ends. Aschenbach, no longer named, but given epithets; the one who is led astray, the confused, the intoxicated, the infatuated, is no longer able to resist, either his own infatuation or the cholera that is infiltrating the whole system. 'Eine sittliche Fabel' Mann calls this, a moral tale. Those artists who give up the dignity of a social position and go in pursuit of beauty will come to disaster. Aha. But it's never that easy, not with Thomas Mann. He delights in undercutting himself, building ambiguities into the work, puzzles for the reader to mull over. What kind of artist is Aschenbach? Are we to admire his mastery and classicizing style? Most of the novel is in free indirect speech, so are we to assume that the narrative voice is Aschenbach's? Those false little classicizing flag-ups at the beginning, a spring afternoon in the year 19.., like Goethe or Kleist, or that over-precious sunrise that Aschenbach sees from his hotel window: after a wingéd word brings a message from Olympia, we are treated to plenty of references to Eos, Cleitos and Cephalos and Orion and then we get this rather recherché description: "At the world's edge began a strewing of roses, a shining and a blooming ineffably pure; baby cloudlets hung illuminated, like attendant amoretti, in the blue and blushful haze; purple effulgence fell upon the sea, that seemed to heave it forward on its welling waves; from horizon to zenith went quivering thrusts like golden lances..." Well, you get the picture - that's the DH Lawrence translation, he really gets that rococo style parody that sloshes into the sentimental. So Aschenbach's claim to be an artist might be seen as doubtful. And we've seen that he equates what he does with Tadzio's beauty, we are constantly told that Tadzio is beautiful, but again, what kind of beauty is this? His teeth are rotten, he's weak and cosseted, the family pet, not at all the athletic Greek God ideal. Effete. Over-pretty, like Aschenbach's sunrise. So is this the story of an artist in pursuit of true beauty, or is it the story of an artist whose artistry is never convincingly portrayed, in pursuit of a beauty that mirrors his own corruption, complaisance and self-mockery? A play of illusions.
review.text_raw:
Lovis Corinth: Self Portrait as Howling Bacchant, 1905, Insel Hombroich

There is a haunted dread in the eyes of this bacchant. That howl - more distress than joy. Mania, frenzy, delirium; a Dionysian letting go.

This is the mental picture that furnished my mind as I read of Gustav von Aschenbach. Aschenbach is the eminent artist of disciplined control, he has based his whole career on fame, he has achieved recognition through hard graft, a hundred little inspirations that have accrued, that have been beaten out and formed into a burnished oeuvre of classicist grace and clarity. But the machine begins to whir out of control, that motus animi continuus, the very heart of eloquence, continues to spin its wheels, refuses to be regulated by the measured rhythm of Aschenbach's daily routine afternoon snooze, spins, spins, whirs on, he must out to quiet the beast.

Very swiftly the stage seems to darken into a dream-like state. The tram stop deserted, the streets bare, a stranger suddenly, inexplicably, at the portico of the mortuary chapel - and Aschenbach experiences an opening of his soul, a sudden unrest. Wanderlust, he calls it. Really? This nightmare vision of a primordial wilderness of the dank and decaying, this jungle of greenery with the glowering eyes of a tiger? A vision so stark that he has to shake his head to rid himself of it? But Aschenbach has always been aware of the need for travel as a hygienic necessity, the needed (earned?) escape from work, that "rigorous, frigid, and ardent duty."

Aschenbach lives his life with a tightly clenched fist - but on his journey to Trieste, then Pula, then Venice, he gradually begins to relax that grip, the fist opens, the arm hangs loose. He is carried on the water, he is rowed by a menacing gondolier who takes him further than he wants to go, warning him that he will pay, he will pay. And pay he will.

But he is delivered safely to the very edge of the civilised world, and there, at the next table in his hotel, waiting for dinner, is a family of young Poles, amongst them a boy of around fourteen. "With astonishment Aschenbach noticed that the boy was perfectly beautiful." Vollkommen schön.

Beauty.

Kant calls it "interesseloses Wohlgefallen" - disinterested pleasure, an echo of Plato's ideas. According to Plato there is a realm of ultimate ideals, where goodness, wisdom, truth exist in changeless perfection. A vision of these ideals is planted into our souls, but man is a forgetful creature, and loses the sharp focus of that vision. Reason, Justice, Virtue cannot be perceived by our senses, but there is one perfect ideal which is visible, and that is Beauty. An earthly image of Beauty reminds us with a frisson of shock of that Absolute. Thus physical beauty here on earth points to a higher reality, a purely spiritual abstract concept.
Ah but. There has to be a but. Beauty is a sensuous pleasure, and can provoke a purely sensual response. To have, to hold, to possess, to enjoy. That is not the entrance to a higher sphere. Disinterested pleasure, remember.

Aschenbach has to be given the opportunity to enjoy this perfection as a high ideal: surely that is why Tadzio is a prepubescent male, the incarnation of the unspoilt perfect body, no unsightly sproutings or eruptions, smooth, slender, harmonious. It ought to be, has to be possible to leave carnal desire out of the account, to admire this creature as divine ideal. And indeed Aschenbach starts quite well, with transcendental thoughts over dinner about the relationship between the general and the particular in human beauty "from there to think about the general problems of form and art and eventually found his thoughts and findings to resemble certain apparently fortuitous ideas in a dream, that on closer inspection reveal themselves to be completely stale and unworkable." And the next morning there is a putrid smell in the lagoon.

Ah but Aschenbach battles. He tries to see the Greek statue, the perfection, himself as a fatherly figure: "And a fatherly awe, the complete devotion of the one who tries to create beauty to the one who is endowed with it filled and moved his heart." He strains to remain the disinterested artist, sees the analogy between his work and this example of youthful perfection, his hewing from the marble of language and this perfect form, are they not similar? "His eyes embraced that noble figure at the bounds of the blue, and in enthusiastic rapture he believed to embrace beauty itself, form as a thought in the mind of God, the one and pure perfection living in the human spirit and of which a human image and analog was erected here for worship." But this is intoxication, 'Rausch', the frenzy of self-deceit. He believes his own poetic musings - but wanton desire is the worm in the rose. Tadzio is no ideal, he is all too human, and it is no longer beauty as such that Aschenbach worships from afar. He wants, he desires human contact. A look, a smile. Recognition.

Well, we know how it ends. Aschenbach, no longer named, but given epithets; the one who is led astray, the confused, the intoxicated, the infatuated, is no longer able to resist, either his own infatuation or the cholera that is infiltrating the whole system. 'Eine sittliche Fabel' Mann calls this, a moral tale. Those artists who give up the dignity of a social position and go in pursuit of beauty will come to disaster. Aha.

But it's never that easy, not with Thomas Mann. He delights in undercutting himself, building ambiguities into the work, puzzles for the reader to mull over. What kind of artist is Aschenbach? Are we to admire his mastery and classicizing style? Most of the novel is in free indirect speech, so are we to assume that the narrative voice is Aschenbach's? Those false little classicizing flag-ups at the beginning, a spring afternoon in the year 19.., like Goethe or Kleist, or that over-precious sunrise that Aschenbach sees from his hotel window: after a wingéd word brings a message from Olympia, we are treated to plenty of references to Eos, Cleitos and Cephalos and Orion and then we get this rather recherché description: "At the world's edge began a strewing of roses, a shining and a blooming ineffably pure; baby cloudlets hung illuminated, like attendant amoretti, in the blue and blushful haze; purple effulgence fell upon the sea, that seemed to heave it forward on its welling waves; from horizon to zenith went quivering thrusts like golden lances..."
Well, you get the picture - that's the DH Lawrence translation, he really gets that rococo style parody that sloshes into the sentimental. So Aschenbach's claim to be an artist might be seen as doubtful. And we've seen that he equates what he does with Tadzio's beauty, we are constantly told that Tadzio is beautiful, but again, what kind of beauty is this? His teeth are rotten, he's weak and cosseted, the family pet, not at all the athletic Greek God ideal. Effete. Over-pretty, like Aschenbach's sunrise. So is this the story of an artist in pursuit of true beauty, or is it the story of an artist whose artistry is never convincingly portrayed, in pursuit of a beauty that mirrors his own corruption, complaisance and self-mockery?

A play of illusions.

review.text: ¿Y si la belleza no fuera una bendición sino una trampa? ¿Y si el arte, en lugar de elevarnos, nos arrastrara al fango con una sonrisa en los labios y un muchacho polaco como cebo? No, no exagero. Thomas Mann tampoco. La muerte en Venecia no es solo una novela corta sobre un escritor envejecido que se encapricha de un adolescente rubio en un hotel del Lido. Es una trampa disfrazada de elegancia. Un paseo por la estética decadente que termina con ese olor dulzón de lo que se pudre despacio. Y uno no se da cuenta de que ha caído hasta que ya está dentro del agua, vestido de blanco, contemplando la figura de un dios menor con los pies en la arena y la muerte en la nuca. La historia es sencilla y, como todo lo verdaderamente peligroso, parece inofensiva: Gustav von Aschenbach, escritor reconocido, hombre disciplinado, entregado a la forma y al deber, decide romper su rutina con un viaje a Venecia. Y allí, en esa ciudad que huele a descomposición con máscara de gloria pasada, se encuentra con Tadzio, un adolescente de belleza sobrenatural. Lo que sigue no es amor, ni deseo exactamente. Es otra cosa. Una especie de fiebre, de fascinación estética llevada hasta el delirio. La obsesión se cuece a fuego lento y huele a incienso. Mann no coloca etiquetas: el texto se mueve en la zona gris donde lo sublime y lo grotesco se dan la mano, como una niebla espesa que cubre con delicadeza lo podrido que se esconde bajo la superficie. Y todo eso —la fiebre, el incienso, la cadencia— está en la forma, no solo en el fondo. Porque la prosa también participa de esa enfermedad lenta y elegante. La prosa es de una precisión casi litúrgica. Cada frase está cincelada como si Mann estuviera escribiendo un réquiem en alemán y lo tradujera al arte. El ritmo es pausado —y bendito sea— porque lo exige el veneno. Aquí no vale pasar páginas a toda prisa. Este es uno de esos libros que te obligan a ir lento, a leer con el estómago y a notar cómo el estilo te atrapa antes que la historia. Hay un uso casi hipnótico del lenguaje, una cadencia que sugiere que todo lo que ocurre está fuera del tiempo y que, a la vez, no podía suceder en ningún otro momento. La tercera persona del narrador no es neutral: hay una especie de complicidad amarga entre el narrador y Aschenbach, como si uno supiera que el otro está perdido y aun así no hiciera nada por evitarlo. Esa pasividad narrativa, ese observar sin intervenir, le da al relato una frialdad casi científica… y por eso duele más. Y ese dolor, tan frío como lúcido, tiene un rostro concreto: el de Aschenbach. El protagonista, Gustav von Aschenbach, escritor famoso en la cincuentena, es uno de esos personajes que podrían haber escrito tratados sobre ética mientras su vida personal se iba al garete. La figura del artista disciplinado que, de pronto, se ve arrastrado por algo que no entiende —y que ni siquiera quiere entender— es una de las grandes construcciones psicológicas de la literatura europea del siglo XX. No es un villano, ni un mártir, ni un enfermo. Es un hombre que, tras una vida de contención, cae rendido ante lo que siempre había despreciado: la belleza por la belleza. Tadzio, por su parte, no es un personaje, sino una figura etérea. Su rol es simbólico: es el ideal platónico, el epifánico adolescente eterno que ni habla ni actúa, pero que funciona como un espejo deforme donde Aschenbach proyecta sus ruinas interiores. No es deseo en sentido biográfico, sino una forma de locura estética, como si contemplar la belleza pura —intocable, imposible, cruel— lo arrastrara a una especie de autoinmolación del yo racional. Y no, no es lo que estás pensando. Esto no es Lolita ni un escándalo edulcorado. No hay deseo carnal, ni impulso sexual narrado. Lo de Aschenbach no va por ahí. Lo suyo es peor, o al menos más trágico: no desea a Tadzio como a una persona real, con voz y carne, sino como a un ideal imposible. Lo mira como quien mira una estatua que no puede tocar, como un cuadro perfecto que te arranca el alma a base de forma. No hay amor, hay obsesión estética. Tadzio no es un adolescente: es una aparición. Y desear una aparición es como enamorarse de un dios: te condena a desaparecer tú. Y aquí es donde la cosa se pone interesante. Porque La muerte en Venecia no va, en realidad, de un deseo escandaloso o de una pasión prohibida. O al menos no solo. Va del colapso del ideal clásico. De cómo la belleza —esa a la que tanto se venera en las artes, en la filosofía, en la religión— puede convertirse en una fuerza destructiva. De la obsesión con la belleza llevada al extremo y cómo ese anhelo puede devorar al que la persigue. Así, Mann pone en escena a un hombre atrapado en su ideal, que acaba perdiendo la razón y la humanidad, porque a veces el arte no salva: nos envenena. Y, por eso, la novela es, en cierto sentido, una crítica brutal a la estética pura. A esa concepción del arte como algo superior a la vida, cuando en realidad puede llevarnos a la disolución. El Tadzio que Aschenbach ve no es el chico real. Es un tótem. Y los tótems, cuando se adoran con demasiada fe, acaban exigiendo sacrificios. Este conflicto entre el arte y la vida no es nuevo en Mann. Está en Tonio Kröger , en Doctor Fausto , incluso en Los Buddenbrook . Pero aquí alcanza una concentración casi venenosa. Hay ecos nietzscheanos (el Apolo que se derrumba ante el Dionisio), resonancias griegas (ese diálogo constante con la idea de lo bello como lo bueno, y lo feo como lo moralmente inferior), y un poso decadentista que haría sonreír a Huysmans desde el fondo de su claustro. Sí, ya veo que entornas los ojos y levantas ligeramente las cejas. Uf, rollo filosófico, piensas. Vale, paro el rollo filosófico un segundo. Sí, este libro es denso y serio, pero no hace falta ser un erudito para disfrutarlo. Es como esa peli que parece un ladrillo, pero que en el fondo te llega por lo básico: un escritor mayor, un chaval que brilla como un faro, y un viaje que acaba siendo un desastre en cámara lenta. O sea, que aunque quieras filosofar hasta la extenuación, también puedes simplemente dejarte llevar y flipar con la atmósfera. Ya solo con eso, el libro ya merece la pena. Y hablando de atmósferas, no puedo evitar pensar en la película de Visconti, que convierte esa novela tan contenida en un espectáculo visual y sonoro que te atrapa desde el primer plano y que te recomiendo muy vivamente si no la has visto. Porque la cámara se detiene en cada gesto, cada mirada de ese joven Tadzio que brilla con luz propia, mientras la música de Mahler —esa banda sonora que parece una elegía interminable— envuelve la decadencia con una belleza imposible de ignorar. Visconti, con toda su pomposidad, consigue que sientas la peste y la gracia en el mismo plano, como si estuvieras atrapado en esa Venecia donde el tiempo se deshace lentamente. Y cómo no mencionar a Dirk Bogarde, cuya interpretación de Aschenbach es un prodigio de contención y melancolía sutil. En cada gesto, Bogarde logra transmitir ese conflicto entre la razón y la obsesión, esa caída silenciosa hacia la perdición que Mann narra con tanta precisión. Su mirada es un poema silencioso que resuena con la música y con la luz mortecina de una Venecia que Visconti pinta con la elegancia de un lienzo en descomposición. Pero más allá de la pantalla, la Venecia de la novela no es un simple decorado: es un personaje más. Con su luz dorada, su humedad corrupta, su hedor y su esplendor fantasmal. Es la antesala perfecta para un hundimiento personal, como si Mann hubiera elegido el escenario más bello posible para narrar la putrefacción moral con la mayor elegancia estética. Pero esa mezcla de belleza formal y podredumbre moral no es exclusiva de Mann. De hecho, reverbera en otras obras donde el estilo deslumbra mientras lo ético se vuelve pantanoso. Y claro, en este punto es inevitable pensar en Nabokov. En Lolita , sí, pero también en La defensa o Pálido fuego , donde lo estilísticamente sublime cohabita con lo moralmente ambiguo. Mann, sin embargo, es más frío, más analítico. No hay ironía aquí, ni guiños al lector. Hay solemnidad. Y una tristeza inmensa, como si estuviera diciendo: “Mirad esto. Mirad lo que somos capaces de hacer cuando confundimos el arte con la salvación”. ¿Hay algo más bajo la superficie? Sí, claro. Toda la novela está escrita como si fuera un iceberg: lo que se ve es apenas un destello. Bajo esa historia mínima se agita un tratado sobre la decadencia europea, sobre el hundimiento de los valores clásicos, sobre la fragilidad del yo frente a lo absoluto. Hay una epidemia de peste en la ciudad, sí, pero también hay peste en el alma. La misma que hace que un hombre culto, noble y racional decida teñirse el pelo de negro y ponerse carmín en los labios para parecer más joven, como un ridículo Fausto sin Mefistófeles que le salve. El mensaje de Mann, si es que hay uno, no es ni moralista ni cínico. Es un susurro trágico: cuidado con aquello que idealizas, porque puede matarte. Y lo hará con la sonrisa más bella del mundo. Por todo esto, La muerte en Venecia no es un libro para leer con prisas ni para comentar con ligereza. Es un texto que hay que dejar reposar, como esos vinos traicioneros que al principio parecen suaves y luego te hacen olvidar tu nombre. Leerlo es aceptar una cierta incomodidad. Es entrar en una sala donde se venera a la belleza, pero donde las flores huelen a formol. Es, también, un recordatorio brutal de que el arte no siempre nos redime. A veces nos consume. A veces nos arrastra. Y a veces —las peores veces — lo hace con tanto estilo que, casi sin darnos cuenta, terminamos brindando con el veneno.

Es como esa peli que parece un ladrillo, pero que en el fondo te llega por lo básico: un escritor mayor, un chaval que brilla como un faro, y un viaje que acaba siendo un desastre en cámara lenta. O sea, que aunque quieras filosofar hasta la extenuación, también puedes simplemente dejarte llevar y flipar con la atmósfera. Ya solo con eso, el libro ya merece la pena.

Y hablando de atmósferas, no puedo evitar pensar en la película de Visconti, que convierte esa novela tan contenida en un espectáculo visual y sonoro que te atrapa desde el primer plano y que te recomiendo muy vivamente si no la has visto. Porque la cámara se detiene en cada gesto, cada mirada de ese joven Tadzio que brilla con luz propia, mientras la música de Mahler —esa banda sonora que parece una elegía interminable— envuelve la decadencia con una belleza imposible de ignorar. Visconti, con toda su pomposidad, consigue que sientas la peste y la gracia en el mismo plano, como si estuvieras atrapado en esa Venecia donde el tiempo se deshace lentamente.

Y cómo no mencionar a Dirk Bogarde, cuya interpretación de Aschenbach es un prodigio de contención y melancolía sutil. En cada gesto, Bogarde logra transmitir ese conflicto entre la razón y la obsesión, esa caída silenciosa hacia la perdición que Mann narra con tanta precisión. Su mirada es un poema silencioso que resuena con la música y con la luz mortecina de una Venecia que Visconti pinta con la elegancia de un lienzo en descomposición.

Pero más allá de la pantalla, la Venecia de la novela no es un simple decorado: es un personaje más. Con su luz dorada, su humedad corrupta, su hedor y su esplendor fantasmal. Es la antesala perfecta para un hundimiento personal, como si Mann hubiera elegido el escenario más bello posible para narrar la putrefacción moral con la mayor elegancia estética.

Pero esa mezcla de belleza formal y podredumbre moral no es exclusiva de Mann. De hecho, reverbera en otras obras donde el estilo deslumbra mientras lo ético se vuelve pantanoso. Y claro, en este punto es inevitable pensar en Nabokov. En Lolita , sí, pero también en La defensa o Pálido fuego, donde lo estilísticamente sublime cohabita con lo moralmente ambiguo. Mann, sin embargo, es más frío, más analítico. No hay ironía aquí, ni guiños al lector. Hay solemnidad. Y una tristeza inmensa, como si estuviera diciendo: “Mirad esto. Mirad lo que somos capaces de hacer cuando confundimos el arte con la salvación”.

¿Hay algo más bajo la superficie? Sí, claro. Toda la novela está escrita como si fuera un iceberg: lo que se ve es apenas un destello. Bajo esa historia mínima se agita un tratado sobre la decadencia europea, sobre el hundimiento de los valores clásicos, sobre la fragilidad del yo frente a lo absoluto. Hay una epidemia de peste en la ciudad, sí, pero también hay peste en el alma. La misma que hace que un hombre culto, noble y racional decida teñirse el pelo de negro y ponerse carmín en los labios para parecer más joven, como un ridículo Fausto sin Mefistófeles que le salve. El mensaje de Mann, si es que hay uno, no es ni moralista ni cínico. Es un susurro trágico: cuidado con aquello que idealizas, porque puede matarte. Y lo hará con la sonrisa más bella del mundo.

Por todo esto, La muerte en Venecia no es un libro para leer con prisas ni para comentar con ligereza. Es un texto que hay que dejar reposar, como esos vinos traicioneros que al principio parecen suaves y luego te hacen olvidar tu nombre. Leerlo es aceptar una cierta incomodidad. Es entrar en una sala donde se venera a la belleza, pero donde las flores huelen a formol. Es, también, un recordatorio brutal de que el arte no siempre nos redime. A veces nos consume. A veces nos arrastra. Y a veces —las peores veces — lo hace con tanto estilo que, casi sin darnos cuenta, terminamos brindando con el veneno.

review.text: "La palabra solo puede celebrar la belleza, no reproducirla". Sin palabras. Una historia bellísima, cargada de significado, atrevida hasta el punto en que es increíble que haya sido publicada en 1912 (43 años antes que Lolita, para tener en cuenta). En un principio me sorprendió lo densa que puede ser, sobre todo las primeras 30 páginas, en las que Mann habla de cosas que en realidad no comprendí muy bien. Cuesta un poco que enganche, pero cuando lo hace, no suelta hasta la página final, en donde toda la carga emocional que lleva preparando desde el inicio de repente estalla para darte algo que llevabas esperando desde el título de la novela. Este libro es demasiado satisfactorio y triste a la vez. Mann es un prodigio de la escritura, sus palabras son elegidas con exactitud y no se retiene al describir, por ejemplo, un atardecer en todo un párrafo de forma deliciosa. Como pequeño pero: ya lo mencioné, tal vez cuenta con ciertas partes un poco densas, lo que sorprende pues es un libro muy corto. Es necesaria una relectura para captar detalles que se escapan, ocultos entre la complejidad de esta conmovedora obra. Altamente recomendada, una de mis mejores lecturas del año.
review.text_raw:
"La palabra solo puede celebrar la belleza, no reproducirla".
Sin palabras. Una historia bellísima, cargada de significado, atrevida hasta el punto en que es increíble que haya sido publicada en 1912 (43 años antes que Lolita, para tener en cuenta).
En un principio me sorprendió lo densa que puede ser, sobre todo las primeras 30 páginas, en las que Mann habla de cosas que en realidad no comprendí muy bien. Cuesta un poco que enganche, pero cuando lo hace, no suelta hasta la página final, en donde toda la carga emocional que lleva preparando desde el inicio de repente estalla para darte algo que llevabas esperando desde el título de la novela. Este libro es demasiado satisfactorio y triste a la vez.
Mann es un prodigio de la escritura, sus palabras son elegidas con exactitud y no se retiene al describir, por ejemplo, un atardecer en todo un párrafo de forma deliciosa.
Como pequeño pero: ya lo mencioné, tal vez cuenta con ciertas partes un poco densas, lo que sorprende pues es un libro muy corto. Es necesaria una relectura para captar detalles que se escapan, ocultos entre la complejidad de esta conmovedora obra.
Altamente recomendada, una de mis mejores lecturas del año.

review.text: Рассказ держит в напряжении все время. Само название сообщает нам, что дело закончится смертью. Неоднократные упоминания о видимой болезненности красивого польского мальчика Тадзио из аристократической семьи, предположения писателя о том, что Тадзио не доживёт до старости, даёт нам уверенность, что умрет он. Когда же рассказчик сообщает о том, что он поел клубнику в то время, как в городе бушует холера, мы уверяемся, что умрет все же писатель. И вот, финальная сцена на пляже, борьба Тадзио с Яшу, кажется, что он нечаянно задушит его в борьбе, но нет, Тадзио жив. Снова Тадзио идёт по песчаной косе, и мы думаем, что вдруг он утонет. Таким образом, до самого конца напряжение не спадает. Это довольно редкая в классической литературе тема гомосексуальной любви, пусть и платонической, хотя, скорее всего Тадзио - несовершеннолетний, и это меняет оценки. В любом случае, рассказ полон эмоциональной силы, насыщенности ожиданий, ведущих к напряжённому развитию сюжета.

review.text: A good book to be taught in tandem with Lolita , methinks. A literary achievement with the psychology of Tolstoy and a Greek commitment to The Story; and that is not the only thing about this book that is 'Greek'. A treatise on Death, Life, Sex, Desire, and Fear, Death in Venice is both enticing and terrifying, and for the self-same reason. Here is the face of wretched animal man, teeth bared, cloudy desperation mocking his vision. Mann's succinct and powerful images are always reversed: the raw and brutal emotion herein is become feral, mitigated only by how it twists back upon itself as only such a morally indistinct, labyrinthine mass may so twist. Eminently pleasing and disturbing, this battle between the barely-restrained Epicurean and the resignedly Absurdist meets the latter's comic fruition in the former's faux-tragic inaccessibility.

review.text: Gustav Aschenbach, scrittore bavarese di grande fama, s'innamora mentre è in vacanza a Venezia di Tadzio, ragazzo polacco per cui in seguito svilupperà un'ossessione. La monumentalità dello stile di Mann è la stessa di Zauberberg , di cui questo breve racconto costituisce il nucleo.
review.text_raw: Gustav Aschenbach, scrittore bavarese di grande fama, s'innamora mentre è in vacanza a Venezia di Tadzio, ragazzo polacco per cui in seguito svilupperà un'ossessione. review.text: مرگ در ونیز: نثر اهنگین غنی و سرشار از تمثیل توماس مان همچو موسیقی از مدخل چشمان به ذهن وارد میشود و همچو تابلویی گسترده که ناگهان از آن پرده بر کشند ما را خیره می سازد..همچو تجدید دیداری با خورشید پس از ترک تاریکی..لحظاتی کورمال خواهیم‌گشت تا که سرانجام‌خود را در برابر تمامیت تابلوی گسترده توماس مان هشیار بیابیم "زیبایی‌ الهه وار ت که مرا مفتون می کند شاید تنها حقیقت حیات بخش زندگی ام باشد" زمان آنچنان می گذرد که از این همه زندگی ما جز خاطره محوی همچو بارقه ای نور نماند,همانگونه که خاطرات جوانی اکنون تصویر محوی بیش نیستند..و روزی از خود خواهیم پرسید که ایا سهم ما از زندگی همه این بود؟..و آن هنگام که به پیری برسیم,سقوط و فساد تدریجی بدن و سلامتی مان رادر قفسه سینه ای گرفته حس میکنیم...آنگاه دیدن تندیسی خدایگان وار از جوانی چه هیجانی در ما بر خواهد انگیخت..هیجان تمام آنچه که می دانیم این انسان الهه رخ در پیش خواهد داشت..بر پیشانی صاف و بلندش چه خورشید ها غروب خواهند کرد..و قلبش که بس بسیار میزبان غلیان احساس ها خواهد شد..او اماده بهره مندی از همه زندگیست..حال که ما ظاهرا تمام سهم خود را زیسته ایم. بس ناعادلانه می آید..ان زمان که دیگران تنها منتظر مرگ مان اند..ما را در قلب هایشان متهم می کنند که کامل و پربار زیسته ایم..کدام زندگی؟..کدام روز ها و سال ها؟ از این همه جز لحظه کوتاه تلالو چراغ چشمک زنی در تاریکی به یاد نداریم..نه..من همچنان میخواهم زنده باشم..مرگ به سویم به پیش می تازد..می خواهد که همگان مرا فراموش کنند..آنچنان که در جوانی دوستانم مرا فراموش کردند..نه..من میخواهم زنده بمانم..می خواهم پرواز پرندگان را یکبار دیگر ببینم

نثر اهنگین غنی و سرشار از تمثیل توماس مان همچو موسیقی از مدخل چشمان به ذهن وارد میشود و همچو تابلویی گسترده که ناگهان از آن پرده بر کشند ما را خیره می سازد..همچو تجدید دیداری با خورشید پس از ترک تاریکی..لحظاتی کورمال خواهیم‌گشت تا که سرانجام‌خود را در برابر تمامیت تابلوی گسترده توماس مان هشیار بیابیم

review.text: Όπως έγινε και με τον Φίλιπ Ροθ έτσι και ο Τόμας Μαν, μου συστήθηκε ως συγγραφέας με κάτι μικρό, με μια νουβέλα. Ο Φίλιπ Ροθ με Το βυζί και ο Τόμας Μαν με τον Θάνατο στη Βενετία. Και τα δυο από τις εκδόσεις γράμματα . Μια νουβέλα των κάτι παραπάνω από 100 σελίδων η οποία μου αποκάλυψε πόσο όμορφη γραφή έχει ο Τόμας Μαν και συνάμα πόσο απαιτητική. Πλοκή δεν υπάρχει, αρκετή, αλλά αυτό που παίζει σημασία δεν είναι η πλοκή αλλά η γλώσσα, ο έρωτας, το πάθος, η εμμονή, και τέλος ο θάνατος. Ένας μεσήλικας Γερμανός διάσημος συγγραφέας πάει διακοπές στην Βενετία. Εκεί θα δει το υπέρτατο κάλλος όπως λέει και το οπισθόφυλλο, στην ύπαρξη του Τάτζιο, ενός δεκατετράχρονου αγοριού από την Πολωνία. Αγνοεί την απειλή για επιδημία χολέρας με σκοπό να θυσιάσει τη ζωή και την αξιοπρέπειά του για χάρη της ομορφιάς και του έρωτα. Ένας έρωτας απαγορευμένος, αλλά πλατωνικός. Ενώ στη Λολίτα του Ναμπόκοφ ο ενήλικας με την εμμονή έρχεται σε επαφή με το αντικείμενο του πόθου του, και όταν λέμε επαφή εννοούμε και κοινωνική, και σεξουαλική, εδώ όχι μόνο δεν αγγίζονται, αλλά ούτε καν ανταλλάζουν κουβέντα. 2-3 κλεφτές ματιές όλες κι όλες. Ο όμορφος Τάτζιο - Ο μεσόκοπος Άσενμπαχ. Και στο κάτω-κάτω της γραφής τι είναι η ομορφιά; Κάτι εφήμερο. Και αν είναι κάτι εφήμερο γιατί από καταβολής κόσμου οι πάντες και τα πάντα θυσιάζονταν γι' αυτή; Αναγνώστη αυτής της κριτικής περιμένεις απάντηση τώρα; Από μένα; Πού να ξέρω εγώ; Αυτό το βιβλίο είχε άφθονες αναφορές στο Συμπόσιο και τον Φαίδρο ή Περί Έρωτος του Πλάτωνα. Από αρχαία ελληνική γραμματεία δεν ξέρω περισσότερα απ' όσα η Ζουμπουλία Αμπατζίδου (το γένος Καραγκιόζη), άρα 'έχασα' πολλές από τις αναφορές αυτές. Αναφορές περί έρωτα, και τι είναι. Και φυσικά αυτό το βιβλίο έρχεται σε διάλογο με τα δύο αυτά έργα του Πλάτωνα. Αυτοί να διαλογίζονται και 'γω να τους κοιτάω σαν Βούδας. Ακριβώς όπως εκείνον που είχε η Μαριλένα Δορκοφίκη, η συμπεθέρα της Ζουμπουλίας. Άρα με όσα κατάφερα να 'πιάσω', ήρθα στο συμπέρασμα ότι αυτό το βιβλίο ήταν ωραίο, που όμως με απαιτητική γραφή, που πρέπει να ξαναδιαβαστεί αφότου εντρυφήσω στον Πλάτωνα πρώτα (σαν τα μούτρα μου θα τα κάνω προβλέπω). Και ότι αυτός ήταν ο πρώτος μου Μαν αλλά όχι ο τελευταίος. Ίσως συνεχίσω με τους Αδερφούς του Ιωσήφ αντί με Μαγικό Βουνό, διότι μόνο κι από τον τίτλο εγώ θα είμαι πότε Βούδας πότε Κούδας. Βαθμολογία: 8/10

Ο όμορφος Τάτζιο - Ο μεσόκοπος Άσενμπαχ.

Και στο κάτω-κάτω της γραφής τι είναι η ομορφιά; Κάτι εφήμερο.
Και αν είναι κάτι εφήμερο γιατί από καταβολής κόσμου οι πάντες και τα πάντα θυσιάζονταν γι' αυτή;


Αναγνώστη αυτής της κριτικής περιμένεις απάντηση τώρα; Από μένα; Πού να ξέρω εγώ;

Αυτό το βιβλίο είχε άφθονες αναφορές στο Συμπόσιο και τον Φαίδρο ή Περί Έρωτος του Πλάτωνα.
Από αρχαία ελληνική γραμματεία δεν ξέρω περισσότερα απ' όσα η Ζουμπουλία Αμπατζίδου (το γένος Καραγκιόζη), άρα 'έχασα' πολλές από τις αναφορές αυτές. Αναφορές περί έρωτα, και τι είναι.
Και φυσικά αυτό το βιβλίο έρχεται σε διάλογο με τα δύο αυτά έργα του Πλάτωνα. Αυτοί να διαλογίζονται και 'γω να τους κοιτάω σαν Βούδας. Ακριβώς όπως εκείνον που είχε η Μαριλένα Δορκοφίκη, η συμπεθέρα της Ζουμπουλίας.

Άρα με όσα κατάφερα να 'πιάσω', ήρθα στο συμπέρασμα ότι αυτό το βιβλίο ήταν ωραίο, που όμως με απαιτητική γραφή, που πρέπει να ξαναδιαβαστεί αφότου εντρυφήσω στον Πλάτωνα πρώτα (σαν τα μούτρα μου θα τα κάνω προβλέπω). Και ότι αυτός ήταν ο πρώτος μου Μαν αλλά όχι ο τελευταίος. Ίσως συνεχίσω με τους Αδερφούς του Ιωσήφ αντί με Μαγικό Βουνό, διότι μόνο κι από τον τίτλο εγώ θα είμαι πότε Βούδας πότε Κούδας.

Βαθμολογία: 8/10

review.text: Super je kad za jedno lenjo popodne koje provedete u ljuljašci izvaljeni kao pola konja ili, da se otmenije izrazim, kao brkati nemački spisatelj u poslednjoj trećini života, sljuštite novelu kojoj se dugo niste vraćali a ona je baš toliko genijalna koliko ste zapamtili. Sve je ovde taman kako treba, istovremeno prozračno od letnje svetlosti, erotično-čulno i na santimetar iznad truleži i smrti, sa klasično odmerenim omažima Platonu i Ničeu i ironičnim osmehom i prigušenim sentimentom, a prevod Anice Savić-Rebac je takav da mislim da nikad neću pročitati original, neka im ga. * iste večeri se još jednom potvrdilo i da je Viskontijeva adaptacija najbolja zamisliva adaptacija Smrti u Veneciji sa najboljom podelom uloga u svemiru, ali i da sam pre 20+ godina bila u pravu (zlikovački mali purista, da, ali U PRAVU) što sam se gnušala ubačenih flešbekova na Malerovu biografiju, Ašenbah nije Gustav Maler, poyy grofe i hvala za sve ostalo.

review.text: Diese Novelle ist eines meiner Trauma aus dem Deutsch-LK zu Oberstufenzeiten. Ich habe alles daran gehasst, anfangen von meiner Deutschlehrerin bis zur Visconti-Verfilmung. Mich hat nichts daran interessiert als Heranwachsender, weder die Mythologie, noch die Anspielung und schon gar nicht die Themen Tod und Homoerotik. Die Kursarbeit war ein Desaster und ich muss mich wundern, dass ich nach der zweiten Mann-Lektüre (Lotte in Weimar, wie bestialisch kann man als Deutschlehrerin noch sein? Da vergeht einem die letzte Lust am großen Dichter) das Kapitel Mann für mich nicht schloss. Später las ich die Buddenbrooks und den Zauberberg mit Begeisterung. Nach knapp 40 Jahren habe ich es nun nochmal probiert mit dem Aschenbach unterzugehen. Ich hatte so gar noch das alte Buch aus Schulzeiten mit unzähligen handschriftlichen Notizen am Rand. Was habe ich gekämpft, um alle Todesboten zu finden, um die Vergänglichkeit in den Anspielungen aufzuspüren und mich mit der antikisierten Sprache anzufreunden. Aber es bleibt dabei: das Einzige, was ich von den Griechen mag, bleibt der Gyrosteller. Ich bin zu blöd und/oder zu uninteressiert für diese Novelle.

review.text: So really, and from an entirely structural and writer's craft point of view (and of course also in my humble opinion), Thomas Mann's 1911 novella Der Tod in Venedig ( Death in Venice ) is most definitely and absolutely brilliant story telling, richly nuanced, full of both exquisite descriptiveness and a power of observation that actually almost seems to even defy reality. And yes, I do therefore also very much and very vociferously proclaim and state that for many readers, including myself, Thomas Mann's way with words is indeed considered utterly magical, that Thomas Mann's writing and his stylistics are absolutely and marvellously wonderful perfection in every manner of speaking. However, and even though with regard to the "how" of Thomas Mann's penmanship, I do in fact and rather obviously from my words above generally think of him as one of my hands-down favourite German authors (and really also usually above and beyond any and all specifically style related criticisms), I nevertheless and rather frustratingly also often do tend to encounter in Thomas Mann's prose (in both his shorter fiction and actually even more so in his novels) an annoying and even rather majorly problematic at times dichotomy and tension between writing style (between Thomas Mann's expressivity and descriptiveness) and that which is used as thematics and contents. And yes indeed, Der Tod in Venedig is most definitely no exception to this, because honestly, albeit that I am totally in reading love with Thomas Mann's writing as it is presented both structurally and with regard to how he uses his printed words to create such an interesting and wonderfully descriptive portrait of turn of the century Venice, the actual contents and the specific thematics of Der Tod in Venedig have always left me both rather unsatisfied at best and also often majorly and unfailingly uncomfortable (with feelings of being far too often full of animosity for and anger at main protagonist Gustaf von Aschenbach as well as far too much being majorly creeped out by von Aschenbach's sexual deviances). For really, Gustaf von Aschenbach is not AT ALL a character I have ever (and since first reading Der Tod in Venedig in 1996) found either all that relatable to or someone I could remotely accept and handle as even just a very casual acquaintance. And yes, that throughout Der Tod in Venedig Gustaf von Aschenbach blithely, selfishly and very much arrogantly keeps following young and innocent Tadzio around throughout Venice, thereby delaying von Aschenbach departing from Venice even though there is an epidemic raging (and yes, even if this homoeroticism is mostly just endangering von Aschenbach's own life by making him act foolishly and imprudently), this has always made Der Tod in Venedig a mostly not all that pleasant and engagingly satisfying personal reading experience, with the only reason why Der Tod in Venedig still rates with three stars for me, being that I do in fact very much enjoy how with regard to style and written expression the story of Gustaf von Aschenbach's decline and death in Venice is being told to us by Thomas Mann, even though, even as I personally really cannot stand and cannot at times even stomach the specific contents of Der Tod in Venedig and especially the pedophilic homoeroticism of Gustaf von Aschenbach.

However, and even though with regard to the "how" of Thomas Mann's penmanship, I do in fact and rather obviously from my words above generally think of him as one of my hands-down favourite German authors (and really also usually above and beyond any and all specifically style related criticisms), I nevertheless and rather frustratingly also often do tend to encounter in Thomas Mann's prose (in both his shorter fiction and actually even more so in his novels) an annoying and even rather majorly problematic at times dichotomy and tension between writing style (between Thomas Mann's expressivity and descriptiveness) and that which is used as thematics and contents. For really, Gustaf von Aschenbach is not AT ALL a character I have ever (and since first reading Der Tod in Venedig in 1996) found either all that relatable to or someone I could remotely accept and handle as even just a very casual acquaintance. And yes, that throughout Der Tod in Venedig Gustaf von Aschenbach blithely, selfishly and very much arrogantly keeps following young and innocent Tadzio around throughout Venice, thereby delaying von Aschenbach departing from Venice even though there is an epidemic raging (and yes, even if this homoeroticism is mostly just endangering von Aschenbach's own life by making him act foolishly and imprudently), this has always made Der Tod in Venedig a mostly not all that pleasant and engagingly satisfying personal reading experience, with the only reason why Der Tod in Venedig still rates with three stars for me, being that I do in fact very much enjoy how with regard to style and written expression the story of Gustaf von Aschenbach's decline and death in Venice is being told to us by Thomas Mann, even though, even as I personally really cannot stand and cannot at times even stomach the specific contents of Der Tod in Venedig and especially the pedophilic homoeroticism of Gustaf von Aschenbach.

review.text: #JunioLGTB | La ciudad de los canales sirve de escenario para contar una historia que narra el encuentro de un escritor en su vejez con un niño llamado Tadzio. Escrita por Thomas Mann en 1912, se dice que la novela tiene un tintes biográficos al relatar un evento que le sucedió al autor en su viaje por Venecia. La novela no se torna explícita, por el contrario, está narrada de manera muy introspectiva y filosófica. Con estilo muy intelectual, la trama empieza algo lenta y descriptiva pero luego logra estar a la par del desarrollo de la historia. La muerte en Venecia, posee una trama polémica e inusual. Nos muestra el amor platónico de un hombre adulto con todo un conflicto espiritual y su búsqueda de la belleza y el amor. Esa búsqueda por la que los artistas viven y mueren. También está el dilema ético que abarca hasta qué punto es correcto sentir una atracción de este tipo pero todo se desarrolla en la imaginación del protagonista donde la pasión y la tragedia se apoderan de él para llevarlo a un final trágico narrado de manera magistral. 🌟🌟🌟🌟
review.text_raw:
#JunioLGTB | La ciudad de los canales sirve de escenario para contar una historia que narra el encuentro de un escritor en su vejez con un niño llamado Tadzio. Escrita por Thomas Mann en 1912, se dice que la novela tiene un tintes biográficos al relatar un evento que le sucedió al autor en su viaje por Venecia. La novela no se torna explícita, por el contrario, está narrada de manera muy introspectiva y filosófica. Con estilo muy intelectual, la trama empieza algo lenta y descriptiva pero luego logra estar a la par del desarrollo de la historia.

La muerte en Venecia, posee una trama polémica e inusual. Nos muestra el amor platónico de un hombre adulto con todo un conflicto espiritual y su búsqueda de la belleza y el amor. Esa búsqueda por la que los artistas viven y mueren. También está el dilema ético que abarca hasta qué punto es correcto sentir una atracción de este tipo pero todo se desarrolla en la imaginación del protagonista donde la pasión y la tragedia se apoderan de él para llevarlo a un final trágico narrado de manera magistral.

🌟🌟🌟🌟

review.text: Es ist Thomas Mann, was soll ich sagen??? Ich liebe einfach seine langen, auf allerbeste Weise, gut komponierten Sätze. In der Novelle geht es um den 50- jährigen Schriftsteller Gustav von Aschenbach. Er hält sich Anfang des 20. Jahrhunderts in Venedig auf. Auf einem Spaziergang entdeckt der den 14 jährigen Tadziu, der in ihm eine Leidenschaft entfesselt. Es führt sogar soweit, dass er ihm regelrecht nachstellt. Mann beschäftigt sich hier mit dem Alter und der Jugend, mit der Disziplin und Leistung und stellt dies dem Chaos, der Leidenschaft und der Verruchtheit gegenüber. Dies nur sehr oberflächlich beschrieben. Man kommt nicht umhin in diesem Text autobiografische Eigenschaften zu entdecken. Wobei Mann sich seiner Anziehung gegenüber dem männlichen Geschlecht nie hingegeben hat. Er hat -im Gegensatz zu Aschenbach- nie ein Begehren zugelassen, welches damals nicht nur nicht angemessen, sondern strafbar war. Hier spreche ich von homosexuellen Beziehungen und meine damit nicht, dass sich ein erwachsener Mann zu einem Kind hingezogen fühlt. Ich muss zugeben , dass dies schon schwierig zu lesen war. Aber für mich steht hier die Reinheit und Schönheit dem Alter/älter werden gegenüber. Aber auch bestimmte Lebenswege einzuhalten/ Erwartungen zu erfüllen, eine Maske zu tragen. Dazu gehört, dass Leistung für ein erfolgreiches Leben oberste Priorität hat. Alles andere wird als Verfall oder Sünde angesehen. Schwierigkeiten bereiten mir oftmals Manns Bezüge zur griechischen Mythologie. Dahingehend würde ich gerne mehr verstehen. Wer also ein gutes Werk zum Einstieg in die griechische Mythologie kennt. Ich bin für jeden Tipp dankbar.

review.text: I had thought that I had read "Death in Venice" before, but reading it the narrative seemed oddly unfamilar , I recognised Ashenbach's conversation with the hairdresser, his getting his hair dyed, and given make up; but the rest had slipped my mind. It is possible I suppose that I had never read it, just flicked through the story and just happened to read that one passage. Anyway reading this time, whether for the first time or not, I noticed the piling up of references to death, not as intensely in Bruges la morte , but still repeated and frequent, and Bruges like Venice was a great trading and mercantile centre of medieval europe, and those two reminded me of a third commercial centre, not as great, but also the place where Thomas Mann was born, Luebeck. All three towns that had long since passed their economic heydays. Dying towns, though transitioning at different speeds towards having tourist economies. Venice in this story is already a tourist trap, just one that has a bad smell. Bruges la Morte offers to its main character an illusion of resurrection or perhaps reincarnation. Thomas Mann goes in a different direction. Death here offers a reconciliation for Ashenbach, the main character, a chance to unify aspects of character, the public man with his hidden shadow. This is a story that in any case feels familiar because of the themes and tropes across his novels and stories. The mixed family heritage of a character which is linked to a duality in their character, art and literature, travel.

Bruges la Morte offers to its main character an illusion of resurrection or perhaps reincarnation. Thomas Mann goes in a different direction. Death here offers a reconciliation for Ashenbach, the main character, a chance to unify aspects of character, the public man with his hidden shadow.

This is a story that in any case feels familiar because of the themes and tropes across his novels and stories. The mixed family heritage of a character which is linked to a duality in their character, art and literature, travel.

review.text: ویرایش شده بعد از تماشای اقتباس ایتالیایی ویسکونتی از رمان: رمان درباره سیر انحطاط یک رمان نویس شهیر آلمانی ست که اعتبار جهانی اش را نادیده میگیرد و در جستجوی بازیافتن روحیه ی متهور و طبع غماز سفری ناگهانی را آغاز میکند و سر از ونیز در می آورد. در آنجا دلباختهء پسرکی لهستانی میشود که منبع الهام هنری او میگردد. این دلباختگی سرمنشا شروع یک انحطاط یا تعارض اخلاقی - فکری در نویسنده میشود و نهایتا در ونیز میمیرد. تعارض اینجاست که اشنباخ هنرمندی در جستجوی تعادل و اخلاق است اما در می یابد در برابر زیبایی اصیل توانایی مقاومت ندارد مسئله اصلی این است که قهرمان داستان بفهمد هنرمند هرگز قدرت خلق و درک و احاطه یافتن بر زیبایی اصیل را ندارد. زیبایی اصیل فقط محصول طبیعت است و هنر را به آن راهی نیست. هنرمندان اگرچه دلباخته زیبایی هستند و جان در این راه میگذارند اما در برابر آن حقیر هستند "مقدمه ابتدای کتاب به نکات خیلی مهمی اشاره کرده اما گویا نکته ها را خیلی روشن و واضح به هم متصل نکرده است. به هر حال من با«هنر دکادانس» یا هنر انحطاط آشنا نبودم که خوب معرفی ش کرده." مقدمه خوب کتاب به این نکته اشاره دارد که نویسنده در واقع ضمن توجه به جهان اخلاقی فردریش نیچه آن را با سرانجامی که برای گوستاو اشنباخ رقم میزند نقد میکند. جهانی که نیچه پیش روی هنرمندان گشود پیوند هنر و زندگی را نوید میداد بر خلاف جریان "هنر انحطاط" که هنر را با مرگ پیوند داده بود. اما توماس مان با انداختن قهرمانش در ورطهء نابودی، نشان داد که وعدهء نیچه نیز دروغ است اقتباس ویسکونتی خیلی به رمان وفادار است اما شخصیت اشنباخ را به جای رمان نویس تبدیل کرده به آهنگساز

review.text: A ver, Thomas Mann, tocas temas interesantes, pero ¿era necesario hacerlo de forma tan tediosa? Sí, es un escritor de esos rompedores en su juventud que se convirtió en el statu quo , en la ortodoxia cultural ahora que es mayor, y se supone que lo vemos todo desde su punto de vista, pero de nuevo, Thomas Mann ¿no era posible escribir esto de manera que me provocara menos sopor? Hay ahí una historia muy interesante sobre cómo una ciudad dependiente del turismo y sobre todo sus autoridades, intentan tapar una epidemia, un problema de salud pública, por miedo a que los turistas dejen de venir y ellos dejen de recibir ese rico, rico dinero. Pero esa historia no se llama "La muerte en Venecia". Y en serio, sé qué quiere contarme Thomas Mann. La forma en la que quiere hacerlo no me puede dar más pereza.
review.text_raw:

Sí, es un escritor de esos rompedores en su juventud que se convirtió en el statu quo, en la ortodoxia cultural ahora que es mayor, y se supone que lo vemos todo desde su punto de vista, pero de nuevo, Thomas Mann ¿no era posible escribir esto de manera que me provocara menos sopor?

Hay ahí una historia muy interesante sobre cómo una ciudad dependiente del turismo y sobre todo sus autoridades, intentan tapar una epidemia, un problema de salud pública, por miedo a que los turistas dejen de venir y ellos dejen de recibir ese rico, rico dinero. Pero esa historia no se llama "La muerte en Venecia".

Y en serio, sé qué quiere contarme Thomas Mann. La forma en la que quiere hacerlo no me puede dar más pereza.

review.text: #JulhoNobel "Assim, Aschenbach sentia um regozijo obscuro pelos fenómenos oficialmente encobertos nas vielas sujas de Veneza - terrível segredo da cidade, que se confundia com o seu próprio segredo e em cuja preservação também ele estava tão empenhado. Porque ao apaixonado só preocupava que Tadzio pudesse partir, e reconhecia, não sem horror, que já não saberia viver se tal acontecesse." Esta Morte em Veneza foi uma agradável surpresa. Não é qualquer escritor que sabe passar tão bem para o papel a obsessão de um homem cinquentão por um jovem adolescente e manter o meu interesse nesta história em que, praticamente, não se passa nada. Talvez por isso o senhor Tomás tenha sido laureado com o prémio Nobel. Merecido, sem dúvida.
review.text_raw:
#JulhoNobel

"Assim, Aschenbach sentia um regozijo obscuro pelos fenómenos oficialmente encobertos nas vielas sujas de Veneza - terrível segredo da cidade, que se confundia com o seu próprio segredo e em cuja preservação também ele estava tão empenhado. Porque ao apaixonado só preocupava que Tadzio pudesse partir, e reconhecia, não sem horror, que já não saberia viver se tal acontecesse."

Esta Morte em Veneza foi uma agradável surpresa. Não é qualquer escritor que sabe passar tão bem para o papel a obsessão de um homem cinquentão por um jovem adolescente e manter o meu interesse nesta história em que, praticamente, não se passa nada. Talvez por isso o senhor Tomás tenha sido laureado com o prémio Nobel. Merecido, sem dúvida.

review.text: Ho paura della fine, non voglio più un inizio Quando Tadzio emerge dalla spuma del mare del Lido di Venezia agli occhi di Aschenbach è come una Venere di Botticelli, ma bellezza efebica di adolescente e i suoi riccioli biondi sono bagnati e gocciolano lungo le spalle, le ascelle delicatamente ancora implumi, la pelle bianca come alabastro. E poi c'è il primo sguardo nel salone dell'Hotel des Bains quando i suoi occhi grigio azzurro incontrano quelli liquidi, velati di vecchiaia di Aschenbach. Sono due traiettorie che si incrociano per volontà o come calamitate da invisibili scariche elettriche. E questa recrudescenza dei sensi nel vecchio scrittore è qualcosa che lo scuote profondamente perché non era prevista, perché rappresenta un contro natura per l'oggetto scelto dalle sue attenzioni e per la differenza di età, qualcosa di torbido e corrotto, come il morbo di colera che sta calando sulla città. Una recrudescenza di sensi che Aschenbach non aveva contemplato quando, costantemente chiuso dentro l'incantesimo della sua arte di scrittore e chino sui suoi scritti nella casa di Monaco di Baviera, per sfuggire ai suoi dover decide di strappare alla sua metodica esistenza un mese di vacanza sabbatica. Fare un viaggio per trovare nuove ispirazioni e fecondi slanci creativi. Il viaggio, come la guerra direbbe Tommaso Marinetti, rappresenta una forma di igiene, perché il viaggio cura e rigenera corpo e spirito, lo ripulisce dalle incrostazioni del lavoro febbrile, della vita polverosa di ogni giorno. E a Venezia..., Venezia è sempre un sogno. Lo fu per il premio Nobel baltico Iosif Brodskij che ogni anno per vent'anni vi fece ritorno, lo fu per Proust nel suo viaggio della maturità che segnò il risvegliarsi della memoria. Aschenbach vi arriva dall'acqua, dalla laguna. Perché è così diverso dall'arrivarci attraversando la stretta striscia di terra che collega Venezia al continente. Visione dal mare di un orizzonte di salotti profilati di colonne e cupole, e merletti e trine di marmo bianco marezzato di rosa, e i suoi piccoli ponti a dorso d'asino. Ma compiere il proprio sogno, percorrerlo fino in fondo significa poi non avere più nulla davanti a sé, perché nel compimento c'è la fine. Ho paura della fine, non voglio più un inizio canta Roberto Vecchioni. E c'è tutta una simbologia di presagi di morte che percorre questo breve capolavoro già così emblematico nel titolo - che è uno spoiler gigantesco - il panno nero della macchina fotografica abbandonato sulla spiaggia deserta, come il nero della gondola, l'unica tra tutte le imbarcazioni che non ha mai cambiato nel tempo, unica che, all'occorrenza, diventa culla (nera) che accoglie e trasporta il feretro nel suo ultimo silente viaggio, come carrozza reale... Simbolo di morte l'aria mefitica, dolciastra di scirocco malato che risale dalle acque stagnanti dei canali, portando l'infezione di colera in città e che il gioco delle autorità sanitarie italiane tendera' a dissimulare nascondendola ai turisti. Bellissimo è anche sapere, ma forse ad alcuni toglierà quell'aura che riveste il breve romanzo di poesia, che in questo racconto Mann trasfuse molto di sé, perché è così carezzevolmente autobiografico. Quel viaggio a Venezia Mann lo fece davvero con la sua famiglia scendendo proprio al Lido all'Hotel des Bains dove, davvero, conobbe una compagnia di ospiti polacchi, e da lì fuggire anzitempo per le prime avvisaglie di sporadiche infezioni di colera che trapelavano in città. Ma Thomas Mann non aveva come Aschenbach qualcosa che là lo trattenesse, nessun sogno da compiere, nessuna bellezza in cui sperare di vedersi riflesso, nessuna umiliazione inflittasi volontariamente, o inflitta da Eros che implacabile e cieco lancia le sue saette. Poi c'è la sua versione, del Professore, di Roberto Vecchioni che sa sempre mescolare letteratura e musica, con dolcezza e timbro di voce, delicatamente rauco e inconfondibile. youtu.be/2-53kZLVKjs

Quando Tadzio emerge dalla spuma del mare del Lido di Venezia agli occhi di Aschenbach è come una Venere di Botticelli, ma bellezza efebica di adolescente e i suoi riccioli biondi sono bagnati e gocciolano lungo le spalle, le ascelle delicatamente ancora implumi, la pelle bianca come alabastro.

E poi c'è il primo sguardo nel salone dell'Hotel des Bains quando i suoi occhi grigio azzurro incontrano quelli liquidi, velati di vecchiaia di Aschenbach.
Sono due traiettorie che si incrociano per volontà o come calamitate da invisibili scariche elettriche.

E questa recrudescenza dei sensi nel vecchio scrittore è qualcosa che lo scuote profondamente perché non era prevista, perché rappresenta un contro natura per l'oggetto scelto dalle sue attenzioni e per la differenza di età, qualcosa di torbido e corrotto, come il morbo di colera che sta calando sulla città.
Una recrudescenza di sensi che Aschenbach non aveva contemplato quando, costantemente chiuso dentro l'incantesimo della sua arte di scrittore e chino sui suoi scritti nella casa di Monaco di Baviera, per sfuggire ai suoi dover decide di strappare alla sua metodica esistenza un mese di vacanza sabbatica.
Fare un viaggio per trovare nuove ispirazioni e fecondi slanci creativi.
Il viaggio, come la guerra direbbe Tommaso Marinetti, rappresenta una forma di igiene, perché il viaggio cura e rigenera corpo e spirito, lo ripulisce dalle incrostazioni del lavoro febbrile, della vita polverosa di ogni giorno.

E a Venezia..., Venezia è sempre un sogno.
Lo fu per il premio Nobel baltico Iosif Brodskij che ogni anno per vent'anni vi fece ritorno, lo fu per Proust nel suo viaggio della maturità che segnò il risvegliarsi della memoria.

Perché è così diverso dall'arrivarci attraversando la stretta striscia di terra che collega Venezia al continente.
Visione dal mare di un orizzonte di salotti profilati di colonne e cupole, e merletti e trine di marmo bianco marezzato di rosa, e i suoi piccoli ponti a dorso d'asino.

Ma compiere il proprio sogno, percorrerlo fino in fondo significa poi non avere più nulla davanti a sé, perché nel compimento c'è la fine.
Ho paura della fine, non voglio più un inizio canta Roberto Vecchioni.

E c'è tutta una simbologia di presagi di morte che percorre questo breve capolavoro già così emblematico nel titolo - che è uno spoiler gigantesco - il panno nero della macchina fotografica abbandonato sulla spiaggia deserta, come il nero della gondola, l'unica tra tutte le imbarcazioni che non ha mai cambiato nel tempo, unica che, all'occorrenza, diventa culla (nera) che accoglie e trasporta il feretro nel suo ultimo silente viaggio, come carrozza reale...
Simbolo di morte l'aria mefitica, dolciastra di scirocco malato che risale dalle acque stagnanti dei canali, portando l'infezione di colera in città e che il gioco delle autorità sanitarie italiane tendera' a dissimulare nascondendola ai turisti.

Bellissimo è anche sapere, ma forse ad alcuni toglierà quell'aura che riveste il breve romanzo di poesia, che in questo racconto Mann trasfuse molto di sé, perché è così carezzevolmente autobiografico.
Quel viaggio a Venezia Mann lo fece davvero con la sua famiglia scendendo proprio al Lido all'Hotel des Bains dove, davvero, conobbe una compagnia di ospiti polacchi, e da lì fuggire anzitempo per le prime avvisaglie di sporadiche infezioni di colera che trapelavano in città.
Ma Thomas Mann non aveva come Aschenbach qualcosa che là lo trattenesse, nessun sogno da compiere, nessuna bellezza in cui sperare di vedersi riflesso, nessuna umiliazione inflittasi volontariamente, o inflitta da Eros che implacabile e cieco lancia le sue saette.

Poi c'è la sua versione, del Professore, di Roberto Vecchioni che sa sempre mescolare letteratura e musica, con dolcezza e timbro di voce, delicatamente rauco e inconfondibile.

review.text: Oh so tragic and rather melodramatic...or maybe I'm just remembering the 1971 Luchino Visconti movie version? A man longing to regain the vitality and vigor of youth, goes on holiday and turns ghoulish at the sight of a young Adonis. Death in Venice walks the line of appreciation and pedophilia. Having no problem with homosexuality, but not being down with the man-boy love thing, I cringed more than once. "Don't cross the invisible line!" I may have shouted in my head more than once while reading, definitely while watching. But if you can move beyond the physical, and I did, Death in Venice truly captures the essence of an old, decrepit man trying to recapture the essence of youth. I understood the meaning when I read/watched this as a early 20 something. As a middle-aged man with my aches, pains and regrets, I truly understand the main character's "desire" deep down in my soul. Life and good health do not last forever.
review.text_raw:
Oh so tragic and rather melodramatic...or maybe I'm just remembering the 1971 Luchino Visconti movie version?

A man longing to regain the vitality and vigor of youth, goes on holiday and turns ghoulish at the sight of a young Adonis.

Death in Venice walks the line of appreciation and pedophilia. Having no problem with homosexuality, but not being down with the man-boy love thing, I cringed more than once. "Don't cross the invisible line!" I may have shouted in my head more than once while reading, definitely while watching.

But if you can move beyond the physical, and I did, Death in Venice truly captures the essence of an old, decrepit man trying to recapture the essence of youth. I understood the meaning when I read/watched this as a early 20 something. As a middle-aged man with my aches, pains and regrets, I truly understand the main character's "desire" deep down in my soul. Life and good health do not last forever.

review.text: Orta yaşlarda tanınmış bir yazarın (belki de T. Mann’ın kendisi) sanatsal güzelliğin peşinde sürüklenişini, kaçışın, çaresizliğin ve mutluluğun birlikte seyrini anlatan kısa bir roman. Güzelliğin sevgiye, sevginin aşka, aşkın tutkuya, tutkunun ölüme dönüşümü ana tema. Bir asır önce yazılan bu eser dönemin edebiyat anlayışına uygun bir göz kamaştırıcı uslupla yazılmış, ancak günümüzde bu dil biraz şatafatlı hatta biraz da zorlamalı oluyor bana göre. Klasik olarak okunması gereken bir roman.

review.text: A very powerful and interesting novella; I loved the writing, so I'm definitely keen to read more by Thomas Mann in the future.
review.text_raw: A very powerful and interesting novella; I loved the writing, so I'm definitely keen to read more by Thomas Mann in the future.

review.text: One of the great homoerotic writings of the late 20th and also the subject of the epic Visconti film. This is, IMHO, not Mann's strongest writing (Magic Mountain and Faustus were better), but still a beautiful evocation of melancholy and death in Venice. The age old problem of separating the artist from his work really stands out here. For me, it depends on how related the two are. Celine was a rabid anti-Semite, but his books are not Nazi tracts about intl Zionism. Faulkner was a Southern racist (and philanderer), but his books are full of three-dimensional black characters and every shade of humanity. In Jo Walton’s excellent Thesssaly trilogy, the last volume defines Macedonians (men attracted to men (and sometimes to women) versus Athenians (men attracted to boys). My problem with Mann is that he carried over his Athenian tendencies to his books. Looking back now, Death in Venice (despite being lofty, beautiful prose) is an ode to pedophilic love. Compare this to Lolita by Nabakov. Humber Humbert is self-aware that he has a problem and he is an awful human (Lolita is a piece of work too). The bath boy story in Death in Venice was based on his own experience.l, a 10 year old. When he was 36That is just gross. It is an admission of guilt that the book advances Tadzik’s age to 14. And his sexual obsession with his son at 13 when he was 50? 🤢 I am not sure that he was very self-aware and that is what disappoints me now. A fallen idol.

The bath boy story in Death in Venice was based on his own experience.l, a 10 year old. When he was 36That is just gross. It is an admission of guilt that the book advances Tadzik’s age to 14. And his sexual obsession with his son at 13 when he was 50? 🤢

I am not sure that he was very self-aware and that is what disappoints me now. A fallen idol.

review.text: 96 yıllık kitaba spoiler alarmı koymalı mıyım? enter. nobel ödülü almış bir kitabın kurgusunun tam ortasındaki ağır pedofili içeriğini gördüğümde yaşadığım şaşkınlığı düşünün ya da kitabı bitirdikten sonra deli olmadığımı kendime kanıtlamak amacıyla diğer değerlendirmelere baktığımda kimsenin bundan bahsetmediğini gördüğümdeki şaşkınlığı düşünün veya hazır başlamışken thomas mann'ın büyülü bir kalemi olduğunu ve anlattığı restoran, sahil veya köprülere ışınlanabildiğimizi, özellikle sahil sahnelerinde ets tur reklamı gibi bir hızla çalışma masasından şezlonga götürüldüğümüzü hayal edin. hayal etmek istemiyorsanız da sorun değil thomas sizi oraya götüremiyorsa da behçet necatigil sizi o sahile (ya da GENEL OLARAK) TEKMELİYOR BEHÇET BU NASIL CÜMLELER sakin ol, bazı cümleler 3 kelimelik olabilir. felsefi yanından ziyade kurgusuna ve imgelerine odaklandım , felsefi yanını anlamak için biraz yardıma ihtiyacım var, daha o kadar da zeki bir kız değilim ilk ilber ortaylı kitabımı yeni bitirdim. eminim bu kitapla ilgili birisi bir deneme yazmıştır. bu kitap bir süre benimle kalacak çünkü bir şehrin ve bir adamın çürüyüşünü bu şekilde okumak, inanır mısınız, insanı biraz etkiliyor. kitap hala aklıma geliyorsa denememi okuyup burayı editlemeyi unutmayacağımı umuyorum, lütfen 14 yaşındaki çocukları rahat bırakın, iyi günler

review.text: I bet someone could write a masterpiece by taking this book’s premise and elongating it into a fuller exploration of the child-adult love taboo. Oh, really? Oh. This book really does read like a Lolita written 40 years prior with Lo’s gender switched and a premature ending just before things get really interesting (if you know what I mean). Death in Venice is equally engrossing and sports a protagonist, Aschenbach, who’s as well developed, far more relatable, and nearly as interesting as our dear Humbert Humbert. The novel does feel cut-off though, as if Mann were afraid to explore the tale any further, and it also includes a not-so-faint whiff of moralizing that’s rather absent in Nabokov’s version. Aschenbach’s portrayal as a driven, successful, and now weary late middle-aged writer is so convincing that I was surprised to learn that Mann wrote this in his mid-30s. The characterization’s so good, in fact, that I was sure it had to be mostly autobiographical. Maybe, maybe not. Either way, it’s damn good writing that’s on display for too few pages. I’ll be returning to Mann, and hopefully soon.

review.text: أولي تجاربي مع توماس مان و كان فيها يبحث بشخصيته آشنباخ عن الجمال المطلق فأصابني بالنعاس لأكثر من مرة . تجربة غير موفقة، حاولت معها كثيرًا فى انتظار شئ محدد لكن كل ما آلت اليه الرواية لم يرق لي .. فى مدينة الجمال البندقية كان الموت يحوم حولها فأصبحت بجمالها مدينة للموت و الوباء الذي انتشر فى أركانها. ياخذنا الكاتب المشهور فى رحلته و أجازته للبحث عن أفكاره من جديد فى هدوء و إعادة النظر مرة اخري فى كل شئ، ياخده الطريق الي مدينة البندقية و بها يقابل العائلة البولونية و طفلهم المراهق تاديسو الذي يسحر الكاتب بجماله المطلق . ‎‏ النهاية أعجبتني كثيرًا لكنها لم تشفع للعمل الذي افقدني شغفي به .

review.text: SHOCKER: This is a novella by Thomas Mann and nevertheless, I really liked it! In this book, Venice turns into a gothic place, full of decay, illness, and melancholy – Mann masterfully evokes a haunting, oppressive atmosphere in which he sets a “tragedy about the loss of dignity” (Mann) revolving around Gustav von Aschenbach, an aging writer. The vanitas/death-motif is ever-present: A red-haired stranger on a graveyard, all kinds of ferrymen (paid und unpaid), tons of mythological allusions, the contrast between youth and age, and so on. By means of discussing Aschenbach, Mann also does some (very interesting) self-analysis regarding his life as an artist, and it could be claimed that in this respect, “Death in Venice” is a sequel to his “Tonio Kröger”. But it is another autobiographical theme of the novella that obviously led to a severe conflict between Mann and his wife Katia: Aschenbach falls in love with a 14-year-old boy (he never touches him or even talks to him though). As Katia Mann confirmed in her memoir, many aspects of the novel go back to a real voyage to Venice that the Mann family undertook. Add that Mann described his novella as “a strange moral self-castigation through a book”, and go figure (there is no hint whatsoever that Mann was a pedophile, but many hints that he was actually gay). After realizing her husband's homosexual tendencies, Katia was apparently shocked - some people even claim that this was the real reason she went on to stay at several sanatoria, most notably in Davos, a place which inspired Thomas Mann to write his masterpiece "The Magic Mountain". The only reason for me not giving this book five stars is a highly subjective and a constant problem that I am having with Mann: His language is highly artistic bordering on artificial, and it always keeps me at arm’s length from his stories. Some people see his specific sound as the core of his literary genius, but while I do not find it outright bad, it certainly does not captivate me. At least this novella gives me an idea why this man was awarded a Nobel! :-)

By means of discussing Aschenbach, Mann also does some (very interesting) self-analysis regarding his life as an artist, and it could be claimed that in this respect, “Death in Venice” is a sequel to his “Tonio Kröger”. But it is another autobiographical theme of the novella that obviously led to a severe conflict between Mann and his wife Katia: Aschenbach falls in love with a 14-year-old boy (he never touches him or even talks to him though). As Katia Mann confirmed in her memoir, many aspects of the novel go back to a real voyage to Venice that the Mann family undertook. Add that Mann described his novella as “a strange moral self-castigation through a book”, and go figure (there is no hint whatsoever that Mann was a pedophile, but many hints that he was actually gay). At least this novella gives me an idea why this man was awarded a Nobel! :-)

review.text: A short novella, just 83pp. in my edition but one which is immersed in debates about art, creation, beauty and desire. It's deeply allusive (Plato's 'Phaedrus' and 'Symposium', Nietzsche's Apollonian/ Dionysian dichotomy from his 'The Birth of Tragedy') and relies on classical, particularly Athenian, intertexts: the surly, 'uncanny' gondolier who becomes a Charon figure rowing von Aschenbach across the Styx to an 'underworld' from which he never returns; the plethora of beautiful male adolescents who appear in Greek myths: Narcissus, Hyacinthus, Hermaphroditus. For me, the dynamics of desire are also reminiscent of Petrarch's sonnets and their Renaissance imitations which delineate the pathologies of erotic obsession which has to remain unconsummated to remain alive. That both Petrarch and also poets like Philip Sidney used the love sonnet sequence as a vehicle to contemplate the writing of poetry itself and the creation of art and beauty, underpins this novella. I've seen Mann described at humourless but disagree: von Aschenbach himself has the self-consciousness to smile at his own behaviour, at least at the start - though, as his obsession deepens, leading to mental decay and the grotesque behaviour that culminates in his dyeing his hair black and putting on make-up to appear younger, the laughter becomes more savage - reminiscent of the Dionysian dream that leaves von Aschenbach 'shattered, unhinged, powerless in the demon's grip'. Venice is a freighted backdrop: the surpassing beauty of the surface, the underlying decay and slow ruination. I'd say this novella needs and deserves some unpacking, and is a powerful, taut, symbolic piece of writing.

review.text: I wanted to read a book that might speak to a moment in history that is on the precipice of major change, and I thought of Thomas Mann’s novella Death in Venice (1911), set in the time just before WWI, with major changes brewing. Elderly writer Gustav von Aschenbach takes a holiday in Venice. He’s a writer who has embraced and celebrities classical, Platonic ideals. He reveres the past and cites nonettenth century canonical literature and ideas. Then on the beach one day he sees a “beautiful boy” and is over time struck by the way the boy is to him a perfect embodiment of classical Beauty, like Greek sculpture. The boy also may be the embodiment of things passing in Aschenbach’s own life, as he ages, and possibly of a particular epoch of German culture that embraced classical ideals, something possibly disrupted by the ensuing war. (Tell me you think I am wrong here). Now, I am well aware that this book is also widely viewed as homo-erotic, the story of an old man obsessed with a beautiful (teen, maybe 13? 14?) boy. But I hadn’t initially thought of focusing on the elderly narrator’s obsession with a “beautiful boy” at the beach, as much as many others surely have. He does look at the boy for hours, and I recall once seeing the Dirk Bogarde film adaptation of the book that is highly sexual in nature, and it’s an interesting and provocative interpretation, and valid--he has a dream about the boy, too, okay--but I just don’t think Mann’s book is necessarily only about the boy as the sexual object of a predatory old man. I think it’s about his embrace of classical beauty. And you know, he has no physical encounters with the boy. Aschenbach is all about the intellect, primarily: Reason, order, control; he might now wish otherwise, sure, get wildly passionate at the end of his life, but the novella is rife with literary and intellectual allusions from antiquity forward, especially about Greek antiquity and to German works (literary, art-historical, musical, visual) from the 18th century. It’s about the passing of a man, an age, a personal and cultural seismic shift, as much as anything else. And to reference the ongoing? just passed? pandemic, there’s the outbreak of the Venetian cholera epidemic in May 1911 that functions as the backdrop of this story (see title), taking place in what is described in 1911 as a "decaying" city. Mann once said the story was about the "voluptuousness of doom," though he also had said, "what I had especially in mind was that of the artist's dignity." This cholera backdrop works as a precursor (just in my own mind here) of the Great Influenza Epidemic (1918-1920) that in part coincided with the war, an epidemic that would kill tens of millions (in addition to the millions killed in the Great War, of course). PS: This is a kind of weird appendage to this review, but in the past week I have just coincidentally experienced three works of literature that feature voyeurism--Patricia Smith’s The Cry of the Owl (1962); George Simenon’s Monsieur Hire’s Engagement (1933, though I most recently viewed Patrice Leconte’s 1989 film adaptation) and Mann's tale of look-don't-touch. I’m not saying these works have any deliberate connections but they are speaking to a similar issue. Truman Capote once said that all literature is gossip, which might be to say it is always the author dishing on something in the real world, and we want in on that dish. Hitchcock said all film was voyeurism, which is to say we are complicit in wanting to see into these lives. Rear Window may be Hitch's best example, but also consider the opening of Psycho, our peek into the “nooner” affair. Dirty secrets, gossip. Monsieur Hire features a man who falls in love with a woman he watches across his courtyard, with his lights turned off. He never initiates contact with her. The guy in The Cry of the Owl (I’m not done with it yet), newly divorced, watches a neighbor woman in the evenings through her window. He tries to make it clear that he has no sexual interest in the woman; she embodies a domestic ideal and happiness he lacks.
