review.text: What can one say about this classic? A spin on a Narcissus myth? An older man becomes obsessed with a beautiful young teen man? Disgusted by his aging looks, he gets a makeover to make him look younger. Desire gets the most of his better angels. In the mean time, city has a cholera outbreak but mum is the word - don’t scare away the tourists! Shades of today. Shall he tell the boy’s family to flee? Shall he flee? Or is obsessive love too much? Ah, Herr Mann, what a tale and you know how most Greek tales end?
review.text_raw:
What can one say about this classic?

A spin on a Narcissus myth? An older man becomes obsessed with a beautiful young teen man? Disgusted by his aging looks, he gets a makeover to make him look younger. Desire gets the most of his better angels.

In the mean time, city has a cholera outbreak but mum is the word - don’t scare away the tourists! Shades of today. Shall he tell the boy’s family to flee? Shall he flee? Or is obsessive love too much?

Ah, Herr Mann, what a tale and you know how most Greek tales end?

review.text: این میل سفر بود. همین و بس؛ ولی به راستی همچون عارضه‌ی ناگهانی دست داده تا حد دگرگونی عاطفی و پریشانی حواس بالا گرفته بود. خیالش از هنگام کار هنوز نیاسوده، از اعجازها و عجایب هراس‌انگیز و متنوع جهان نمونه‌هایی در نظر می‌آورد: منظره‌ای را می‌دید، منطقه‌ای باتلاقی، منطقه‌ای استوایی زیر آسمانی پوشیده از ابر و مه، نمناک، انبوه و دهشت انگیز. دنیای وحشی کهنه با جزیره‌ها، باطلاق‌ها و دماغه‌های پر از گل و لای... .»

review.text: باید اعتراف کنم که، چیزی ازش نفهمیدم. حتّی برام حوصله سر بر بود که به‌خاطر همون نفهمیدنه. شاید یه‌وختی، یه‌روزی، برگشتم خوندمش و فهمیدمش. ولی فعلاً نه خوشم اومد و نه فهمیدمش. حتّی به‌نظرم یه‌جور تفاخر نویسنده اومد در جهت این‌که نشون بده چقد خوب بلده از وسط یه‌داستان سیاه و سفید، گریز بزنه به‌آلگوری‌ها و.. من نفهمیدم واقعاً، شاید خیلی هم حرکت قشنگی بوده..

review.text: Um livro com laivos pandémicos, bem escrito, mas com uma história que pouco ou nada me disse. Talvez tenha de tentar ler outra coisa deste autor, porque talvez com outro enredo a escrita me tivesse satisfeito mais.
review.text_raw: Um livro com laivos pandémicos, bem escrito, mas com uma história que pouco ou nada me disse. Talvez tenha de tentar ler outra coisa deste autor, porque talvez com outro enredo a escrita me tivesse satisfeito mais.

review.text: Tadzio: *Gives Aschenbach a 'sup nod.* Aschenbach received that smile and turned away with it as though entrusted with a fatal gift. So shaken was he that he had to flee from the lighted terrace and front gardens and seek out with hurried steps the darkness of the park at the rear. Reproaches strangely mixed of tenderness and remonstrance burst from him: "How dare you smile like that! No one is allowed to smile like that!" He flung himself on a bench, his composure gone to the winds, and breathed in the nocturnal fragrance of the garden. He leaned back, with hanging arms, quivering from head to foot, and quite unmanned he whispered the hackneyed phrase of love and longing-impossible in these circumstances, absurd, abject, ridiculous enough, yet sacred too, and not unworthy of honour even here: "I love you!" Who says the French are dramatic? This part made me smile. Wonderful little novella of narcissism and illness, and the captivation of a mirror. And a minor. I'll need to reread this when I'm older. I didn't understand half of the mythological references. Update (11/2): I have re-read the novel and have questioned the correlation between beauty's abyss and divinity. Not necessarily a review. As Jeffrey Eugenides explains, ecstasy is not euphoria—in fact, it is not even pleasure, per definition—but rather a state of disassociation, and sometimes self-transcendence. Dionysus, God of religious ecstasy, serves an allegorical purpose in relation to Aschenbach’s desire of Tadzio. Tadzio is no Dionysus, but, radiant as the sun, he expels incomparable beauty that sends Aschenbach into a state of worship, mindless and devoted. Intoxicated with the poison of Tadzio’s form, Aschenbach rides a seesaw, both sides lined with divinity and abyss; two extremes representative of the paths to heaven and hell. And both have the same outcome. Though Tadzio does not consciously (or actively) expel lust-inducing gestures, Aschenbach is overtaken with “frightful emotional excesses”. In his downfall of ecstasy, which also serves as a heightened sense of adoration, Aschenbach experiences revelation, in that his devotion to an epicenter of human perfection is a power stronger than the pull of societally-implemented logic. All his natural inhibitions flee as he is overcome with allegiance to beauty. Whether that serves as destruction or saving is up to the devotee: routine, cyclical life is just as good as death for Aschenbach and to abscond from this hazy dream-world would be destruction, for it would, devastatingly, “bring him back to his senses”. As the cholera seeps into his bones, he’d become just as sick in his hometown, rotting without the light of Tadzio shining upon him. Inevitable depression would overtake him. Which raises the question: divine or the abyss—is it the same thing? Both outcomes lead Aschenbach to his inevitable downfall, a corpse on the beach. In the end, he achieved both the divine and the abyss, but both extremes elicited the same outcome—the titular Death in Venice. Because his appreciation of beauty led to dedication, and then holiness, the undeniable divinity of beauty affected Aschenbach to the point of religious exaltation. His unconscious has adopted an unshakable devotion, even, blessing “the stranger god”. At a peak so high, desire overtakes him, mindless adoration eats at him, and he is full of that ecstatic poison. And without this feeling, abandonment of beauty would send him to lows incontrovertibly beyond repair. The gorgeousness of ideal human form has taken its toll on a simple man unable to achieve or recreate that perfection. A modern Bacchus, Tadzio has infected Aschenbach just as thoroughly as the Asiatic cholera has, and his decay is brought upon from pure, overtaking voyeurism of what he perceives as godlike comeliness. When a feeling so intense instills itself within a man, to be rid of it is suicide. It is the drug no rehabilitation can cure. Beauty, like religion, results in the user doping up on doses of hope, in exchange offering worship. Aschenbach exits the salon a doomed and “beguiled lover”. To break the spell of enchantment is to kill appreciation of life. But that enchantment is all-consuming, overwhelming, and realizing there will be nothing better in life, Aschenbach’s death “shakes the world". The readers of the world mourn the author’s death and his artistry, without realizing said author has viewed the culmination of art in a human Eros, and cannot recreate in word what a glorious sight he’s beheld.
review.text_raw:
Tadzio: *Gives Aschenbach a 'sup nod.*

Aschenbach received that smile and turned away with it as though entrusted with a fatal gift. So shaken was he that he had to flee from the lighted terrace and front gardens and seek out with hurried steps the darkness of the park at the rear. Reproaches strangely mixed of tenderness and remonstrance burst from him: "How dare you smile like that! No one is allowed to smile like that!" He flung himself on a bench, his composure gone to the winds, and breathed in the nocturnal fragrance of the garden. He leaned back, with hanging arms, quivering from head to foot, and quite unmanned he whispered the hackneyed phrase of love and longing-impossible in these circumstances, absurd, abject, ridiculous enough, yet sacred too, and not unworthy of honour even here: "I love you!"

Who says the French are dramatic? This part made me smile.

Wonderful little novella of narcissism and illness, and the captivation of a mirror. And a minor.

I'll need to reread this when I'm older. I didn't understand half of the mythological references.

Update (11/2): I have re-read the novel and have questioned the correlation between beauty's abyss and divinity. Not necessarily a review.

As Jeffrey Eugenides explains, ecstasy is not euphoria—in fact, it is not even pleasure, per definition—but rather a state of disassociation, and sometimes self-transcendence. Dionysus, God of religious ecstasy, serves an allegorical purpose in relation to Aschenbach’s desire of Tadzio. Tadzio is no Dionysus, but, radiant as the sun, he expels incomparable beauty that sends Aschenbach into a state of worship, mindless and devoted. Intoxicated with the poison of Tadzio’s form, Aschenbach rides a seesaw, both sides lined with divinity and abyss; two extremes representative of the paths to heaven and hell. And both have the same outcome.

Though Tadzio does not consciously (or actively) expel lust-inducing gestures, Aschenbach is overtaken with “frightful emotional excesses”. In his downfall of ecstasy, which also serves as a heightened sense of adoration, Aschenbach experiences revelation, in that his devotion to an epicenter of human perfection is a power stronger than the pull of societally-implemented logic. All his natural inhibitions flee as he is overcome with allegiance to beauty. Whether that serves as destruction or saving is up to the devotee: routine, cyclical life is just as good as death for Aschenbach and to abscond from this hazy dream-world would be destruction, for it would, devastatingly, “bring him back to his senses”. As the cholera seeps into his bones, he’d become just as sick in his hometown, rotting without the light of Tadzio shining upon him. Inevitable depression would overtake him.

Which raises the question: divine or the abyss—is it the same thing? Both outcomes lead Aschenbach to his inevitable downfall, a corpse on the beach. In the end, he achieved both the divine and the abyss, but both extremes elicited the same outcome—the titular Death in Venice. Because his appreciation of beauty led to dedication, and then holiness, the undeniable divinity of beauty affected Aschenbach to the point of religious exaltation. His unconscious has adopted an unshakable devotion, even, blessing “the stranger god”. At a peak so high, desire overtakes him, mindless adoration eats at him, and he is full of that ecstatic poison. And without this feeling, abandonment of beauty would send him to lows incontrovertibly beyond repair. The gorgeousness of ideal human form has taken its toll on a simple man unable to achieve or recreate that perfection. A modern Bacchus, Tadzio has infected Aschenbach just as thoroughly as the Asiatic cholera has, and his decay is brought upon from pure, overtaking voyeurism of what he perceives as godlike comeliness.

When a feeling so intense instills itself within a man, to be rid of it is suicide. It is the drug no rehabilitation can cure. Beauty, like religion, results in the user doping up on doses of hope, in exchange offering worship. Aschenbach exits the salon a doomed and “beguiled lover”. To break the spell of enchantment is to kill appreciation of life. But that enchantment is all-consuming, overwhelming, and realizing there will be nothing better in life, Aschenbach’s death “shakes the world". The readers of the world mourn the author’s death and his artistry, without realizing said author has viewed the culmination of art in a human Eros, and cannot recreate in word what a glorious sight he’s beheld.

review.text: Bu kitabı okumaya başlarken beni heyecanlandıran iki şey vardı. Birincisi Thomas Mann ikinicisi de Venedik. Thomas Mann'ı uzun zamandır okumak istememe rağmen tuğla kitaplarına (Büyülü Dağ, Büddenbrooklar) cesaret edememiştim. Ama bu kitap ne de olsa 100 sayfa ve bir oturusta bitiririm diye elime alıp okumaya başladım. Bundan sonrası kitabı tanıtıcı bilgi içereceğinden kitap hakkında önyargı oluşturmaması adına hassasiyeti olanları şimdiden uyarıyorum. Öncelikle kitabı rahat bitiririm diye düşünmem beni yanılttı çünkü 700 sayfalık Düne kitabını neredeyse bir günde bitirirken bu kitap üç günde zor bitti. Baştaki betimlemeler ağır tahliller biraz bunaltıcı olsa da ana karakter Gustav von Aschenbach Venedik'e doğru yolculuğa çıkınca kitap biraz daha hareketlendi. Ancak kitaptan tam keyif almaya başlayacağım diye heveslenmişken kötü bir süpriz ile karşılaştım. Aschenbach, kaldığı otele gelen bir grup Polonyalı turist içinden 14 yasındaki Tadzio adlı bir çocuğa ilgi duymaya başlar ve duyduğu bu ilgi zamanla aşka dönüşür. Hatta öyle bir boyut alır ki bu aşk, sonunda Venedik'i terketmeyip ölüme razı olmaya kadar götürecektir kendisini. İnanın kendimi zorladım, sanatçılık duyarlılığına ve güzele hayran olma ve güzelliğe duyulan ilgi psikolojisi ile empati yapmaya çalıştım ama hiçbir bahane pedofiliye kılıf olmamalı diye düşünüyorum. Zira, bazı bölümler var paylaşmak istemiyorum, ciddi anlamda rahatsız etti beni. Bana asıl ilginç gelen bu eserin yorumları arasında bu olaya değinen pek az kimsenin olması. Hatta bir elin parmak sayını geçmiyor bu konudan rahatsız olanlar. Ben yine de değerlendirirken olabildiğince objektif olmaya çalışarak, anlatımın başarısı ve tahlillerin etkileyiciliği nedeniyle 3 yıldız verdim. Ama bu kitap eğer daha genç birisine aşık olmak, bu nedenle kendini genç hissettirecek çabalara girmek (sonlara doğru Aschenbach kendine bakım yapıp saçlarını bıyıklarını boyatma çabalarına girmişti) ve bunun hissiyatını bize vermek istiyorsa, bu pekala genç bir kadın/erkek üzerinden yapılabilirdi. Ama konu çocuk olunca bunu bir türlü kabullenemedim.
review.text_raw:
Bu kitabı okumaya başlarken beni heyecanlandıran iki şey vardı. Birincisi Thomas Mann ikinicisi de Venedik. Thomas Mann'ı uzun zamandır okumak istememe rağmen tuğla kitaplarına (Büyülü Dağ, Büddenbrooklar) cesaret edememiştim. Ama bu kitap ne de olsa 100 sayfa ve bir oturusta bitiririm diye elime alıp okumaya başladım.

Bundan sonrası kitabı tanıtıcı bilgi içereceğinden kitap hakkında önyargı oluşturmaması adına hassasiyeti olanları şimdiden uyarıyorum.

Öncelikle kitabı rahat bitiririm diye düşünmem beni yanılttı çünkü 700 sayfalık Düne kitabını neredeyse bir günde bitirirken bu kitap üç günde zor bitti. Baştaki betimlemeler ağır tahliller biraz bunaltıcı olsa da ana karakter Gustav von Aschenbach Venedik'e doğru yolculuğa çıkınca kitap biraz daha hareketlendi.

Ancak kitaptan tam keyif almaya başlayacağım diye heveslenmişken kötü bir süpriz ile karşılaştım. Aschenbach, kaldığı otele gelen bir grup Polonyalı turist içinden 14 yasındaki Tadzio adlı bir çocuğa ilgi duymaya başlar ve duyduğu bu ilgi zamanla aşka dönüşür. Hatta öyle bir boyut alır ki bu aşk, sonunda Venedik'i terketmeyip ölüme razı olmaya kadar götürecektir kendisini. İnanın kendimi zorladım, sanatçılık duyarlılığına ve güzele hayran olma ve güzelliğe duyulan ilgi psikolojisi ile empati yapmaya çalıştım ama hiçbir bahane pedofiliye kılıf olmamalı diye düşünüyorum. Zira, bazı bölümler var paylaşmak istemiyorum, ciddi anlamda rahatsız etti beni.

Bana asıl ilginç gelen bu eserin yorumları arasında bu olaya değinen pek az kimsenin olması. Hatta bir elin parmak sayını geçmiyor bu konudan rahatsız olanlar. Ben yine de değerlendirirken olabildiğince objektif olmaya çalışarak, anlatımın başarısı ve tahlillerin etkileyiciliği nedeniyle 3 yıldız verdim. Ama bu kitap eğer daha genç birisine aşık olmak, bu nedenle kendini genç hissettirecek çabalara girmek (sonlara doğru Aschenbach kendine bakım yapıp saçlarını bıyıklarını boyatma çabalarına girmişti) ve bunun hissiyatını bize vermek istiyorsa, bu pekala genç bir kadın/erkek üzerinden yapılabilirdi. Ama konu çocuk olunca bunu bir türlü kabullenemedim.

review.text: Βαθμολογία: 7/10 Πρώτο βιβλίο του Τόμας Μαν που διαβάζω, το διάλεξα περισσότερο λόγω του μικρού μεγέθους, αλλά και γιατί είναι από τα πιο γνωστά έργα του μεγάλου και πολυσυζητημένου αυτού συγγραφέα. Δεν μπορώ να πω ότι ενθουσιάστηκα. Σίγουρα ο Τόμας Μαν είναι μάστορας του Λόγου, η γραφή του είναι γεμάτη λυρισμό, ποιητικότητα, συμβολισμούς και βάθος σε νοήματα και σκέψεις, όμως η όλη ιστορία δεν θα έλεγα ότι με συγκίνησε ή, έστω, ότι με άγγιξε με οποιονδήποτε τρόπο. Ένας μεσόκοπος συγγραφέας, διάσημος στον κόσμο των Γραμμάτων, ο Γουσταύος Φον Άσενμπαχ, αποφασίζει να αφήσει το Μόναχο για λίγες εβδομάδες διακοπών στην Μεσόγειο, με τελικό προορισμό την Βενετία. Σ'αυτήν την Ιταλική πόλη θα δει εντελώς τυχαία ένα πανέμορφο δεκατετράχρονο αγόρι ονόματι Τάτζιο, το οποίο θα του γίνει έμμονη ιδέα. Η επιδημία χολέρας που θα χτυπήσει κάποια στιγμή την πόλη δεν θα σταθεί ικανή να τον κρατήσει μακριά από τον νεαρό Τάτζιο. Μου άρεσαν οι λιτές περιγραφές της Βενετίας, καθώς και οι περιγραφές της ανέμελης ζωής του πρωταγωνιστή συγγραφέα, μιας και με μετέφεραν σε μια όμορφη και ιδιαίτερη πόλη, μια εποχή εντελώς μακρινή και διαφορετική από αυτήν που ζούμε. Κατά τ'άλλα, όμως, δεν δέθηκα ιδιαίτερα με τον πρωταγωνιστή και τις σκέψεις του. Και υπήρξαν κάποια σημεία που με κούρασαν (ειδικά στην αρχή). Σίγουρα αξίζει μια ανάγνωση, όχι μόνο επειδή είναι κλασικό και από τα βιβλία που "πρέπει να διαβάσει κανείς πριν πεθάνει", αλλά γιατί η γλώσσα είναι αξιοσημείωτη και ξεχωριστή. Απλά, ίσως, το όλο ύφος δεν ταίριαξε με τα γούστα μου. Κάποια στιγμή στο μέλλον θα ψάξω να βρω και να δω την ομότιτλη ταινία του Luchino Visconti που βασίζεται στο βιβλίο, ενώ θα πιάσω οπωσδήποτε και το "Το Μαγικό Βουνό", το οποίο είμαι σίγουρος ότι θα αποτελέσει μια διαφορετική και ιδιαίτερα ξεχωριστή αναγνωστική εμπειρία.
review.text_raw:
Βαθμολογία: 7/10

Πρώτο βιβλίο του Τόμας Μαν που διαβάζω, το διάλεξα περισσότερο λόγω του μικρού μεγέθους, αλλά και γιατί είναι από τα πιο γνωστά έργα του μεγάλου και πολυσυζητημένου αυτού συγγραφέα. Δεν μπορώ να πω ότι ενθουσιάστηκα. Σίγουρα ο Τόμας Μαν είναι μάστορας του Λόγου, η γραφή του είναι γεμάτη λυρισμό, ποιητικότητα, συμβολισμούς και βάθος σε νοήματα και σκέψεις, όμως η όλη ιστορία δεν θα έλεγα ότι με συγκίνησε ή, έστω, ότι με άγγιξε με οποιονδήποτε τρόπο.

Ένας μεσόκοπος συγγραφέας, διάσημος στον κόσμο των Γραμμάτων, ο Γουσταύος Φον Άσενμπαχ, αποφασίζει να αφήσει το Μόναχο για λίγες εβδομάδες διακοπών στην Μεσόγειο, με τελικό προορισμό την Βενετία. Σ'αυτήν την Ιταλική πόλη θα δει εντελώς τυχαία ένα πανέμορφο δεκατετράχρονο αγόρι ονόματι Τάτζιο, το οποίο θα του γίνει έμμονη ιδέα. Η επιδημία χολέρας που θα χτυπήσει κάποια στιγμή την πόλη δεν θα σταθεί ικανή να τον κρατήσει μακριά από τον νεαρό Τάτζιο. Μου άρεσαν οι λιτές περιγραφές της Βενετίας, καθώς και οι περιγραφές της ανέμελης ζωής του πρωταγωνιστή συγγραφέα, μιας και με μετέφεραν σε μια όμορφη και ιδιαίτερη πόλη, μια εποχή εντελώς μακρινή και διαφορετική από αυτήν που ζούμε. Κατά τ'άλλα, όμως, δεν δέθηκα ιδιαίτερα με τον πρωταγωνιστή και τις σκέψεις του. Και υπήρξαν κάποια σημεία που με κούρασαν (ειδικά στην αρχή).

Σίγουρα αξίζει μια ανάγνωση, όχι μόνο επειδή είναι κλασικό και από τα βιβλία που "πρέπει να διαβάσει κανείς πριν πεθάνει", αλλά γιατί η γλώσσα είναι αξιοσημείωτη και ξεχωριστή. Απλά, ίσως, το όλο ύφος δεν ταίριαξε με τα γούστα μου. Κάποια στιγμή στο μέλλον θα ψάξω να βρω και να δω την ομότιτλη ταινία του Luchino Visconti που βασίζεται στο βιβλίο, ενώ θα πιάσω οπωσδήποτε και το "Το Μαγικό Βουνό", το οποίο είμαι σίγουρος ότι θα αποτελέσει μια διαφορετική και ιδιαίτερα ξεχωριστή αναγνωστική εμπειρία.

review.text: وقتی زیبایی دست در گردن مرگ می‌اندازد، وقتی میل، بی‌آن‌که لب بگشاید، روح را فرو می‌بلعد، آنگاه «مرگ در ونیز» آغاز می‌شود. رمانی کوتاه از توماس مان که در دل خود نه‌تنها وسوسه‌ای ممنوع را جای داده، بلکه پرسشی ابدی را بازمی‌گشاید: مرز میان تعالی و سقوط، میان ستایش و تسلیم، کجاست؟ آشنباخ، نویسنده‌ای پیر و نیک‌نام، گرفتار وسوسه‌ای خاموش و نابالغ می‌شود. او به ونیز می‌گریزد، شاید به گمان آسایش، شاید به امید رهایی. اما در این شهر بیمار، در این بهشت متعفن، آن‌چه انتظارش را می‌کشد عشق نیست، جنون است. چشمان نوجوانی زیبا، بی‌هیچ کلامی، او را به ورطه‌ای می‌کشند که نه با عقل می‌توان از آن برگشت، نه با اخلاق. «مرگ در ونیز» درباره عشق نیست. درباره حقیقت هم نیست. درباره سرکشی زیباشناختی ذهنی خسته‌ است که عمری را صرف مهار خویش کرده و اکنون، در واپسین تابستان زندگی‌اش، فریبنده‌ترین فاجعه را می‌چشد: زیبا دیدن آن‌چه نباید، و نابود شدن در برابرش. توماس مان این‌جا در مرز زبان و سکوت می‌نویسد. در هر جمله‌اش هم عطش شناخت هست، هم پذیرش شکست. شخصیت اصلی نه درگیر رابطه‌ای جسمانی‌ست، نه حتی گفت‌وگویی. فقط نظاره می‌کند. و همین نظاره، بی‌آن‌که به عمل بدل شود، او را از پا درمی‌آورد. این‌جا، شور در خاموشی‌ست، تب در نجواست، و مرگ، لبخندزنان از پس چشمان نوجوانی یونانی نزدیک می‌شود. اقتباس سینمایی لوکینو ویسکونتی از این اثر، تصویری ناب و خفقان‌آور از همین شور آمیخته با زوال است. موسیقی ماهلر بر پیکره‌ی فیلم جاری‌ست، چهره‌ی پیر و خسته‌ی آشنباخ زیر آفتاب ونیز می‌سوزد، و نگاه‌ها، نگاه‌هایی که هیچ‌گاه به سخن نمی‌انجامند، ما را از فرط سکوت کر می‌کنند. «مرگ در ونیز» کتابی‌ست درباره سرسپردگیِ بی‌سرانجام، درباره میل به زیبایی‌ای که فراتر از مجاز است. و سرانجام، درباره انسانی که پیش از آن‌که دست بزند، در درون خودش تمام می‌شود. مرگی آرام، در ساحل شهر اشباح.

«مرگ در ونیز» درباره عشق نیست. درباره حقیقت هم نیست. درباره سرکشی زیباشناختی ذهنی خسته‌ است که عمری را صرف مهار خویش کرده و اکنون، در واپسین تابستان زندگی‌اش، فریبنده‌ترین فاجعه را می‌چشد:
زیبا دیدن آن‌چه نباید، و نابود شدن در برابرش.

اقتباس سینمایی لوکینو ویسکونتی از این اثر، تصویری ناب و خفقان‌آور از همین شور آمیخته با زوال است. موسیقی ماهلر بر پیکره‌ی فیلم جاری‌ست، چهره‌ی پیر و خسته‌ی آشنباخ زیر آفتاب ونیز می‌سوزد، و نگاه‌ها، نگاه‌هایی که هیچ‌گاه به سخن نمی‌انجامند، ما را از فرط سکوت کر می‌کنند.

«مرگ در ونیز» کتابی‌ست درباره سرسپردگیِ بی‌سرانجام، درباره میل به زیبایی‌ای که فراتر از مجاز است. و سرانجام، درباره انسانی که پیش از آن‌که دست بزند، در درون خودش تمام می‌شود. مرگی آرام، در ساحل شهر اشباح.

review.text: مرگ در ونیز برای من که از ادبیات آلمان خیلی کم واز ادبیات ابتدای قرن بیستم به مراتب کمتر از آن می دانم و مطالعه کرده ام در ابتدا سنگین بود. هر خطی که می خواندم باید بار دیگر با زمزمه برای خودم می خواندمش تا مقصود توماس را درک کنم، کمی که گذشت فهمیدم این صد و خورده ای کتاب چه فاخر است! ادبیاتی که گامی فراتر از ادبیات کلاسیک گذاشته است اما هنوز از دیدگاه من گامی در ادبیات کلاسیک دارد. از آن گذر کرده است هنگامی که از تمثیل های جسورانه ای برای پیک مرگ استفاده می کند یا استعمار کشورهای تازه شرق شناخته شدۀ آن زمان در اروپا را در لفافه می پیچد و به ما عرضه می کند، در آن باقی مانده است چون وجه بارز تمثیلش به شاهدبازی هنرمند یا همان آشنباخ اشاره می کند و این شخیت چه در واگویه های شخصی خود با خود چه در دیدگاه خود خواستۀ نویسنده شباهت بی بدیلی به الهه ها و افسانه های یونانی دارد! از این کلاسیک تر؟ علاوه بر همه این ها مرگ در ونیز زیبایی منحط و اغواگر ونیز قرن بیست را در طبق تقدیم خواننده می کند، ونیزی که اگر در کوچه پس کوچه های پر پیچ و خمش گم شوی خبری از نشان و نمادی در افق بی کران اسمان و دریا نیست که از گمگشتگی نجاتت دهد، ونیزی که اسیر وبای خانمان سوزی شده که ارمغان زیاده خواهی و ولع تمام ناشدنی اروپای قرن بیست است، ولع کشف کشورهای تازه کشف شدۀ به اصطلاح شرقی ! اصل کلام این است: مرگ در ونیز را باید خواند، با پذیرش این که با نثر و محتوا ساده ای رو به رو نیستیم. مرگ در ونیز فاخر است.

علاوه بر همه این ها مرگ در ونیز زیبایی منحط و اغواگر ونیز قرن بیست را در طبق تقدیم خواننده می کند، ونیزی که اگر در کوچه پس کوچه های پر پیچ
و خمش گم شوی خبری از نشان و نمادی در افق بی کران اسمان و دریا نیست که از گمگشتگی نجاتت دهد، ونیزی که اسیر وبای خانمان سوزی شده که ارمغان زیاده خواهی و ولع تمام ناشدنی اروپای قرن بیست است، ولع کشف کشورهای تازه کشف شدۀ به اصطلاح شرقی !
اصل کلام این است: مرگ در ونیز را باید خواند، با پذیرش این که با نثر و محتوا ساده ای رو به رو نیستیم. مرگ در ونیز فاخر است.

review.text: Thomas Mann is an extremely talented writer and I enjoyed his wonderful The Magic Mountain, but no matter how well written is Death in Venice, an old man’s pining for and stalking a young innocent boy is at best unsettling and at worst disgusting. Lolita is a novel with a similar theme but in contrast I have to admit that I liked Lolita very much. While different in sexual orientation one could say both books glorify sexual exploitation. But the differences are important. First in the storyline: Lolita was hardly innocent; the experienced precocious Lolita blatantly entices Humbert Humbert to cross the line. Also Nabokov presents Humbert Humbert as scurrilous and his “love” as a crass obsession. In Death in Venice the boy is unawares, simply an object and Mann presents Aschenbach as gifted and refined elevating his “love” to a testament to beauty. Second in the style: Nabokov’s tongue in cheek satire in Lolita belittles Humbert Humbert casting him as a compulsive dirty old man. Strangely, it is Mann’s great skill in exquisitely portraying Aschenbach’s adoration of the boy that exalts Aschenbach and makes Death in Venice offensive. I find pedophilia dignified through art to be very disturbing.

review.text: De lange zinnen hebben een hypnotiserende werking en dwingen je tot diepe aandachtigheid en nadenken over de “belachelijke” verliefdheid van een ouwe bok voor een zeer jong, mannelijk, blaadje. “Op het grasveld echter, dat zacht glooide, zo, dat men als men lag het hoofd rechtop kon houden, rustte een tweetal, hier geborgen voor de gloed van de dag, een oudere man en een jonge, een lelijke en een schone, de wijze bij de beminnenswaardige.” Duidelijk is dat de hoofdpersoon hier zichzelf nog als wijs beschouwt. Geen zorgen, het is van korte duur. Gedrenkt in verklaringen met veel volgbare verwijzingen naar mythologie en filosofie, draait en draaft de schrijver-hoofdpersoon snel door. “Hij sprak hem van de hevige ontsteltenis die de voelende bevangt als zijn oog een gelijkenis van de eeuwige schoonheid ontwaart; hij sprak hem van de begeerten van degene die de wijding niet heeft, de minderwaardige, die de schoonheid niet kan denken wanneer hij haar beeld ziet en niet tot eerbied in staat is; sprak van de heilige vrees die de edele bevangt wanneer een godgelijkend gelaat, een volmaakte lichaamsgestalte voor hem verschijnt, – hoe hij dan begint te beven en zichzelf niet meer meester is en het nauwelijks waagt te kijken en diegene vereert die de schoonheid bezit, ja, aan hem zou offeren als aan een godsbeeld als hij niet moest vrezen in de ogen der mensen een dwaas te schijnen.” (Wat zullen de buren of de mensheid wel niet denken) “Een strooien van rozen begon daar aan de rand van de wereld, een onzegbaar lieflijk lichten en bloeien, kinderlijke wolkjes, omstraald, doorschenen, zweefden als dienende amoretten in het rozig, blauwachtig waas, purper viel op de zee, die het deinend voorwaarts leek te spoelen, gouden speren schoten van beneden naar het zenit, de glans werd een brand, geluidloos, met goddelijke overmacht woelden zich gloed en vurigheid en laaiende vlammen omhoog, en met reppende hoeven stegen haar broeders heilige renpaarden boven het wereldrond op.” (Mooi he?) “Eenzaamheid, de vreemde omgeving en het geluk van een late en diepe extase moedigden hem aan en haalden hem ertoe over om van zichzelf ook het meest bevreemdende zonder schaamrood of gêne te accepteren, en zo was het dan voorgekomen dat hij, ’s avonds laat uit Venetië terugkerend, op de eerste verdieping van het hotel bij de kamerdeur van de schone stil was blijven staan, zijn hoofd in volstrekt dronken roes tegen het scharnier van de deur had gelegd en lange tijd niet bij machte was geweest van die plek te scheiden, op gevaar af in een dergelijke idiote houding aangetroffen en betrapt te worden.” (Dagboek van een gluurder) De dood aan het eind was onafwendbaar. En voor mij als lezer toch wel een opluchting dat het niet nog 800 pagina’s duurde.

(Dagboek van een gluurder)

De dood aan het eind was onafwendbaar. En voor mij als lezer toch wel een opluchting dat het niet nog 800 pagina’s duurde.

review.text: Esta será talvez a opinião mais difícil que tive que dar até ao momento. Acontece que fiquei muito dividida sobre o que dizer sobre este livro, pelo que pensei que o melhor seria dividir a minha opinião em pontos fortes e pontos menos fortes para simplificar. São muitos os pontos fortes deste livro. Em primeiro lugar é um produto literário magnífico. O autor manuseia as palavras de forma genial, criando um discurso cativante e ambíguo que leva o leitor a imergir-se por completo na ambiguidade do enredo e dos sentimentos do personagem principal (Aschenbach). Um outro ponto forte, foi a forma como o autor trata o conceito de "beleza", daquilo que é puro e belo. Para tal faz-se valer de um sublime engenho linguístico, que tem como alavanca a pureza, inocência e beleza idealizada de um rapaz adolescente (personagem de nome Tadzio), e o exotismo, beleza e magnificência da cidade de Veneza (local da acção). Algo que também achei fantástico neste livro, foi como o autor consegue fazer passar esta história, mantendo o fascínio que Aschenbach sente por Tadzio envolto numa capa de admiração platónica pela beleza em si mesma, como se admirasse uma obra arte, isenta de desejo sexual pelo rapaz. No entanto, existe uma altura em que esta admiração é erotizada, ainda que apenas em pensamento pelo personagem principal. Apesar de ter estado até essa altura a fazer um esforço por acreditar nesse amor ou fascínio apenas platónico, deixando-me levar pela ambiguidade do discurso, nesse momento, tudo para mim caíu por terra. E aqui começam os pontos menos fortes. A partir do momento em que a relação entre os personagens foi erotizada, ainda que tenha sido só em pensamento por parte do personagem principal, tudo se tornou terrívelmente errado para mim. A partir do momento em que Aschenbach leva um pouco mais longe os seus pensamentos, não pude deixar de começar a pensar em Aschenbach de uma forma completamente diferente. Se antes via um artista, amante das artes e do que é belo, em busca de si próprio , tentando reviver de alguma forma a sua própria juventude, bebendo da juventude e da beleza daquele jovem e da cidade de Veneza, depois comecei a ver um homem abjecto que dissimula as suas perversões, ainda que para si mesmo e de forma bastante hábil, sob a capa da arte . Alguns aspectos de que algo mais doentio poderia estar a acontecer ao íntimo de Aschenbach, tornaram-se evidentes : a obsessão, o voyeurismo, e até a idealização de que os seus sentimentos eram de alguma forma retribuidos, ao interpretar erradamente gestos ou olhares casuais do adolescente. Tão doentios que o levam a um desenlace trágico, tal como o título do livro indica. Devo realçar que a questão da homosexualidade me é totalmente indiferente. O meu problema aqui é o "roçar" de elementos que remetem para a pedofilia. Se Tadzio fosse uma rapariga adolescente, seria exactamente igual a minha opinião. Ainda que Aschenbach nunca tenha (felizmente) transformado o seu desejo em comportamento, isso não quer dizer que esteja menos errado. O ponto exposto anteriormente foi o meu principal motivo de desagrado, mas também fiquei um pouco desiludida com o próprio enredo. A história quase não existe. Já tinha mais de metade do livro lido e não tinha acontecido rigorosamente nada. É claro que a habilidade literária do autor disfarça o facto de que não tem nada para contar, mas ainda assim, gostaria de ter tido maior complexidade no enredo. Um outro ponto menos forte deste livro e aqui Thomas Mann não tem qualquer ónus, é a edição que li. Em primeiro lugar a capa não é adequada ao livro, pois dá a sensação de que vamos ler um policial de categoria B, e em segundo, o texto tem bastantes gralhas e incorrecções. Aconselho, caso desejem ler este livro, a escolherem uma outra edição.
review.text_raw:
Esta será talvez a opinião mais difícil que tive que dar até ao momento. Acontece que fiquei muito dividida sobre o que dizer sobre este livro, pelo que pensei que o melhor seria dividir a minha opinião em pontos fortes e pontos menos fortes para simplificar.

São muitos os pontos fortes deste livro. Em primeiro lugar é um produto literário magnífico. O autor manuseia as palavras de forma genial, criando um discurso cativante e ambíguo que leva o leitor a imergir-se por completo na ambiguidade do enredo e dos sentimentos do personagem principal (Aschenbach).

Um outro ponto forte, foi a forma como o autor trata o conceito de "beleza", daquilo que é puro e belo. Para tal faz-se valer de um sublime engenho linguístico, que tem como alavanca a pureza, inocência e beleza idealizada de um rapaz adolescente (personagem de nome Tadzio), e o exotismo, beleza e magnificência da cidade de Veneza (local da acção).

Algo que também achei fantástico neste livro, foi como o autor consegue fazer passar esta história, mantendo o fascínio que Aschenbach sente por Tadzio envolto numa capa de admiração platónica pela beleza em si mesma, como se admirasse uma obra arte, isenta de desejo sexual pelo rapaz. No entanto, existe uma altura em que esta admiração é erotizada, ainda que apenas em pensamento pelo personagem principal. Apesar de ter estado até essa altura a fazer um esforço por acreditar nesse amor ou fascínio apenas platónico, deixando-me levar pela ambiguidade do discurso, nesse momento, tudo para mim caíu por terra. E aqui começam os pontos menos fortes.

A partir do momento em que a relação entre os personagens foi erotizada, ainda que tenha sido só em pensamento por parte do personagem principal, tudo se tornou terrívelmente errado para mim. A partir do momento em que Aschenbach leva um pouco mais longe os seus pensamentos, não pude deixar de começar a pensar em Aschenbach de uma forma completamente diferente. Se antes via um artista, amante das artes e do que é belo, em busca de si próprio , tentando reviver de alguma forma a sua própria juventude, bebendo da juventude e da beleza daquele jovem e da cidade de Veneza, depois comecei a ver um homem abjecto que dissimula as suas perversões, ainda que para si mesmo e de forma bastante hábil, sob a capa da arte . Alguns aspectos de que algo mais doentio poderia estar a acontecer ao íntimo de Aschenbach, tornaram-se evidentes : a obsessão, o voyeurismo, e até a idealização de que os seus sentimentos eram de alguma forma retribuidos, ao interpretar erradamente gestos ou olhares casuais do adolescente. Tão doentios que o levam a um desenlace trágico, tal como o título do livro indica. Devo realçar que a questão da homosexualidade me é totalmente indiferente. O meu problema aqui é o "roçar" de elementos que remetem para a pedofilia. Se Tadzio fosse uma rapariga adolescente, seria exactamente igual a minha opinião. Ainda que Aschenbach nunca tenha (felizmente) transformado o seu desejo em comportamento, isso não quer dizer que esteja menos errado.

O ponto exposto anteriormente foi o meu principal motivo de desagrado, mas também fiquei um pouco desiludida com o próprio enredo. A história quase não existe. Já tinha mais de metade do livro lido e não tinha acontecido rigorosamente nada. É claro que a habilidade literária do autor disfarça o facto de que não tem nada para contar, mas ainda assim, gostaria de ter tido maior complexidade no enredo.

Um outro ponto menos forte deste livro e aqui Thomas Mann não tem qualquer ónus, é a edição que li. Em primeiro lugar a capa não é adequada ao livro, pois dá a sensação de que vamos ler um policial de categoria B, e em segundo, o texto tem bastantes gralhas e incorrecções. Aconselho, caso desejem ler este livro, a escolherem uma outra edição.

review.text: This is a novella detailing the decline and death of aged respectable author, who has subjugated his entire adult life to his formidable intellect. The repressed unconscious material emerges in three symbolical orgiastic manifestations: 1) paranoia of ginger men and feeling that they keep popping up everywhere; 2) hysterical disgust at an aged man he sees, who tries to fraternize greasily with strapping young lads; 3) the aged author’s increasingly disturbing fascination with beautiful 14 year old boy he sees in his hotel in Venice. Gustav von Aschenbach never does confront the root of his intellect/orgiastic impulses split, but instead fights the impulses until they overwhelm his tired aged frame, and then submits to them, at the same time as the pre-antibiotic Venice infects him with cholera. Lovingly written with many asides, for which I love Mann so, this is an impeccable presentation of the decline of the psyche and organism of an aged author, and his final grasps at the straw of youth, health, and joy of life – through his fascination with 14 year old Tadzio. Warning: you have reached the Butthurt portion of review There are certain people... they think Death in Venice would be a much ‘better’ book if a) Tadzio was 18, and the story ended with him and von Aschenbach singing ‘We Are Family’ or b) if the author tried to make out with Tadzio and was then tried for pedophilia. Same people who think Anna Karenina is a slow moving historical romance; that The Great Gatsby is a slow moving crime thriller/romance. ‘If only these old authors knew how to really write’, they imply. 'If only they were as enlightened as we are, and knew how to write cliffhangers, preferably inside a Hero’s Journey superstructure. Or at least were not afraid to deal with issues correctly and openly, like we do today, for we are the culmination of humanity’s history and actually get stuff.' People, stop torturing yourself and others with these attempts to deal with the classics, especially non-Anglosaxon such. If you’re only reading them for prestige, the effect is quite counterproductive. Stick to reality shows and fashionable contemporary fiction. Best for all concerned, really. A good author is aware of many of his impulses which people ordinary repress beyond consciousness. Homosexual impulses, incest impulses, murder impulses, rape impulses, defecate where I want to impulses, stealing impulses, retreat into catatonia impulses, etc. Now, before anyone has a rage-induced anal prolapse, I’d like just to clarify that I’m not implying that homosexual impulses are equal to murder impulses, I’m just enumerating. If you’d like your personal fetish/lifestyle to not be included, please send application form A 6. Mann was very aware of his homosexual impulses and pedophilic such. Today’s full of it critics call it his ‘struggle with homosexuality’, implying that poor repressed coward didn’t have the guts to come out of the closet, leading the European elite into a new era, and maybe even preventing WWI and II. But I digress. We are all made up of impulses. It’s one thing to recognize them and juggle their interaction, allowing one 20% freedom, another 70%, and a third one – no freedom, only sublimation in one form or another. It’s quite another thing to automatically take one’s own character structure as ‘how everyone should be’ and look with pity and some gloating at the unfortunate imbeciles who do not have your laser intellect and iron courage to be exactly like you. I don’t know why I think Mann should be defended, and that I should be the one to bother. I’m certainly not taking this up as a day job, or even a hobby. But today I just felt that someone had to say all this. And I did. Cheers.

Gustav von Aschenbach never does confront the root of his intellect/orgiastic impulses split, but instead fights the impulses until they overwhelm his tired aged frame, and then submits to them, at the same time as the pre-antibiotic Venice infects him with cholera.

Lovingly written with many asides, for which I love Mann so, this is an impeccable presentation of the decline of the psyche and organism of an aged author, and his final grasps at the straw of youth, health, and joy of life – through his fascination with 14 year old Tadzio.

There are certain people... they think Death in Venice would be a much ‘better’ book if a) Tadzio was 18, and the story ended with him and von Aschenbach singing ‘We Are Family’ or b) if the author tried to make out with Tadzio and was then tried for pedophilia.

Same people who think Anna Karenina is a slow moving historical romance; that The Great Gatsby is a slow moving crime thriller/romance.

‘If only these old authors knew how to really write’, they imply. 'If only they were as enlightened as we are, and knew how to write cliffhangers, preferably inside a Hero’s Journey superstructure. Or at least were not afraid to deal with issues correctly and openly, like we do today, for we are the culmination of humanity’s history and actually get stuff.'

People, stop torturing yourself and others with these attempts to deal with the classics, especially non-Anglosaxon such. If you’re only reading them for prestige, the effect is quite counterproductive. Stick to reality shows and fashionable contemporary fiction.

Best for all concerned, really.

A good author is aware of many of his impulses which people ordinary repress beyond consciousness. Homosexual impulses, incest impulses, murder impulses, rape impulses, defecate where I want to impulses, stealing impulses, retreat into catatonia impulses, etc.

Now, before anyone has a rage-induced anal prolapse, I’d like just to clarify that I’m not implying that homosexual impulses are equal to murder impulses, I’m just enumerating. If you’d like your personal fetish/lifestyle to not be included, please send application form A 6.

Mann was very aware of his homosexual impulses and pedophilic such. Today’s full of it critics call it his ‘struggle with homosexuality’, implying that poor repressed coward didn’t have the guts to come out of the closet, leading the European elite into a new era, and maybe even preventing WWI and II. But I digress.

We are all made up of impulses. It’s one thing to recognize them and juggle their interaction, allowing one 20% freedom, another 70%, and a third one – no freedom, only sublimation in one form or another. It’s quite another thing to automatically take one’s own character structure as ‘how everyone should be’ and look with pity and some gloating at the unfortunate imbeciles who do not have your laser intellect and iron courage to be exactly like you.

I don’t know why I think Mann should be defended, and that I should be the one to bother. I’m certainly not taking this up as a day job, or even a hobby. But today I just felt that someone had to say all this. And I did.

Cheers.

review.text: 4,8 ⭐️ “Death in Venice” tells the adventures of a renowned writer who, faced with his decadence materialized in a young red-haired man, while walking through Munich, decides to travel and ends up choosing Venice as his destination. There, he falls in love with a Polish child, the motif of this beautiful novel with autobiographical traits. The Freudian psyche echoes throughout the plot, in particular the ideas of the subconscious and repression. Ascenbach lives a true intrapsychic conflict between id, ego and super-ego. His asceticism and renunciation of pleasure as a young man come into conflict with voluptuous impulses (pederastric and homoerotic) that do not assuage with common sense in adulthood, in line with the Freudian theory (of which Mann was a staunch defender). In a way, choosing Venice can be considered a trip to the subconscious. The omnipresence of the sea and the canals, reflect the duality of the city: the sea described as a source of rejuvenation and the canals always shrouded in miasma and twilight. It is in this city populated by peculiar characters that the protagonist embarks on an internal journey of self-knowledge, discovering new dimensions of his psyche. Mann also takes advantage of the novel to lecture on art, aesthetics and beauty. Mann makes clear his belief that art and artist are indissociable, materializing this idea in the artist's self-identification in his austere characters or when he imagines himself in a platonic dialogue with Fedron. The artist's considerations of aesthetics are influenced by Nietzsche's idea of ​​the duality between the Apollonian (rational, orderly, austere) and the Dionysian (intoxication, rebellion and daring). Considerations about the beautiful arise mainly through voyeurism and contemplation that lead to descriptions by Tadzio that allude to hubris and to concepts of beauty defended by the Greeks (in fact, many of Tadzio's descriptions have clear Hellenic influences). In short, “Death in Venice” is a rich novel that takes the reader on two journeys: one outside Venice and one inside a complex, conflicting mind. Using rich, adjective language and yet easy to understand, Mann talks about interesting topics, inviting the reader to reflect while at the same time enjoying beautiful descriptions of the serene city.

His asceticism and renunciation of pleasure as a young man come into conflict with voluptuous impulses (pederastric and homoerotic) that do not assuage with common sense in adulthood, in line with the Freudian theory (of which Mann was a staunch defender). In a way, choosing Venice can be considered a trip to the subconscious. The omnipresence of the sea and the canals, reflect the duality of the city: the sea described as a source of rejuvenation and the canals always shrouded in miasma and twilight. It is in this city populated by peculiar characters that the protagonist embarks on an internal journey of self-knowledge, discovering new dimensions of his psyche.

Mann also takes advantage of the novel to lecture on art, aesthetics and beauty. Mann makes clear his belief that art and artist are indissociable, materializing this idea in the artist's self-identification in his austere characters or when he imagines himself in a platonic dialogue with Fedron. The artist's considerations of aesthetics are influenced by Nietzsche's idea of ​​the duality between the Apollonian (rational, orderly, austere) and the Dionysian (intoxication, rebellion and daring). review.text: Non si può ottenere quel che si vuole, questo sussurrano e a volte urlano le pagine di questo libro. Quel che si desidera è rinchiuso in una teca i cui vetri sono così spessi che a malapena vi si vede attraverso. Non si ottiene quel che si vuole, mai. Si è ad un palmo da esso, lo si può quasi sfiorare, ma non lo si riesce a toccare. Aschenbach allunga le braccia verso Tadzio e poi le ritrae, sudando freddo. Annusa la sua pelle e poi volta la faccia, vergognandosene. Appoggia la fronte alla sua porta, ma poi scappa, colmo di paure. E vorrei poterti dire, Aschenbach, che lo so, come ci si sente. A volerlo sfiorare, ma ad avere i piedi incollati al suolo. Incatenati. Ad averlo ad un passo e a non riuscire a toccare la sua pelle. A tremare. Ad aver passato così tanto tempo nella propria testa da non riuscire a guardare le persone negli occhi. Ad aver paura degli sguardi che ci dicono quel qualcosa in più, degli sguardi che pretendono qualcosa in più. Ad aver le parole poggiate sulle labbra schiuse. A voler accarezzare il suo profilo e gli angoli dei suoi occhi. Ad amare ciò che non si dovrebbe mai amare. E che so cosa si prova poi ad arrendersi a tutto ciò. Ad accettare il fatto che poche cose sono afferrabili. A scappare via. Ammetto che questo libro non sia entrato tra i miei preferiti, eppure c’è qualcosa che mi ha presa e portata via. A parte la mia soggettività, il tema di cui tratta Thomas Mann è delicatissimo e importante. Oggettivamente si scrive di un uomo sulla cinquantina che si innamora di un quattordicenne, quando in realtà si legge di uomo bloccato nelle sue paure e confinato nelle sue mura che ad un tratto scopre la Bellezza e ne rimane folgorato. Aschenbach è un esteta e non si innamora di una persona, ma di un concetto. Di un’idea. E le idee non si possono stringere. Ho amato le strade di Venezia offuscate da nebbia e polveri e coperte dall’odore pesante di disinfettante. Le scene surreali. I cittadini e le compagnie di musicisti che con risate isteriche negano l’evidenza e distraggono con comica tragedia i visitatori dalla realtà, dalla Morte. La spiaggia che ricorda il palco di un teatro e il mare il sipario che chiude. Questo libro è una fotografia da guardare, osservare, contemplare, senza esprimere giudizi a riguardo.

review.text: Thomas Mann não é um autor fácil, tem uma escrita elaborada e requintada com reflexões profundas e complexas que se espraiam ao longo de parágrafos extensos e capítulos enormes. As descrições dos ambientes são rigorosas e muito sensoriais. Quase se sentem os cheiros e os ruídos, nos passeios a pé ou de gôndola através da cidade. É uma leitura que exige concentração - algo difícil nestes dias de calor abrasador - e por vezes terminava uma frase, sem já saber como ela tinha começado. Foram tantas as vezes que voltei atrás na leitura, que, bem feitas contas, devo ter lido o livro umas três vezes. Morte em Veneza é uma narrativa sem diálogos, onde só se toma conhecimento dos pensamentos da personagem principal, Aschenbah, um escritor conhecido e admirado, cuja vida se pautou pelo rigor e disciplina. De férias em Veneza, apaixona-se por um adolescente que é a personificação da beleza ideal e pura. Entrega-se de forma obcecada àquela paixão platónica, e começa uma análise retrospectiva de toda a sua vida, e a encarar o futuro sobre uma nova perspetiva. Lança-se então num perseguição feroz ao rapaz, que o vai colocar em situações por vezes ridículas e em última instância, a desafiar a própria morte. Dizem as más línguas que este livro tem muito de autobiográfico, também Thomas Mann de passagem por Veneza, terá ficado deveras impressionado com um jovem de grande beleza que conheceu no mesmo hotel em que se passa esta história. Ainda não sei se bastou este livro para ficar fâ convicta de Mann, mas sei que foi suficiente para me deixar com vontade de ler mais. Em espera estão mais dois - pequeninos - depois...bem, depois verei se tenho coragem, ou não, para escalar A Montanha Mágica.

review.text: کاملا تصادفی و بدون برنامه، پایان خوانش کتاب همزمان شد با زادروز توماس مان! «مرگ در ونیز» داستانی از سیر سقوط نویسنده‌ای به نام گوستاو اشنباخه که اعتبار جهانیش رو نادیده می‌گیره و سفرش رو آغاز می‌کنه تا به ونیز می‌رسه. در هتل محل اقامتش شیدای پسر نوجوان لهستانی میشه و این پسر، میشه منبع الهام اشنباخ. همزمان با این شوریدگی، وبا هم در ونیز شایع میشه و بقیه‌ش رو خودتون بخونید.🙂 «مرگ در ونیز» رمان کوتاهی با مایه‌های فلسفیه که اگر موفق بشید پنجاه صفحه‌ی اولش رو بخونید، به احتمال نودونه درصد تا پایانش ادامه می‌دید. نثر کتاب، پر از تمثیل و استعاره است که من باهاش مشکلی نداشتم اما ممکنه حوصله‌ی خیلی‌ها رو سر ببره. مهم‌ترین نقطه ضعف کتاب هم از دید من ترجمه‌ش بود. با این که کتاب رو تا پایان خواندم (و این یعنی برام غیر قابل تحمل نبود!) اما بعید می‌دونم باز هم سراغ اثری از توماس مان برم؛ دست کم نه با چنین ترجمه‌ای.
review.text_raw:
کاملا تصادفی و بدون برنامه، پایان خوانش کتاب همزمان شد با زادروز توماس مان!
«مرگ در ونیز» داستانی از سیر سقوط نویسنده‌ای به نام گوستاو اشنباخه که اعتبار جهانیش رو نادیده می‌گیره و سفرش رو آغاز می‌کنه تا به ونیز می‌رسه. در هتل محل اقامتش شیدای پسر نوجوان لهستانی میشه و این پسر، میشه منبع الهام اشنباخ. همزمان با این شوریدگی، وبا هم در ونیز شایع میشه و بقیه‌ش رو خودتون بخونید.🙂
«مرگ در ونیز» رمان کوتاهی با مایه‌های فلسفیه که اگر موفق بشید پنجاه صفحه‌ی اولش رو بخونید، به احتمال نودونه درصد تا پایانش ادامه می‌دید. نثر کتاب، پر از تمثیل و استعاره است که من باهاش مشکلی نداشتم اما ممکنه حوصله‌ی خیلی‌ها رو سر ببره. مهم‌ترین نقطه ضعف کتاب هم از دید من ترجمه‌ش بود.
با این که کتاب رو تا پایان خواندم (و این یعنی برام غیر قابل تحمل نبود!) اما بعید می‌دونم باز هم سراغ اثری از توماس مان برم؛ دست کم نه با چنین ترجمه‌ای.

review.text: It begins with a journey to Venice & concludes with a death. Goodness, how many poetic layers there are in between. We have a full page that describes a sunrise which includes some of the Greek pantheon. It's a story that slowly extends outward, becoming richer with the more allusions you are familiar with. These allusions are what make the layers become poetic because they invite you to read it with various interpretations in mind. The translation I read was by Kenneth Burke, 1925, Alfred A. Knopf (Reprinted in the Everyman's Modern Library)

review.text: I don't know what's wrong with me. I started this book just fine, Mann's writing was something familiar to me, I liked it. But then, it began to bore me to death. This happened a couple of years ago. But still, I think I'm going to give it another shot. Soon. In a couple of days weeks. Definitely before Christmas.

review.text: İlk 20 sayfanın özellikle psikolojik tahliller ve soyut betimlemeler nedeniyle beni zorladığını itiraf etmeliyim. Bu kitap hep böyle mi devam edecek diye düşünürken, yazarın olayları ve Tadzio’yu devreye sokması ile kitap benim açımdan anlaşılabilir hale geldi. Sıfıra yakın diyalog ve bol betimleme içermesinden ötürü okuyucuyu zorlasa da, güzel bir öykü anlattığı için 5 star☺️

review.text: Un clásico entre los clásicos al que le tenía mucho respeto por temor a ser una lectura muy densa. Densa no es, se lee muy bien pero tiene una alta carga simbólica a la que le tienes que pillar el punto. Me han gustado muchísimo todos los temas que trata: la belleza, la inspiración, la comparación entre la juventud y el ocaso de la vida... Es una historia bellísima con un hilo argumental que te hace reflexionar y que te deja un poso enorme una vez lo terminas. Recomendadísmo.

review.text: "Solitude produces originality, bold & astonishing beauty, poetry. But solitude also produces perverseness, the disproportionate, the absurd, and the forbidden." Death in Venice: When unreciprocated attractions go beyond the concept of beauty. Thomas Mann's novella was published in 1912 and centres around the suffering events of a German writer who visits Venice. Gustav von Aschenbach, the protagonist, is a famous German author in his early fifties who was widowed at a young age. He is a dedicated writer, disciplined and severe. As the story opens and goes by, strange events occur in a cemetery that leaves Gustav embarrassed and aroused. Afterwards, he resolves to take a holiday. Reaching Pula on the Austro-Hungarian coast, the protagonist decides to go to Venice and takes a suite in the Grand Hôtel des Bains on the Lido island. When the German writer arrives into his Hotel, he finds that there's a Polish family among the guests. At dinner time, among the Polish guests, there's a young boy of about fourteen in a sailor suit. Like a sort of epiphany, Aschenbach finds the boy extremely beautiful, like a deity. By the name of "Tadzio", Gustav gets highly attracted to the youthful beauty of the young lad, developing into an irrational obsession. Speechless and embarrassed, no word is spoken to the boy throughout the narrative. Aschenbach's attraction gets critical, where his concept of beauty reaches absurd levels of love towards the boy. As the story goes on, external and natural events will determine Gustav's faith. It's important to mention the intertextual link to the Philosophy of Plato (Symposium and Phaedrus) and the contrast between the God of restraint, Apollo, and the God of excess and pleasure, Dionysus. The Nietzschean reference is highly detailed in here and mainly taken from the book "The Birth of Tragedy". Aschenbach restraints his irrational obsession and he, therefore, denies the powers of Dionysus. He finds himself dragged into an obsession that exceeds rationality and morality. Consequently, a suffering severeness is held within his emotions. Tadzio, the young Polish boy was, in fact, a young boy that Thomas Mann had seen during his Venice trip in 1911. Mann, therefore, recreated his inner experience into a novella. Venice, as an artistic city, has its allusive role in the plot to highlight the dramatic events that take place throughout the story. The Protagonist had suffered tragic events in the past, and he did put his suffering into art and poetry. Under the perspective of the protagonist, what may happen when the beauty of a young lad strikes us to the point of eroticism and love? The effects of his past and his solitude indicate the outcome of his irrational attraction. Therefore, his suppressed emotions are at the same time the main cause of his demise. According to the Dualism of Apollo and Dionysus, a dilemma between pleasure and restraint take place. It's through this dilemma that the author manages to address it with elegant prose. A kind of prose that highlights tense events with tragic outcomes. The concept of beauty and attraction is limitless, but at times, it doesn't mean it's right. It's through right and wrong, pleasure and restraint that Thomas Mann artistically approaches that in a dramatic way. For those who are interested in Cinema, I totally recommend Luchino Visconti's movie. Stunningly elegant with Gustav Mahler's soundtrack, this 1971 film, successfully expresses the deepest and darkest emotions that the book so accurately represents. Rating: 4/5 Stars
