review.text: به نام او یک توماس مان یکی از محبوبترین نویسنده‌های من است، از نسل نویسنده‌های قدر قدرتی که صرف تفنن نمی‌نوشتند و به هیچ‌وجه نباید از سر تفنن با آثارشان مواجه شد. در این هفته برای بار دوم مرگ در ونیز را خواندم و بیش از پیش از آن لذت بردم و ظرایف آن را دریافت کردم، علاوه بر متن داستان مقدمه حسن نکوروح بسیار خواندنی و آموزنده است. دو قسمتی از کتاب تفسیرهای زندگی نوشته ویل دورانت (انتشارات نیلوفر) در مورد مرگ در ونیز: "این حال و هوای خوشدلانه در نوشته‌های مان، چندان نپایید گرچه خود او بسیار شادمانه‌تر از کتابهایش بود. در بهار ۱۹۱۱ مدت یک ماه در لیدو به سر برد. گویا شهر ونیز سیمای ابری و بارانیش را در آن روزها به او نشان داده باشد، هنگامی که مرواریدهای معماری آن را در پرده‌ای از مه پیچیده شده است و جلوه‌های چشمگیر آن مبهم و ناپیداست. هرچه بود توماس مان دستمایه یکی غم‌انگیزترین و بهترین داستانهای خود مرگ در ونیز را از همان جا گرفت. قسمت عمده داستان از تجربه‌هایش مایه می‌گرفت: نوجوانی لهستانی و زیبا، بیماری واگیر وبا و گم شدن چمدان که خروج از شهر را به تعویق انداخته بود. همه چیز از قبل مشخص شده است حتی شخصیت اصلی داستان، او خود توماس مان بود که در هیئتی دیکر به انحطاط بالقوه نهفته در هنر که از نظم و منهیات اخلاقی گریزان بود می‌اندیشید" سه من پیش تر از این به دلیل جدیدتر بودن ترجمه حدادی اول آن را خریدم و بعد چنان پشیمان شدم که مگو، واقعا نخواستنی و نخواندنی بود. خودتان ببینید و قضاوت کنید.
review.text_raw:
به نام او

یک
توماس مان یکی از محبوبترین نویسنده‌های من است، از نسل نویسنده‌های قدر قدرتی که صرف تفنن نمی‌نوشتند و به هیچ‌وجه نباید از سر تفنن با آثارشان مواجه شد.
در این هفته برای بار دوم مرگ در ونیز را خواندم و بیش از پیش از آن لذت بردم و ظرایف آن را دریافت کردم، علاوه بر متن داستان مقدمه حسن نکوروح بسیار خواندنی و آموزنده است.

دو
قسمتی از کتاب تفسیرهای زندگی نوشته ویل دورانت (انتشارات نیلوفر) در مورد مرگ در ونیز:
"این حال و هوای خوشدلانه در نوشته‌های مان، چندان نپایید گرچه خود او بسیار شادمانه‌تر از کتابهایش بود. در بهار ۱۹۱۱ مدت یک ماه در لیدو به سر برد. گویا شهر ونیز سیمای ابری و بارانیش را در آن روزها به او نشان داده باشد، هنگامی که مرواریدهای معماری آن را در پرده‌ای از مه پیچیده شده است و جلوه‌های چشمگیر آن مبهم و ناپیداست. هرچه بود توماس مان دستمایه یکی غم‌انگیزترین و بهترین داستانهای خود مرگ در ونیز را از همان جا گرفت. قسمت عمده داستان از تجربه‌هایش مایه می‌گرفت: نوجوانی لهستانی و زیبا، بیماری واگیر وبا و گم شدن چمدان که خروج از شهر را به تعویق انداخته بود. همه چیز از قبل مشخص شده است حتی شخصیت اصلی داستان، او خود توماس مان بود که در هیئتی دیکر به انحطاط بالقوه نهفته در هنر که از نظم و منهیات اخلاقی گریزان بود می‌اندیشید"

سه
من پیش تر از این به دلیل جدیدتر بودن ترجمه حدادی اول آن را خریدم و بعد چنان پشیمان شدم که مگو، واقعا نخواستنی و نخواندنی بود. خودتان ببینید و قضاوت کنید.

review.text: Aschenbach, com 65 anos, escritor, compositor e maestro no fim de carreira, que decide partir para Veneza. Sem família e sem amigos, viaja sozinho e aloja-se numa estância de férias. Sem nada para fazer ocupa os seus dias a observar as pessoas que frequentam o hotel e a praia de Lido. Tadzio, é um jovem polaco aristocrata que se encontra de férias com a mãe e duas irmãs. Com 14 anos, é belo e tem um corpo perfeito. Aschenbach fica fascinado pela beleza do jovem, sente-se atraído, tem consciência que esse amor é impossível e ilícito, mas não consegue evitar de o perseguir. Certo dia, Aschenbach ao observar-se ao espelho, sente-se velho, com o cabelo grisalho e rugas no rosto, a juventude perdida, a velhice e o final da sua vida que se aproxima, sente-se triste. Procura o barbeiro mais amiúde afim de melhorar o aspecto visual. Com o excesso de calor e a grande afluência de turistas, as águas de Veneza estão contaminadas, a polícia manda colocar germicidas nos canais da cidade, para evitar a propagação de doenças. Embora as precauções as pessoas adoecem, e assim que os boatos se espalham os turistas abandonam Veneza. Tadzio e sua família partem e Aschenbach observa a sua partida

review.text: Beautiful writing with vivid and magnificent descriptions. The main character is an aging writer who has made a reputation for himself and then comes to a point in his life where he laments a sense of isolation and mental fatigue. He is torn between a perfectionism and dignity that he has fashioned from an early age through hard work, and long restrained passions and impulses which he would like to give free rein to. The Venitian setting is wonderfully depicted and the characterization is almost brought to perfection, but the simple lack of substantial action taking place leaves me with a feeling of incompleteness. This novella seems to be more concerned with aesthetics and in this respect it is an excellent one. Nonetheless, it is not easily accessible for its numerous philosophical or mythological references. It remains fairly enjoyable though.
review.text_raw:
Beautiful writing with vivid and magnificent descriptions. The main character is an aging writer who has made a reputation for himself and then comes to a point in his life where he laments a sense of isolation and mental fatigue. He is torn between a perfectionism and dignity that he has fashioned from an early age through hard work, and long restrained passions and impulses which he would like to give free rein to.

The Venitian setting is wonderfully depicted and the characterization is almost brought to perfection, but the simple lack of substantial action taking place leaves me with a feeling of incompleteness. This novella seems to be more concerned with aesthetics and in this respect it is an excellent one. Nonetheless, it is not easily accessible for its numerous philosophical or mythological references. It remains fairly enjoyable though.

review.text: Es una novela corta, unas 80 páginas nada más, pero no por ello es simple. Está escrita en tercera persona, pero ese narrador omnisciente logra un relato reflexivo e íntimo de los pensamientos del protagonista, un escritor laureado de su carrera que entrando en la vejez se cuestiona su obra. Su obsesión por el joven Tadzio va acompañada de reflexiones, teorías, filosofía y justificaciones de todo tipo, e incluso de una correspondencia que solo existe en su cabeza. El inicio es algo lento, pero luego se agiliza el relato, veo un paralelismo entre la corrupción de la ciudad, (la de la enfermedad y la burocrática), y la propia corrupción interna del escritor que se va degradando hasta convertirse en el monigote grotesco que tanto critica y juzga al principio. Lo recomiendo. es un clásico imperdible.
review.text_raw:
Es una novela corta, unas 80 páginas nada más, pero no por ello es simple.

Está escrita en tercera persona, pero ese narrador omnisciente logra un relato reflexivo e íntimo de los pensamientos del protagonista, un escritor laureado de su carrera que entrando en la vejez se cuestiona su obra.
Su obsesión por el joven Tadzio va acompañada de reflexiones, teorías, filosofía y justificaciones de todo tipo, e incluso de una correspondencia que solo existe en su cabeza.

El inicio es algo lento, pero luego se agiliza el relato, veo un paralelismo entre la corrupción de la ciudad, (la de la enfermedad y la burocrática), y la propia corrupción interna del escritor que se va degradando hasta convertirse en el monigote grotesco que tanto critica y juzga al principio.

Lo recomiendo. es un clásico imperdible.

review.text: خيبة كبيرة....مع الاسف اول لقاء مع الاسم الكبير توماس مان اخرج منه خالي الوفاض، لا اعرف كم من نسبة فشل هذه القراءة تتحملها الترجمة السيئة لكمال داغر وكم منها الرواية نفسها، كان الايقاع سريعا والافكار متوالية وغير مترا��طة، كانما الكاتب يحاول القفز من هنا الى هناك وانت متشبث به تحاول ان لا تسقط، مرتين او ثلاث ضغطت على نفسي كي اكمل القراءة. بشكل عام فان الاهتمام والترجمة للأدب الالماني ضعيفين بصورة واضحة عندنا في العالم العربي، فلا مجال للمقارنة بترجمة هذا الادب مع الادبين الانجليزي والفرنسي بسبب الارتباطات الاستعمارية والتواصل، وحتى الادب الروسي حصل على اهتمام أكبر بكثير، لذلك في المرة القادمة سافكر مرتين قبل ان اقرأ كتابا المانياً باللغة العربية.

review.text: Il celeberrimo soggiorno al Lido di Venezia di Gustav von Aschenbach, letterato solitario, di salute cagionevole e umore volubile. L’infatuazione per il giovane Tadzio non fa che amplificare la sua lotta interiore, la vergogna della disfatta fisica, i sogni caotici. Nel frattempo, in città scoppia un’epidemia di colera che le autorità cercano di tenere nascosta, tra silenzi e smentite. Tra le altre cose, è un mini-compendio di estetica decadente, desideri inesplorati e fisiognomica applicata. [76/100]
review.text_raw:
Il celeberrimo soggiorno al Lido di Venezia di Gustav von Aschenbach, letterato solitario, di salute cagionevole e umore volubile. L’infatuazione per il giovane Tadzio non fa che amplificare la sua lotta interiore, la vergogna della disfatta fisica, i sogni caotici.
Nel frattempo, in città scoppia un’epidemia di colera che le autorità cercano di tenere nascosta, tra silenzi e smentite.
Tra le altre cose, è un mini-compendio di estetica decadente, desideri inesplorati e fisiognomica applicata.

[76/100]

review.text: Profoundly troubling and multi-layered, this novel recounts a fictitious but imo everyday kind of tragedy. Not because of Venice. Or strawberries. Or forbidden love. No, to me, the main character, Gustav von Aschenbach, was an author who lost his way in reality , falling in love with a good-looking ("beautiful") boy, then passively allowing himself to be destroyed. Although the story was powerfully presented, I felt the usual (to me) heavy sense of doom -- to me, the heavy existential signature of Thomas Mann.
review.text_raw:
Profoundly troubling and multi-layered, this novel recounts a fictitious but imo everyday kind of tragedy. Not because of Venice. Or strawberries. Or forbidden love.

No, to me, the main character, Gustav von Aschenbach, was an author who lost his way in reality, falling in love with a good-looking ("beautiful") boy, then passively allowing himself to be destroyed.

Although the story was powerfully presented, I felt the usual (to me) heavy sense of doom -- to me, the heavy existential signature of Thomas Mann.

review.text: I couldn't finish this nauseating book, and gave up on it after reading 30 or 40 pages of the author rhapsodizing in the most erotic terms about a pre-pubescent boy. This is not in itself a fatal flaw in a work of literature, but the manner of its treatment is what I find unbearable. Unlike, say, Lolita , which was written by an author fully in conscious control of its energies, this novella reads more like an intensely-neurotic eruption of barely-repressed erotic energy on the part of its author, whose attraction to young boys is well-known.

review.text: What a beautiful yet sad novella! it is a ode to beauty and the end of life, the loss of beauty and the confusion over what is beautiful. It is a story about yearning for something one can no longer obtain. It is a tale about narcissism and a tragic one at that. I'm glad I read this now. I do not believe the young version of me would have got it.

I do not believe the young version of me would have got it.

review.text: Un piccolo capolavoro che filosofeggia dal tempo degli dei greci al ventesimo secolo e raffinatamente paragona i sentimenti di due persone a quelli di due dei, trasuda stati d'animo profondi e rende vero un personaggio che cerca di trovare se stesso fino alla fine del romanzo.

review.text: Romanian review: Este frustrant că omenirea, ca un tot, este atât de obsedată de sexualitate, încât prima întrebare pe care și-o pune oricine începe cartea este dacă personajul principal este pedofil. Nu voi fi ipocrit- și eu mi-am pus aceeași întrebare- însă în momentul în care intrepretezi cartea ca pe povestea unui bărbat de vârstă mijlocie obsedat sexual de un băiat de 14 ani, pierzi aproape în întregime valorea cărții. Știu că orientarea sexuală a lui Thomas Mann este un subiect oarecum controversat și că se consideră că a fost homosexual sau poate bisexual, având în vedere că s-a căsătorit și a avut șase copii- deși asta nu spune chiar atât de multe, luând în considerare epoca în care a trăit. Totuși, nu pot spune că am simțit în vreun moment că personajul principal, Gustav von Aschenbach, ar fi simțit o atracție trupească pentru Tadgio. Tadgio este mai mult un personaj simbolic, o metaforă. El simbolizează tinerețea, vitalitatea, frumusețea ideală. Tadgio este tot ce și-ar dori Gustav să fie, tot ce a pierdut și nu mai poate recupera. Cartea este mai degrabă despre pierderea tinereții decât despre pofta trupească. Au fost mai multe momente în care Gustav von Aschenbach se gândește la tinerețea pierdută, cel mai important fiind acela în care încearcă să se întinerească forțat spre sfârșitul nuvelei. Acel moment este o trimitere la un personaj introdus la începutul cărții ale cărui încercări de a arăta din nou tânăr îl făceau ridicol și pentru care personajul principal îl disprețuia. Cea mai evidentă temă este obsesia și consecințele ei tragice, obsesie care îl face pe Gustav von Aschenbach să nu părăsească o Veneție decimată de epidemia de holeră, deși s-a plâns de mai multe ori pe parcusul nuvelei de problemele de sănătate. Cartea este și o reflecție cu privire la artă: Veneția a fost unul dintre locurile unde s-a născut Renașterea, așa că are sens să fie și locul în care arta moare, simbolizată prin moartea personajului principal, un scriitor celebru. (Nu este un spoiler, dacă ai citit titlul înainte să citești cartea.) Cu toată tematica interesantă, nu i-aș fi dat acestei nuvele cinci stele dacă proza lui Thomas Mann nu era atât de superbă. ,,Moarte în Veneția" este scrisă atât de bine, încât se simte ca un vis confuz al unei minți zbuciumate de febră. Voi încheia recenzia printr-un citat suberb despre singurătate și despre arta de a scrie care, într-un fel, rezumă întreaga nuvelă. Singurătatea l-a făcut pe Gustav von Aschenbach să fie un scriitor atât de bun, dar i-a și cauzat obsesia care i-a pricinuit moartea. Ultima observație pe care vreau să o fac este că de-abia aștept să citesc romanele lui Mann. Omul singuratic în muţenia lui vede şi simte lucrurile în acelaşi timp mai şters, dar şi mai pătrunzător decît cel ce trăieşte în mijlocul mulţimii; gîndurile îi sînt mai grele, mai ciudate şi au totdeauna un iz de tristeţe. Imagini şi constatări ce ar putea fi uşor îndepărtate cu o privire, un zîmbet sau cu un schimb de păreri îl preocupă peste măsură, capătă în tăcere proporţii, se adîncesc, se prefac în eveniment, în aventură, în sentiment. Singurătatea dă naştere originalităţii, frumuseţii îndrăzneţe şi stranii, creează poezia. Dar în acelaşi timp singurătatea generează absurdul, falsul, disproporţionatul şi nepermisul. English review: It’s frustrating that humanity, as a whole, is so obsessed with sexuality that the first question anyone asks when starting this book is whether the protagonist is a pedophile. I won’t be hypocritical—I asked myself the same question—but when you interpret the book solely as the story of a middle-aged man sexually obsessed with a 14-year-old boy, you lose almost all the value of the novel. I know Thomas Mann’s sexual orientation is a somewhat controversial topic, with many considering him to have been homosexual or possibly bisexual, given that he married and had six children—although that doesn’t say much, considering the era in which he lived. Still, at no point did I feel that the protagonist, Gustav von Aschenbach, had a carnal attraction to Tadzio. Tadzio is more of a symbolic character, a metaphor. He represents youth, vitality, and ideal beauty. Tadzio embodies everything Gustav wishes to be, everything he has lost and can never regain. The book is more about the loss of youth than physical desire. There are several moments where Gustav reflects on his lost youth, the most significant being when he attempts to artificially rejuvenate himself toward the end of the novella. This moment directly recalls a character introduced at the beginning of the story—someone whose desperate attempts to appear young made him look ridiculous and whom Gustav openly scorned. The most obvious theme is obsession and its tragic consequences, as Gustav von Aschenbach refuses to leave a Venice ravaged by a cholera epidemic, despite complaining several times throughout the novella about his fragile health. The book also serves as a reflection on art: Venice, a birthplace of the Renaissance, fittingly becomes the setting for the death of art, symbolized by the demise of the protagonist, a celebrated writer. (This isn’t a spoiler if you’ve read the title before reading the book.) Despite its intriguing themes, I wouldn’t have given this novella five stars if it weren’t for Thomas Mann’s exquisite prose. "Death in Venice" is written so beautifully that it feels like a feverish, hazy dream of a restless mind. I’ll end this review with a stunning quote about loneliness and the art of writing, which, in a way, encapsulates the entire novella. Loneliness made Gustav von Aschenbach the great writer he was, but it also led to the obsession that caused his death. I’ll conclude by saying that I eagerly look forward to reading Mann’s novels. A solitary, unused to speaking of what he sees and feels, has mental experiences which are at once more intense and less articulate than those of a gregarious man. They are sluggish, yet more wayward, and never without a melancholy tinge. Sights and impressions which others brush aside with a glance, a light comment, a smile, occupy him more than their due; they sink silently in, they take on meaning, they become experience, emotion, adventure. Solitude gives birth to the original in us, to beauty unfamiliar and perilous-to poetry. But also, it gives birth to the opposite: to the perverse, the illicit, the absurd.
review.text_raw:

review.text: Death in Venice by Thomas Mann Death in Venice follows Gustav von Aschenbach, an aging, disciplined German writer who travels to Venice in search of artistic renewal only to become obsessed with the beauty of a Polish boy named Tadzio while the city suffers a quiet cholera outbreak. As Aschenbach’s admiration for Tadzio intensifies from distant fascination to an all‑consuming fixation, he begins to neglect his health and dignity, staying in Venice despite mounting danger, and ultimately collapses and dies on the Lido beach as the cholera epidemic closes in. Mann’s spare, symbolic prose meditates on mortality, aesthetic obsession, and the tension between reason and passion in a story that is both intimate and haunting. For me, the novella felt like an emotional study in longing that unsettled and stayed with me, because Aschenbach’s rigid self‑control gives way to a yearning that is equal parts yearning for beauty and dread of what that yearning reveals about his own fears of aging, death, and loss; Venice’s decaying splendor and the lurking epidemic only deepen that tension. The themes of desire, decay, and the artist’s place in a changing world gave me a chill as much as fascination. Rating: 4 out of 5 Because it is a deeply introspective, beautifully crafted exploration of beauty, obsession, and our fragile hold on life.
review.text_raw:
Death in Venice by Thomas Mann

Death in Venice follows Gustav von Aschenbach, an aging, disciplined German writer who travels to Venice in search of artistic renewal only to become obsessed with the beauty of a Polish boy named Tadzio while the city suffers a quiet cholera outbreak. As Aschenbach’s admiration for Tadzio intensifies from distant fascination to an all‑consuming fixation, he begins to neglect his health and dignity, staying in Venice despite mounting danger, and ultimately collapses and dies on the Lido beach as the cholera epidemic closes in. Mann’s spare, symbolic prose meditates on mortality, aesthetic obsession, and the tension between reason and passion in a story that is both intimate and haunting.

For me, the novella felt like an emotional study in longing that unsettled and stayed with me, because Aschenbach’s rigid self‑control gives way to a yearning that is equal parts yearning for beauty and dread of what that yearning reveals about his own fears of aging, death, and loss; Venice’s decaying splendor and the lurking epidemic only deepen that tension. The themes of desire, decay, and the artist’s place in a changing world gave me a chill as much as fascination.

Rating: 4 out of 5
Because it is a deeply introspective, beautifully crafted exploration of beauty, obsession, and our fragile hold on life.

review.text: Emocionāli skaists un reizē skumji traģisks stāsts par Rakstnieku un viņa liktenīgajām attiecībām ar Skaistumu. Iemīlēšanās saviļņojums, pacilātais jūtu spriegums un neatvairāma iedvesma rakstīt mijas ar apsēstību, izmisumu un pašcieņas mazināšanos. "Rakstnieka laime ir doma, kas pilnīgi spēj kļūt par jūtām, jūtas, kas pilnīgi spēj kļūt par domu." "Katrā ziņā ir labi, ka pasaule pazīst tikai šo skaisto darbu, bet ne tā dzinuļus, ne tā tapšanas apstākļus; jo, zinādami avotus, no kuriem mākslinieks smēlies iedvesmu, tie bieži apjuktu, izbītos, un lieliskā snieguma iedarbība zustu." Tomasa Manna "Nāve Venēcijā" izpildījuma kvalitātes ziņā iet cieši roku rokā ar Viskonti lielisko filmu "Nāve Venēcijā". Skaisti par Skaistumu un sadegšanu tajā.

review.text: L'inquietante ricerca della bellezza Gustav von Aschenbach è un celebre scrittore tedesco, uomo tutto d'un pezzo dalla moralità e senso civico inflessibile. Ha lavorato duramente per arrivare al successo, nonostante il fisico debole e le mille difficoltà. Un giorno, ormai anziano, decide improvvisamente di partire e arriva in una Venezia lugubre e degradata, dove il cielo è plumbeo, le acque melmose, il tanfo insopportabile, le gondole nere che con i loro velluti e cuscini rossi gli ricordano casse da morto e dove i gondolieri borbottano minacciosi. Perché il viaggio? Cosa va cercando. Aschenbach? Cerca il bello, l'interessante, qualcosa che lo smuova dalla noia che lo attanaglia. E il bello lo trova in un quattordicenne polacco, Tadzio. Aschenbach ce lo descrive con una bellezza quasi divina, senza personalità, una statua bellissima ma senz'anima, la rappresentazione della perfezione. Tadzio per Aschenbach rappresenta il tempo perduto, la giovinezza; e se ne invaghisce. Inquietante, diciamo. Perché trattasi di amore tra un anziano e un ragazzino. E' "pedofilia" alla Lolita maniera? No, tutt'altro. Aschenbach è confuso, l'idea della fine della sua vita si fa sempre più assillante e si rende conto di aver vissuto più con la mente che col cuore. L'avvicinarsi della morte però lo spinge a cambiare priorità, a lasciarsi andare alla passione a discapito della razionalità. Ma la sua passione è tutta interiore, perché lui e il ragazzo non si rivolgono nemmeno la parola e tra i due non accade nulla; questa passione però lo fa riflettere, lo fa divenire un personaggio ambiguo, lo riempie di sensi di colpa. Ma le passioni più forti sono quelle inappagate e incomplete: "Niente è più singolare, più imbarazzante che il rapporto tra due persone che si conoscono solo attraverso gli occhi, che si vedono tutti i giorni a tutte le ore, si osservano e nello stesso tempo sono costretti dall'educazione o dalla bizzarria a fingere indifferenza e a passarsi accanto come estranei, senza saluto né parola. Fra di loro c'è inquietudine ed esasperata curiosità, l'isteria di un bisogno insoddisfatto, innaturale e represso di conoscersi e di comunicare e soprattutto una sorta di ansiosa attenzione. Infatti l'uomo ama e onora l'uomo fino a che non è in grado di giudicarlo, e il desiderio è il frutto di una conoscenza incompleta" La città malata e in disfacimento è una metafora del decadimento di quelli che sono stati, fino a quel momento, i punti fermi e le convinzioni di Aschenbach. La morte a Venezia non è quindi tanto quella fisica del protagonista, quanto quella della sua rigida morale e dei suoi principi. La morte a Venezia è un romanzo sul rapporto tra arte e vita borghese. Arte, che non è solamente sregolatezza ma anche godimento e felicità. E vita borghese, che è salute ed efficienza ma anche noia e sottomissione alle leggi dell'economia. "È certamente un bene che il mondo conosca soltanto la bella opera e non le sue origini, non le condizioni e le circostanze del suo sviluppo; giacché la conoscenza delle fonti onde scaturisce l'ispirazione dell'artista potrebbe turbare, spaventare, e così annullare gli effetti della perfezione." Il romanzo ha una partenza lenta, ma va via via accelerando, in un continuo crescendo. La vita iniziale del protagonista, semplice e tranquilla, contrasta con quella della fine, dove Aschenbach è in balia dei sensi senza inibizioni. Anche il linguaggio si arricchisce di metafore con il procedere della narrazione e tende progressivamente ad incupirsi per mostrare i tormenti interiori di un uomo di arte che, dopo aver fatto della sua vita un esempio di moralità, si abbandona alla fine con tutti i sensi ad inseguire la "bellezza" . Alla fine, "la morte a Venezia" è un libro sull'amore, che è irrazionale, ci domina e ci spinge a comportarci in modo spesso insensato. Un sentimento, l'amore, cui tutti sono soggetti, nessuno escluso, indipendentemente dall’età, dal sesso e dal ceto sociale. Anzi, a volte dopo una certa età il bisogno d'amore può essere ancora maggiore. Non c'è niente da fare, adoro Thomas Mann....

Non c'è niente da fare, adoro Thomas Mann....

review.text: An interesting and strange little story. I listened to the audio, translated by Michael Henry Heim. The prose is lyrical and easy to listen to. In the introduction by Michael Cunningham, he states that this translation is more humane and kind to Gustav von Aschenbach than the earlier translation by Porter. Porter apparently made Aschenbach into a lecherous, vile old man. I don’t know if that is true but keeping it in mind, I have to say that this translation didn't make me think of Aschenbach as such. Aschenbach was a sad figure of a man, for sure, having spent a lifetime in social, personal and professional constraints and confinements. Here is a man who, except for a brief & happy marriage, had no fun or humour in his life at all. He did what he was expected to do, when he was expected to do it and responded as expected….at all times. The pressure of such a life whether one is conscious of it or not, must be enormous. He goes to Venice, where the city is sweltering under brutal summer heat and suffering from some unnamed infectious disease that carries people off to the graveyard in the background. On the beach he sees a beautiful 14-year old boy named Tadzio and becomes besotted with him. As the story unfolds, one could look at the story as Porter apparently described it and see an old man following a young boy around town and lusting after him. On the other hand, as it comes across to me in Heim’s translation, it could also be of an old man seeing a young free boy who has his life ahead of him being carefree, enjoying that life and Ashenbach becoming besotted with that (the freedom & life). The boy is a symbol of all that Aschenbach never had in his life. The boy could be a young Aschenbach given a second opportunity. Aschenbach and the boy could be one and the same except for the different circumstances molding them. Heim gave Aschenbach a dignity of sorts. Sure, he’s chasing after a boy but he never touches him or seems to want to touch him. He just wants to observe him and somehow be a part of that life & joy that Tadzio experiences. Aschenbach wants that freedom and joy of living, rather than wanting the boy. Aschenbach is a sad figure, having lived such a dull, restrained life but he embraces life in the end in this small, observational way and the small amount of freedom he got through watching and following Tadzio gave Aschenbach’s life a dignity it didn't have before. I’d like to read the book one day. I think there’s a lot more depth to this than I got out of the audio while commuting through traffic. It’s beautifully worded, it’s a sad story, it could possibly have lecherous aspects to it but it’s also about dignity in some aspect and this translation removed the lechery feeling the story could have had. I think there’s more depth and meaning to this than I got out of it while listening. This is the first of Mann’s books that I’ve read and I look forward to his other works in future.

The boy is a symbol of all that Aschenbach never had in his life. The boy could be a young Aschenbach given a second opportunity. review.text: Bu yılın şimdiye kadar beni en zorlandığı kitap oldu Venedik'te Ölüm. Güçlükle okuduğum kitabı pek de engin olmayan felsefik, sanatsal ve mitolojik birikimimle -haddim olmadan- yorumlamak da kolay olmayacak elbette. Thomas Mann’ın 1911 yılında (36 yaşındayken) yazdığı bu kült eser, otobiyografik özelliğe sahip bir novella olma özelliği taşıyor. Hakkında doktora tezleri yazılan çok derinlikli bir yazardan çıkan çok derinlikli bir eser dersek hiç abartmış olmayız. Eserin belki de yüzyıldan uzun bir süre önce yazılmış olması bugün metni biraz anlaşılması zor hale getiriyor. Durağan ilerleyen olayların yanında çok fazla tasvire yer verilmesi cümleleri takip edilmesi zor bir hale getiriyor (ilk iki bölüm) ancak sonrasında -ana karakterin Venedik’e gelmesiyle- metin dili daha anlaşılır hale geliyor. 50-60 yaşlarında, ünlü ve prestijli bir yazar olan Gustav von Aschenbach’ın yaratıcılık konusunda sıkıntılar yaşadığı bir dönemde dinlenmek ve belki de sorunlarıyla yüzleşmek üzere geldiği Venedik ’te geçen zaman dilimini işliyor eser. Kaldığı otelde Polonyalı bir ailenin genç oğlu Tadzio’da yakaladığı tanrısal güzellik algısı aşka ve derin bir tutkuya dönüşüyor öyle ki bu derin tutku Aschenbach'ı, elinde fırsat varken, ölümden kaçma arzusundan bile alıkoyuyor. Aşk ve ölüm motifleri, eserin en temel öğelerini oluşturuyor. Hikayedeki akış sanatçının trajik çıkmazlarının, sıkışmışlığının, yaşadığı gelgitlerin en yakın şahidi yapıyor bizi. Okunması zor ancak kültürel derinliği olan bir yapıt. Euripides'ten mitolojik referanslar da içeriyor. Sizi de 20. yüzyılın en büyük Alman romancılarından biri olan Thomas Mann ile tanışmaya davet ediyorum. 🖌️ T.

review.text: Aldanan Kadın'dan sonra okuduğum ve aynı temaları daha detaylı, daha derin ve zaman zaman ürpertici bir şekilde işlediği kitabında Mann, bütün bunlara ek olarak güzellik, sanat ve aşk üzerine de oldukça düşünmüş. Çevirisi kaynaklı da olabileceğini düşündüğüm anlatım tarzı - çevirisi Behçet Necatigil'e ait - oldukça doyurucuydu Aldanan Kadın kitabına göre. Kitapta Entelektüel doyguluğa ulaşmış ve kariyerinin zirvesinde olan orta yaşlarındaki yazar Aschenbach'ın, Venedik'i ziyareti ve kaldığı otelde güzeeliğine aşık olduğu ondört yaşındaki bir erkek çocuğu üzerinden güzellik ve aşk kavramları üzerine antik yunan mitolojisini de kullanarak derin monologlar yazmış. Kimi zaman pedofiliye varan bu 'Platonik' aşk monologları Öyle güzel ayarlanmış ki,rahatsız edici boyuta geldiği anda kesiliyor. Hatta okurken uzun süre bunun sapkınlık değil, çocuk üzerinden güzelliğe övgü olduğunu düşünmek istedim ama yine de metin bu konuda zaman zaman rahatsız etti beni. Dikkat!!! Spoiler olabilir!!! . Kİtabın sonu ise kafamda soru işaretleri oluşturdu; Bu yazarın kahramanından intikam alma yolu mu yoksa kahramanı önemsizleştirme isteğinin bir sonucu mu, yoksa sevdiğini kaybeden birinin ani yokoluşu mu?
review.text_raw:
Aldanan Kadın'dan sonra okuduğum ve aynı temaları daha detaylı, daha derin ve zaman zaman ürpertici bir şekilde işlediği kitabında Mann, bütün bunlara ek olarak güzellik, sanat ve aşk üzerine de oldukça düşünmüş. Çevirisi kaynaklı da olabileceğini düşündüğüm anlatım tarzı - çevirisi Behçet Necatigil'e ait - oldukça doyurucuydu Aldanan Kadın kitabına göre. Kitapta Entelektüel doyguluğa ulaşmış ve kariyerinin zirvesinde olan orta yaşlarındaki yazar Aschenbach'ın, Venedik'i ziyareti ve kaldığı otelde güzeeliğine aşık olduğu ondört yaşındaki bir erkek çocuğu üzerinden güzellik ve aşk kavramları üzerine antik yunan mitolojisini de kullanarak derin monologlar yazmış. Kimi zaman pedofiliye varan bu 'Platonik' aşk monologları Öyle güzel ayarlanmış ki,rahatsız edici boyuta geldiği anda kesiliyor. Hatta okurken uzun süre bunun sapkınlık değil, çocuk üzerinden güzelliğe övgü olduğunu düşünmek istedim ama yine de metin bu konuda zaman zaman rahatsız etti beni.
Dikkat!!! Spoiler olabilir!!!
.
Kİtabın sonu ise kafamda soru işaretleri oluşturdu; Bu yazarın kahramanından intikam alma yolu mu yoksa kahramanı önemsizleştirme isteğinin bir sonucu mu, yoksa sevdiğini kaybeden birinin ani yokoluşu mu?

review.text: 4.5 Es un viaje maravilloso que recorre la belleza contemplativa, la decadencia del cuerpo, el deseo como motor. El lenguaje y la narrativa son hermosos, es un gran clásico. El protagonista es un escritor que trata de salir de su entorno y viaja a Venecia para descansar y despejarse para escribir, el autor nos traza un retrato de sus preferencias sexuales con mucha sutileza. Hay reflexiones muy interesantes. “… se trataba de un joven falsificado: era un viejo, sin duda alguna”.

review.text: Re-read this b/c thought it was relevant to something I'm writing. I think the plot is cool but the prose is mostly lofty, impenetrable pontification. Not all that fun and makes me think I won't read more Mann, which is a relief b/c Magic Mountain is one of those books I scowl at in bookshops: I can sense it sneering at me for not having read it.

review.text: Wonderful. A much more malignant Venice than we might imagine today is represented. An almost post-apocalyptic city with menacing poverty and the suggestion of plague hanging in the air. Despite the subject of illness and mortality, as with all of Thomas Mann's books, there is an elegance and beauty to compensate and comfort.

Despite the subject of illness and mortality, as with all of Thomas Mann's books, there is an elegance and beauty to compensate and comfort.

review.text: 3.5⭐ La muerte en Venecia es una novela corta, maravillosamente escrita. La prosa de Thomas Mann es lírica, enrevesada y llena de simbolismo. La historia sigue a Gustav Aschenbach, un escritor alemán que viaja a Venecia buscando descanso e inspiración. Allí conoce a Tadzio, un joven polaco cuya belleza lo deslumbra y lo obsesiona. Lo interesante es cómo Mann narra el recorrido interno de Aschenbach: pasa de la admiración y la inspiración a perderse en el deseo, del control absoluto al caos personal. El texto está lleno de referencias a la mitología griega, donde la belleza era una cualidad divina. Tadzio no solo es un muchacho bello, sino que encarna la belleza ideal platónica: para Aschenbach, contemplarlo es como mirar una obra de arte viva. Al mismo tiempo, la Venecia de fondo se va deteriorando poco a poco bajo el avance de una epidemia, un paralelismo con la propia decadencia del protagonista. La ciudad refleja su interior: lo que comienza como esplendor se convierte en descomposición. En este sentido, la novela muestra con mucha fuerza cómo belleza y muerte se entrelazan. Lo único que me resultó difícil fue conectar emocionalmente con el protagonista. El discurso de Mann es más filosófico que sentimental, y eso me mantuvo en una mirada más externa, sin llegar a conmoverme del todo con el destino de Aschenbach. Dicho esto, creo que es un libro que vale la pena explorar si te atraen las novelas filosóficas, con reflexiones sobre el arte, el deseo y la decadencia, y con un final trágico e inevitable.
review.text_raw:
3.5⭐

La muerte en Venecia es una novela corta, maravillosamente escrita. La prosa de Thomas Mann es lírica, enrevesada y llena de simbolismo.

La historia sigue a Gustav Aschenbach, un escritor alemán que viaja a Venecia buscando descanso e inspiración. Allí conoce a Tadzio, un joven polaco cuya belleza lo deslumbra y lo obsesiona. Lo interesante es cómo Mann narra el recorrido interno de Aschenbach: pasa de la admiración y la inspiración a perderse en el deseo, del control absoluto al caos personal.

El texto está lleno de referencias a la mitología griega, donde la belleza era una cualidad divina. Tadzio no solo es un muchacho bello, sino que encarna la belleza ideal platónica: para Aschenbach, contemplarlo es como mirar una obra de arte viva.

Al mismo tiempo, la Venecia de fondo se va deteriorando poco a poco bajo el avance de una epidemia, un paralelismo con la propia decadencia del protagonista. La ciudad refleja su interior: lo que comienza como esplendor se convierte en descomposición. En este sentido, la novela muestra con mucha fuerza cómo belleza y muerte se entrelazan.

Lo único que me resultó difícil fue conectar emocionalmente con el protagonista. El discurso de Mann es más filosófico que sentimental, y eso me mantuvo en una mirada más externa, sin llegar a conmoverme del todo con el destino de Aschenbach.

Dicho esto, creo que es un libro que vale la pena explorar si te atraen las novelas filosóficas, con reflexiones sobre el arte, el deseo y la decadencia, y con un final trágico e inevitable.

review.text: Gustav von Aschenbach is a 50-ish writer who has a desire to leave his current place and take a sojourn elsewhere to recapture inspiration and zeal for his life and his occupation, a need for something different. “It was a desire to flee, he had to admit to himself, this yearning for the distant and the novel, this desire for liberty…” Obviously, this work has some symbolism and allegory that goes beyond the surface level of the narrative of this man traversing into a new place and, once there, dealing with his obsession and compulsion with a boy named Tadzio. However, I just had a problem with some aspects of this book and how important themes were handled. I just felt like there wasn’t much here, and a lot of it was handled in a rather overt, overly showy presentation (from Aschenbach’s point of view). Because of this, the overall tone felt a little pretentious and showy, as if the author was trying to “wow” us with style over substance. I mean, there is lovely prose here and there, but the main character is presented in a rather trite, self-important way that sort of grates on the reader’s nerves. It was a little off putting, and it creates a disconnect between the protagonist and the reader, a distantness that makes us not as invested in the outcome. I did appreciate the backdrop of the setting, and the historical aspect rings forth in an authentic way with the cholera epidemic adding a devastating dimension to the tale. However, even at a very brief one hundred plus pages, Death in Venice still felt like a long read. I’ll say 2.5 stars, rounded down.
review.text_raw:
Gustav von Aschenbach is a 50-ish writer who has a desire to leave his current place and take a sojourn elsewhere to recapture inspiration and zeal for his life and his occupation, a need for something different.

“It was a desire to flee, he had to admit to himself, this yearning for the distant and the novel, this desire for liberty…”

Obviously, this work has some symbolism and allegory that goes beyond the surface level of the narrative of this man traversing into a new place and, once there, dealing with his obsession and compulsion with a boy named Tadzio. However, I just had a problem with some aspects of this book and how important themes were handled.

I just felt like there wasn’t much here, and a lot of it was handled in a rather overt, overly showy presentation (from Aschenbach’s point of view). Because of this, the overall tone felt a little pretentious and showy, as if the author was trying to “wow” us with style over substance.

I mean, there is lovely prose here and there, but the main character is presented in a rather trite, self-important way that sort of grates on the reader’s nerves. It was a little off putting, and it creates a disconnect between the protagonist and the reader, a distantness that makes us not as invested in the outcome.

I did appreciate the backdrop of the setting, and the historical aspect rings forth in an authentic way with the cholera epidemic adding a devastating dimension to the tale.

However, even at a very brief one hundred plus pages, Death in Venice still felt like a long read. I’ll say 2.5 stars, rounded down.

review.text: Irgendwie irre Geschichte, die einerseits wahnsinnig kurz war, aber am Ende doch total rund. Überhaupt mochte ich das Ende sehr. Es war überraschend düster und mystisch und hat auch noch einmal Machtverhältnisse über den Haufen geworfen. Je mehr ich darüber nachdenke, desto spannender ist, was Thomas Mann hier erzählt, auch wenn mir das Ganze während ich es gelesen habe, eher platt und pathetisch vorkam. Immerhin muss ich sagen, dass diese wenigen Seiten mir sehr viel Stoff zum Nachdenken gegeben haben, was super ist. Außerdem ist Thomas Manns Sprache einfach bemerkenswert schön, präzise und ausgeklügelt. Allein der Stil ist eine Lektüre wert. Auch wenn ich sagen muss, dass der gute Mann mir eindeutig etwas zu belehrend ist :D

review.text: [4.5] The story is full of atmospheric visual and sensory detail about Aschenbach's surroundings in Venice and I was repeatedly reminded of The Smiths' line " the air hangs heavy like a dulling wine". The first few pages of Death in Venice are particularly dense, and I abandoned it twice. Yet eventual perseverance was worthwhile as the style and / or translation soon improve, and the story packs a lot of interesting ideas into its 79 pages. When I first bought the book, I assumed it was about a gay crush on a boy in his late teens - and when I noticed out the object of admiration was actually so young, became reluctant to read it. Finding out recently that Aschenbach doesn't actually do anything with or to the boy, even speak to him (though there is a lot of creepy-stalky watching from a distance) lifted this apprehension, and so I set out to read the book from cover to cover this time. It is a fable of reason vs abandoning oneself to feeling, Apollonian vs Dionysian - and an allegory for the rise and fall of the aesthetic movement. A distinguished writer who prides himself on his order and rationality finds himself seized by feelings and urges: to travel, succumb to obsessive admiration of beauty, make impulsive decisions, and ultimately choose to stay in a cholera-ridden city and die, all because of his infatuation with the boy Tadzio. (Alsc echoes Wilde's arrest, which he could have avoided by going abroad.) "Decadence" is a word I've grown used to hearing in a positive sense, but Death in Venice demonstrates its original associations with seediness and decay - and those of unacceptable sexual and romantic feelings which the aesthetic movement had in the eyes of the mainstream late nineteenth and early twentieth century. So, after nearly a couple of decades with a positive and contemporary view of Wilde and the aesthetes, I found it rather interesting to experience the mixture of distaste and interest provoked by different aspects of Aschenbach's fatal foray into surrender to his feelings, and get a sense of how people at the time may have seen the subculture. As the arc of the story essentially takes a negative stance against aestheticism, this can be frustrating if you'd rather take a positive view about the cultural movement (and consider that feeling & reason don't have to be opposed) and separate that side of the book from Aschenbach's creepy obsessive interest in a young teenage boy. Yet there are more universals here than one might expect - or than I expected - from this aspect of the plot: it's also a morality tale about sacrificing your health, career and reasonable self-interest on the altar of an infatuation. And on a lighter note, it was surprisingly easy to relate to Aschenbach's sudden wish to get out of his old haunts in Germany, and his haphazard decisions at the stage where he ends up staying in Venice because of a suitcase that went astray. He's complex enough to provoke identification, understanding, frustration and disgust at different times in the story: a well-drawn character. There are Plato references here which I would have got more out of if I'd read those texts - instead they're over there on a shelf, unopened. It is easy to see why this book is a classic, as it has the complexity to deserve that designation - though perhaps there are better versions than this 1928 translation. LATER ADDITION: Visconti's film of Death in Venice is worthwhile as a companion piece, especially if you find the book unemotional. His Aschenbach (Dirk Bogarde) is a composer and the verbal philosophising is confined to a few flashback conversational scenes. I felt that the rival Apollonian v Dionysian ideas and the characters were shown with little or no value judgement, unlike in Mann's writing. Aschenbach's struggles with himself about his fixation with the boy, and his lack of actual action should be enough to satisfy morally. If you are comfortable with slow-paced arthouse films (I confess to sometimes finding it dull on first watch just after reading the book, but absolutely loved it the second time about a year later), it captures feeling far more than the book ever does: the decay of the man and the decay of place... a sense that Aschenbach has been in decline since the death of his own family and that Tadzio is a coda at life's end: a psychologically troubling one in the reality of his being a teenage boy, but the ideas he represents are also a revelation of a different approach to life.

review.text: One could hardly accuse literary lion Gustav von Aschenbach, protagonist of this novel, of leading a colorful life. Even the erupting passions of his youth were safely confined to the characters of his imagination and further circumscribed by a prevailing rationalism. A lengthy third person narrative imprisons the readers in an introspective review of his life, one guided by an obsessive devotion to intellectual rigor. He attributes his literary success to an intractable self-discipline (a kind of personal “triumph of the will”). But even his success was beginning to feel empty: “Gradually something official, didactic, crept into Gustav Aschenbach's productions, his style in later life fought shy of any abruptness and boldness, any subtle and unexpected shadings; he inclined toward the fixed and standardized, the conventionally elegant, the conservative, the formal, the formulated, nearly.” (p. 15) Without that creative spark, how empty that must have felt to a fifty-year old author who has devoted his entire life to Art, who even justifies his desire for a long lifespan in terms of the accumulated wisdom age must surely confer on future achievement. Such thoughts consume his mind as he strolls through what feels like a fin-de-Siecle Munich. He passes a cemetary and glimpses an odd looking tourist. The author provides some subtle humor for the reader. Aschenbach observes the lofty epitaphs and idealized headstone carvings. “...[H]e found a grave diversion in spelling out the formulas and letting his mind's eye lose itself in their transparent mysticism.” (p.2) The idea of a diversion has appeal – something alien, yet not too adventurous. Fate intervenes. He ends up in Venice, a symbol of Renaissance decay. The image is reinforced by the sight of an old man garrishly made-up to present a counterfeit of youthfulness. Aschenbach is filled with disgust. An abrupt contrast follows. A well-to-do Polish family is staying at his hotel. The 14 year-old boy becomes an object of obsession, which he compares to the perfection of a young Adonis. At the same time, a pallor suggests a tragically brief future. Perfect beauty – so ephemeral. His obsession blinds him to obvious portents. He recalls how a visit to the city in his youth left him sickened. He now notes the fetid odor of the waters. The air becomes a stifling miasma. There are furtive rumors and the hotel begins to empty prematurely. Even the fruit takes an unwholesome turn: “He could no longer endure the thirst that was torturing him, and he looked around for some immediate relief. From a little vegetable store he bought some fruit – strawberries, soft and overly ripe – and he ate them as he walked.” (p.82) Mann draws on diverse images. The boy is an idealized vision of perfect beauty and innocence. Aschenbach's own lapse in creativity is a disruption of dynamic between Apollonian and Dionysian urges. His surrender to homoeroticism is a rebuke of bourgeois morality. He contemplates society's attempts to deny the Artist an emotional license essential to Art. "But the fact was that Aschenbach did not want soberness: his intoxication was too precious. Who can explain the stamp and the nature of the artist? Who can understandd this deep instinctive welding of discipline and license? For to be unable to want wholesome soberness is license." (p.54) Thomas Mann assumes his reader is conversant with the turbulent state of fin-de-Siecle German culture. The book was published in 1912. However, I could not escape the knowledge of how this culture was perverted by the Nazi mythology that followed in the succeeding decades. Aschenbach's obsession with the 14 year-old boy felt creepy rather than symbolic. Aschenbach himself felt remote. His death (no spoiler there) was regrettable but also pathetic. I chose this particular edition, a translation by Kenneth Burke, because of the introduction by Colm Tóibin. There are actually a number of translations available, each with a slightly different but nuanced spirit. NOTES: A comparison between Helen Lowe-Porter and Michael Henry Heim: https://lizzysiddal.wordpress.com/201... Novelist Michael Cunningham's essay on the translations: https://pen.org/death-in-venice-2/ Author Eric McMillan's review: https://pen.org/death-in-venice-2/
review.text_raw:
One could hardly accuse literary lion Gustav von Aschenbach, protagonist of this novel, of leading a colorful life. Even the erupting passions of his youth were safely confined to the characters of his imagination and further circumscribed by a prevailing rationalism. A lengthy third person narrative imprisons the readers in an introspective review of his life, one guided by an obsessive devotion to intellectual rigor. He attributes his literary success to an intractable self-discipline (a kind of personal “triumph of the will”). But even his success was beginning to feel empty: “Gradually something official, didactic, crept into Gustav Aschenbach's productions, his style in later life fought shy of any abruptness and boldness, any subtle and unexpected shadings; he inclined toward the fixed and standardized, the conventionally elegant, the conservative, the formal, the formulated, nearly.” (p. 15) Without that creative spark, how empty that must have felt to a fifty-year old author who has devoted his entire life to Art, who even justifies his desire for a long lifespan in terms of the accumulated wisdom age must surely confer on future achievement.

Such thoughts consume his mind as he strolls through what feels like a fin-de-Siecle Munich. He passes a cemetary and glimpses an odd looking tourist. The author provides some subtle humor for the reader. Aschenbach observes the lofty epitaphs and idealized headstone carvings. “...[H]e found a grave diversion in spelling out the formulas and letting his mind's eye lose itself in their transparent mysticism.” (p.2)

The idea of a diversion has appeal – something alien, yet not too adventurous. Fate intervenes. He ends up in Venice, a symbol of Renaissance decay. The image is reinforced by the sight of an old man garrishly made-up to present a counterfeit of youthfulness. Aschenbach is filled with disgust. An abrupt contrast follows. A well-to-do Polish family is staying at his hotel. The 14 year-old boy becomes an object of obsession, which he compares to the perfection of a young Adonis. At the same time, a pallor suggests a tragically brief future. Perfect beauty – so ephemeral.

His obsession blinds him to obvious portents. He recalls how a visit to the city in his youth left him sickened. He now notes the fetid odor of the waters. The air becomes a stifling miasma. There are furtive rumors and the hotel begins to empty prematurely. Even the fruit takes an unwholesome turn: “He could no longer endure the thirst that was torturing him, and he looked around for some immediate relief. From a little vegetable store he bought some fruit – strawberries, soft and overly ripe – and he ate them as he walked.” (p.82)

Mann draws on diverse images. The boy is an idealized vision of perfect beauty and innocence. Aschenbach's own lapse in creativity is a disruption of dynamic between Apollonian and Dionysian urges. His surrender to homoeroticism is a rebuke of bourgeois morality. He contemplates society's attempts to deny the Artist an emotional license essential to Art. "But the fact was that Aschenbach did not want soberness: his intoxication was too precious. Who can explain the stamp and the nature of the artist? Who can understandd this deep instinctive welding of discipline and license? For to be unable to want wholesome soberness is license." (p.54)

Thomas Mann assumes his reader is conversant with the turbulent state of fin-de-Siecle German culture. The book was published in 1912. However, I could not escape the knowledge of how this culture was perverted by the Nazi mythology that followed in the succeeding decades. Aschenbach's obsession with the 14 year-old boy felt creepy rather than symbolic. Aschenbach himself felt remote. His death (no spoiler there) was regrettable but also pathetic.

I chose this particular edition, a translation by Kenneth Burke, because of the introduction by Colm Tóibin. There are actually a number of translations available, each with a slightly different but nuanced spirit.

NOTES:

A comparison between Helen Lowe-Porter and Michael Henry Heim: https://lizzysiddal.wordpress.com/201...

Novelist Michael Cunningham's essay on the translations: https://pen.org/death-in-venice-2/

Author Eric McMillan's review: https://pen.org/death-in-venice-2/

review.text: * Bản dịch Tiếng Việt* Về dịch giả Một trong số ít những tựa sách được dịch tử tế mà tôi được đọc trong sáu tháng đổ lại. Thường thì với những cây bút kì công trong câu chữ, các bản dịch thường bị tối nghĩa, lủng củng, như một anh tí hon luộm thuộm trong bộ cánh vương giả quá cỡ. Trong truyện, Thomas sử dụng rất nhiều điển tích thần thoại và lối hành văn cầu kì, xen lẫn nhiều cảm xúc phức tạp và những mộng mơ được kịch tính hóa, nhưng dịch giả vẫn chuyển tải khá thành công. Về nội dung Thành công của chuyện nằm ở các lớp nội tâm phức tạp của Aschenbach (nhân vật chính). Lớp thứ nhất là sự bức bối và túng quẫn của một kẻ mắc kẹt. Con người ấy ngỡ có được tất cả - làm công việc mình yêu thích, được người đời kính trọng, một nếp sống quy củ và nề nếp. Nhưng, khi tất cả đã trở thành vùng an toàn, khi cuộc sống yên ả trôi, không có những biến chuyển lớn, nó trở thành một điện tâm đồ phẳng. Người ta thấy mình trống rỗng và vô cảm. Họ khát khao một sự đổi mới. Lớp thứ hai là quá trình hồi sinh của xúc cảm, khi Aschenbach yêu. Một nghệ sĩ vì yêu cái đẹp mà mến mộ một khuôn mặt đẹp; từ mến mộ trở thành ngưỡng mộ, rồi thần tượng, và rồi yêu. Cuộc sống không còn là những vòng lặp lại, mà trở thành một cuộc theo dõi, một trò đuổi bắt, một chuyến đi hồi hộp, một chuỗi ngày của mơ tưởng, mộng mị, khao khát và mong chờ. Aschenbach đón nhận và ngấu nghiến những cảm xúc ấy như kẻ hành hương trên sa mạc đói khát đón những cơn mưa. Ngòi bút tưởng vô hồn như được phả một hồn sống mới. Ông say sưa yêu, say sưa viết. Những con chữ quay trở lại, nhảy múa, phập phồng. Tôi không là một cây bút chuyên nghiệp, nhưng tôi hiểu cảm giác ấy - cảm giác mỗi ngày vẫn viết, nhưng thấy chữ nghĩa vô hồn; rồi một nguồn hứng khởi đến, chữ nghĩa như những linh hồn nhảy múa, lấp lánh cười. Lớp thứ ba là sự ghê tởm và ánh nhìn tối tăm trước một xã hội tư sản suy đồi. Venice - đằng sau những hoa lệ, được ca tụng, là cảm hứng của giới nghệ sĩ - là một ổ dịch tả, là những cái chết được bưng bít, là lòng tham, dối trá và sự buông xuôi. Venice, thực chất là một cái xác thối rữa- đậm mùi hôi thối, mùi thuốc sát trùng, những trò bịp - được tô điểm để đóng giả là 'công chúa ngủ trong rừng'. Venice, trong truyện, thực chất chỉ là một biểu tượng - cho sự suy tàn của xã hội bấy giờ. Về cái kết "Và bất chợt, như sực nhớ ra, như có điều gì thôi thúc, cậu quay nửa người, một tay chống bên hông, với vẻ duyên dáng cố hữu của mình ngoái đầu qua vai nhìn vào bờ. Người chiêm ngưỡng ngồi đó, như ông từng ngồi, khi lần đầu tiên ánh mắt xám sẫm hoàng hôn kia được trao gửi từ ngưỡng cửa phòng ăn tới gặp ánh mắt ông. Đầu ông đang ngả trên lưng ghế, từ từ quay theo cử động của người bước đi ngoài xa; lúc này mái đầu ấy ngẩng cao lên, như để đón nhận ánh mắt người kia, rồi gục xuống ngực, mắt trợn ngược, trong lúc nét mặt ông giãn ra, thanh thản như chìm vào giấc ngủ say." Có ý kiến bảo truyện tối tăm và bế tắc. Nhưng tôi nghĩ, kết như thế đã là một cái kết có hậu. Aschenbach lúc ấy vẫn đối mặt với nỗi sợ hãi rằng lúc quay về, ông sẽ trở lại những chuỗi ngày trống rỗng. Mà đối với một nghệ sĩ, sống với một tâm hồn rỗng, một ngòi bút cạn cảm xúc, còn kinh khủng hơn cái chết. Và dù ông có ở lại hòn đảo ấy, Tadzio cũng không còn ở đó. Ông sẽ nếm trải những dằn vặt thương nhớ, và cũng sẽ chết bởi dịch tả. Thomas đã giải phóng Aschenbach khỏi sự trống rỗng, và cũng rất nhân đạo khi để ông đón lấy cái nhìn cuối của Tadzio trong phút lìa đời. Cái ngoái nhìn ấy là biểu tượng cho việc tình yêu của ông phần nào được đáp trả. Và trong bối cảnh tăm tối đó, có gì nhân đạo hơn một cái kết mà người ta được giải phóng khỏi sợ hãi và lìa đời khi đang yêu và phần nào được đáp lại. " Ông cảm thấy như thần Psychagog (4) trắng toát và yêu kiều ngoài khơi đang mỉm cười với ông, vẫy gọi ông; hình như vị thần ấy vừa nhắc bàn tay đặt trên hông chỉ ra xa, rồi chấp chới bay lên dẫn đường vào khoảng không mênh mông đầy hứa hẹn. Và như bao lần trước, ông đứng dậy cất bước đi theo." sạn Có lẽ, tôi không hợp với lối hành văn cầu kì cho lắm. Giọng văn của Thomas thỉnh thoảng làm tôi thấy xa cách, như một kẻ bình dân ngồi ăn tối trong nhà hàng hạng sang lắm mồi, nhiều món. Có đôi phần không quen.

Có lẽ, tôi không hợp với lối hành văn cầu kì cho lắm.

review.text: One of those proclaimed classics that not only met but exceeded my expectations. For a story in which not much happens, it's surprisingly tense and suspenseful, especially ending. And then there's the prose: "There he sat, the master, the eminently dignified artist, the author of 'A Wretched Figure,' who had rejected Bohemian excess, and the murky depths in a form of exemplary purity, who had renounced all sympathy for the abyss and reprehended the reprehensible, climbed the heights, and, having transcended his erudition and outgrown all irony, accepted the obligations that come with mass approbation, a man whose fame was official, whose name had been made noble, and whose style schoolboys were exhorted to emulate -- there he sat, his eyes closed, with only an occasional, rapidly disappearing sidelong glance, scornful and sheepish, slipping out from under them and a few isolated words issuing from his slack, cosmetically embellished lips, the result of the curious dream logic of his half-slumbering brain." (135-36) Well, you either love or abhor this kind of stuff. Over-wrought? Maybe, but my God, what would you leave out? And there's that brilliantly placed dash, which not only helps you catch your breath after that long string of modifiers and reboots the sentence, but also signals the shift from abstract language to concrete sensory detail, which appears, to my ear, to undermine, if not mock, the pretensions of those previously embraced encomiums. Or perhaps just swap one embarrassing revelation for another one. I think so, anyway. Might change my mind with a dozen more readings, but such is it's mesmerizing effect that I would not resent the effort one bit. Of course, it's easier to lose yourself in such lush prose when it lasts for a mere 140 pgs. Too much more than that would prove exhausting, I'm sure. My first reading of DIV, so I can't compare it to other editions, but I would recommend this one on basis of Michael Cunningham's excellent introduction, in which he reflects on his own experience with taking up the book again many years after his first reading. One of the better intro's I've read of any work. It enriched my reading.

For a story in which not much happens, it's surprisingly tense and suspenseful, especially ending.

review.text: One scene at the end of this short, powerful novella gave me the shudders. A middle-aged man is sitting in his barber's chair, gazing delightedly at himself in a mirror. Due to his ridiculous and repulsive obsession with a beautiful fourteen year old boy, he has dyed his hair black and applied makeup and rouge in a sad effort to look youthful. Without realizing it, he has transformed into the very thing that disgusted him at the beginning of his journey. This is a story of love as obsession. Gustav von Aschenbach, a famous writer, has made a career of carefully avoiding any form of love, preaching instead a passion of the intellect. Of course, this all goes terribly wrong. When he does fall in love with the aforementioned beautiful boy, he is completely defenseless, a helpless slave to his passion, pathetically trailing the boy and his family throughout the plagued city of Venice. As the days go by, the other tourists disappear. The beach empties. The piazzas of the city are haunted and deserted. Troubling rumors of a plague of deadly cholera drift through the air, though everyone connected with the tourist industry vigorously denies it. Finally, Aschenbach confirms that the rumors are true--and that the hospitals are overflowing with the sick and the dying. He knows he should flee Venice. He suspects that the boy's family is unaware of the situation, since they only speak Polish, and that he should inform them of the danger they're in. But he doesn't. I smiled as I read this book. It's the absolute opposite of current writing trends, which dictate "Show, don't tell." Thomas Mann is all about the telling. He tells you exactly which obscure Classical and mythical characters his characters resemble. He tells you about the society they live in, the fabric in the clothes they wear, about their parents, their educations, the philosophies that guide them, the color of their hair, the shape of their faces. in 2014, an editor would have jettisoned literally half of this book. But the tale of obsession it tells is so real, so relentlessly current, that it makes Death in Venice utterly, fearlessly modern.
review.text_raw:
One scene at the end of this short, powerful novella gave me the shudders.

A middle-aged man is sitting in his barber's chair, gazing delightedly at himself in a mirror. Due to his ridiculous and repulsive obsession with a beautiful fourteen year old boy, he has dyed his hair black and applied makeup and rouge in a sad effort to look youthful. Without realizing it, he has transformed into the very thing that disgusted him at the beginning of his journey.

This is a story of love as obsession. Gustav von Aschenbach, a famous writer, has made a career of carefully avoiding any form of love, preaching instead a passion of the intellect.

Of course, this all goes terribly wrong. When he does fall in love with the aforementioned beautiful boy, he is completely defenseless, a helpless slave to his passion, pathetically trailing the boy and his family throughout the plagued city of Venice.

As the days go by, the other tourists disappear. The beach empties. The piazzas of the city are haunted and deserted. Troubling rumors of a plague of deadly cholera drift through the air, though everyone connected with the tourist industry vigorously denies it. Finally, Aschenbach confirms that the rumors are true--and that the hospitals are overflowing with the sick and the dying.

He knows he should flee Venice. He suspects that the boy's family is unaware of the situation, since they only speak Polish, and that he should inform them of the danger they're in.

But he doesn't.

I smiled as I read this book. It's the absolute opposite of current writing trends, which dictate "Show, don't tell." Thomas Mann is all about the telling. He tells you exactly which obscure Classical and mythical characters his characters resemble. He tells you about the society they live in, the fabric in the clothes they wear, about their parents, their educations, the philosophies that guide them, the color of their hair, the shape of their faces. in 2014, an editor would have jettisoned literally half of this book.

But the tale of obsession it tells is so real, so relentlessly current, that it makes Death in Venice utterly, fearlessly modern.

review.text: Điểm: 9/10 Độc đáo và bất ngờ Tác phẩm được xuất bản năm 1912 nhưng nội dung rất táo bạo. Đây là tiểu thuyết mang nặng tính tự truyện của Thomas Mann với hầu hết tình tiết trong tác phẩm là những chuyện trong đời thực. Câu chuyện kể về một tình yêu xảy ra ở thành phố Venice mộng mơ. Một tình yêu mãnh liệt, táo bạo nhưng cũng phi lý, vô đạo đức (theo như nhận định của chính nhân vật). Bối cảnh của tiểu thuyết được viết ra đã hơn 100 năm nhưng vấn đề mà tác phẩm đề cập đến vẫn còn nguyên giá trị ở hiện tại. *** Lưu ý: Phần sau đây có thể tiết lộ tình tiết quan trọng của tác phẩm Nhận vật chính là Gustav von Aschenbach, một nhà văn nổi tiếng. Trong một chuyến du lịch đến Vinice, ông đã gặp cậu bé Tadzio. Cậu bé này có nét đẹp như một thiên thần trong các tác phẩm nghệ thuật. Ban đầu là sự thích thú, sau đó là sự thần tượng mà nhà văn dành cho cậu bé. Và cuối cùng là điều mà người đọc không hề nghĩ đến đã xảy ra: nhà văn đã thừa nhận rằng ông đã ... yêu cậu bé. Bối cảnh của câu chuyện tình yêu đồng tính giữa một người trung niên và một cậu bé là thành phố Venice bị hoành hành bởi dịch bệnh thổ tả và mọi người đang cố che dấu bằng sự dối trá và vô trách nhiệm. Cái chết của nhà văn ở đoạn kết là điểm nhấn hoàn hảo trong việc mô tả một xã hội suy đồi. Vấn đề được đề cập đến trong tiểu thuyết này vẫn thường gặp trong xã hội hôm nay.

review.text: "الجمال هو الطريق التى تقود الإنسان الحساس إلى الروح، فقط الطريق،وسيلة وحسب. إلّا أن الجمال هنا، يقود إلى الاضطراب الرهيب فى الروح، لى فقدان التوازن، إلى الموت." توماس مان، وأولى تجاربى مع الأدب الألمانى. نحن هنا أمام رواية قصيرة، كتبها توماس تحت تأثير فلسفة نيتشه وشبنهاور. التشاؤم العميق الذى ورثه عن شوبنهاور مقترنًا بطغيان الموت وهيبة العدم. ونلاحظ نفاذ البصر وبُعد الرؤيا والرهافة السيكولوجيةالخارقة التى كان مان يعجب بها لدى نيتشه. الرواية تدور حول فنان، روائى مشهور..جوستاف أشنباخ. يُعانى من روتينية الحياة، ووحدتها (فيذهب للبحث عن السلام فى المدينة السحرية(البندقية لكنه بدلًا من أن يجد ضالته فى هذا الجمال، يقع فريسة للانبهار المميت بمراهق تحت تأثير جماله الملائكى. وكان هذا بمثابة الطريق إلى النهاية. دفعه هذا الانبهار لمطاردته في شوارع المدينة التي تقع في يد وباء الكوليرا أصبح ذلك الحب هو النقطة التي عندها انفجر جموح وطاقة أشنباخ المكبوته التي كبحها منذ مدة، فعاش فترة من التشاؤم والسقوط انتهت بموته. الرواية أسلوبها مميز، اعتمدت على السرد والوصف المُطوّل للأشياء وافتقدت للغة الحوار. إنها قصة عن الجمال، عن الاضطراب الروحي المُرعِب الذي يحدثه الجمال الفائق في النفوس. هي أيضاً قصة عن الوحدة، عن البحث العبثي عن الكمال، عن الحنين للشباب الضائع، عن الموت..عن الموت الساحر في البندقية.

لكنه بدلًا من أن يجد ضالته فى هذا الجمال،

أصبح ذلك الحب هو النقطة التي عندها انفجر جموح وطاقة أشنباخ المكبوته التي كبحها منذ مدة، فعاش فترة من التشاؤم والسقوط انتهت بموته.

review.text: La Muerte en Venecia es una obra muy corta, de apenas 100 páginas, pero su pequeño tamaño esconde una complejidad, una belleza y una riqueza literaria sin parangón en un espacio tan reducido. Es una oda a la belleza, cargada de simbolismo, de contrastes; es un monumento al arte y al artista, y una reflexión sobre su naturaleza y su papel en el mundo. El tema principal de la obra tiene reminiscencias de El Retrato de Dorian Gray de Oscar Wilde, pero es una obra de otra magnitud. Un artista que ha reprimido toda su vida sus impulsos en favor de la creación apolínea, ordenada, moral, descubre a través de la belleza de un joven adolescente la dimensión dionisiaca del arte, los aspectos prohibidos que había renunciado a conocer por su noción equivocada del arte y del artista. El artista no tiene moral que ejercer o enseñar, su único canon es la belleza, su único destino es aquel donde la búsqueda de la belleza lo conduzca. Sin embargo, el protagonista realiza su descubrimiento demasiado tarde, en medio de una decadencia vital que encuentra su espejo en la decadencia de la ciudad que lo rodea.

review.text: This is the first Thomas Mann I have tackled in German, hitherto having read most of his novels in English translation. The first impression one has is the sheer range of his vocabulary! One expects this in a Victorian novel for example but for a relatively contemporary author this is unusual I feel. Once the Large Oxford/Duden dictionary had been located I settled down to enjoy what is not a complex tale, but makes up for this in the richness of its vocabulary and the power of Mann's descriptions of that most fabulous of cities - Venedig. Considered to be somewhat autobiographical by the critics, the novel is on one level the story of Aschenbach's infatuation with the young Pole Tadzio, culminating in the curious scene where Aschenbach has his hair dyed and make-up applied to hide his advancing years. For me this sheer beauty of Mann's delicious prose made this a worthwhile read; his facility in piling up adjectives, the cadence of his phrasing, the sheer music of his prose make this a small gem.
review.text_raw:
This is the first Thomas Mann I have tackled in German, hitherto having read most of his novels in English translation. The first impression one has is the sheer range of his vocabulary! One expects this in a Victorian novel for example but for a relatively contemporary author this is unusual I feel.

Once the Large Oxford/Duden dictionary had been located I settled down to enjoy what is not a complex tale, but makes up for this in the richness of its vocabulary and the power of Mann's descriptions of that most fabulous of cities - Venedig. Considered to be somewhat autobiographical by the critics, the novel is on one level the story of Aschenbach's infatuation with the young Pole Tadzio, culminating in the curious scene where Aschenbach has his hair dyed and make-up applied to hide his advancing years.

For me this sheer beauty of Mann's delicious prose made this a worthwhile read; his facility in piling up adjectives, the cadence of his phrasing, the sheer music of his prose make this a small gem.

review.text: "La dicha del escritor es su posibilidad de transmutar enteramente la idea en sentimiento; el sentimiento, totalmente en idea. (...) El sentimiento de que la naturaleza se estremecía de goce cuando el espíritu se inclinaba en reverencia y homenaje ante la belleza" El escritor Gustav von Aschenbach busca una nueva inspiración, decide viajar y llega a Venecia, donde ve a un chico polaco, el cual representa para Aschenbach la representación absoluta de la belleza. Esta pequeña novela es sumamente profunda, compleja en su técnica y esconde dentro de sí más de lo que aparenta. Suponer sobre la sexualidad del autor o sobre la moralidad del personaje principal limitaría contundentemente al lector, que se mostraría como un mero aficionado. La muerte en Venecia plantea una serie de cuestionamientos sobre el arte, sobre la belleza, la inspiración, la moral y el deseo, dentro de una Venecia azotada por la peste, una metáfora que funciona quizá, como crítica a la sociedad burguesa de la época.

review.text: Impossível resistir a tão belo enquadramento de um sentimento tão arrebatador quanto condenável. Uma parábola de tácita reprovação a uma vida dominada pela razão, austeridade e contenção, tudo em nome de uma fama e obra reputadamente genial, que num momento de quase inconsciente relaxamento, permite um excessivo transbordo emocional que acaba por ser fatal. Literalmente fatal. Sem dúvida, uma crítica belamente fraseada. “Pois o homem ama e venera outro homem desde que o não possa julgar, e o desejo é o testemunho de um conhecimento imperfeito”. Mann era sem dúvida um homem muito corajoso: tanto na sua vida pessoal como nos temas a que dedicava a sua brilhante escrita. Para mim, bastava uma destas facetas para o admirar. Juntas, é caso para quase o venerar, tanto mais que dificilmente o poderei julgar.

review.text: Romanzo breve molto introspettivo (la maggioranza degli eventi avviene nella testa del protagonista). Narra di uno scrittore approdato di nuovo a Venezia, nonostante in passato il suo clima non gli avesse giovato. Per via di quest'ultimo motivo sta per andarsene, ma l'incontro con il giovane Tadzio lo blocca: in questa infatuazione segreta, sconosciuta anche al ragazzo, il protagonista vive uno scampolo di nuova giovinezza, mentre fa i conti con un orientamento sessuale a lungo represso. Lo sfondo veneziano, con il suo aspetto un po' gotico, è perfetto per rappresentare i vari presagi di morte e la segretezza di questa infatuazione (non per nulla, anche Ritorno a Brideshead ha la sua parentesi veneziana). Un testo da leggere tutto d'un fiato, per meglio immergersi nella mente del protagonista e fra le gondole.

review.text: A highly captivating novella! It follows Gustave von Aschenbach, a German writer, who travels to Venice for his health only to fall into an obsessive passion for a young, beautiful boy. The writing and descriptions were stunning, and I must admit, as someone who has a strange fascination with Italy, I really enjoyed the experience despite the obvious and purposefully questionable themes (pedophilia, lowkey stalking etc). But it also deals with beauty, obsession, repression, art and youth, all of which I find really interesting coming from a story written in the early 1900s. Was a little caught off guard when an illness suddenly started spreading though, reading this in our lovely 2021...

review.text: Streng komponierte Novelle mit inspirierendes Gedanken zu Kunst und Künstler*innentum. Ich mochte auch, wie der Verfall der Stadt den Verfall von Aschenbachs angekündigt hat. Es gab mehrere Todesboten, die die Katastrophe ankündigten. Auch dass von Aschenbach einfach nicht wegkommt aus Venedig nach mehreren Versuchen, gibt der Erzählung etwas Bedrohliches. Ich musste auch mehrfach an die Zeit der Pandemie denken, bei Mann wird die Cholera von der Politik aber verschwiegen. Insgesamt macht die Erzählung Lust auf mehr von Mann. Ich werde vermutlich mit anderen Erzählungen weitermachen wie "Schwere Stunde" oder "Tristan".

review.text: Smrt v Benátkách jsem poslouchal jako audioknihu. Rozhodl jsem se tak proto, že ji namluvil úžasný Jiří Hromada, jehož hlas si zamilujete po první větě. K Thomasi Mannovi jsem se dostal náhodou, trošku mě sice odrazoval rok vydání (1912) hlavně kvůli staršímu jazyku. I proto jsem rád, že jsem knihu poslouchal, užil jsem si ji daleko víc. Co se týče knihy, jde o jeden z prvních moderních LGBT příběhů, což je mi velmi blízké. A navíc Jiří Hromada je nejznámější český LGBT aktivista, takže to spojení nemohlo být lepší.

review.text: Tan corta y a la vez tan abrumadora. Thomas Mann describe en su obra la decadencia de la burguesía que rodeada de una cadencia lenta y un tedio voluminoso se deja conducir hacia la depravación del vicio y la superficialidad. Así, Gustav Von Aschenbach se acaba comportando como el ruinoso hombre mayor que aparenta ser joven, que se encuentra en su viaje hacia Venecia. Esa complejidad tan frondosa como un pubis en los años ochenta le resta una pizca de sangre al relato, pero también lo hace fácilmente revisitable.

Esa complejidad tan frondosa como un pubis en los años ochenta le resta una pizca de sangre al relato, pero también lo hace fácilmente revisitable.

review.text: So, even if I've skipped "The Magic Mountain" or "Doctor Faustus" so far, I read Thomas Mann, didn't I? ;) The novella is really good, the film must have played an important role as well, first I watched it and then I wanted to read the book. Gustave Aschenbach is one of those old men who have that aura of dignity and sophistication that leaves me speechless. And Venice! Venice has nothing to do with all the fascination and stupid carnivals I'm so sick of!

review.text: Escrita hace más de 100 años, sentí que la imagen que evoca de Venecia era muy vigente, ¿serán ecos del Covid y la resistencia de una ciudad acostumbrada a las masas a no querer cerrar? Quizá. “Porque la Belleza, Fedro, tenlo muy presente, solo la Belleza es a la vez visible y divina, y por ello es también el camino de lo sensible, es, mi pequeño Fedro, el camino del artista hacia el espíritu”… pág 154.
