review.text: I liked reading this more modern translation (by Heim). I had read the original “standard” 1930 translation by H. T. Lowe-Porter (the great-grandmother of British PM Boris Johnson). She was a friend of Mann’s American publisher Alfred Knopf. She wanted to do the translations and was designated his official translator. As I scan the internet for translation advice, the 'standard' by Lowe-Porter is not well liked. I listened to the audio-book version of this Heim translation. Their were powerful, deep English words in this book that give it the feel that it was not done by a robotic-translator, but rather with a great deal of study. This is a very short novella, where an aging Gustav von Aschenbach goes off on a vacation, only to become infatuated with a 14 year old Polish boy who he sees daily. The boy is constantly with his two older sisters, and maybe his mother or possibly friends on the beach. This clandestine love for the boy will definitely make you uncomfortable as you read this book. The early part of the book establishes the conservative nature of von Aschenbach, as he eventually works he way to the hotel on the island. This slow part was rather necessary to develop his character. The final 2/3 of the book is von Aschenback ramping up his stalking of the boy until it consumed his day. I like this website I found for both a critique of the translators and the book itself. https://publishingperspectives.com/20... This surely had to be a controversial book upon its release. I would highly recommend this Heim 2005 translation. My discomfort of the stalking of the boy reminds me how these types of books that make you squirm when you read them due to the subject matter chosen by the author are often the stories that will remain in my memory. My previous review was 4*, but this better translation allows me to let it tag 5*. Just be ready for the pederasty that rules this story. ------------------------ Here is an earlier review I wrote in Aug 2022 for the 1930 translation: Death in Venice and Seven Other Stories It took 20+ pages to get used to the 1911 vernacular. Once Tadzio arrived with "the lad's perfect beauty. His face recalled the noblest moment of Greek sculpture", I was hooked into a singular reading session to complete this novella. The tough early reading is brought on by passages like: pg 11 "With that world of Aschenbach's creation were exhibited ... (181 total words in this sentence) ... born of weakness." Whew! Advice? Just read through quickly, since there really is not too deep of a plot early in this book. The writing very much settles down for the final 2/3 of the story. There were references to works by Plato, that I found myself googling while I read this book, but I'll have to go back to those eventually. I wanted to keep reading! The Wikipedia page for this book has lots of good side-reference material. The story has a very smooth build-up of the older (50's) Gustave Aschenbach's obsession with the young (14) Polish boy Tadzio that happens to be staying at his hotel with his two sisters and family, including Governess. There are MANY quotes I'll need to look up in the quote section here on goodreads to capture. Very solid 4+. Just get me past that 1911 tedious vernacular! But, it does take you 'back', which helped me mentally be at the time/place as I read this book. This edition of the book was translated by H.T. Lowe-Porter. I read in Wiki that a better translation is available: Wiki: "H. T. Lowe-Porter's authorized translation, which appeared in 1928, has been less well received by critics due to her reducing Mann's treatment of sexuality and homoeroticism. A translation published in 2005 by Michael Henry Heim won the Helen and Kurt Wolff Translator's Prize. " I'll need to try this version: Death in Venice Notes: 11/15/23 https://the-bibliophage-space.quora.c... Corwyn B perpetual student of literature and sometime writer2y I cannot tell you the “best,” but I can tell you my own impressions from having examined more than one. First, I have read Mann’s complete fiction, a lot of his essays, his personal memoir, and some of his letters. I have read books and essays about Mann’s work, at times. I have read most of Mann’s work in both French and German, but I have read certain of his work in English translations, also. Every translation of a literary work is an interpretation, and I have found several translations to be illuminating examples of a close reading — readings that discerned and pointed out some aspects that I found very interesting additions to my understanding. Now, a warning. The original “standard” translations of Mann’s work were by H. T. Lowe-Porter (the great-grandmother of British PM Boris Johnson). She was a friend of Mann’s American publisher Alfred Knopf. She wanted to do the translations and was designated his official translator. There were problems: Her command of German was weak. She frequently misread Mann’s text, sometimes in silly ways. Her ability to write good English prose was limited. Her sentences are often marred by her limitations, and will mix an occasional felicitous phrase in the midst of turgid and inelegant diction. She was an arrogant and somewhat stupid person. Mann wanted his translations into English to be good because they were the vehicle by which he was introduced to the anglophone public. He wanted a different person to be the translator, but because she was selected to do the job, he worked to support her efforts. He read and spoke English fairly well, and he found numerous inaccuracies in her translations. He communicated this to Lowe-Porter, and she basically ignored him and told him to bugger off. She took unjustified liberties. There is a place early in Der Tod in Venedig where she rearranged the sequence of Mann’s presentation because she just didn’t like it. Lowe-Porter’s translations of Buddenbrooks, Der Zauberberg, and Doktor Faustus are dire. But what she did to some of his stories (e.g., “Tonio Kröger,” “Tristan,” and Der Tod in Venedig come to mind) was appalling. She combined an inability to parse some German with unwillingness to consult the author and a bad prose style. So stay the hell away from her translations. She’s bloody awful. Of the English translations of Der Tod in Venedig that I have read, I thought the translation by Professor David Luke was excellent, and it captured Mann’s style and tone very nicely. He also did an excellent translation of the other major stories. Joachim Neugroschel’s translations of the stories also seem capable to me. After those two, the translations seem either bad (e.g., Lowe-Porter) or flat-footed but accurate. Naturally, you should go with the translation that you prefer. I am simply telling what I have observed. And on the head of H. T. Lowe-Porter, and unto her 7th generation, a curse seems justified. https://publishingperspectives.com/20... Mann’s “Death in Venice” – A Century of the Translated Trope In Feature Articles by Kevin DiCamilloJanuary 11, 2016 For a century now, Thomas Mann’s novella Death in Venice has endured and takes on different interpretations in the hands of different translators and readers. By Kevin T. Di Camillo “You didn’t even read Death In Venice until I gave it to you!” “That’s right: you only gave me books with the word ‘Death’ in the title.” — from Woody Allen’s Annie Hall Death in Venice 2Death in Venice may be to Thomas Mann what The Dead is to James Joyce: the novella you can read without having to read The Magic Mountain or Ulysses and yet still feel as though you have the right to say, “Yes, I’ve read Mann!” or “Yes, I’ve read Joyce!” The story, which first appeared in German in 1912 from Berlin publisher S. Fischer Verlag, was translated into English 13 years later — making this its 91st anniversary in English. Unlike many other works of that pre-World War I era, Death in Venice has not only remained in print but also continues to be a touchstone book of how we view the Old World — along with the elderly — and how you, the reader, see Venice. And here the readers of Death in Venice fall neatly into two camps: you either love Venice (all romantic gondolas, East-meets-West architecture, silence along the ancient canals) or you hate Venice (the world’s all-time worst tourist trap where you actually have to PAY to enter a church, San Marco Basilica, along with floods, and a surfeit of insufferable honeymooners). No one is apathetic about The Most Serene Republic. Depending on how you feel about Venice is going to color how you view Mann’s minor masterpiece. What’s remarkable about Venice as a city is that Mann captured its moribund zeitgeist which had infected the city since at least the time of Napoleon’s plundering, or even earlier, its hold-out against the Ottoman Empire. A Place for Death… Death in VEniceGenerally speaking, Venice became a place to die (Baron Corvo, Ezra Pound, Titian) or go insane (George Eliot’s honeymoon was ruined when her newlywed husband went mad and jumped out a window and into a canal) or go to prison (Casanova) via The Bridge of Sighs. In a sense, Venice is not unlike New York’s Chelsea Hotel with its hit-list of famous writers and artists who died here. In Venice, then, death is quite romantic (to quote Bob Dylan), and that’s of course, part of the appeal. Venice lives, or at least survives, in the face of death, whether in the form of the plague (which took down Titian), or the interdicts placed on it by the Popes, or Bonaparte’s invasion and vast looting, or its uneasy balance between its independence, land-and-sea-grabs by the Austro-Hungarian Empire, and the now defunct Kingdom of Italy. Death in Venice is an uncomfortable story on a number of levels, not the least of which is the obvious outcome. Unlike Joyce’s The Dead, where no one actually dies in the story, Death In Venice is truth in title-ing: someone is definitely going to die, and it’s either going to be the old author with writer’s block or the young boy whom he lusts after. …Pederasty If death is an uncomfortable subject, pederasty can’t be far behind. However, what the protagonist Gustav von Aschenbach merely dares to dream about — namely carnal relations with Tadzio, a pre-teen-aged Polish boy — another real-life author who actually did die in Venice, in 1913, the very year Death in Venice appeared in German was Baron Corvo aka Fredrick Rolfe — who lived out what Aschenbach dreamt. Rolfe reveled in his homosexual-fantasies with a kind of Oscar-Wilde-meets-Liberace abandon: acting as an epistolary pimp for his friends whom he implored to come to Venice in letters that even today make one blush. Rolfe relished his relations with young Venetian boys and, along with the photos he took of the naked Italian youths, the very real Rolfe made the fictional von Aschenbach’s verboten relations his dying reality. Choose Your Own Translation Death in Venice has gone through numerous translations, especially into English. Ten years ago the late Professor Michael Henry Heim was awarded Helen and Kurt Wolff Translation Prize for his version of it (Harper Perennial, 2005). However, for the past twenty years I’ve favored Stanley Applebaum’s Dover Thrift Edition, replete with a Foreword, Notes, and Commentary, all for only a dollar (which is what the Heim ebook costs). But there are plenty of other fine choices: H.T. Lowe-Porter did one of the earlier English translations (Knopf/Vintage) in 1930, which remained the standard for decades. Jefferson S. Chase, in his 1998 Signet Classic Edition, stated that “My translation focus not only on content but also seek to render the complexity of Mann’s style into contemporary American English.” [emphasis added] (p. xvii.) Professor Joachim Neugroschel’s 1997 Penguin Classic translation tells us: “In Death In Venice, the artist as outsider is one theme. On its simplest level the story is Balzacian — and Freudian: a man who has always repressed his sexuality and sublimated it entirely into his creative duties is suddenly overwhelmed by a love that is both platonic and plutonic. His Protestant ethic and discipline both drown in his physical and spiritual hormones…to fight against the loss of self-control, against the overpowering libido, Mann’s syntax clamps down on the sun-drenched, salt-sprayed voluptuousness…” (p. xv). The pleasure of this text, to mis-use Barthes’s term, is that so much is packed into such a short book. It is routine to say that Joyce’s The Dead was a thumbnail sketch for A Portrait of the Artist as a Young Man, and I suppose scholars can make the case that Death In Venice is a thumbnail sketch for The Magic Mountain. Themes of passion, psychological and physical illness, spiritual and emotional paralysis — plus the introduction of Freudian and Nietzchian leitmotivs — are here in miniature and we are spared Mann’s ham-fisted, lengthy moralizing that pervades The Magic Mountain. In sum, it is a love story — but to me at least, it’s not “simply” a story of forbidden and (unlike Lolita) therefore unrequited love between a very old man and a very young boy. Rather, and I’m hardly the first to come up with this, it is a love story of a city — very much like Rolfe’s The Desire & Pursuit of The Whole: A Venetian Romance (written c. 1910-13, but not published until 1934). A sinking city, a city built on a swamp, a city that almost shouldn’t exist practically speaking, but for those of us who love it — and here I unashamedly cast myself in that camp — a city you could die in. About the Author Kevin DiCamillo Kevin DiCamillo is a freelance editor and writer whose most recent work has been on The Pope Francis Resource Library (Crossroad Publishing). He has published three books of poetry, been anthologized in Wild Dreams: The Best of Italian-Americana, and awarded the Foley Poetry Prize from America Magazine. He has edited over 100 books for Paulist Press/HiddenSpring/Stimulus Books, Penguin/Celebra and Crossroad/Herder&Herder. A graduate of the University of Notre Dame, he regularly attends the Yale Publishing Course.

https://the-bibliophage-space.quora.c...
Corwyn B
perpetual student of literature and sometime writer2y

I cannot tell you the “best,” but I can tell you my own impressions from having examined more than one.

First, I have read Mann’s complete fiction, a lot of his essays, his personal memoir, and some of his letters. I have read books and essays about Mann’s work, at times.

I have read most of Mann’s work in both French and German, but I have read certain of his work in English translations, also. Every translation of a literary work is an interpretation, and I have found several translations to be illuminating examples of a close reading — readings that discerned and pointed out some aspects that I found very interesting additions to my understanding.

Now, a warning. The original “standard” translations of Mann’s work were by H. T. Lowe-Porter (the great-grandmother of British PM Boris Johnson). She was a friend of Mann’s American publisher Alfred Knopf. She wanted to do the translations and was designated his official translator.

There were problems:

Her command of German was weak. She frequently misread Mann’s text, sometimes in silly ways.
Her ability to write good English prose was limited. Her sentences are often marred by her limitations, and will mix an occasional felicitous phrase in the midst of turgid and inelegant diction.
She was an arrogant and somewhat stupid person. Mann wanted his translations into English to be good because they were the vehicle by which he was introduced to the anglophone public. He wanted a different person to be the translator, but because she was selected to do the job, he worked to support her efforts. He read and spoke English fairly well, and he found numerous inaccuracies in her translations. He communicated this to Lowe-Porter, and she basically ignored him and told him to bugger off.
She took unjustified liberties. There is a place early in Der Tod in Venedig where she rearranged the sequence of Mann’s presentation because she just didn’t like it.

Lowe-Porter’s translations of Buddenbrooks, Der Zauberberg, and Doktor Faustus are dire. But what she did to some of his stories (e.g., “Tonio Kröger,” “Tristan,” and Der Tod in Venedig come to mind) was appalling. She combined an inability to parse some German with unwillingness to consult the author and a bad prose style.

So stay the hell away from her translations. She’s bloody awful.

Of the English translations of Der Tod in Venedig that I have read, I thought the translation by Professor David Luke was excellent, and it captured Mann’s style and tone very nicely. He also did an excellent translation of the other major stories.

Joachim Neugroschel’s translations of the stories also seem capable to me.

After those two, the translations seem either bad (e.g., Lowe-Porter) or flat-footed but accurate.

Naturally, you should go with the translation that you prefer. I am simply telling what I have observed. And on the head of H. T. Lowe-Porter, and unto her 7th generation, a curse seems justified.

Mann’s “Death in Venice” – A Century of the Translated Trope

In Feature Articles by Kevin DiCamilloJanuary 11, 2016
For a century now, Thomas Mann’s novella Death in Venice has endured and takes on different interpretations in the hands of different translators and readers.

By Kevin T. Di Camillo

“You didn’t even read Death In Venice until I gave it to you!” “That’s right: you only gave me books with the word ‘Death’ in the title.” — from Woody Allen’s Annie Hall

Death in Venice 2Death in Venice may be to Thomas Mann what The Dead is to James Joyce: the novella you can read without having to read The Magic Mountain or Ulysses and yet still feel as though you have the right to say, “Yes, I’ve read Mann!” or “Yes, I’ve read Joyce!”

The story, which first appeared in German in 1912 from Berlin publisher S. Fischer Verlag, was translated into English 13 years later — making this its 91st anniversary in English. Unlike many other works of that pre-World War I era, Death in Venice has not only remained in print but also continues to be a touchstone book of how we view the Old World — along with the elderly — and how you, the reader, see Venice.

And here the readers of Death in Venice fall neatly into two camps: you either love Venice (all romantic gondolas, East-meets-West architecture, silence along the ancient canals) or you hate Venice (the world’s all-time worst tourist trap where you actually have to PAY to enter a church, San Marco Basilica, along with floods, and a surfeit of insufferable honeymooners). No one is apathetic about The Most Serene Republic. Depending on how you feel about Venice is going to color how you view Mann’s minor masterpiece.

What’s remarkable about Venice as a city is that Mann captured its moribund zeitgeist which had infected the city since at least the time of Napoleon’s plundering, or even earlier, its hold-out against the Ottoman Empire.
A Place for Death…

Death in VEniceGenerally speaking, Venice became a place to die (Baron Corvo, Ezra Pound, Titian) or go insane (George Eliot’s honeymoon was ruined when her newlywed husband went mad and jumped out a window and into a canal) or go to prison (Casanova) via The Bridge of Sighs. In a sense, Venice is not unlike New York’s Chelsea Hotel with its hit-list of famous writers and artists who died here.

In Venice, then, death is quite romantic (to quote Bob Dylan), and that’s of course, part of the appeal. Venice lives, or at least survives, in the face of death, whether in the form of the plague (which took down Titian), or the interdicts placed on it by the Popes, or Bonaparte’s invasion and vast looting, or its uneasy balance between its independence, land-and-sea-grabs by the Austro-Hungarian Empire, and the now defunct Kingdom of Italy.

Death in Venice is an uncomfortable story on a number of levels, not the least of which is the obvious outcome. Unlike Joyce’s The Dead, where no one actually dies in the story, Death In Venice is truth in title-ing: someone is definitely going to die, and it’s either going to be the old author with writer’s block or the young boy whom he lusts after.
…Pederasty

If death is an uncomfortable subject, pederasty can’t be far behind. However, what the protagonist Gustav von Aschenbach merely dares to dream about — namely carnal relations with Tadzio, a pre-teen-aged Polish boy — another real-life author who actually did die in Venice, in 1913, the very year Death in Venice appeared in German was Baron Corvo aka Fredrick Rolfe — who lived out what Aschenbach dreamt.

Rolfe reveled in his homosexual-fantasies with a kind of Oscar-Wilde-meets-Liberace abandon: acting as an epistolary pimp for his friends whom he implored to come to Venice in letters that even today make one blush. Rolfe relished his relations with young Venetian boys and, along with the photos he took of the naked Italian youths, the very real Rolfe made the fictional von Aschenbach’s verboten relations his dying reality.
Choose Your Own Translation

Death in Venice has gone through numerous translations, especially into English. Ten years ago the late Professor Michael Henry Heim was awarded Helen and Kurt Wolff Translation Prize for his version of it (Harper Perennial, 2005). However, for the past twenty years I’ve favored Stanley Applebaum’s Dover Thrift Edition, replete with a Foreword, Notes, and Commentary, all for only a dollar (which is what the Heim ebook costs).

But there are plenty of other fine choices: H.T. Lowe-Porter did one of the earlier English translations (Knopf/Vintage) in 1930, which remained the standard for decades. Jefferson S. Chase, in his 1998 Signet Classic Edition, stated that “My translation focus not only on content but also seek to render the complexity of Mann’s style into contemporary American English.” [emphasis added] (p. xvii.) Professor Joachim Neugroschel’s 1997 Penguin Classic translation tells us:

“In Death In Venice, the artist as outsider is one theme. On its simplest level the story is Balzacian — and Freudian: a man who has always repressed his sexuality and sublimated it entirely into his creative duties is suddenly overwhelmed by a love that is both platonic and plutonic. His Protestant ethic and discipline both drown in his physical and spiritual hormones…to fight against the loss of self-control, against the overpowering libido, Mann’s syntax clamps down on the sun-drenched, salt-sprayed voluptuousness…” (p. xv).

The pleasure of this text, to mis-use Barthes’s term, is that so much is packed into such a short book. It is routine to say that Joyce’s The Dead was a thumbnail sketch for A Portrait of the Artist as a Young Man, and I suppose scholars can make the case that Death In Venice is a thumbnail sketch for The Magic Mountain. Themes of passion, psychological and physical illness, spiritual and emotional paralysis — plus the introduction of Freudian and Nietzchian leitmotivs — are here in miniature and we are spared Mann’s ham-fisted, lengthy moralizing that pervades The Magic Mountain.

In sum, it is a love story — but to me at least, it’s not “simply” a story of forbidden and (unlike Lolita) therefore unrequited love between a very old man and a very young boy. Rather, and I’m hardly the first to come up with this, it is a love story of a city — very much like Rolfe’s The Desire & Pursuit of The Whole: A Venetian Romance (written c. 1910-13, but not published until 1934). A sinking city, a city built on a swamp, a city that almost shouldn’t exist practically speaking, but for those of us who love it — and here I unashamedly cast myself in that camp — a city you could die in.
About the Author
Kevin DiCamillo

Kevin DiCamillo is a freelance editor and writer whose most recent work has been on The Pope Francis Resource Library (Crossroad Publishing). He has published three books of poetry, been anthologized in Wild Dreams: The Best of Italian-Americana, and awarded the Foley Poetry Prize from America Magazine. He has edited over 100 books for Paulist Press/HiddenSpring/Stimulus Books, Penguin/Celebra and Crossroad/Herder&Herder. A graduate of the University of Notre Dame, he regularly attends the Yale Publishing Course.

review.text: It is generally acknowledged that an artist essentially draws from "Life" and the experiences provided by it, or from direct observation of it to create her/his artistic work. That makes me wonder: Where from, Thomas Mann comes up with the idea of a novel about a platonic paedophile obsession? Mann himself admitted in "A Sketch of My Life", Nothing in Death in Venice is invented. The traveller by the Northern Cemetery in Munich, the gloomy boat from Pola, the aged fop, the dubious gondolier, Tadzio and his family, the departure prevented by a mix-up over luggage, cholera, the honest clerk in the travel agency, the malevolent street singer, or whatever else you might care to mention – everything was given. He also wrote to a friend in 1911 that he was working on: a novella, serious in tone, concerning a case of pederasty in an ageing artist. Thomas Mann is what I call an "onion writer": his work always consisted of layers upon layers of symbologies and hidden meanings and feelings. The words written, which we can rationally identify because we are able to read them in the paper, are the tip of the iceberg; a huge mountain hidden underneath the dark waters of the magnificently complex Mann's way of thinking. We have to dive and hold our breath to try to understand what he wanted to tell us. Mann’s style is unique. He’s detailed and atmospheric. Every word he wrote in his books is there for a reason, and never just to "fill" a few more pages. To me, one of the layers of the "onion" that is this story is the awakening awareness of the slow decay of the aging process of Aschembach/Mann in contrast with the stunning beauty of youth, that the character/author stumbles upon when already, slowly feels life slipping from one's fingers, and not, as it had been said over and over, anything to do with some emergent auto-repressed paedophile homosexuality of the author. Thomas Mann uses in this book Nietzsche's concept from the "Birth of Tragedy", where the poles apart, Apollonian (Reason, rationality, order, and harmony) and Dionysian (Chaos, thrill, ecstasy, and dreams) are the two essential forces of Art and Life, in a constant battle inside the artist and from which emerges Art and the inevitable Tragedy, to explain the conflicting emotions of Aschenbach. Seen from that point of view the book is about self-awareness. The eternal struggle between the passions of nature and the restraints of rationality. And if we stop to think that two philosophers of the magnitude of Plato and Nietzsche (among others) dedicated a good deal amount of thought to the subject, then that one of the most important writers of the XIX/XX centuries dedicates a book to the subject is not surprising. What is a surprise, to me, is the context he uses to expose his thoughts. The main character is a self-repressed and disciplined man by habit who values reason above everything else, like Mann himself, and as the story evolves he increasingly wields to the wilderness of passions and becomes obsessively emotional to the border of the irrational. All that matters, in the end, is the sensuality of "Beauty" represented by Tadzio, a fourteen-year-old boy that fascinates Aschenbach with his youthful, godlike beauty . Aschenbach begins this story as an extremely rational Apollonian and ends as an excessive Dionysian and is consumed by the platonic passion that leads to his death. In exploring these opposing feelings, Mann states (like Nietzsche) that it is this duality that governs not only art but the artist as well and Life as Its whole. Mann finishes the story with Aschenbach realizing how meaningless and devoided of interest or value, life is without Beauty and Love. The confrontation with the materialization of those two concepts that he kept in check all his entire life is shattering and he acknowledges that he will never be able to put the pieces back together. So, facing the void that wraps what rests on his life, he becomes conscious of a disturbing idea about Life and himself; For Beauty, Phaedrus, mark well! only Beauty is at one and the same time Divine and Visible, and so it is indeed the sensuous lover’s path…. and when he realizes what a void the rest of his life will be because inevitably he will lose the object of this powerful obsession that has destroyed everything he was, then he chooses, perhaps unconsciously, not to continue living (and the closing Maan adopts to the book is superb in my opinion) he "chooses" an unconscious "suicide" by doing nothing and just staying in a city taken by the plague because the only option left to him in order to get rid of all the despair and pain awakened in him is to be dead but he would die in contemplation of "Beauty". The best of all things is something entirely outside your grasp: not to be born, not to be, to be nothing. But the second best thing for you is to die soon. F.N. The last image (in my opinion!) of von Aschenbach dying alone on the beach, as he observes the object of his obsession and despair, makes me wonder if it is only at the End that we become aware of all those little things that we discard as unimportant and that we let pass by without realizing its real value. And when our conscience awakens, it's too late and we can only regret everything we never had because we were so sure that what we had was the most important thing (success, money, etc.) and getting everything else would be so natural and easy as breathing, and not realizing that one day everything would disappear because we didn't pay attention to it or we fed the wrong values and, we only wasted our time in search of vague illusions born of the "rat race" of our material lives. And that makes me think that Nietzsche was right: we are governed by the battle between Dionysus and Apollo always throbbing in our consciousness, and if we don't pay attention and dive headlong into this conflict, in the End, we are only left with Yeats: And bending down beside the glowing bars, Murmur, a little sadly, how Love fled And paced upon the mountains overhead And hid his face amid a crowd of stars. This is a book that I respect as a work of a powerful intellect but that I profoundly dislike. I wonder why….? I hope that someone could find these thoughts of mine of some interest. As I said in my profile, my "reviews" are not objectively qualitative but always and only subjectively sensorial.

We have to dive and hold our breath to try to understand what he wanted to tell us.

The best of all things is something entirely outside your grasp: not to be born, not to be, to be nothing. But the second best thing for you is to die soon.F.N.

The last image (in my opinion!) of von Aschenbach dying alone on the beach, as he observes the object of his obsession and despair, makes me wonder if it is only at the End that we become aware of all those little things that we discard as unimportant and that we let pass by without realizing its real value. And when our conscience awakens, it's too late and we can only regret everything we never had because we were so sure that what we had was the most important thing (success, money, etc.) and getting everything else would be so natural and easy as breathing, and not realizing that one day everything would disappear because we didn't pay attention to it or we fed the wrong values and, we only wasted our time in search of vague illusions born of the "rat race" of our material lives. And that makes me think that Nietzsche was right: we are governed by the battle between Dionysus and Apollo always throbbing in our consciousness, and if we don't pay attention and dive headlong into this conflict, in the End, we are only left with Yeats:

And bending down beside the glowing bars,
Murmur, a little sadly, how Love fled
And paced upon the mountains overhead
And hid his face amid a crowd of stars.

This is a book that I respect as a work of a powerful intellect but that I profoundly dislike. I wonder why….?

I hope that someone could find these thoughts of mine of some interest. As I said in my profile, my "reviews" are not objectively qualitative but always and only subjectively sensorial.

review.text: Ένα αρκετά κακό βιβλίο με μακριές τραβηγμένες προτάσεις γεμάτες δυσκολονόητες φράσεις και αναπάντεχους συνδυασμούς λέξεων. Φαίνεται πως ο συγγραφέας προσπαθούσε υπερβολικά για να δείξει τον εσωτερικό διάλογο και τη σκέψη του ήρωα, το οποίο αφενός κούραζε και αφετέρου δείχνει να γίνεται για τον εντυπωσιασμό του αναγνώστη. Ίσως πάλι δεν ήμουν αρκετά έξυπνη για να καταλάβω τα υψηλά νοήματα του βιβλίου περί τέχνης και αρετής, δεδομένου ότι συχνά διάβαζα την ίδια πρόταση 2-3 φορές για να μπορέσω να καταλάβω τι εννοεί. Από την άλλη ούτε η ''πλοκή'' μου άρεσε ιδιαίτερα, μάλλον γιατί σε όλο το -ευτυχώς σύντομο- βιβλίο δε γίνονται και πολλά. Ένας 50άρης ταξιδεύει στη Βενετία, όπου ξεμυαλίζεται με ένα 14χρονο αγόρι και ''ανακαλύπτει'' κρυμμένες πλευρές του εαυτού του, αγνοώντας παράλληλα οτιδήποτε άλλο γίνεται γύρω του. Και ναι, σαφώς ο ήρωας έχει παιδοφιλικές τάσεις, οι οποίες όσο κι αν απαλύνονταν με ηθικούς ενδοιασμούς εξακολουθούσαν να είναι πολύ ενοχλητικές. Γενικά τα μόνα στοιχεία που έσωσαν το βιβλίο από το 1* ήταν κάποιες στιγμές που με έκανε να σταματήσω και να σκεφτώ κι έτσι κάποιες από τις αφηρημένες σκέψεις του συγγραφέα/πρωταγωνιστή πήραν σχήμα στο δικό μου μυαλό. Διαφορετικά δεν καταλαβαίνω καν το λόγο ύπαρξης αυτού του βιβλίου. Δεν οδηγεί κάπου, δεν έχει κάποιο μεγάλο φινάλε, κάποια σημαντική εξέλιξη, δεν προσφέρει στον αναγνώστη κάποια ευχαρίστηση ούτε ηθικό δίδαγμα, διαβάζεται απλά σαν έκθεση ιδεών. Δεν το συστήνω...
review.text_raw:
Ένα αρκετά κακό βιβλίο με μακριές τραβηγμένες προτάσεις γεμάτες δυσκολονόητες φράσεις και αναπάντεχους συνδυασμούς λέξεων. Φαίνεται πως ο συγγραφέας προσπαθούσε υπερβολικά για να δείξει τον εσωτερικό διάλογο και τη σκέψη του ήρωα, το οποίο αφενός κούραζε και αφετέρου δείχνει να γίνεται για τον εντυπωσιασμό του αναγνώστη. Ίσως πάλι δεν ήμουν αρκετά έξυπνη για να καταλάβω τα υψηλά νοήματα του βιβλίου περί τέχνης και αρετής, δεδομένου ότι συχνά διάβαζα την ίδια πρόταση 2-3 φορές για να μπορέσω να καταλάβω τι εννοεί.

Από την άλλη ούτε η ''πλοκή'' μου άρεσε ιδιαίτερα, μάλλον γιατί σε όλο το -ευτυχώς σύντομο- βιβλίο δε γίνονται και πολλά. Ένας 50άρης ταξιδεύει στη Βενετία, όπου ξεμυαλίζεται με ένα 14χρονο αγόρι και ''ανακαλύπτει'' κρυμμένες πλευρές του εαυτού του, αγνοώντας παράλληλα οτιδήποτε άλλο γίνεται γύρω του. Και ναι, σαφώς ο ήρωας έχει παιδοφιλικές τάσεις, οι οποίες όσο κι αν απαλύνονταν με ηθικούς ενδοιασμούς εξακολουθούσαν να είναι πολύ ενοχλητικές.

Γενικά τα μόνα στοιχεία που έσωσαν το βιβλίο από το 1* ήταν κάποιες στιγμές που με έκανε να σταματήσω και να σκεφτώ κι έτσι κάποιες από τις αφηρημένες σκέψεις του συγγραφέα/πρωταγωνιστή πήραν σχήμα στο δικό μου μυαλό. Διαφορετικά δεν καταλαβαίνω καν το λόγο ύπαρξης αυτού του βιβλίου. Δεν οδηγεί κάπου, δεν έχει κάποιο μεγάλο φινάλε, κάποια σημαντική εξέλιξη, δεν προσφέρει στον αναγνώστη κάποια ευχαρίστηση ούτε ηθικό δίδαγμα, διαβάζεται απλά σαν έκθεση ιδεών. Δεν το συστήνω...

review.text: Siempre había querido leer a Thomas Mann, y quise buscar una novela corta para poder conocerlo, antes de aventurarme a esa Montaña Mágica. Esta novela es corta pero realmente profunda, no pude esperar nada menos de este gran autor alemán. Muerte en Venecia es una afirmación y a la vez una resistencia al mundo de los valores antiburgueses de la época . Aschenbach representa una crítica (bien escrita ) en la medida en que presenta la idea de que el artista que quiere reconocimiento debe mantenerse alejado de la vida, aunque esa distancia conduzca al estancamiento de la imaginación. Esta novela nos introduce varios temas en los que podemos reflexionar, entre ellos la decadencia física y moral , el conflicto entre el intelecto y la inspiración, la belleza, el arte, la pasión y la juventud. Es una novela bárbara que recomiendo leer.

review.text: No sé cómo podría redactar nada sobre este libro sin sonar demasiado subjetiva y/o sectaria. No me gustan los señores rancios (qué le voy a hacer), me cuesta MUCHO disfrutar de su escritura...para leer una sarta de reflexiones de un cuarentón pedante cojo un libro de Javier Marías que al menos están mucho mejor escritos

review.text: A minha estreia com Thomas Mann. Um livro precioso que por coincidência li após o tratado de Sêneca sobre a brevidade da vida. Aflora esta realidade do ócio e da essência dele nas nossas vidas, percorre os caminhos dos Deuses Gregos que tanto me fascinam e que eu teimo em não explorar e fala da fragilidade da vida e na brevidade do amor. O amor, esse conceito vasto, extenso que não se limita ao ato frouxo e breve de um relacionamento entre um homem e uma mulher. O amor; puro, subtil, belo.
review.text_raw:

review.text: Tadzio ile tanışıncaya kadar müthiş bir okuma zevkim vardı. Ancak dünya üzerinde geçen nalet bir süreç içinde okuyunca, kitap bir anda itici geldi. Zamanlamam kötü oldu okumak için.

review.text: <> optimista y con una semi-perfección.>> ▪️Thomas Mann narra la historia de un escritor que descontento e inconforme decide viajar a Venecia en busca de conocimientos, de inspiración y de nuevas ideas sobre las que reflexionar y escribir. En su trayecto encuentra un motivo sencillo y suficiente para dedicarle horas y pensamientos: un chico joven, hijo de una familia adinerada que para el protagonista será el encargado de encarnar la más pura belleza. Su mirada se centra en el chico y pasa los días observándole, encontrando la verdadera esencia de lo bello. Mientras, en Venecia ha llegado la peste y la población, las calles, los comercios... se están viendo afectados por la pandemia. El protagonista, lejos de preocuparse por este motivo, parece encontrar en su viaje la calma y la tranquilidad que buscaba: se refugia en la belleza que cree tener delante, una belleza que se mezcla con la muerte. ¿Es probable que lo realmente bello se encuentre cerca de la nada, en el reposo que encierra la eternidad de no ser? Thomas Mann escribe: "Quien se esfuerza por alcanzar lo excelso, nota el ansia de reposar en lo perfecto. ¿Y la nada no es acaso una forma de perfección?" Puede que solo aquel que se encuentre siempre cerca del agotamiento, de la lucha y la constancia por alcanzar lo sublime, pueda entender la verdadera belleza que esconde el reposo. Y este libro, que a primera vista no es más que la historia de un viaje en el que no pasa nada especial, es en sí mismo ese camino hacia la belleza y la perfección que el protagonista necesita y encuentra en la tranquilidad culminante de la muerte.🌹

review.text: Gustav von Aschenbach, a German literary giant in his twilight years, decides to leave home for reasons barely understand. His vague desires lead him to travel to Venice where he comes across his own version of perfection, embodied in the figure of a Polish boy. The first time I read this book, it was all about the sentences. I had read the more contemporary British interpretation Gilbert Adair's "Love and Death in Long Island," a book filled with wonderful sentences. After reading that book, I thought to myself, let me learn about the origins of that book in the earlier German version. And so I did. I read these books in my early university years, hoping to master the art of the very short novel with the postcard-thin plot. I came away with a fascination for sentence-level writing. Both books had prose so beautiful it could easily be called poetry. Now, about fifteen years later, I've read "Death in Venice" for the second time. And...I had that feeling all over again. Even translated from German, the sentences sparkle. What else is there to say about this novel? This is essentially a novel about a writer and writing. If you're someone like me, someone about 20 years into a decadent writing addiction, it will make you think about the great questions of writing. As the author writes, mirroring the thoughts of the protagonist, good writing comes "out of daily increments of hundreds upon hundreds of bits of inspiration" (p. 15). Or what about this quote on the quandary of becoming a writer: "Solitude begets originality, bold and disconcerting beauty, poetry. But solitude can beget perversity, disparity, the absurd and the forbidden." (p. 43). I might never write anything so wonderful about the act of writing and the problems of the writer's life. The novel violates many of the "rules" of good writing (at least by American standards) -- it lacks scenes in many parts, it has internal narration, a great deal of the book is spent telling instead of showing. It also has a character who rarely acts. And yet... The book is a marvel. The first two chapters demonstrate how to write a character study, a useful thing for a story or novel but something perhaps best left out of the story itself. These character elements should be revealed slowly through scenes and dialogue, or even better, action. And yet, it is forgivable. There is action -- there is the decision to travel, there is the decision to leave, the decision to stay, and finally, during the end, Aschenbach's decision to risk his life to see the face of beauty just a little longer. Then there is that other bit to talk about -- to name the story. No matter how many Greek myths are invoked, no matter how much Gustav Von Aschenbach frames his journey as passionate, dignified, noble, and spiritual, he is a German author stalking a Polish boy. It is a stalker tale. A literary stalker tale, but a stalker tale none the less. This is something wonderfully captured in Adair's "Love and Death on Long Island". Both are books about the "Real of Desire" and looking foolish in the pursuit of that Desire. Most real love is a fool's errand, no matter how you try to tell it (so why not tell it as the tale of a fool or the tale of a dignified man made foolish). In the end, the book is instructive about the limits of writing about the inner world, about the possibilities of the minimalist plot, and the power of beautiful language. The book can be read as a dark comedy or as something dreadfully serious, depending on how you interpret the aristocratic tones. For my own tastes, I like to think of the book both as the death of a particular character and as the death of the self-aggrandizing aristocratic author. Any way you choose to read it, it is a masterpiece.
review.text_raw:
Gustav von Aschenbach, a German literary giant in his twilight years, decides to leave home for reasons barely understand. His vague desires lead him to travel to Venice where he comes across his own version of perfection, embodied in the figure of a Polish boy.

The first time I read this book, it was all about the sentences.

I had read the more contemporary British interpretation Gilbert Adair's "Love and Death in Long Island," a book filled with wonderful sentences. After reading that book, I thought to myself, let me learn about the origins of that book in the earlier German version.

And so I did.

I read these books in my early university years, hoping to master the art of the very short novel with the postcard-thin plot. I came away with a fascination for sentence-level writing. Both books had prose so beautiful it could easily be called poetry.

Now, about fifteen years later, I've read "Death in Venice" for the second time. And...I had that feeling all over again. Even translated from German, the sentences sparkle.

What else is there to say about this novel?

This is essentially a novel about a writer and writing. If you're someone like me, someone about 20 years into a decadent writing addiction, it will make you think about the great questions of writing.

As the author writes, mirroring the thoughts of the protagonist, good writing comes "out of daily increments of hundreds upon hundreds of bits of inspiration" (p. 15).

Or what about this quote on the quandary of becoming a writer: "Solitude begets originality, bold and disconcerting beauty, poetry. But solitude can beget perversity, disparity, the absurd and the forbidden." (p. 43).

I might never write anything so wonderful about the act of writing and the problems of the writer's life.

The novel violates many of the "rules" of good writing (at least by American standards) -- it lacks scenes in many parts, it has internal narration, a great deal of the book is spent telling instead of showing.

It also has a character who rarely acts.

And yet...

The book is a marvel.

The first two chapters demonstrate how to write a character study, a useful thing for a story or novel but something perhaps best left out of the story itself. These character elements should be revealed slowly through scenes and dialogue, or even better, action.

And yet, it is forgivable.

There is action -- there is the decision to travel, there is the decision to leave, the decision to stay, and finally, during the end, Aschenbach's decision to risk his life to see the face of beauty just a little longer.

Then there is that other bit to talk about -- to name the story.

No matter how many Greek myths are invoked, no matter how much Gustav Von Aschenbach frames his journey as passionate, dignified, noble, and spiritual, he is a German author stalking a Polish boy.

It is a stalker tale. A literary stalker tale, but a stalker tale none the less.

This is something wonderfully captured in Adair's "Love and Death on Long Island". Both are books about the "Real of Desire" and looking foolish in the pursuit of that Desire. Most real love is a fool's errand, no matter how you try to tell it (so why not tell it as the tale of a fool or the tale of a dignified man made foolish).

In the end, the book is instructive about the limits of writing about the inner world, about the possibilities of the minimalist plot, and the power of beautiful language.

The book can be read as a dark comedy or as something dreadfully serious, depending on how you interpret the aristocratic tones. For my own tastes, I like to think of the book both as the death of a particular character and as the death of the self-aggrandizing aristocratic author.

Any way you choose to read it, it is a masterpiece.

review.text: Why wasn't I informed that this is a book about pedophilia? How is it possible that this book shows up on my feed so often, people are rating it and NOT commenting on this specific fact? I can't imagine seeing a Lolita review without someone screaming about impropriety and somehow, Thomas Mann's boring book is just there, in plain sight. I hated this man since The Magic Mountain , because, hands down, that was the worst book I have read in my life. I remember reading it for months, picking it up with pure hatred for the story and dreading the inevitable snooze which would make me read the same page all over again. At the time, I prided myself on never DNF-ing a book after reading 20 pages, so that's the origin story about me hating Thomas Mann. Why did I pick up another book written by this man? It is considered a classic and as such, it automatically qualifies for my TBR list. Even though I hate myself for reading both Magic Mountain and Death in Venice, I know that Buddenbrooks are still waiting. If you don't know what Death in Venice is about, here it goes. An old man, writer just like Thomas Mann (the autobiographical elements, I fear, are numerous), decides to travel to find at least some pleasure in his boring, uneventful life. He is well-known and respected, but it seems that nothing and no one can make this guy smile. The book starts off in a cemetery, so you can imagine the atmosphere and his contemplations about impending death. After being an absolute pain in the ass to everyone who serves him during his trip (the man is an aristocrat, but still), he decides to go to Venice. Long story short, he complains about everything until he sees a beautiful 14-15year old boy. Watching him doing completely normal things with his family and friends while on a beach, this dirtbag of a man is having such thoughts about the boy that would have you calling the police. He is basically stalking him and when he doesn't see the boy, his day is ruined. Dirtbag never actually does anything, but that doesn't take away from the fact that this guy is disgusting. Without spoiling the end, I have to say that I am aware of the symbolism that many people are using as a defence for Thomas Mann - the youth of the boy is what he is actually craving, it is not sexual, and if it is, it is platonic, blah blah blah. If I wanted to write a book about that and this story came to mind, I would sit some more and think harder to find another allegory. To be completely honest, I don't rate books based on moral disagreements with the writer. I actually loved All the Ugly and Wonderful Things which revolves around a minor falling in love with an adult man. I think I am able to notice a good structure, style or overall message even though I don't like writer's choices. However, this garbage book Death in Venice just flicked a switch in me and I was fuming the whole time I was reading it. I hate Thomas Mann. Two stars are here because I can't say that the writing was bad. Unfortunately, it was good at times.

review.text: ¿Quién podría descifrar el enigma de la naturaleza del artista? ¿Quién puede comprender esa fusión instintiva de disciplina y desenfreno en que consiste? (p.91) Es una novela hermosa, perfectamente narrada. La empecé a leer sin saber muy bien de qué iba, pero creo que como temas principales pueden considerarse: primero, al artista; segundo a la belleza (intrínsicamente ligada al artista); y tercero a la enfermedad. Aschenbach, un escritor muy correcto y estimado, viaja a Venecia para sentirse cómodo y para vivir algo diferente, algo que no le entrega la ciudad alemana en donde reside. Allí, después de un momento de indecisión sobre quedarse o irse a alguna otra ciudad, conoce a Tadrio, un joven excepcionalmente bello. Aschenbach, a pesar del aire enrarecido de Venecia, decide quedarse. Él se da cuenta de que algo empieza a azotar a la ciudad, gracias al continuo olor a desinfectante: una peste (que puede ser la tuberculosis), una calamidad. La enfermedad es algo que no pasa desabercibida, por lo menos para el protagonista, pues es algo que huele y le causa incomodidad. Siente el olor a desinfectante y nadie más parece notarlo. La enfermedad, en este caso, como diría Sontag, da belleza, prestigio y hace único a quien la padece. Tadrio, según se va diciendo, se ve enfermizo, pero sigue siendo hermoso. No llegará a viejo, como dice Aschenbach. Sin embargo, está la otra cara de la enfermedad y de la ciudad que no es vista por los turistas: una ciudad pestilente, envilecida y llena de oscuridad. Para mí, se desarrolla bastante bien la historia en un mundo lleno de belleza pero también de engaños: la verdad no es dicha por el temor que se tiene a nombrarla. Nadie parece poseer la verdad. Me gustaron mucho las reflexiones que se hacen sobre la labor del artista, porque, en cierto modo, mitifica el poder de la soledad y el trabajo arduo para crear una obra que merezca ser leída. La soledad engrendra lo original, lo atrevido, lo extraordinariamente bello; la poesía. Pero engendra también lo desagradable, lo inoportuno, absurdo e inadecuado (p.47) Unido a esto, está la belleza, el ideal que persiguen los grandes artistas y que a su vez, tiene relación con el amor (Eros) y con la posesión de un ente bello. Lo bello es sinónimo de perfección. Quien se esfuerza por alcanzar lo excelso, nota el ansia de reposar en lo perfecto. ¿Y la nada no es acaso una forma de perfección? (p.59) Es un libro hermoso, y es una delicia leerlo.

review.text: The last months I've been glutted with bad fiction. Fiction so bad it actually has the audacity to think it's good, without giving readers the pleasant balm of campy enjoyment. I needed something well-written. I've tried reading Thomas Mann three times. The first time I read Death in Venice, I didn't get farther than the first page. Felix Krull was strange, and Joseph and HIs Brothers was not what I expected. Reading Death in Venice a second time was... Well, I read the whole thing in a day. This is a novella of almost perfect form, where not a word is misplaced, not a scene is without several layers of meaning, not a visual image offered that doesn't come back again in horrifying echoes. Some have compared it to a piece of music, and music does play an important role in the story. Mann describes the main character, also a writer, as someone who is able to catch "eros in the word." He obviously meant himself, and he was right. The prose is simply gorgeous, luxurious, sultry, almost erotic, but still economical, with no hint of pompousness. What makes it great literature is the complexity of its theme--the "tendency toward the abyss" hidden in any artistic temperament, especially when it gives way to passion. The gradual movement of the main character's obsession with the boy Tadzio from an elevated appreciation of classical Beauty to tawdry lust is terrifying precisely because it describes such a universal condition--the madness and insanity of an all-consuming passion for another. Making such a sedate, "Protestant" (in Mann's own words) character--his mind is so well tuned that it can turn any logical supposition inside out, arguing both sides of an issue so well that no truth is possible--such easy prey for a passion leading to death is particularly brilliant. Obviously Mann was making a point about the decadence of Europe at the turn of the century, but everything he says (and leaves unsaid) is still true of today.

What makes it great literature is the complexity of its theme--the "tendency toward the abyss" hidden in any artistic temperament, especially when it gives way to passion. review.text: Adoro Thomas Mann, mas esta obra, que vinha sobejamente bem recomendada, apesar de muito interessante, não me tocou. “Morte em Veneza” (1912) é um pequeno conto escrito por Mann, exactamente a meio caminho entre o seu primeiro livro “Buddenbrooks” (1901) e a sua obra-prima “Montanha Mágica” (1924). Deste modo podemos encontrar uma escrita notável, mas ainda assim longe do que nos viria a oferecer mais tarde. Mann parece ter querido fazer desta história um momento de pausa e reflexão sobre o seu trabalho artístico, enquanto escritor, daí que se tenha dedicado a dissecar um episódio passado consigo próprio numa visita a Veneza (mais tarde confirmado pela própria esposa). Para o fazer vai chamar à discussão a mitologia grega, e assim tentar desconstruir os seus desejos, as suas obsessões. Desta discussão emergem momentos imensamente belos, nomeadamente na discussão do belo, mas que vão sendo entrecortados com momentos de menor interesse, sendo que o tamanho da obra acaba por não permitir o aprofundamento e adensamento que seria de esperar deste tema. Não podemos deixar de assumir a data em que foi escrito, ainda assim o que mais me incomodou foi o tratamento dado por Mann à homosexualidade, se assumirmos a interpretação da cidade de Veneza como metáfora do jovem belo. Assim, temos constantes descrições do mau cheiro das águas da cidade, que apesar de verdadeiras são no final elevadas à condição de epidemia e mortais, dando conta de um conflito, provavelmente sentido interiormente por Mann. Ou seja, como que para afagar dentro de si o desejo, Mann opta por enlamear a relação, opondo a atração pelo belo à aversão pelo mesmo sexo, rotulando a potencial relação como doentia, e com um único fim expectável, a morte. 3.5/5

review.text: Terceiro livro que leio de Thomas Mann, o segundo de ficção. Que dizer? Acho que posso colocá-lo na lista dos meus escritores preferidos e não é por ter sido Nobel em 1929. É mesmo porque admiro a sua escrita. Não é um escritor fácil de compreender nem de leituras rápidas e superficiais. É profundo, superior, pensado, estruturado. E eu gosto disso. Aqui é o fascinio pela beleza que dá o tema à história, ou seu centro se preferirem. Aschenbach, o escritor, está deslumbrado pela beleza do adolescente Tadzio, sendo que esse deslumbramento é tal que se reflecte na própria escrita de Thomas Mann. Forte, arrebatadora, de cortar a respiração (no bom sentido, no muito bom sentido, entenda-se). É dificil não se ficar apaixonado pela paixão de Aschenbach por Tadzio...! E eu só os conheço pelas palavras de Mann. "A Morte em Veneza" não é a história de um crime (como eu, erradamente, pensava; apesar do título poder sugeri-lo), mas sim um livro cuja escrita nos transporta muito para o plano espiritual e superior. Num certo sentido é filosófico. Aliás, nesta sua novela, Thomas Mann oferece-nos muito de Platão, sobretudo na sua relação com a beleza e com as emoções. E eu adoro Platão! É preciso dizer mais alguma coisa?! Posto isto, estou a pensar seriamente se não começo a ler "A Montanha Mágica" muito em breve... Será loucura?! Não sei, o que sei é que Thomas Mann está a "infiltrar-se em mim", definitivamente.

Forte, arrebatadora, de cortar a respiração (no bom sentido, no muito bom sentido, entenda-se). É dificil não se ficar apaixonado pela paixão de Aschenbach por Tadzio...! E eu só os conheço pelas palavras de Mann.

"A Morte em Veneza" não é a história de um crime (como eu, erradamente, pensava; apesar do título poder sugeri-lo), mas sim um livro cuja escrita nos transporta muito para o plano espiritual e superior. Num certo sentido é filosófico. Aliás, nesta sua novela, Thomas Mann oferece-nos muito de Platão, sobretudo na sua relação com a beleza e com as emoções. E eu adoro Platão! É preciso dizer mais alguma coisa?!

Posto isto, estou a pensar seriamente se não começo a ler "A Montanha Mágica" muito em breve... Será loucura?! Não sei, o que sei é que Thomas Mann está a "infiltrar-se em mim", definitivamente.

review.text: One of my rare sojourns into classic, German Literature. I read this in the original German version, as part of Death in Venice: Making and Unmaking a Master . I listed both books, otherwise I will be endlessly confused, whenever one of them pops up on my feed without beeing ticked off as read. And, alas, my review ended up being bilingual, because it was easier... Kein Text, der sich einem einfach so beim darüber lesen erschließt. Ich habe meine übliche Lesegeschwindigkeit um einiges herunter schrauben müssen, um zu verstehen, was ich lese. Und um der schönen Sprache Genüge zu tun. Ultimativ war diese Novelle eine Mischung aus schöner Sprache und Langeweile. Da diese Novelle eine der wichtigsten Arbeiten Mann's ist, würde ich sagen, das die Langeweile an mir liegt! Das Thema der Novelle war außerdem weit außerhalb meiner Comfort Zone. Diese obsessive Faszination Aschenbachs mit dem Junge Tazio hatte für mich keinen Wert, es hat mir nichts gegeben, darüber zu lesen. Ich habe mich unwohl gefühlt mit den sexuellen Untertönen. Ich werde Thomas Mann von nun an immer als tragische Person sehen. Sorry, hat mir nicht wirklich gefallen.
review.text_raw:
One of my rare sojourns into classic, German Literature.

I read this in the original German version, as part of Death in Venice: Making and Unmaking a Master.

I listed both books, otherwise I will be endlessly confused, whenever one of them pops up on my feed without beeing ticked off as read. And, alas, my review ended up being bilingual, because it was easier...

Kein Text, der sich einem einfach so beim darüber lesen erschließt. Ich habe meine übliche Lesegeschwindigkeit um einiges herunter schrauben müssen, um zu verstehen, was ich lese. Und um der schönen Sprache Genüge zu tun.

Ultimativ war diese Novelle eine Mischung aus schöner Sprache und Langeweile. Da diese Novelle eine der wichtigsten Arbeiten Mann's ist, würde ich sagen, das die Langeweile an mir liegt!

Das Thema der Novelle war außerdem weit außerhalb meiner Comfort Zone. Diese obsessive Faszination Aschenbachs mit dem Junge Tazio hatte für mich keinen Wert, es hat mir nichts gegeben, darüber zu lesen. Ich habe mich unwohl gefühlt mit den sexuellen Untertönen. Ich werde Thomas Mann von nun an immer als tragische Person sehen.

Sorry, hat mir nicht wirklich gefallen.

review.text: Una lectura corta pero bastante densa y polémica. Recomiendo a los nuevos lectores iniciar este libro con la mente abierta y con la idea de que la trama toca los límites de la moral, ya que de no hacerlo así se puede llegar a perder mucho de lo bueno que hay en este libro. Aschenbach es un escritor que goza de fama en Munich, pero que en su edad madura siente que ha perdido la inspiración, ésto lo lleva de viaje a Venecia, en donde encuentra lo que anhela encarnado en la figura de un hermoso niño polaco llamado Tadzio. A partir de este descubrimiento el artista se consume en pensamientos estéticos inspirados por su adonis. En tan pocas páginas se tocan temas complejos como la búsqueda de la inspiración, la belleza inalcanzable, la juventud perdida y el amor prohibido que se vuelve una obsesión al extremo de olvidarse de uno mismo. Todo lo anterior llevado de una forma tan acertada, llena de simbolismos y elementos opuestos que se contraponen dando pie a reflexiones profundas que hacen que uno deba releer para encontrar nuevas cosas. Personalmente debo destacar el trasfondo de la pandemia por Cólera donde se desarrollan los hechos y que me recordó tanto a los tiempos actuales, la historia es algo que tiende a repetirse.

review.text: Cuộc đời mực thước của Aschenbach đã bị chính ông tước đi bằng sự suy đồi, trong lòng một Venice tráng lệ và thiu ôi. Nhìn lại, cái phỉnh phờ định mệnh của dịch bệnh khi đó dường như đã đồng lõa cùng cái dục vọng trái khoáy nhưng đong đầy đam mê của ông, tuồn dẫn ông vào ngã hủy diệt. Vậy mà ông kệ, ông để mình say. Say một Tadzio bé nhỏ, ngất ngây. • Đời ý nghĩa gì khi không thể một lần vùng vẫy? Vậy nên Aschenbach, từ đắn đo tới quyết sống ra trò, đã vứt bỏ thẳng thừng những huy hoàng khi trước, đặng quăng bản thân vào vô đạo đức, vào thật dạ thật lòng. Cái thể xác khô rạc của ông, lần khân trước vẻ tươi tắn như nắng hè của cậu bé vận áo thủy thủ khuy vàng kia, ngoài mê mẩn, còn là muốn trút bỏ hết mình, dẹp những luân lý, và âu yếm cái đẹp đến thiên thu. • Thứ Aschenbach dành trọn những ngày cuối đời để ngưỡng vọng, mãi mãi ông cũng không thể chạm tới. Song giữa mênh mông sóng biển ngoài kia, ánh mắt xám sẫm hoàng hôn nọ đã ngoái lại trông vào ông đôi chút, chẳng đọng nghĩ suy, đủ để ông thả mình vào hư không, khép lại cõi lòng. • Đọc lại lần hai sau 6 năm.

review.text: Lo primero de todo: Marisa tq, eres mi bff y tienes un gusto exquisito, así que voy a hacerme la loca cada vez que me hables de este libro y escucharte con atención cada vez que te emociones con Thomas Mann. Bueno, ahora viene la tralla. Este libro habla de un pedófilo egoísta y creepy. Y la parte que menos me ha molestado ha sido la de pedófilo porque por lo menos no hay una interacción entre ellos dos, sino que el mc idealiza al niño por su belleza. Se me han hecho largas todas las metáforas de la mitología griega, y eso que soy fan. El autor abusa de las metáforas y de referencias que piensa que tienen mucho significado cuando en realidad, si te paras a analizarlo, dicho significado es simplemente hueco y débil. El egoísmo del protagonista se hace pesado porque su hilo de pensamiento es INTERMINABLE (¿¿de verdad es necesario describir en 5 páginas cómo cantan cuatro venecianos??). Una estrella porque a pesar de que hay muy poco argumento, la parte de cómo se comporta la sociedad veneciana en los tiempos del cólera ha sido interesante.

review.text: Yıllar sonra tekrar okuduğum bir kitap oldu Venedik’te Ölüm. Sanat, estetik temaları ağırlıklı, tutkunun başrolde olduğu güçlü bir novella olarak nitelendirilebilir. Eşcinsel edebiyatın kült kitaplarından biri olarak da değerlendirilebilir tabii. Behçet Necatigil’in dilimize çevirmiş olması ayrı bir güzellik. Bu arada yazarın daha o dönemlerde Venedik’i, turist avcısı ve pis bir kent olarak tasvir etmesini de ayrıca not etmek gerekir.
review.text_raw: Yıllar sonra tekrar okuduğum bir kitap oldu Venedik’te Ölüm. Sanat, estetik temaları ağırlıklı, tutkunun başrolde olduğu güçlü bir novella olarak nitelendirilebilir. Eşcinsel edebiyatın kült kitaplarından biri olarak da değerlendirilebilir tabii. Behçet Necatigil’in dilimize çevirmiş olması ayrı bir güzellik. Bu arada yazarın daha o dönemlerde Venedik’i, turist avcısı ve pis bir kent olarak tasvir etmesini de ayrıca not etmek gerekir.

review.text: Thomas Mann does what every English teacher tells a student not to do in an exam, and is therefore rather disappointing. Essentially it is about writing and obsession and how man is generally flawed; so not really different to any novel. I am sure there are those who think this novella is excellent but to me it is generally flat and dull. I personally would not recommend this novel.

review.text: Me sorprende que hasta en una obra tan breve Thomas Mann te obliga a detenerte y tomar notas, regresar, releer. No sólo en su Doktor Faustus , el libro más difícil de leer en la historia de la literatura, sino también en un libro que de cualquier otro autor (por su extensión) me hubiera acabado en una tarde. Los maniáticos que subrayan todo lo que es interesante, aquí se van a acabar su lapicero. Los que ponen notas adhesivas, aquí se les irá medio paquete. Y es que no se le puede faltar el respeto como pretendía yo, no es una novela de descanso entre un libro gordo y otro. Pese a la brevedad y a la relectura, todo el tiempo tienes la intuición de que algo se te escapa. No el diálogo platónico, no la reflexión profunda sobre la belleza y la creación artística. Aquí Gustav Aschenbach contemplando al efebo Tadzio y la palabra como celebración de la belleza: una belleza inaprensible, enigmática, obsesiva. Sin justificar alguna conducta aberrante, podemos sentir empatía por Gustav por lo tan común y corriente como amar en secreto a una persona. Algunos de nosotros fuimos esos jóvenes que le escribían cartas y poemas a la chica bonita y popular que creíamos que jamás nos haría caso. Es esa empatía la que el lector común puede sentir, dejando de lado edades, géneros y situaciones jurídicas. Es paradigmático que Thomas Mann cite al Fedro y no al Banquete . En el Fedro el diálogo discurre sobre la belleza, la retórica y el amor. En éste diálogo se postula el mito del auriga alado: nuestra alma es una carreta jalada por dos caballos, uno bueno y noble y el otro malo y obstinado. La pasión es un caballo indómito que debemos controlar, porque el amor le da alas a nuestra alma y es difícil controlarla. Recordemos que Aschenbach se había hecho un nombre como escritor debido a su disciplina, es un hombre racional, pero al contemplar a Tadzio por primera vez su alma se vuelve alada y le es difícil controlar al caballo indómito, negro, terrible, de la pasión. La novela (tal vez lo digo porque a la par estoy releyendo los Diálogos de Platón) está plagada de referencias a los ideales de belleza griegos. Recordemos que para los griegos lo bello, bueno y justo eran una tríada indisoluble. Es inevitable la idealización, pues si Tadzio era bello, también debería de ser bueno y justo; Tadzio de tanto ser contemplado como objeto fenoménico sobre el cual se arroja la intencionalidad, queda reducido a una esencia, a saber, infinitamente inaprensible. Aschenbach no toca a Tadzio, es más, jamás habla con él. No es el Banquete , decía, de Platón, porque no hay contacto erótico. Aquí hablamos de la belleza en sí misma y por esto siempre se nos escapará, ¿cómo retener la belleza en sí misma? El mismo libro, muchas de sus páginas, huye de nuestro entendimiento y está bien. Las hojas, la tinta, es la pasión viva, por eso es un clásico. “La muerte en Venecia, como el propio Mann sostenía, trata de la pérdida de la dignidad del artista, pero Mann examina también la relación entre el arte y la vida” (1001 libros) El escritor se horroriza cuando ve a un viejo maquillado que se acerca a convivir con jóvenes. Es un golpe con guante blanco que Thomas Mann le da a los lectores de todas las épocas: nos convertimos en lo que odiamos. Esta novela, igual que Lolita , se rechaza antes de ser leída. A la gente le interesa más cancelar la literatura que leerla, analizarla y desentrañarla. Es más fácil cancelar un libro que leerlo y analizarlo a fondo. Me parece un libro que puede ser actual por la situación pandémica que acabamos de vivir. Muchas personas, víctimas de una pasión, no tomaron las precauciones pertinentes y murieron o murieron sus familiares. No sólo es la relación arte-vida, es la relación arte-vida-muerte. Desde los griegos, de nuevo, Eros y Tánatos en una lucha continúa: a veces gana Tánatos. Pienso en una última notación: ¿no será la inversión de Goethe? Ya no es el joven, es el viejo el que muere de amor, pero es igual de romántico y alemán. #248 de 1001 libros que hay que leer antes de morir #61 de 100 mejores novelas de la literatura universal #4 canon de Mario Vargas Llosa

Aquí hablamos de la belleza en sí misma y por esto siempre se nos escapará, ¿cómo retener la belleza en sí misma? El mismo libro, muchas de sus páginas, huye de nuestro entendimiento y está bien. Las hojas, la tinta, es la pasión viva, por eso es un clásico.
“La muerte en Venecia, como el propio Mann sostenía, trata de la pérdida de la dignidad del artista, pero Mann examina también la relación entre el arte y la vida” (1001 libros)

El escritor se horroriza cuando ve a un viejo maquillado que se acerca a convivir con jóvenes. Es un golpe con guante blanco que Thomas Mann le da a los lectores de todas las épocas: nos convertimos en lo que odiamos. Esta novela, igual que Lolita, se rechaza antes de ser leída. A la gente le interesa más cancelar la literatura que leerla, analizarla y desentrañarla. Es más fácil cancelar un libro que leerlo y analizarlo a fondo.

Me parece un libro que puede ser actual por la situación pandémica que acabamos de vivir. Muchas personas, víctimas de una pasión, no tomaron las precauciones pertinentes y murieron o murieron sus familiares. No sólo es la relación arte-vida, es la relación arte-vida-muerte. Desde los griegos, de nuevo, Eros y Tánatos en una lucha continúa: a veces gana Tánatos. Pienso en una última notación: ¿no será la inversión de Goethe? Ya no es el joven, es el viejo el que muere de amor, pero es igual de romántico y alemán.

#248 de 1001 libros que hay que leer antes de morir #61 de 100 mejores novelas de la literatura universal #4 canon de Mario Vargas Llosa

review.text: "...where the hideous death lurked in secret—at such times the atrocious seemed to him rich in possibilities, and laws of morality had dropped away." The novel astonished me. It wasn't what I expected. It is quite brilliant, a masterpiece. Unlike others, I didn't find this novel explicitly about pedophilia--unlike Lolita, which distinctly and undeniably is. I know that won't sit well with everyone. I can understand that. When I watched the 1971 film of the novel, that pedophilia lust was a major aspect and exacerbated by the shameful exploitation and lack of protection for "the most beautiful boy in the world," the real youth, Björn Andrésen. But the novel is about so much more than that. It is about lust, but also lust for life, for youth, for beauty, for being loved in spite of outward appearances (ironic, I know) by a misguided man who never so much as even speaks a word to the boy. The man is Gustav von Aschenbach, age 50, who, up to this point, has over-intellectualized his whole life, putting Art with a capitol A as the most desirable and potent of all things. He has never been a physically robust or a remarkably attractive person and does not seem to, thus far, possess any passion, except in theory. He has a grown daughter and had a wife who has since died. He describes their marriage as having been happy, although that seems faint praise since he doesn't even call her by name. From an early age, he has given his truest devotions to his work. That is what has provided his life, if not passion, its meaning, satisfaction, staid acclimation, respectability, honors, and financial reward. His work has been accomplished with deliberate study and steadfastness. His whole identity is Artist. A tightly controlled one. When he decides to go for a vacation to Venice ("Not far, not all the way to the tigers"), it is the first sign Aschenbach is not quite as satisfied as he once was. He is repulsed by ugliness, and especially the ugliness of, we slowly learn, of aging. And he is aging. The brilliance of Mann is how deftly he takes readers down the emotionally stifled deterioration of Aschenbach, through so many successively progressive stages, until ultimately to his complete humiliated, unraveled, unfettered indulgence of his long ignored emotions. Upon seeing a remarkably beautiful 14 year old boy at his Venice hotel (we can agree that we too can be momentarily smitten by a remarkable beauty, at any age; we too have eyes)--begins in earnest his slow but pointed rejection of his own aging and transforms the rejection into an absurd hope, then obsession, and then a truly wicked disregard for danger. All for the sake of a ridiculous projected fantasy of a reciprocal budding love with this boy. An elixir. It's not an easy novel to experience, but it's not exactly about outright sexual deviance either. It is full moral abandonment of self-regard due to fear of aging and death. It's a story of such pathos, repugnance, and, under Mann's genius, deep sadness, even pity. Aschenbach began a needlessly tragic journey with a mere natural, but much belated observation about his own capacity for lust that he has dismissed since his youth for the sake of his plans for success. When his intellectualizing about Eros and Socrates and whatever Classicism he has hitherto relied on for balance and harmony, when those begin to ring more and more hollow, he progressively becomes consumed by "the tigers" he once avoided, to self obliteration. And it started with a small belated recognition of delight in feelings, "Past emotions, precious early afflictions and yearnings which had been stifled by his rigorous programme of living, were now returning in such strange new forms. With an embarrassed, astonished smile, he recognized them..."

review.text: 4,5 Ich habe endlich begonnen, Thomas Mann zu lesen, wie es sich für einen anständigen Homosexuellen nun mal gehört. Ich muss gestehen, dass die ersten fünfzehn Seiten, die nun auch eher einer philosophischen Abhandlung glichen, ein Kampf waren; obwohl ich viele der komplexen, verschachtelten Sätze mehrfach las, erschloss sich mir ihre Bedeutung oft nicht gänzlich. Das änderte sich, als die Handlung mit der Reise nach Venedig an Fahrt aufnahm und plötzlich konnte ich das Buch kaum noch aus der Hand legen, war wie gebannt von Manns Sprache und seiner außergewöhnlich immersiven Art, die Geschichte zu erzählen. Ich halte „Der Tod in Venedig“ für ein äußerst gelungenes, hochinteressantes und für seine Zeit durchaus subversives Werk. Denn Aschenbachs überraschend unverblümt geschildertes Begehren - so unangenehm obsessiv-idealisierend dieses daherkommt und so fragwürdig es natürlich ist, dass es sich auf einen pubertierenden Jungen richtet - müsste doch 1912 für einen literarischen Skandal gesorgt haben. Kaum zu glauben, dass Manns sexuelle Orientierung sich erst zwanzig Jahre nach seinem Ableben manifestierte, als seine Tagebücher 1975 erstmals publik wurden. Andererseits führt seinen Protagonisten die kaum auszuhaltende Attraktion ins Verderben, schließlich in den Tod. Es ist zwar die Cholera, die ihn dahinrafft, aber eigentlich wohl vielmehr die Krankheit eines unzulässigen Begehrens, die ihn hinterrücks befällt, und also fest in ihrem Griff hält, ihm dann die Flucht vor der aufkeimenden Epidemie verwehrt. Es erscheint bloß wahrscheinlich, dass sich die Angst Manns vor einem Ausleben sexueller Impulse (ich spreche jetzt von solchen homosexueller Natur, nicht denen einer potentiellen Pädophilie) hier metaphorisch widerspiegelt. Sehr gefallen hat mir im Übrigen die düstere Atmosphäre im Venedig des frühen 20. Jahrhunderts, dieses Gothic-Element hatte ich nicht mit dem Autoren assoziiert, Erinnerungen an den Film „Wenn die Gondeln Trauer tragen“ stiegen empor. Es liegt eine unheilvolle Vorahnung in der Luft, die siechende Stadt fungiert als Omen und der allseits florierende Verfall ist so eindringlich beschrieben, dass es mich schauderte. Insgesamt bin ich nach einem holprigen Start offenkundig doch recht begeistert! Vielleicht wage ich mich an den Zauberberg, vielleicht auch nicht, eine Rezension folgt im ersteren Fall hoffentlich.

Es ist zwar die Cholera, die ihn dahinrafft, aber eigentlich wohl vielmehr die Krankheit eines unzulässigen Begehrens, die ihn hinterrücks befällt, und also fest in ihrem Griff hält, ihm dann die Flucht vor der aufkeimenden Epidemie verwehrt. review.text: “En onder complimenten en geestrijk wervende scherts onderrichtte Socrates Phaidros over verlangen en deugd. Hij sprak hem van de hevige ontsteltenis die de voelende doormaakt als zijn oog een gelijkenis van de eeuwige schoonheid ontwaart; hij sprak hem van de begeerten van de ongewijde en slechte, die de schoonheid niet kan denken wanneer hij haar beeld ziet en niet tot eerbied in staat is; sprak van de heilige vrees die de edele bevangt wanneer een godgelijkend gelaat, een volmaakte lichaamsgestalte voor hem verschijnt, - hoe hij dan begint te beven en zichzelf niet meer meester is en het nauwelijks waagt te kijken en hem vereert die de schoonheid heeft, ja, aan hem zou offeren als aan een godsbeeld als hij niet moest vrezen in de ogen der mensen een dwaas te schijnen. Want de schoonheid, mijn Phaidros, alleen zij is beminnenswaardig en zichtbaar tegelijk: ze is, onthoud dat goed! de enige vorm van het geestelijke die wij met de zinnen ontvangen, met de zinnen verdragen kunnen.” Dit is een citaat uit De dood in Venetië . Het toeval wil dat ik onlangs een poging heb gewaagd Platonische Liefde te lezen waarin is opgenomen Symposium en Phaedrus (hier vertaalt als Phaidros). Deze novelle van Mann over schoonheid en verval is duidelijk geïnspireerd op Plato’s verhalen. Maar het verhaal zegt misschien ook iets over Thomas Mann zelf die naar ik ergens las worstelde met zijn homoseksualiteit. Het boek stamt uit 1911. Deze vertaling maakt het soms wat lastig leesbaar (het citaat is er een voorbeeld van). Een nieuwe vertaling zou het verhaal zeker ten goede komen.
