“En onder complimenten en geestrijk wervende scherts onderrichtte Socrates Phaidros over verlangen en deugd. Hij sprak hem van de hevige ontsteltenis die de voelende doormaakt als zijn oog een gelijkenis van de eeuwige schoonheid ontwaart; hij sprak hem van de begeerten van de ongewijde en slechte, die de schoonheid niet kan denken wanneer hij haar beeld ziet en niet tot eerbied in staat is; sprak van de heilige vrees die de edele bevangt wanneer een godgelijkend gelaat, een volmaakte lichaamsgestalte voor hem verschijnt, - hoe hij dan begint te beven en zichzelf niet meer meester is en het nauwelijks waagt te kijken en hem vereert die de schoonheid heeft, ja, aan hem zou offeren als aan een godsbeeld als hij niet moest vrezen in de ogen der mensen een dwaas te schijnen. Want de schoonheid, mijn Phaidros, alleen zij is beminnenswaardig en zichtbaar tegelijk: ze is, onthoud dat goed! de enige vorm van het geestelijke die wij met de zinnen ontvangen, met de zinnen verdragen kunnen.”

Dit is een citaat uit De dood in Venetië. Het toeval wil dat ik onlangs een poging heb gewaagd Platonische Liefde te lezen waarin is opgenomen Symposium en Phaedrus (hier vertaalt als Phaidros). Deze novelle van Mann over schoonheid en verval is duidelijk geïnspireerd op Plato’s verhalen. Maar het verhaal zegt misschien ook iets over Thomas Mann zelf die naar ik ergens las worstelde met zijn homoseksualiteit.

Het boek stamt uit 1911. Deze vertaling maakt het soms wat lastig leesbaar (het citaat is er een voorbeeld van). Een nieuwe vertaling zou het verhaal zeker ten goede komen.

review.text: bu yil buddenbrooklar'i okumak istiyorum, mann ile bir an once tanismak istedim bu yuzden. venedik'te ölüm rahatsiz edici bir uzun oyku. meslegi yazarlik olan aschenbach'in kucuk bir cocuk olan tadzio'ya duydugu ilgiyi okuyoruz. ben sevdim bu metni cunku mann, aschenbach'i yuceltmiyor ve romantiklestirmiyor. tam aksine ahlaki pusulasini kaybetmis, acinasi ve kuculen biri haline getiriyor. bir yerden sonra aschenbach artik yazan, ureten biri degil; bakan, izleyen ve donup kalan bir figur. metnin sonlarina dogru aschenbach phaidros'a hitap ediyor ama onu ters yuz edici sekilde. mann'in platon'a yaniliyorsun deme sekliydi bu sanirim ve benim icin cok vurucuydu o kismi. plaidros guzelligin ruhu yukari tasiyacagini soyler. aschenbach'in guzellik sanrisi ise onu ucuruma goturur.

review.text: The title gives it all away, doesn't it - can a title be a spoiler? The book is based at least in part on Thomas Mann's own experience in Venice during the 1911 cholera epidemic in the city. Gustav von Aschenbach is an aging German writer who travels to Venice hoping to find some inspiration but instead develops a deep, silent obsession with a beautiful Polish boy named Tadzio. I found this book really difficult to read as part of me found it rather disturbing and sinister that a middle-aged / older man would feel this way about a boy he's never met or spoken to. There's far too much description and not enough story, there's introspection but I don't recall von Aschenbach channelling his 'passion' for Tadzio into creativity.
review.text_raw:
The title gives it all away, doesn't it - can a title be a spoiler?

The book is based at least in part on Thomas Mann's own experience in Venice during the 1911 cholera epidemic in the city.

Gustav von Aschenbach is an aging German writer who travels to Venice hoping to find some inspiration but instead develops a deep, silent obsession with a beautiful Polish boy named Tadzio.

I found this book really difficult to read as part of me found it rather disturbing and sinister that a middle-aged / older man would feel this way about a boy he's never met or spoken to. There's far too much description and not enough story, there's introspection but I don't recall von Aschenbach channelling his 'passion' for Tadzio into creativity.

review.text: "Der Tod in Venedig" ist das erste Buch von Thomas Mann, das ich gelesen habe und soweit ich es beurteilen kann ein guter Einstieg in sein Werk. Darüber, dass es sich um ein literarisch wahnsinnig gelungenes Werk handelt und dass beim Lesen ersichtlich wird wie jedes Detail von Mann genaustens geplant wurde, muss ich wahrscheinlich nicht viel sagen. Der Schreibstil macht es allerdings auch wirklich nicht einfach, sich dieses Buch zu erschließen und hat bei mir auch dafür gesorgt, dass das Lesen selber kein großer Genuss, sondern eher Arbeit war. Gleichzeitig hat es Spaß gemacht, sich der intellektuellen Herausforderung dieses Buches zu stellen: Das konzentrierte Lesen von zutiefst verschachtelten Sätzen, die nicht enden wollen, das Ausdeuten von subtilen und weniger subtilen Anspielungen, das Recherchieren von griechischer Mythologie, auf die immer wieder Bezug genommen wird. Interessant an der Handlung des Buches ist meiner Meinung nach nicht das "was" - das ist ziemlich schnell und einfach erzählt, sondern viel mehr das "wie": Mann gelingt es, sowohl die sinnlichen Empfindungen einer (äußerst fragwürdigen) intensiven Attraktion authentisch zu vermitteln, und diese gleichzeitig zur "Tragödie einer Entwürdigung" (der von Mann gewählte Titel für die Novelle) zu machen. Für mich war es eines dieser Bücher, das in mir die Lust geweckt hat, tiefer zu graben: Mich mit dem Autor auseinanderzusetzen, einen Podcast über das Buch zu hören, um die Eindrücke anderer Menschen auf mich wirken zu lassen und trotz der Anstrengung, die ich während dem Lesen der Novelle empfunden habe, blicke ich mit einer gewissen Vorfreude auf meine nächste Mann-Lektüre.

Gleichzeitig hat es Spaß gemacht, sich der intellektuellen Herausforderung dieses Buches zu stellen: Das konzentrierte Lesen von zutiefst verschachtelten Sätzen, die nicht enden wollen, das Ausdeuten von subtilen und weniger subtilen Anspielungen, das Recherchieren von griechischer Mythologie, auf die immer wieder Bezug genommen wird.

Interessant an der Handlung des Buches ist meiner Meinung nach nicht das "was" - das ist ziemlich schnell und einfach erzählt, sondern viel mehr das "wie": Mann gelingt es, sowohl die sinnlichen Empfindungen einer (äußerst fragwürdigen) intensiven Attraktion authentisch zu vermitteln, und diese gleichzeitig zur "Tragödie einer Entwürdigung" (der von Mann gewählte Titel für die Novelle) zu machen.

Für mich war es eines dieser Bücher, das in mir die Lust geweckt hat, tiefer zu graben: Mich mit dem Autor auseinanderzusetzen, einen Podcast über das Buch zu hören, um die Eindrücke anderer Menschen auf mich wirken zu lassen und trotz der Anstrengung, die ich während dem Lesen der Novelle empfunden habe, blicke ich mit einer gewissen Vorfreude auf meine nächste Mann-Lektüre.

review.text: Μια καλοκαιρινή αύρα Μεσογείου. Η Σοφία Αρβανίτη που τραγουδά: «Μη μου μιλάς για καλοκαίρια». Ο Πιρς Μπρόσναν με floral πουκάμισο και καπέλο Panama στο «Τελευταίο κόλπο». Μικρές κύριες που ακολουθούν το savoir vivre. Ένας συγγραφέας σαν μια άλλη Σωσώ από τα εγκλήματα που όλα του φταίνε, αλλά με όλα είναι καλά!! Φεύγει από τη Γερμανία για ένα ταξίδι στη Βενετία. Ένα πυκνογραμμένο - από άποψη νοημάτων και αλληγοριών - κείμενο. Worth reading

review.text: Tristan Richard Wagner saw the premier of his revolutionary opera Tristan und Isolde at the Königliches Hof- und Nationaltheater in Munich on 10 June 1865 with Hans von Bülow conducting. It was revolutionary for the music was unlike any the audience had heard before; specifically the "Tristan chord" with which the opera begins and which remains unresolved until the final moments of the opera, and marked the beginning of a new age of music that would see the rise of composers from Mahler to Debussy, and Schoenberg with the second Viennese circle. But this music, and the philosophy of Arthur Schopenhauer with which it is imbued, influenced the artistic world beyond music. One of those influenced was a young author from northern Germany who, at the age of twenty-six in 1901, had just published a major family saga and a handful of short stories. This author, Thomas Mann, would write a novella entitled Tristan, setting it in a sanatorium called Einfried, "Enclosure", beside which "the mountains, massive, fir green, and softly rugged, tower toward the heavens." Mann uses music as an integral part of this short story about a middle-class woman, Gabrielle Kloterjahn, who comes under the spell of a writer, Detlev Spinell, who in addition to his writing (he had published one book) was an affable, affectionate, and even enthusiastic aesthete. He was often "carried away in sheer admiration for something beautiful: the harmony of two colors, a vase with a noble shape, the mountains illuminated by the sunset." His response would be simply "How beautiful!" While he is a vain and pompous man, he is capable of great influence with is intense pursuit of his own aesthetic purity. Gabrielle found herself alone in the sanatorium as her burger husband had departed and she was interested to find that there was a "writer" present for she "had never before met a writer face-to-face." It was not long before Herr Spinell was socializing with her and moved quickly from being merely "helpful" to being "devoted" to her. For Gabrielle was an artist herself, as a amateur musician who played the piano. She is at Einfried to rest and recover from a general malaise and weakness following giving birth to a child. She was prescribed a rest cure as part of her potential return to health. While this precluded playing the piano she could not resist the insistence of the charming Spinell to play the piano for him. What harm could there be in yielding to the enjoyment of a simple, yet beautiful, nocturne by Frederic Chopin. This moment that seems so innocent is ironically the moment when the story turns; when the yearning of Gabrielle for something beyond reality, beyond "mere appearances", that has been suggested by her conversations with Her Spinell, becomes something much darker. Mann is not subtle with the coming of sunset yielding phrases like "darkness is already setting in." And Gabrielle observing that "yesterday we still had broad daylight at this time; and now it's already dusk." Thus playing a nocturne is quite appropriate, but she moves on to play another and another. Then Spinell offers her a piano transcription of Wagner's liebestod music from Tristan und Isolde. "the yearning motif, a lonesome and wandering voice in the night, softly utters its anxious question. A stillness and a waiting. And lo, a response: the same timid and lonesome strain, only clearer, only more delicate. Another hush. And now, with that muted and wonderful sforzando, which is like passion rousing itself and blissfully flaring up, the love motif emerged, ascended, rapturously struggled upward to sweet interlacing, sank back, dissolving, and, with their deep crooning of grave and painful ecstasy, the cellos came to the fore and carried the melody away . . . ." This moment, this music, is the signal that Gabrielle will not recover, that the love she and Spinell have will only last till her death. Her husband is asked to return and, is presented with a strange letter written by Spinell to Herr Kloterjahn, a letter in which Spinell describes his vision of beauty as experienced in and with Gabrielle, but also condemns Herr Kloterjahn as the enemy, the antithesis of true beauty and love. Herr Kloterjahn really has no idea what Spinell means, yet Spinell is also a sickly example, a pale imitation of the true aesthete. The beauty of Wagner's magnificent motif merging Eros and Thanatos is wasted on the merely melodramatic and overwrought pair. The novella ends not just with the death of Gabrielle, but also with Spinell trying to mentally escape from the aesthetic moment he had experienced at Einfried.

Tristan

It was revolutionary for the music was unlike any the audience had heard before; specifically the "Tristan chord" with which the opera begins and which remains unresolved until the final moments of the opera, and marked the beginning of a new age of music that would see the rise of composers from Mahler to Debussy, and Schoenberg with the second Viennese circle.

But this music, and the philosophy of Arthur Schopenhauer with which it is imbued, influenced the artistic world beyond music. One of those influenced was a young author from northern Germany who, at the age of twenty-six in 1901, had just published a major family saga and a handful of short stories. This author, Thomas Mann, would write a novella entitled Tristan, setting it in a sanatorium called Einfried, "Enclosure", beside which "the mountains, massive, fir green, and softly rugged, tower toward the heavens."

This moment that seems so innocent is ironically the moment when the story turns; when the yearning of Gabrielle for something beyond reality, beyond "mere appearances", that has been suggested by her conversations with Her Spinell, becomes something much darker. "the yearning motif, a lonesome and wandering voice in the night, softly utters its anxious question. A stillness and a waiting. review.text: On the second page of this fine short novel the protagonist, Gustave von Aschenbach, goes on a walk to "refresh" himself and soon finds himself in a cemetery whose mortuary is "a structure in the Byzantine style". Like a wind from the East the place mesmerizes him with mystical symbols until he is "brought back to reality by the sight of a man standing in the portico". This man presents an exotic visage with red hair and represents a motif that will recur several times during the story. The image of this man, perhaps, leads Aschenbach to a simple longing for travel and then a hallucination that suggests the impulse of Eros or the throes of Dionysus. Whichever it is the setting is ominous as we are reminded that "his life was on the wane" and he plans to travel south on a journey. The narrative takes the writer Gustav von Aschenbach to Venice, where he falls in love with an adolescent boy before subsequently dying in the cholera-stricken city. Mann’s masterly command of language and play with mythology, his psychological profile of the artistic mind, and the novella’s contrast between cold artistic discipline and the power of Eros is magnificent both in its form and substance. Aschenbach is introduced as an esteemed author who has produced literary works known for their formalism and neo-classical style. He has chosen an ascetic, disciplined life, a life of “noble purity, simplicity and symmetry”, for the sake of his creativity, success and national reputation. At the beginning of Death in Venice, we find the fifty-three year old writer unable to write a perfectly balanced work. The walk he takes at the beginning of the narrative occurs in an unnamed town that can be identified as Munich. The year, presented in the text as “19—”, is actually 1911. Since Mann opted not to provide a precise date, the narrative contains a timeless, ahistorical dimension despite being grounded in contemporary events. In the figure of a stranger whom Aschenbach sees at the mortuary, Mann alludes to medieval personifications of death, and also to the Greek god Hermes, the guide to the Underworld. But the messenger of death is also a messenger of life. The text links him to the cult of life and the god of Asian origins, Dionysus. Mann's original intention was to write a treatise on the Nietzschean contrast between the god of reason, Apollo, and the god of unreason, Dionysus. In his description of Aschenbach’s journey into Venice, Mann includes encounters with a Charon-like figure, and an old queen of a man bereft of dignity. These characters echo the original man he met in the cemetery and serve as messengers signalling Aschenbach’s looming fate, and as conspicuous representations of the transience and ugliness of life. The Venice depicted by Mann is "the fallen queen, flattering and dubious beauty . . . half fairy tale, half tourist trap". It is a vision presented in its sordid reality and in its mythical splendor. At the hotel Aschenbach catches sight of a beautiful, fourteen-year-old Polish boy named Tadzio who is vacationing with his family. Aschenbach is immediately attracted to his idealized perfection, comparing him to a Greek statue and an artistic masterpiece. Although the sultry air of Venice makes him feel unwell, he reverses his intention to leave the city. From now on, his life is controlled by Eros, his desire, as he continues to observe Tadzio. With references to the Platonic idea that physical attraction inspired by Eros leads to spiritual knowledge, Mann diverts readers from the fact that Aschenbach’s attraction to Tadzio is primarily physical, not metaphysical. The ability of Thomas Mann to weave together character and theme and setting to achieve this perfection is uncanny and I do not believe he achieved any better in his longer fictions, great as they are. This is also one of the few novels that received a superlative treatment on film though, in the end, Visconti's film does not surpass the original.

review.text: Right from its first sentence, Death in Venice emits ominous vibes. Of course, with a title like that what did I expect? The main character of this novella (70 pages) is Gustav von Aschenbach. He is an aging poet, the offspring of a father who is a businessman and a mother who is passionate. "That marriage between businesslike, spartan sedulity and darker, more fiery impulses had created an artist, and this artist in particular." One day Gustav sees a foreigner with a fiery red beard (perhaps the devil?) who ignites his desire to leave cold Germany and 'go south'. He eventually arrives in Venice. At breakfast one morning he observes a Polish family. Of four children, one is a boy, about 14, who attracts Gustav's attention "With astonishment Aschenbach noticed that the boy was perfectly beautiful." Sparknotes, in its summary of the book, describes the ending as follows: "(Gustav) becomes progressively daring in his pursuit of the boy, gradually becoming more and more debased, until he finally dies of the cholera, degraded, a slave to his passions, stripped of his dignity." Thomas Mann is a German author, writing in the time of Hitler. This novella depicts his highly symbolic insight into the psychology of the artist and the intellectual. A worthy read, but painful. 3 stars

"That marriage between businesslike, spartan sedulity and darker, more fiery impulses had created an artist, and this artist in particular."

One day Gustav sees a foreigner with a fiery red beard (perhaps the devil?) who ignites his desire to leave cold Germany and 'go south'.

Thomas Mann is a German author, writing in the time of Hitler. This novella depicts his highly symbolic insight into the psychology of the artist and the intellectual.

A worthy read, but painful.

3 stars

review.text: Bello, complicado y arriesgado. “Aschenbach llevaba ya varios minutos de seria distracción descifrando esas fórmulas y dejando que las miradas de su espíritu se perdiesen en las transparencias de aquel misticismo cuando, volviendo de su ensoñación, divisó en el pórtico, por encima de las dos bestias apocalípticas que vigilaban la escalera, a un hombre cuyo inusitado aspecto marcó un rumbo totalmente distinto a sus pensamientos.” La Muerte en Venecia es un libro muy corto, de seguramente alrededor de 100 páginas, que, sin embargo, no se lee rápido ni fluido puesto que tiene una narración no solo enrevesada y con palabras muy peculiares (y lo digo en serio, en más de una ocasión he tenido que frenar la lectura y hacer uso del diccionario), con pocos diálogos y muchas descripciones, sino que además hace énfasis en un montón de referencias a otros aspectos del arte. Y es seguro que muchos me pasaron desapercibidos. La historia no es su fuerte y tal vez no sea tan memorable para algunos. El libro se enfoca totalmente en el protagonista, su viaje, y todo lo que observa durante el mismo, y hasta su encuentro con un chico de temprana edad con el que se obsesiona y al que comienza a seguir allá a donde vaya. No es un libro erótico ni romántico ni mucho menos el típico M/M, sino una narrativa detallada del choque que representa el observarse a uno mismo y encontrarse con que uno ya no es lo que era antes. Es una historia sobre nuestro propio envejecimiento, y el miedo que tenemos de la percepción de los demás hacia nosotros. O al menos así lo interpreto yo. El final, que, aunque abrupto, no podía ser de otra forma, es satisfactorio para la travesía en la que nos embarcamos al abrir por primera vez este libro, y consigue que sintamos el dolor que sufre su protagonista desde mucho antes de comenzar su viaje. Los más puristas de la literatura, que, en mi opinión, ni siquiera deberían de existir, pueden leer esta obra sin preocupaciones: no encontrarán ningún tipo de “contenido inapropiado”. “Las observaciones y vivencias del solitario taciturno son a la vez más borrosas y penetrantes que las del hombre sociable, y sus pensamientos, más graves, extraños nunca exentos de cierto halo de tristeza.” Lo Mejor: Una de las prosas más bellas que he leído. Lo Peor: No es una lectura fácil, y es claro que muchas referencias me pasaron desapercibidas. Eso es más mi culpa que del autor, claramente.
review.text_raw:
Bello, complicado y arriesgado.

“Aschenbach llevaba ya varios minutos de seria distracción descifrando esas fórmulas y dejando que las miradas de su espíritu se perdiesen en las transparencias de aquel misticismo cuando, volviendo de su ensoñación, divisó en el pórtico, por encima de las dos bestias apocalípticas que vigilaban la escalera, a un hombre cuyo inusitado aspecto marcó un rumbo totalmente distinto a sus pensamientos.”

La Muerte en Venecia es un libro muy corto, de seguramente alrededor de 100 páginas, que, sin embargo, no se lee rápido ni fluido puesto que tiene una narración no solo enrevesada y con palabras muy peculiares (y lo digo en serio, en más de una ocasión he tenido que frenar la lectura y hacer uso del diccionario), con pocos diálogos y muchas descripciones, sino que además hace énfasis en un montón de referencias a otros aspectos del arte. Y es seguro que muchos me pasaron desapercibidos.

La historia no es su fuerte y tal vez no sea tan memorable para algunos. El libro se enfoca totalmente en el protagonista, su viaje, y todo lo que observa durante el mismo, y hasta su encuentro con un chico de temprana edad con el que se obsesiona y al que comienza a seguir allá a donde vaya.
No es un libro erótico ni romántico ni mucho menos el típico M/M, sino una narrativa detallada del choque que representa el observarse a uno mismo y encontrarse con que uno ya no es lo que era antes. Es una historia sobre nuestro propio envejecimiento, y el miedo que tenemos de la percepción de los demás hacia nosotros. O al menos así lo interpreto yo.

El final, que, aunque abrupto, no podía ser de otra forma, es satisfactorio para la travesía en la que nos embarcamos al abrir por primera vez este libro, y consigue que sintamos el dolor que sufre su protagonista desde mucho antes de comenzar su viaje.

Los más puristas de la literatura, que, en mi opinión, ni siquiera deberían de existir, pueden leer esta obra sin preocupaciones: no encontrarán ningún tipo de “contenido inapropiado”.

“Las observaciones y vivencias del solitario taciturno son a la vez más borrosas y penetrantes que las del hombre sociable, y sus pensamientos, más graves, extraños nunca exentos de cierto halo de tristeza.”

Lo Mejor: Una de las prosas más bellas que he leído.

Lo Peor: No es una lectura fácil, y es claro que muchas referencias me pasaron desapercibidas. Eso es más mi culpa que del autor, claramente.

review.text: Aunque el propio autor avisaba que el argumento de esta historia es sobre <>, y no hay ninguna escena desagradable, más allá de un sueño erótico del protagonista, no me ha gustado demasiado. La obsesión que alcanza este hombre por un adolescente roza lo maníaco, lo lascivo, no perdiendo de vista ni un momento al muchacho, acechándole por toda la ciudad. No obstante, el autor deja que el libro fluya, y obviamente rechaza cualquier comportamiento que tenga que ver con la pederastia y lo condena. Aún así, se me ha quedado corto: me hubiera gustado saber la parte contraria, cómo se sentía Tadzio, qué pensaba su familia de ese acosador anciano. Es cierto que se cita un par de veces el hecho de que la familia lo mira mal, pero esa parte se queda corta. No me parece que el castigo que el protagonista recibe sea el justo, porque no tiene nada que ver con el hecho de fantasear y perseguir, no solo con la mirada como en un principio, sino físicamente después, al chico. Aún así, he visto la descripción de esa degradación de la que hablaba el Mann, muy obvia y certera, pero esperaba más de la sociedad, de los adultos que rodeaban a Tadzio y Aschenbach en Venecia. Necesitaba que alguien hiciera algo al respecto. La lectura de esta novela es rápida, no solo por su brevedad, apenas poco más de cien páginas, sino por la rapidez de los acontecimientos. Durante los dos primeros capítulos, la lectura empieza algo densa y filosófica, pero al ser cada capítulo más bien corto, no se hace muy pesado. En esta primera parte del libro conocemos al protagonista, Gustavo von Aschenbach, y lo que ha sido su vida hasta ahora. Ya después de esta pequeña introducción, comienza la historia que Mann nos quiere contar, y la lectura se hace más fluida e interesante. Tras leer La montaña mágica, del mismo autor, creía que este libro me iba a gustar también, que iba a disfrutar de lectura tanto como con la otra. No obstante me ha decepcionado bastante, no me parece que esté a la altura de esa maravillosa obra.

review.text: 3.5 "Önemli bir fikir veriminin derhal geniş ve derin bir etki yaratabilmesi için, eser sahibinin kişisel hayatıyla çağdaş kuşağın genel kaderi arasında gizli bir yakınlık, hatta bir uyum bulunmalıdır. İnsanlar bir sanat eserini niçin şöhrete eriştirdiklerini bilmezler. Sanat anlayışından yoksun, eserde bunca ilgiyi haklı gösterecek yüzlerce üstünlük bulduklarını düşünürler ama alkışın asıl nedeni, tartıya gelmeyen bir şeydir: yakınlık duygusu!" Sf:23 "Birbirleriyle sadece göz aşınası olan, her gün, hatta her saat karşılaştıkları, birbirlerini inceledikleri halde, adetlerin hükmüne ya da kendi kuruntularına tabi olarak ne selam ne konuşma, görünüşte kayıtsız bir yabancılığı devam ettirmek zorunda kalan insanlar arasındaki ilişkiden daha garip, daha nazik bir şey olur mu? Aralarında bir huzursuzluk, hastalık derecesinde bir merak, tanışmak ve fikir alışverişi ihtiyacının tatmin edilmemiş, yapay bir şekilde bastırılmış olmasından doğan bir isteri, özellikle bir tür gergin bir dikkat havası eser. Çünkü insan insanı, hakkında bir yargıda bulunamadığı sürece sever, yüceltir; özlem, eksik tanımanın bir sonucudur." Sf:72

"Önemli bir fikir veriminin derhal geniş ve derin bir etki yaratabilmesi için, eser sahibinin kişisel hayatıyla çağdaş kuşağın genel kaderi arasında gizli bir yakınlık, hatta bir uyum bulunmalıdır. İnsanlar bir sanat eserini niçin şöhrete eriştirdiklerini bilmezler. Sanat anlayışından yoksun, eserde bunca ilgiyi haklı gösterecek yüzlerce üstünlük bulduklarını düşünürler ama alkışın asıl nedeni, tartıya gelmeyen bir şeydir: yakınlık duygusu!" Sf:23

"Birbirleriyle sadece göz aşınası olan, her gün, hatta her saat karşılaştıkları, birbirlerini inceledikleri halde, adetlerin hükmüne ya da kendi kuruntularına tabi olarak ne selam ne konuşma, görünüşte kayıtsız bir yabancılığı devam ettirmek zorunda kalan insanlar arasındaki ilişkiden daha garip, daha nazik bir şey olur mu? Aralarında bir huzursuzluk, hastalık derecesinde bir merak, tanışmak ve fikir alışverişi ihtiyacının tatmin edilmemiş, yapay bir şekilde bastırılmış olmasından doğan bir isteri, özellikle bir tür gergin bir dikkat havası eser. Çünkü insan insanı, hakkında bir yargıda bulunamadığı sürece sever, yüceltir; özlem, eksik tanımanın bir sonucudur." Sf:72

review.text: Feels to me like Mann has condensed the entire life of the artist into this short book. It’s almost as if he is letting us watch Aschenbach’s life flash before his eyes just before he dies, but metaphorically. You’ve got the paradisiacal aspects of constantly being inspired by your work (Venice), some grotesque stuff, too; and tedium, and self-doubt, and lonely inconstancy, but above all -- living in the presence of raw beauty before one has had a chance to sculpt it into a form that an audience can appreciate (Tadzio). Beyond this Mann provides more explicit philosophical problems in his expositions of mythology and Classical antiquity. I’d love to delineate here exactly what Mann tells us, but I’m too lazy to read the work again; and that is what this book is for in the end, anyway. So better to leave it to Mann himself. I would say though that how he manages the tension in the story strikes me as illustrative. Almost all of the edge in this work comes out of the title. If the title had not told me that death was hovering over this tale, I might not have known it. However, because he told me in the title I was cringing all the way through. Who dies? No, not the boy! Is it Venice itself through the malady? The last person I expected was the protagonist himself. Marvelous! From this I deduce Mann’s most dramatic description of the life of the artist -- That though the artist is obsessed and exalted by beauty, always … always death is right there, too, next to him, over his shoulder, heightening beauty’s potency, even giving beauty its meaning. Important to note that Mann seems to see ‘beauty’ and ‘the divine’ as interchangeable ideas. I think that red-headed dude with the bast hat at the beginning is the grim reaper.

review.text: Morte em Veneza "A felicidade do escritor é o pensamento que se quer transformar totalmente em sensação e a sensação que se quer transformar em pensamento." (79) Este livro aborda a história de um escritor, Aschenbach, que já tem alguma idade. Este vai de férias, vai à procura de alguma calma, de alguma espiritualidade e até chega mesmo a desistir do seu destino. Ao chegar a Veneza encontra algo que não estava à espera, encontra o conceito de beleza e de morte. Mas aqui a beleza centra-se na beleza da juventude. Não sei se estarei errada, mas foi a leitura que fiz. O escritor acaba por ter uma paixão platónica por um jovem rapaz, Tadzio, de quem lemos longas descrições físicas e da sua roupa. Penso que num todo, o escritor queria a beleza da juventude. Aschenbach sentia-se cansado e não queria esse cansaço de um ser já mais velho. O que ambicionava era mesmo a beleza da juventude eterna. Às vezes entusiasmo-me a escrever e sinto que posso estar a dizer asneiras! 😅 "As observações e as vivências do solitário que só fala consigo próprio são simultaneamente mais indistintas e intensas do que as do homem social (...)" (42) Adorei as descrições de Thomas Mann, a maneira como ele descreve as coisas, cidades, horizontes é completamente mágica. Veneza deve ser realmente bonita, mas se a visitarmos acompanhados das descrições deste autor ainda se deve tornar mais bonita. Eu nunca visitei, se algum dia o fizer, este livro vai comigo. Leiam este livro, Thomas Mann tem mesmo que ser lido. 4🌟
review.text_raw:
Morte em Veneza
"A felicidade do escritor é o pensamento que se quer transformar totalmente em sensação e a sensação que se quer transformar em pensamento." (79)

Este livro aborda a história de um escritor, Aschenbach, que já tem alguma idade. Este vai de férias, vai à procura de alguma calma, de alguma espiritualidade e até chega mesmo a desistir do seu destino. Ao chegar a Veneza encontra algo que não estava à espera, encontra o conceito de beleza e de morte. Mas aqui a beleza centra-se na beleza da juventude. Não sei se estarei errada, mas foi a leitura que fiz.
O escritor acaba por ter uma paixão platónica por um jovem rapaz, Tadzio, de quem lemos longas descrições físicas e da sua roupa. Penso que num todo, o escritor queria a beleza da juventude. Aschenbach sentia-se cansado e não queria esse cansaço de um ser já mais velho. O que ambicionava era mesmo a beleza da juventude eterna.
Às vezes entusiasmo-me a escrever e sinto que posso estar a dizer asneiras! 😅

"As observações e as vivências do solitário que só fala consigo próprio são simultaneamente mais indistintas e intensas do que as do homem social (...)" (42)

Adorei as descrições de Thomas Mann, a maneira como ele descreve as coisas, cidades, horizontes é completamente mágica. Veneza deve ser realmente bonita, mas se a visitarmos acompanhados das descrições deste autor ainda se deve tornar mais bonita. Eu nunca visitei, se algum dia o fizer, este livro vai comigo.

Leiam este livro, Thomas Mann tem mesmo que ser lido. 4🌟

review.text: Pese a su brevedad, por sus complejas reflexiones filosóficas no resulta ésta una lectura amena. Mann se veía muy inspirado por las ideas de grandes pensadores; más concretamente, en esta ocasión, de las ideas de Platón y de Nietzsche. Para el lector poco formado en filosofía que quiera enfrentarse a esta corta novela, o se tiene la suerte de disponer de una edición con un completo prólogo o resulta indispensable revisar antes algún artículo ensayístico sobre el texto. Pues conviene entender, entre otros, conceptos como la dicotomía nietzscheana de lo apolíneo y lo dionisíaco para un mayor disfrute y entendimiento del texto de Mann. A mí me ha costado, no lo voy a negar. Y eso que me encantó "La montaña mágica", con las tediosas reflexiones de Settembrini y de Naphta; pero éstas se leen con agrado, apoyadas por la ironía de la voz narradora o las ingeniosas réplicas del personaje de Hans. No sucede lo mismo, por desgracia, en "Muerte en Venecia", tratándose de una lectura más breve pero también más exigente. Sea como sea, la magnífica prosa de Mann -cuando no se pierde en exceso en sus disquisiciones filosóficas- y, sobre todo, el buen desenlace arreglan bastante las cosas. Por lo demás, aquel que prefiera quedarse en una historia de amor homosexual y pederasta imposible, se queda en una lectura totalmente superficial de esta obra. Habiendo valido la pena en un primer acercamiento, sin duda creo que este libro ganará mucho en una relectura. Hasta pronto, querido Thomas.

review.text: Aschenbach es un afamado escritor que busca unas pacíficas vacaciones en algún lugar lejos de su rutina. Acaba en un hotel de Venecia, donde sus enredados canales y pasadizos son su mayor atractivo. O no. Aschenbach no puede retirar los ojos de un muchacho polaco rubio, que le transmite una belleza cautivadora siempre que le echa un vistazo. Tarda en arrancar este libro de Mann, que se abre con un lento transcurrir, hasta que toma dos virajes inesperados. El primero, cuando Aschenbach ve aparecer a Tadrio. Desde ese encuentro, para el escritor Tadrio se convierte en una obsesión y busca tenerlo constantemente en su enfoque. Admira cada parte del físico del joven polaco rubio y llama con fuerza a su imparable sentido estético. El segundo giro es la epidemia de peste que cubre silenciosa e implacable toda Venecia. Y aquí se conjura la verdadera batalla de la obra, que es la fuerza de la belleza contra la imparable degradación de la misma, simbolizada en cada paisaje de la ciudad italiana, que se debate entre su atractivo y el bochorno infeccioso que la acompaña. El libro aborda constantemente el tema de la estética, desde el Fedón hasta cada descripción del muchacho. Aschenbach se convierte en un devenir pasivo ante tales pasiones, y todo no es sino una lucha contra la aceptación de sí mismo en un mundo al borde de la retirada.

review.text: There are some wonderful short stories in this collection, but the real meat of the book is, of course, the title novella, Death in Venice . I'm in no way diminishing the other stories and highly recommend them but still, moving right along... Gustav von Aschenbach is a middle-aged writer who decides he needs to do a little traveling to find self-fulfillment and chooses Venice as his destination. While there he falls in love with (or obsessed with, it's all a very thin line) a young Polish boy, Tadzio; Aschenbach tells himself that his interest in the boy is merely creative fascination and nothing more than that. Uh huh . As time passes and his interest in the boy grows, Aschenbach battles his own sexual appetite and his own mortality. Fearing the aging process Aschenbach has himself dolled up to look younger, in actuality making himself look exactly as he would have despised in the beginning of the story, but his own passion overrides what he is trying to become. Not just another gay story, the relationship between Aschenbach and Tadzio is actually quite beautiful and heartwrenching. I found myself cheering for the dirty old man chasing down the little boy. Which then in turn made me feel dirty. But really... it's a wonderful story.

review.text: Mann per me è un diesel. Parte in sordina, a tratti mi annoia e mi appesantisce. Poi pian piano scatta qualcosa che infiamma. E l'attenzione si desta. Venezia è una città che ispira alla meditazione e alla malinconia Affascina per i suoi angoli, per l'acqua che la lambisce, per i colori che la avvolgono. L'atmosfera è eterna, il tempo si ferma ed è qui che il nostro Aschenbach vive il suo piccolo momento di estasi. Ho rivissuto la stessa sensazione di familiarità che ho trovato anche in Fondamenta degli incurabili . Il desiderio del protagonista è un languore, per la città, per un sentimento che non comprende fino in fondo, che lo tormenta e che al tempo stesso lo galvanizza. Venezia è amore e morte per Aschenbach, amore per il piccolo Tadzio e morte per il suo corpo, ma nonostante questo il protagonista non si tira indietro, sa che sta arrivando. E per quei piccoli momenti di deliquio in compagnia (a distanza) del giovane fanciullo polacco, rischia e soccombe. Mi è piaciuto. Molto. " Riposare nella perfezione è il sogno di chi si affatica per giungere all'eccellenza."

review.text: (Breve parere scritto come parte della Reading Challange 2020 del gruppo Il Circolo della Lettura ) Premessa obbligatoria: ADORO il film di Visconti. Adoro il suo essere così contemplativo e sfuggente, il suo protrarsi in 2 ore di sguardi, inseguimenti furtivi, silenzi. Ero a conoscenza che l'adattamento della novella di Mann per il cinema è dovuta scendere a parecchi compromessi e finalmente leggendolo posso capirne il perchè. Pur nella sua brevità, La Morte A Venezia è un viaggio intimo nella mente di un uomo che sente il peso della vecchiaia, del tempo che passa, dell'invidia per i giovani e il loro totale abbandono alle passioni, pieni di un inesperienza che di fatto è vita, scoperta, sorpresa. Ed è per questo che, pur non amando lo stile tronfio e barocco dell'autore, non ho potuto fare altro che abbandonarmi tra le sue pagine, sentire 40 anni in più di quelli che ho e vivere con trasporto anche la sua storia d'amore maledetta, mai palesata, di fatto impossibile. Il finale è perfetto, ed è forse l'unica parte a cui il film, seppur gestendolo in modo egregio, non riesce a dar giustizia. Leggetene tutti! ★★��★☆

review.text: Ο θάνατος στη Βενετία. Τόμας Μαν Ένας έρωτας βιωμένος εκβιαστικώς πλατωνικά και κατ' αποκλειστικότητα με εσωτερικοποίηση της εγκόσμιας ορμής του, προφυλαγμένος στο υποχρεωτικό περιθωριακό πλαίσιο που ανήκει λόγω των συνθηκών του, καταδικασμένος από την υπέρβαση των ορίων, για να τελειωθεί αυτοβούλως κατόπιν συναίνεσης του ώριμου- εκ των δυο μερών- συναισθηματικού φορέα παραδιδόμενος στον θάνατο. Όταν πρόκειται για τον Τόμας Μαν τα λόγια περιττεύουν και αστοχούν στην όποια προσπάθεια παρουσίασης του έργου του. A love lived under duress, compelled to remain purely platonic and inward, its worldly desire suppressed and internalised; a feeling confined within the margins imposed by circumstance, destined for ruin once those boundaries are transgressed. It reaches its end through a voluntary surrender, as the more mature of the two emotional participants consents to his own demise. When it comes to Thomas Mann, words seem both superfluous and inadequate in any attempt to present or interpret his work.

Όταν πρόκειται για τον Τόμας Μαν τα λόγια περιττεύουν και αστοχούν στην όποια προσπάθεια παρουσίασης του έργου του.



A love lived under duress, compelled to remain purely platonic and inward, its worldly desire suppressed and internalised; a feeling confined within the margins imposed by circumstance, destined for ruin once those boundaries are transgressed. It reaches its end through a voluntary surrender, as the more mature of the two emotional participants consents to his own demise.

When it comes to Thomas Mann, words seem both superfluous and inadequate in any attempt to present or interpret his work.

review.text: Very intringuing read, especially the way it intertwined the disease plot with the 'love' plot. This was definitely my favourite part, which is a shame since it only started very late in the novella. It definitely is one of those stories that can be analysed extensively and have many interpretations, so reading it in class would be the preferable way to experience and enjoy the book. "Do you see now perhaps why we writers can be neither wise nor dignified? That we necessarily go astray, necessarily remain dissolute emotional adventurers? The magisterial poise of our style is a lie and a farce, our fame and social position are an absurdity, the public’s faith in us is altogether ridiculous, the use of art to educate the nation and its youth is a reprehensible undertaking which should be forbidden by law. For how can one be fit to be an educator when one has been born with an incorrigible and natural tendency toward the abyss?"

review.text: 4,5🌟 "Nada resultaba más extraño ni más irritante que las relaciones que se establecen entre hombres que sólo se conocen de vista, que diariamente, a todas horas, se tropiezan, se observan, viéndose obligados, por la etiqueta o por capricho a no saludarse ni cruzar palabra, manteniendo el engaño de una indiferencia perfecta. [...] Es la historia de un deseo de conocerse y tratarse insatisfecho, artificiosamente contenido, y, en especial, de una especie de estimación exaltada. Pues el hombre ama y honra al hombre mientras no puede juzgarle. " No esperaba para nada la historia que he encontrado en este libro, me ha sorprendido mucho (no había leído la sinopsis). A veces es más importante el cómo se cuentan los acontecimientos, las descripciones y reflexiones sobre ello, que la historia en sí misma. Es el caso de este libro. Me maravilla el estilo del autor, la maestria con la que usa las palabras. Estoy deseando leer más obras suyas 😍

Pues el hombre ama y honra al hombre mientras no puede juzgarle."

No esperaba para nada la historia que he encontrado en este libro, me ha sorprendido mucho (no había leído la sinopsis).
A veces es más importante el cómo se cuentan los acontecimientos, las descripciones y reflexiones sobre ello, que la historia en sí misma. Es el caso de este libro. Me maravilla el estilo del autor, la maestria con la que usa las palabras. Estoy deseando leer más obras suyas 😍

review.text: Ο Θάνατος στην Βενετία είναι ένα έργο δίχως σασπένς, μια σύντομη και συρτή εξιστόρηση για ένα "άφημα". Ο Φον Άσενμπαχ μεταβαίνει μακριά από την πρωτινή ζωή του σε έναν νέο τόπο, στην Βενετία, και με χαλαρωμένους τους ρυθμούς αλλά και τους ηθικούς ενδοιασμούς απλά αφήνεται στην σχόλη, στην γοητεία τού άγνωστου έφηβου, του θελκτικού Τάτζιο και τελικώς στον θάνατο. Όλη η Βενετία συρρικνώνεται και γίνεται Τάτζιο για τον Φον Άσενμπαχ. Όμως πίσω από τον Τάτζιο, στο φόντο, η Βενετία γίνεται σιγά-σιγά και χολερικός θάνατος. Έτσι, με αδυνατισμένες τις αντιστάσεις ο Φον Άσενμπαχ αφήνεται και βυθίζεται κάθε μέρα σε αυτήν την μικρή Βενετία που είναι Τάτζιο και θάνατος μαζί. Δίχως δυνατή πλοκή είναι μια περίτεχνη εξιστόρηση. review.text: I acknowledge to have read the book after having seen the film of Visconti. Difficult to forget the Lido, Dick Bogarde and the adagio of the 5° symphony of Malher. There is all in this short account: life and death, old age and youth, the desire and homosexuality, the beauty and the ugliness, there is all. Aschenbach wishes Tadzio because his beauty fascinate him. Allusions to Greece are there to attenuate the homosexual aspect. But there is a risk to see only that and the film of Visconti is there for something. Epidemy is here. The danger is there around threatening. It kills and Aschenbach himself will die about it. This brilliant company isolated on the beach from the Lido is encircled. The cholera approaches. This book was published in 1912. Difficult not to see there a metaphor on the First World War.

review.text: Wow. A lot to think about about for such a slim book. Did anybody else read this as a ghost story? Possibly my interpretation was warped by reading it in late December, but I thought I detected parallels with Dickens’ A Christmas Carol of all things (the old, grossly made-up dandy on the boat crossing as the ghost of Christmas future, for example. The beautiful, aloof youth the ghost of Christmas past?). Perhaps not. Either way, a couple of scenes were sufficiently haunting that they recurred in my dreams/nightmares some days later, which is generally a mark of top quality writing as far as I’m concerned. I’m late to Thomas Mann but sufficiently intrigued to go further. Any tips on where to go next much appreciated.

I’m late to Thomas Mann but sufficiently intrigued to go further. Any tips on where to go next much appreciated.

review.text: I have never read 87 pages for such long time. I fell asleep every time I tried to read it, it was a very slow, boring and painful experience for me. It does not read well, it is very difficult to focus. The author uses terribly long sentences with terrible amount of descriptions and a little amount of plot. The main theme - a platonic love of an aging author towards a fourteen years old boy - was also quite uninteresting for me. Honestly, I do not know why this book counts to one of the classics. If you do not like a difficult style of writing and do not know much about the Greek mythology (there are some similes), then it is probable that you will not like this book.

review.text: Alte Rezension: Vielschichtig, manchmal sogar ein wenig unergründlich. Ein paar Ergänzungen hierzu: Ich weiß nicht, warum ich einem von Manns besten Werken nichts abgewinnen kann und vielleicht ändert sich meine Einstellung, sobald wir es im Seminar besprochen haben, aber ich finde "Der Tod in Venedig" an manchen Passagen unheimlich einschläfernd. Wie immer ist die Motivik in der Geschichte meisterhaft und ich mag die Themen, den düsteren Tod, die Begierde und wie die Begierde schließlich den Tod (in gewisser Art und Weise) auch bedingt. Dennoch, ich musste mich durch die Seiten quälen und es gibt einige seiner Kurzgeschichten, die mir viel mehr bedeuten.

review.text: This book makes clear the deleuzian-guattarian concept of desiring-machines. How the subject is just a residue of his thousands of desiring-machines. Who is Aschenbach? The desiring-machine that writes or the desiring-machine that wants the young boy. The subjectal residue just creeps along, being driven by all this machines. The intensities that sculpt the body without organs are nicely introduced gradually by Thomas Mann. It could be a boring book, it is also a grandious one. Socrates was a scandalous person by corrupting young men. What Aschenbach feels is also scandalous in our times but how can you restrict yourself in front of pure enjoyment?

review.text: A stunning early work of a grand author. A profound and impactful book. A loving and careful portrait of a man who has risen to great heights in literature, but who knows himself to be an empty shell deep within, Death ever lingering above his head, both in morality and purpose, his journey laced with beautiful symbolism as the reader follows his search for meaning that can never be satisfied, yet the struggle remains ever intriguing. A must-read. This book grants itself many epithets because there are no proper words to describe it.

A must-read. This book grants itself many epithets because there are no proper words to describe it.

review.text: This short novella is alternatingly brilliant, poetic genius of deep emotions, and pretentious nonsense. I was bored at first but slowly became fascinated with the growing sense of emotional turmoil, loneliness and approaching doom. While Mann was philosophizing over the erotic nature of artistic inspiration, I was increasingly creeped out by Aschenbach's object of desire and where all this was going.

review.text: I really wasn’t expecting anything in particular when I picked this up but it floored me. A very haunting piece on the ways in which people delude themselves during periods of their life when they feel like they’ve run out of new experiences or new choices to make. Profoundly relatable and pathetic. Our ideas of perfection, physical or otherwise is often just a projection of our own insecurities and would seem totally inane if described to others.

review.text: Thomas Mann, on his birthday June 6 “Solitude produces originality, bold & astonishing beauty, poetry. But solitude also produces perverseness, the disproportionate, the absurd, and the forbidden.” ― Thomas Mann, Death in Venice Beautiful words questioning the value and pursuit of Beauty, like an Ourosbouros as a narrative swallowing its own tail; Thomas Mann uses disease and death as metaphors of spiritual and sociopolitical enfeeblement and decay, the fragmentation and senility of nations and of civilization itself as reflected in the lives of its people. A fascination with sin and the origins of evil, with penance and redemption, runs through all of his work; his novel on the life of Pope Gregory, The Holy Sinner, can be read as a coda on the whole. Doctor Faustus: The Life of the German Composer Adrian Leverkuhn as Told by a Friend discusses all of this in context of the Nazi destruction of German culture, with a lot of illuminating asides on music theory. His idea that listening to Beethoven has the power of redemption from evil was taken as a challenge by Anthony Burgess, who wrote A Clockwork Orange in rebuttal. Run out of Germany for his speeches against the Nazis (whom he also gave speeches against via the BBC during the war) and out of America for his defense of the Hollywood Ten before Congress during the McCarthy purges (they called him a communist for defending the right of free speech, when he feared the tyranny of communism as much as he did any other and said so in print and to anyone who would listen), he had a special dislike for totalitarian governments and thought of them as a spiritual disease of debased values. And so we come to his two Great Books and classics of world literature, The Magic Mountain and Death in Venice. The Magic Mountain recasts Plato's Dialogues as a forum of modern ideologies in a hospital ward for the dying, a kind of Congress of Possible Nations . Herein Thomas Mann diagnoses and explores the malaise and rebirth of civilization. His major influences include Goethe, Nietzsche, Schopenhauer, Wagner, and Tolstoy. In his 1939 Princeton lecture Thomas Mann discussed the idea that his novel belongs to a quest tradition, which makes its hero a type of the Grail Knight, Parsifal; and suggests an awareness of Emma Jung's work on the subject. In Death in Venice the progress of the plague mirrors that of the narrators moral degeneration and psychological dissolution as he becomes unmoored by an impossible Beauty and begins to drift away. It is a hauntingly beautiful elegy of the Ideal and a critique of Platonic Romanticism; Nabokov took up its themes and devices for his great novel Lolita, a denunciation of the idealism that led to his father's execution by the communists. Together Mann and Nabokov are among our civilization's finest conservatives, in the best sense of conserving the values and ideas that have enabled us to adapt to changes and survive. For Beauty as an ideal is here corruptive of its own values, consuming itself as a leprous disease as Venice is consumed by the plague of its lost glory. And all ends in decadence and in death. How very Wagnerian- yet there is hope. Once all the evils have escaped Pandora's Box, there is a last gift left inside, an unexpected surprise, and from this source do all things awaken renewed. To behold the impossible is a shattering, transformative event, but also one which connects us with the Infinite and with one another. The quest of Thomas Mann to find a path forward to the rebirth of civilization, to resolve and transcend the internal paradoxes and dichotomies of history which led to its destruction and the subversion of democracy by fascist tyranny, offers its own solution; life requires not stability, but growth. We require a dynamically unstable, chaotic system, multiplying possibilities and reflecting alternatives infinitely; order, but within the context of adaptation and change. When paradigms of if/then reasoning fail, shift to and/both logic. A conserving force, and a revolutionary force; the Appolonian and the Dionysian as a primal dyad.

Thomas Mann, on his birthday June 6
“Solitude produces originality, bold & astonishing beauty, poetry. But solitude also produces perverseness, the disproportionate, the absurd, and the forbidden.”
― Thomas Mann, Death in Venice

Beautiful words questioning the value and pursuit of Beauty, like an Ourosbouros as a narrative swallowing its own tail; Thomas Mann uses disease and death as metaphors of spiritual and sociopolitical enfeeblement and decay, the fragmentation and senility of nations and of civilization itself as reflected in the lives of its people.
A fascination with sin and the origins of evil, with penance and redemption, runs through all of his work; his novel on the life of Pope Gregory, The Holy Sinner, can be read as a coda on the whole.
Doctor Faustus: The Life of the German Composer Adrian Leverkuhn as Told by a Friend discusses all of this in context of the Nazi destruction of German culture, with a lot of illuminating asides on music theory. His idea that listening to Beethoven has the power of redemption from evil was taken as a challenge by Anthony Burgess, who wrote A Clockwork Orange in rebuttal.
Run out of Germany for his speeches against the Nazis (whom he also gave speeches against via the BBC during the war) and out of America for his defense of the Hollywood Ten before Congress during the McCarthy purges (they called him a communist for defending the right of free speech, when he feared the tyranny of communism as much as he did any other and said so in print and to anyone who would listen), he had a special dislike for totalitarian governments and thought of them as a spiritual disease of debased values.
And so we come to his two Great Books and classics of world literature, The Magic Mountain and Death in Venice.
The Magic Mountain recasts Plato's Dialogues as a forum of modern ideologies in a hospital ward for the dying, a kind of Congress of Possible Nations . Herein Thomas Mann diagnoses and explores the malaise and rebirth of civilization. His major influences include Goethe, Nietzsche, Schopenhauer, Wagner, and Tolstoy. In his 1939 Princeton lecture Thomas Mann discussed the idea that his novel belongs to a quest tradition, which makes its hero a type of the Grail Knight, Parsifal; and suggests an awareness of Emma Jung's work on the subject.
In Death in Venice the progress of the plague mirrors that of the narrators moral degeneration and psychological dissolution as he becomes unmoored by an impossible Beauty and begins to drift away. It is a hauntingly beautiful elegy of the Ideal and a critique of Platonic Romanticism; Nabokov took up its themes and devices for his great novel Lolita, a denunciation of the idealism that led to his father's execution by the communists.
Together Mann and Nabokov are among our civilization's finest conservatives, in the best sense of conserving the values and ideas that have enabled us to adapt to changes and survive. For Beauty as an ideal is here corruptive of its own values, consuming itself as a leprous disease as Venice is consumed by the plague of its lost glory. And all ends in decadence and in death.
How very Wagnerian- yet there is hope. Once all the evils have escaped Pandora's Box, there is a last gift left inside, an unexpected surprise, and from this source do all things awaken renewed.
To behold the impossible is a shattering, transformative event, but also one which connects us with the Infinite and with one another. The quest of Thomas Mann to find a path forward to the rebirth of civilization, to resolve and transcend the internal paradoxes and dichotomies of history which led to its destruction and the subversion of democracy by fascist tyranny, offers its own solution; life requires not stability, but growth. We require a dynamically unstable, chaotic system, multiplying possibilities and reflecting alternatives infinitely; order, but within the context of adaptation and change. When paradigms of if/then reasoning fail, shift to and/both logic. A conserving force, and a revolutionary force; the Appolonian and the Dionysian as a primal dyad.

review.text: Verbose and didactic, buffoonish and ghoulish, one is unsure—as with the period in which it was written—whether to place Death in Venice among the last of the Victorian novels or at the threshold of literary modernism. Published in 1912, shortly before the mass ritual bloodlettings of Ypres and the Marne, the tale of stolid Aschenbach’s erotic disintegration thrums with percussive Dionysian cadences, culminating in a shattering of the transfixed writer’s personality and a tragicomic demise not altogether unlike that of Nietzsche, the strangergod’s self-immolating apostle. Gustav von Aschenbach, an author renowned but lately deaf to inspiration, breaks from Munich to Venice in the hope of rekindling his creative energies. There he encounters—from an indiscrete distance—a fourteen-year-old Polish boy named Tadzio, who the greying poet finds exquisitely beautiful, and for whom he fields an impossible love that craters into a doom-driven obsession as Venice herself is wracked by a devastating pestilence. Aschenbach’s muse is no mere object of perverse desire, nor chiefly a pellucid shade of irretrievable innocence, but an icon of beauty itself in its serene platonic formality; one for which knowledge and pathos, structure and intoxication, Apollo and Dionysus, bend in vain to grasp before tumbling into an abyss: “Now dost thou see that we poets can be neither wise nor dignified? That we must needs go astray, ever be wanton and adventurers of the emotions? The magisterial guise of our style is all falsehood and folly, our fame and prestige a farce, the faith that the public places in us nothing if not ludicrous, and the use of art to educate the nation and its youth a hazardous enterprise that should be outlawed. For how can a man be worthy as an educator if he have a natural, inborn, incorrigible penchant for the abyss? Much as we renounce it and seek dignity, we are drawn to it. Thus do we reject, say, analytical knowledge: knowledge, Phaedrus, lacks dignity and rigor; it is discerning, understanding, forgiving, and wanting in discipline and form; it is in sympathy with the abyss; it is the abyss. We do therefore firmly resolve to disavow it and devote ourselves henceforth to beauty alone, which is to say, simplicity, grandeur and a new rigor, a second innocence, and form. But form and innocence, Phaedrus, lead to intoxication and desire; they may even lead a noble man to horrifying crimes of passion that his own beautiful rigor reprehends as infamous; they lead to the abyss; they too lead to the abyss. They lead us poets thither, I tell thee, because we are incapable of taking to the heavens, we are capable only of taking to profligacy.” Aschenbach’s tragedy is that of the futility of art. Whether his end is unalloyed vanity or a surreptitious triumph is a question offered up to the judgement of the reader.

But form and innocence, Phaedrus, lead to intoxication and desire; they may even lead a noble man to horrifying crimes of passion that his own beautiful rigor reprehends as infamous; they lead to the abyss; they too lead to the abyss. They lead us poets thither, I tell thee, because we are incapable of taking to the heavens, we are capable only of taking to profligacy.”

Aschenbach’s tragedy is that of the futility of art. Whether his end is unalloyed vanity or a surreptitious triumph is a question offered up to the judgement of the reader.

review.text: Αυτό το βιβλίο το θυμάμαι να το διαβάζω πριν μπορεί και 10 χρόνια, σαν σε όνειρο. Σαν να ανήκω και εγώ στην ομιχλώδη ατμόσφαιρα της Βενετίας όταν την επισκέπτεται ο πρωταγωνιστής μας. Βλέπω τον Τάτζιο από μακριά να μου ρίχνει κλέφτες ματιές. Ακούω τα γέλια της εφηβικής του παρέας. Παίρνω τα μπαγάζια μου και φεύγω μακριά από την Βενετία, αποφασισμένη σαν τον συγγραφέα να μην παραμείνω στην πνιγηρή της ατμόσφαιρα. Ή μήπως μένω; σκέφτομαι πως θα παραμείνω στην πόλη και ας έχει επιδημία χολέρας. Πρέπει να ξαναβρεθώ με τον Τάτζιο, νοιωθω συναισθήματα για αυτόν που δεν τα φανταζόμουν. Γίνομαι έρμαιο των συναισθημάτων μου. Πεθαίνω έχοντας υποκύψει στην αγάπη. "Nothing is more curious and awkward than the relationship of two people who only know each other with their eyes — who meet and observe each other daily, even hourly and who keep up the impression of disinterest either because of morals or because of a mental abnormality. Between them there is listlessness and pent-up curiosity, the hysteria of an unsatisfied, unnaturally suppressed need for communion and also a kind of tense respect. Because man loves and honors man as long as he is not able to judge him, and desire is a product of lacking knowledge.”

review.text: درسته که لحن عصاقورت داده و خشک راوی آدمو ذله میکرد اما در کل کتاب بدی نبود.. احساس می کنم نویسنده باوجودیکه فهمیده و روشنفکره اما زیاد سرد و گرم چشیده نیست! اونطوریکه تصور میکردم نتونستم باهاش ارتباط برقرار کنم.. سعی کردم به بهونه ی پایان چالش 2018 هرطور شده کتاب رو تموم کنم چون خیلی خسته کننده شده بود و نگران بودم اگه الان تمومش نکنم باید کل سال رو یه صفحه یه صفحه بخونم برم جلو امیدوارم کوه جادو با من مهربون تر باشه همیشه زندگی آدمهایی مثل کاراکتر اصلی این کتاب واسم پرسش برانگیز بوده، این که صرف آداب دانی در زندگی کفایت می کنه؟ این که مورد احترام اجتماع باشی و ذهنت درگیر مسائل کاری و فرهنگی باشه کافیه؟ اینطور به نظر میاد چنین افرادی هیچوقت فرصت نمی کنند به خودشون و خواسته های درونیشون بپردازند یا اینکه اهمیت نمیدن غرور این کاراکتر منو یاد تکبر خودم میندازه با این تفاوت که من سعی کردم بزرگ شم و خواه ناخواه به مسیر دیگه ای کشیده شدم تا درک کنم نه من و نه جامعه به این میزان غرور نیاز نداریم اما آقای هنرمند کتاب تا لحظه ی آخر هم متو��ه کمبودهاش نشد و نیازهای درونی و روحیشو درک نکرد جمله ای که خیلی واسم جالب توجه بود: شیفتگی، محصول شناخت ناقص است

review.text: Posiblemente sea el libro más complejo y denso que he leído nunca. Cualquiera puede reconocer una obra maestra cuando la lee aunque no todos entenderla y yo, desafortunadamente, no estoy entre ellos. En algunos años volveré aquí con la esperanza de conseguir captar todos los matices del libro, por ahora, lo cierro con la sensación de que la literatura es muy muy grande

review.text: “…El que está fuera de sí, nada aborrece tanto como volver a su propio ser”. En medio del éxito profesional, Aschenbach, un escritor de mediana edad, enfrenta una crisis existencial , como consecuencia del escaso convencimiento del valor de su trabajo. Su vida y su obra han sido metódicas y organizadas, pero carentes de pasión y emoción. Así, el protagonista huye de sí mismo y viaja a Venecia buscando inspiración y el replanteamiento de su vida. Su conflicto existencial se recrudece tras el encuentro con el adolescente Tadzio, quien simboliza el ideal máximo de la belleza. Atraído de un modo incontenible por el joven, la admiración inicial da paso a una obsesión enfermiza por la juventud y el poder que la belleza del adolescente ejerce sobre los demás. Frente al franco declive de su propia existencia, Aschenbach sublima en Tadzio el anhelo por la “vida ideal” que nunca pudo lograr. La frustración por saber que, sin la visión del bello adolescente, deberá regresar a su vida decadente y sin sentido, y tendrá que volver a ser él mismo, lo lleva a tomar una decisión de consecuencias trágicas. Es la primera vez que leo a Thomas Mann y, aunque sabía que me enfrentaría a un libro que exigiría máxima concentración, debo decir que la razón principal de mi calificación no está basada en la premisa de la obra —la cual es extraordinaria, pues todos en algún momento de nuestra vida nos enfrentamos a un conflicto existencial—, sino básicamente en la narración introspectiva y filosófica, el uso de un lenguaje tan intelectual y el excesivo uso de las metáforas.

Su vida y su obra han sido metódicas y organizadas, pero carentes de pasión y emoción. Así, el protagonista huye de sí mismo y viaja a Venecia buscando inspiración y el replanteamiento de su vida.

Su conflicto existencial se recrudece tras el encuentro con el adolescente Tadzio, quien simboliza el ideal máximo de la belleza. Atraído de un modo incontenible por el joven, la admiración inicial da paso a una obsesión enfermiza por la juventud y el poder que la belleza del adolescente ejerce sobre los demás. Frente al franco declive de su propia existencia, Aschenbach sublima en Tadzio el anhelo por la “vida ideal” que nunca pudo lograr. La frustración por saber que, sin la visión del bello adolescente, deberá regresar a su vida decadente y sin sentido, y tendrá que volver a ser él mismo, lo lleva a tomar una decisión de consecuencias trágicas.

Es la primera vez que leo a Thomas Mann y, aunque sabía que me enfrentaría a un libro que exigiría máxima concentración, debo decir que la razón principal de mi calificación no está basada en la premisa de la obra —la cual es extraordinaria, pues todos en algún momento de nuestra vida nos enfrentamos a un conflicto existencial—, sino básicamente en la narración introspectiva y filosófica, el uso de un lenguaje tan intelectual y el excesivo uso de las metáforas.

review.text: When I was called out for teaching some provocative contexts in my “Strange Children” course this quarter, my supervisor came to my defense by saying, “Well, it’s not like you’re teaching Death in Venice, or anything.” I had heard of Thomas Mann’s novella — and really enjoyed reading the obscure The Transposed Heads, which I consider a really masterful work despite some scathing criticisms to the contrary — but had never read it. I headed to the library and checked it out right away. Would Mann prove to be more dirty and provocative than Alice’s Adventures? Was it possible? Death in Venice is plenty provocative and dirty, alright, if you’re into affect theory. If you’re a gender or sexuality theorist, however, I must say, it misses the mark somewhat. The story alarmed me much more for its depiction of feeling than it ever could have for its wispy, barely lukewarm invocation of pedophilia. After four pages of Mann’s text I was struck by his fierce exploration of both hidden and blatant affect — which occurs sometimes simultaneously. (In fact, I am determined to teach it as a course on emotion in literature.) The foreboding lifelessness that stretches itself out before Gustav Aschenbach’s life can be summarized by a series of words/phrases that arise consistently or at key moments, such as: “red,” “false midsummer,” or “diseased city.” But the “unchastity and fury of decay” that epitomizes Aschenbach’s experience in Venice — and with the sickly Polish “god” with whom he falls in love from afar — is best articulated through Mann’s overuse of adjectives that relate directly to rampant emotion and, conversely, emotionlessness. Feeling is a sense like smell. The graying legend Aschenbach is surrounded by smells (of hospital, gaiety, food, beach) that bring out depth of affect which runs the gamut of “sinister revels of emotion:” sympathy, fear, pity, hopelessness, desire, shame, elation, anxiety, satisfaction, etc. For Mann’s protagonist, “passion is our exultation” but also his demise. Passion is the strong feeling of desire or excitement, but I am also reminded of the definition of passion from House of Leaves: “Passion has little do with euphoria and everything to do with patience. It is not about feeling good. It is about endurance. Like patience, passion comes from the same Latin root: pati. It does not mean to flow with exuberance. It means to suffer.” Aschenbach’s passion reflects both definitions: it is suffering and jubilation. Yet, despite the war of feeling that he locks in his aged body, he shows little external signal of such power until, on the day of his death, he heads to the salon to be made up in a fashion that reflects the grotesque fop whom he criticizes on the ship. The disease that finally kills him seems less the sickness of Venice from the Middle East (which reminded me somewhat of Bruges-la-Morte) and more the inevitable finale of emotion of this caliber. Death is the ultimate portrayal of patience. Is it, then, a useful symbol of passion? For Aschenbach, such seems to be the case. How beautiful. So beautiful, in fact, that I almost forgot about the Platonic “beautiful boy,” that god Eros who catalyzes the war. Then again, Mann suggests that this war was raging before Tadzio comes along, doesn’t he? I was inclined to toss Tadzio away. He is like the wine that brings out the flavor of an aged and beautiful cheese.

review.text: Although I could see the value of this book, I found it a painful read. What worked best for me was the author's ability to create a mood. From the first sentence I found this book to be ominous, tense and dark. The main character is an aging poet who one day sees a red-bearded foreigner which leads to Gustav's desire (obsession?) to leave his cold, dreary home in Germany. Eventually he makes his way to Venice, where he soon observes a Polish family and becomes enchanted by their 14 year old son whom Gustav finds beautiful. He is soon pursuing the boy, and unable to question his own actions or desires. As the title suggests, he eventually dies in Venice having lost his dignity, control and self-respect.
review.text_raw:
Although I could see the value of this book, I found it a painful read.

What worked best for me was the author's ability to create a mood. From the first sentence I found this book to be ominous, tense and dark. The main character is an aging poet who one day sees a red-bearded foreigner which leads to Gustav's desire (obsession?) to leave his cold, dreary home in Germany. Eventually he makes his way to Venice, where he soon observes a Polish family and becomes enchanted by their 14 year old son whom Gustav finds beautiful. He is soon pursuing the boy, and unable to question his own actions or desires. As the title suggests, he eventually dies in Venice having lost his dignity, control and self-respect.

review.text: Con la lectura de esta obra he podido disfrutar de un texto verdaderamente especial y significante. A la hora de pensar qué es lo que destacaría, no puedo decidirme por unas partes específicas o ni siquiera por unos determinados capítulos de esta breve pero intensísima novela, ya que la inmensa mayoría de sus renglones, por unos motivos o por otros, suponen para el lector un deleite total. Aunque las más hermosas líneas pueden sobre todo relacionarse con el personaje de Tadzio, desde el mismísimo comienzo de la novela ya se plasman en ella unas ideas que, a través de la mente del protagonista (Gustav Aschenbach), invitan a una profunda reflexión sobre puntos como la filosofía, la moral o el arte y todo aquel que dedica su vida a este mágico fenómeno. Por ejemplo, me quedo con fragmentos como: “el artista, aunque viva exteriormente en un retiro claustral, se siente al fin y al cabo poseído de un refinamiento, un cansancio, y una curiosidad de los nervios, más intensos de los que puede engendrar una vida llena de pasiones y goces violentos”. Sin embargo, como decía, he encontrado quizás las ideas más bonitas de la obra en todo lo que se relaciona con Tadzio, ese joven de tan bello “rostro, pálido y preciosamente austero, encuadrado de cabello color de miel”. Con la cercanía de este muchacho, no paran de producirse en la intelectual y artística cabeza de Aschenbach una serie de bellas e interesantísimas ideas, alimentadas por la continua “visión de aquella figura viviente, tan delicada y tan varonil al mismo tiempo, con sus rizos húmedos y hermosos como los de un dios mancebo que, saliendo de lo profundo del cielo y del mar, escapaba al poder de la corriente”. Con todo esto, además de unas hermosísimas descripciones del chico y de las circunstancias que lo rodean, Thomas Mann crea, conectadas con este joven personaje de su novela, unas profundas y filosóficas reflexiones sobre la belleza y cómo ésta, a través de la aventura de los sentidos, puede llevarnos a lo sublime y a lo abismal al mismo tiempo. En este sentido, ha de destacarse especialmente la magistral utilización que Mann realiza del “Fedón” de Platón para llegar de forma completa a estas elevadas reflexiones y, a la vez, poder reflejarlas perfectamente en lo narrado en su ficción. En definitiva, me ha parecido una obra muy interesante de leer y que recomendaría a todo lector que, aficionado a unas lecturas más especiales, quiera descubrir un texto con una prosa brillante y hermosa y, al mismo tiempo, unas profundísimas ideas que, sin duda, maravillarán por siempre a todas las mentes motivadas por el aprendizaje y la reflexión. De hecho, ya es impresionante el hecho de únicamente contemplar cómo, por la existencia de un chico bello, se pueden llegar a estas reflexiones y, sobre todo, estos excesivos límites descritos en la novela. Quizás es que los más bellos deberían aplicarse aquello que, pensando en Tadzio, exclama Aschenbach: “¡No debes sonreír así! ¡No se debe sonreír así a nadie!”.

A la hora de pensar qué es lo que destacaría, no puedo decidirme por unas partes específicas o ni siquiera por unos determinados capítulos de esta breve pero intensísima novela, ya que la inmensa mayoría de sus renglones, por unos motivos o por otros, suponen para el lector un deleite total. Aunque las más hermosas líneas pueden sobre todo relacionarse con el personaje de Tadzio, desde el mismísimo comienzo de la novela ya se plasman en ella unas ideas que, a través de la mente del protagonista (Gustav Aschenbach), invitan a una profunda reflexión sobre puntos como la filosofía, la moral o el arte y todo aquel que dedica su vida a este mágico fenómeno. Por ejemplo, me quedo con fragmentos como: “el artista, aunque viva exteriormente en un retiro claustral, se siente al fin y al cabo poseído de un refinamiento, un cansancio, y una curiosidad de los nervios, más intensos de los que puede engendrar una vida llena de pasiones y goces violentos”. Sin embargo, como decía, he encontrado quizás las ideas más bonitas de la obra en todo lo que se relaciona con Tadzio, ese joven de tan bello “rostro, pálido y preciosamente austero, encuadrado de cabello color de miel”. Con la cercanía de este muchacho, no paran de producirse en la intelectual y artística cabeza de Aschenbach una serie de bellas e interesantísimas ideas, alimentadas por la continua “visión de aquella figura viviente, tan delicada y tan varonil al mismo tiempo, con sus rizos húmedos y hermosos como los de un dios mancebo que, saliendo de lo profundo del cielo y del mar, escapaba al poder de la corriente”. Con todo esto, además de unas hermosísimas descripciones del chico y de las circunstancias que lo rodean, Thomas Mann crea, conectadas con este joven personaje de su novela, unas profundas y filosóficas reflexiones sobre la belleza y cómo ésta, a través de la aventura de los sentidos, puede llevarnos a lo sublime y a lo abismal al mismo tiempo. En este sentido, ha de destacarse especialmente la magistral utilización que Mann realiza del “Fedón” de Platón para llegar de forma completa a estas elevadas reflexiones y, a la vez, poder reflejarlas perfectamente en lo narrado en su ficción.
En definitiva, me ha parecido una obra muy interesante de leer y que recomendaría a todo lector que, aficionado a unas lecturas más especiales, quiera descubrir un texto con una prosa brillante y hermosa y, al mismo tiempo, unas profundísimas ideas que, sin duda, maravillarán por siempre a todas las mentes motivadas por el aprendizaje y la reflexión. De hecho, ya es impresionante el hecho de únicamente contemplar cómo, por la existencia de un chico bello, se pueden llegar a estas reflexiones y, sobre todo, estos excesivos límites descritos en la novela. Quizás es que los más bellos deberían aplicarse aquello que, pensando en Tadzio, exclama Aschenbach: “¡No debes sonreír así! ¡No se debe sonreír así a nadie!”.

review.text: "Encontrar o repouso na perfeição é o sonho daquele que se esforça por atingir a excelência; e o nada não é também uma forma de perfeição?" Aschenbach, um escritor viúvo, famoso mas solitário, aos 50 anos decide voltar a Veneza, por um impulso provocado por um encontro casual com um desconhecido misterioso, num cemitério. Na cidade sobre a àgua, a beleza de um rapaz adolescente fascina-o, envolve-o e domina todas as suas emoções. O escritor enfraquecido rejeita partir, mesmo quando as razões são forte, mas não ousa aproximar-se ou falar com o rapaz que é o centro dos seus dias. "Porque a beleza, Fedro, repara bem, só a beleza é divina e simultaneamente visível, e por isso ela é também caminho do artista para o espírito. Mas diz-me agora, meu querido amigo, acreditas que se pode atingir alguma vez a sabedoria e verdadeiro valor viril quando se caminha para o espiritual por via dos sentidos? Ou acreditas antes (e és livre de o decidir) que esse caminho é cheio de atraentes perigos, na realidade um caminho de desacerto e pecado, que induz necessariamente ao erro? Pois tens de saber que nós, poetas, não podemos embarcar no caminho da beleza sem que Eros nos acompanhe e se arvore em líder; podemos bem ser heróis à nossa maneira e guerreiros disciplinados, mas não deixamos de ser como as mulheres, pois a paixão é para nós sublimação e o nosso desejo deve permanecer amor - é esse o nosso prazer, é essa a nossa vergonha. Vês agora que nós, os poetas, não podemos ser sábios nem dignos? Que embarcamos necessariamente no erro, permanecemos necessariamente devassos e aventureiros do sentimento? A mestria do nosso estilo é mentira e logro, a nossa fama e respeitabilidade, uma farsa, a confiança da multidão em nós, altamente risível, a educação do povo e da juventude pela arte, um empreendimento ousado, a interdizer. Pois como podia prestar para educador aquele que possui uma tendência inata, incorrigível e natural para o abismo?" Não tenho ousadia suficiente para vos falar do significado deste conto, do seu contexto e das suas consequências. Tal como as palavras de Thomas Mann são suficientes para compreendermos o seu peso, também as minhas são insuficientes para vos transmitir o que este livro me fez sentir. "É de facto bom que o mundo só conheça a obra bela, acabada, e não também as suas origens, nem os condicionalismos do seu nascimento; porque o conhecimento das fontes a partir das quais jorra a inspiração do artista iria muitas vezes confundir o seu público, intimidá-lo, anulando assim os efeitos da excelência da obra."
