review.text: At the very beginning the central character's desire to travel is ignited by seeing an "itinerant"-looking man from the countryside, he presumes, and that's all the motive he's given for embarking on the Venicean holiday he spends the rest of the novella on. In contrast to his epiphany in the sight of the rustic man, and the romantically wild nature he represents ("he saw it, saw a landscape, a tropical swampland under a cloud-swollen sky, moist and lush and monstrous, a kind of primeval wilderness"), he spends his time sitting in expensive cafés, gondolas and lounging on the beach, thinking of little else but his lust for a "beautiful" young boy. Aschenbach dresses his peverted sexual urges up in dignified allusion and symbolism. He's written with little charisma, and instead skulks around alone withdrawn in his own thoughts. I don't mind, in principle, central characters who are creepy and antisocial, but I do mind it when all they do is sit musing in cafés and sunbathing. Sometimes, as the sun was sinking behind Venice, he would sit on a bench in the hotel park to watch Tadzio, dressed in white with a colorful sash, at play on the rolled gravel tennis court; and in his mind's eye he was watching Hyacinthus, doomed to perish because two gods loved him. He could even feel Zephyr's grievous envy of his rival, who has forgotten his oracle and his bow and his zither to be forever playing with the beautiful youth; he saw the discus, steered by cruel jealousy, strike the lovely head; he himself, turning pale too, caught the broken body in his arms, and the flower that sprang from that sweet blood bore the inscription of his underlying lament. [page 65, David Luke trans. A writer (Mann) writes from the point of view of a wealthy writer (but fictional) ... musing on intellectual history. It's either a portrait of middle class ennui and unproductive intellectualism or a delicate and thoughful treatise on the life of an artist. I didn't enjoy it.

Sometimes, as the sun was sinking behind Venice, he would sit on a bench in the hotel park to watch Tadzio, dressed in white with a colorful sash, at play on the rolled gravel tennis court; and in his mind's eye he was watching Hyacinthus, doomed to perish because two gods loved him. [page 65, David Luke trans.


A writer (Mann) writes from the point of view of a wealthy writer (but fictional) ... musing on intellectual history. It's either a portrait of middle class ennui and unproductive intellectualism or a delicate and thoughful treatise on the life of an artist. I didn't enjoy it.

review.text: — از تنهایی زیبایی پدید می‌آید، زیبایی شگفت انگیز و افسون‌ساز، شعر. رمان کوتاه «مرگ در ونیز» (۱۹۱۲) اثر توماس مان، یکی از شاهکارهای ادبیات مدرن که به شیوه‌ای هنرمندانه وسواس، زیبایی، زوال و کشمکش درونی آدمی را به تصویر می‌کشد. داستان، زندگی گوستاو فون آشنباخ، نویسنده‌ای مشهور و منضبط را دنبال می‌کند که در جریان سفری به ونیز، مجذوب پسری لهستانی به نام تاچیو می‌شود. این شیفتگی که ابتدا به نظر می‌رسد یک تحسین ساده از زیبایی است، به‌تدریج به وسواسی ویرانگر تبدیل می‌شود که تمام بنیان‌های عقلانی آشنباخ را فرو می‌ریزد و سرنوشت او را به تباهی می‌کشاند. مان در این اثر به شکلی عمیق به تضاد میان نظم و انضباط عقلانی (آپولونی) و شور و هیجان بی‌مهار (دیونیزوسی) می‌پردازد. آشنباخ در آغاز نمادی از کنترل و خویشتن‌داری است که عمر خود را وقف هنر و اخلاق کرده است. اما ورود به ونیز، شهری که همزمان زیبا و فاسد است، باعث می‌شود که او به تدریج در جذابیت زیبایی غوطه‌ور شده و نظم زندگی‌اش فروپاشد. شهر ونیز با خیابان‌های پیچ در پیچ، کانال‌های پرابهام و هوای مسموم از بیماری وبا، به عنوان نمادی از فریبندگی و فساد، نمایانگر مسیر سقوط روحی و جسمی آشنباخ است. از نظر روان‌شناختی، شخصیت آشنباخ بسیار پیچیده و چندلایه است. او که همیشه در جستجوی کمال هنری بوده، اکنون با تجسم این کمال در چهره‌ی تاچیو روبرو می‌شود. تاچیو نه به عنوان یک شخصیت واقعی، بلکه به عنوان نمادی از زیبایی مطلق و دست‌نیافتنی جلوه می‌کند. نگاه‌های خاموش و حرکات ظریف تاچیو به آشنباخ حسی از اشتیاق و سرگشتگی می‌بخشد که او را به ورطه‌ی جنون می‌کشاند. سبک نوشتاری مان در این اثر پر از ظرافت‌های ادبی و فلسفی است. توصیفات دقیق و فضاهای سنگین، همراه با استفاده از نمادهایی همچون اساطیر یونانی، گرمای طاقت‌فرسای تابستان، و صدای امواج دریا، باعث ایجاد جوی غنی و چندلایه می‌شود که با اضطراب و زوال همراه است. زبان رسمی و پیچیده‌ مان نیز با محتوای عمیق داستان همخوانی دارد. «مرگ در ونیز» علاوه بر داستانی شخصی، بازتابی از بحران‌های فرهنگی و اخلاقی اوایل قرن بیستم است. این اثر با الهام از اندیشه‌های نیچه و فروید، به نقد وسواس بر زیبایی و هنر، و پیامدهای سرکوب امیال انسانی می‌پردازد. «مرگ در ونیز» اثری است تأثیرگذار و پرمعنا که پرسش‌هایی درباره هنر، میل و مرگ را به شکلی جاودانه مطرح می‌کند.
review.text_raw:
— از تنهایی زیبایی پدید می‌آید، زیبایی شگفت انگیز و افسون‌ساز، شعر.

رمان کوتاه «مرگ در ونیز» (۱۹۱۲) اثر توماس مان، یکی از شاهکارهای ادبیات مدرن که به شیوه‌ای هنرمندانه وسواس، زیبایی، زوال و کشمکش درونی آدمی را به تصویر می‌کشد. داستان، زندگی گوستاو فون آشنباخ، نویسنده‌ای مشهور و منضبط را دنبال می‌کند که در جریان سفری به ونیز، مجذوب پسری لهستانی به نام تاچیو می‌شود. این شیفتگی که ابتدا به نظر می‌رسد یک تحسین ساده از زیبایی است، به‌تدریج به وسواسی ویرانگر تبدیل می‌شود که تمام بنیان‌های عقلانی آشنباخ را فرو می‌ریزد و سرنوشت او را به تباهی می‌کشاند.

مان در این اثر به شکلی عمیق به تضاد میان نظم و انضباط عقلانی (آپولونی) و شور و هیجان بی‌مهار (دیونیزوسی) می‌پردازد. آشنباخ در آغاز نمادی از کنترل و خویشتن‌داری است که عمر خود را وقف هنر و اخلاق کرده است. اما ورود به ونیز، شهری که همزمان زیبا و فاسد است، باعث می‌شود که او به تدریج در جذابیت زیبایی غوطه‌ور شده و نظم زندگی‌اش فروپاشد. شهر ونیز با خیابان‌های پیچ در پیچ، کانال‌های پرابهام و هوای مسموم از بیماری وبا، به عنوان نمادی از فریبندگی و فساد، نمایانگر مسیر سقوط روحی و جسمی آشنباخ است.

از نظر روان‌شناختی، شخصیت آشنباخ بسیار پیچیده و چندلایه است. او که همیشه در جستجوی کمال هنری بوده، اکنون با تجسم این کمال در چهره‌ی تاچیو روبرو می‌شود. تاچیو نه به عنوان یک شخصیت واقعی، بلکه به عنوان نمادی از زیبایی مطلق و دست‌نیافتنی جلوه می‌کند. نگاه‌های خاموش و حرکات ظریف تاچیو به آشنباخ حسی از اشتیاق و سرگشتگی می‌بخشد که او را به ورطه‌ی جنون می‌کشاند.

سبک نوشتاری مان در این اثر پر از ظرافت‌های ادبی و فلسفی است. توصیفات دقیق و فضاهای سنگین، همراه با استفاده از نمادهایی همچون اساطیر یونانی، گرمای طاقت‌فرسای تابستان، و صدای امواج دریا، باعث ایجاد جوی غنی و چندلایه می‌شود که با اضطراب و زوال همراه است. زبان رسمی و پیچیده‌ مان نیز با محتوای عمیق داستان همخوانی دارد.

«مرگ در ونیز» علاوه بر داستانی شخصی، بازتابی از بحران‌های فرهنگی و اخلاقی اوایل قرن بیستم است. این اثر با الهام از اندیشه‌های نیچه و فروید، به نقد وسواس بر زیبایی و هنر، و پیامدهای سرکوب امیال انسانی می‌پردازد. «مرگ در ونیز» اثری است تأثیرگذار و پرمعنا که پرسش‌هایی درباره هنر، میل و مرگ را به شکلی جاودانه مطرح می‌کند.

review.text: Siempre que un autor escribe una novela acerca de un escritor siento que se entrega casi por completo, desnudo, sincero. Expuestos así, todos sus miedos, ambiciones y obsesiones, revela lo más profundo de su ser donde cabe la toda la inmensidad de su naturaleza artística y la obscenidad de sus peores deseos y pensamientos. Eso es lo que sucede en esta novela, una novela con poco argumento (no plot just vibes) en donde lo peor de las crisis del protagonista es la realidad de sus pensamientos, gran parte de todo aquello que lo mortifica y lo hace sufrir ni siquiera llega a suceder, encarna un hombre solitario, atribulado, obsesionado y lleno de contradicciones, y son estas las que lo llevan a actuar toscamente, incluso de manera juvenil e inexperta. Esta espesura mental lo ahoga emocionalmente y llega a enfermarlo físicamente, nubla su juicio y lo lleva a tomar decisiones apresuradas y débiles. Thomas Mann expresa todo este escenario con una atmosfera Veneciana densa, asfixiante, mefítica llegando a ser repugnante pero también embriagante y bella. Compara este ambiente con la sofocación que le provoca su propia imaginación y lo hace magistralmente con una prosa espesa, elegante y cargada de esas cavilaciones filosóficas que suelen ser más graves y extrañas en un escritor, un sujeto taciturno y solitario en cuyo interior, crece irrefrenablemente la presión creada por el conflicto de sus principios y sus deseos. La muerte en Venecia es un libro sobre el arte, la belleza, el dolor, la soledad, el sufrimiento, la vejez, la decadencia, la obsesión y la representación minuciosa y detallada, como solo Thomas Mann puede hacerlo, del caos latente que llevamos en nuestro ser y con tan solo un desbalance suscitado por un pequeño apetito carnal, puede ser la llama previa al descontrolado incendio que lleve a cenizas nuestra vida.

review.text: A brilliant piece of work, great prose, So, I'm still thinking over everything I've just read. A few notes here to keep me aware that a better review is still to come: A middle-aged man pursuing a boy of childhood age. The backdrop of the story is Venice in the summer of 1867 when the fourth cholera pandemic finally hit Italy and hundred-thousand lives perished to the disease. Yet, according to the opening paragraph, it's year 19-something. Having finished the story and reflecting upon it for review purposes and detailed text, I've noticed that it twists around in perspective a little. It introduces a selection of characters, but I'm not sure who is the one committing the said act in two paragraphs back of this review. It does primarily suggest it's the Gustav character. But is it a dream, and is it actually the Frederick of Prussia character, the writer of 'The Wretch', of which the transgressions throughout suggest it to be? Either way, for my interest, I'm breaking it down to better identify that, I need to get it straightened out in my own thoughts. Also, pages 67-97 are pure gold.
review.text_raw:
A brilliant piece of work, great prose, So, I'm still thinking over everything I've just read.

A few notes here to keep me aware that a better review is still to come:

A middle-aged man pursuing a boy of childhood age.

The backdrop of the story is Venice in the summer of 1867 when the fourth cholera pandemic finally hit Italy and hundred-thousand lives perished to the disease. Yet, according to the opening paragraph, it's year 19-something.

Having finished the story and reflecting upon it for review purposes and detailed text, I've noticed that it twists around in perspective a little. It introduces a selection of characters, but I'm not sure who is the one committing the said act in two paragraphs back of this review. It does primarily suggest it's the Gustav character. But is it a dream, and is it actually the Frederick of Prussia character, the writer of 'The Wretch', of which the transgressions throughout suggest it to be?

Either way, for my interest, I'm breaking it down to better identify that, I need to get it straightened out in my own thoughts.

Also, pages 67-97 are pure gold.

review.text: Meraviglia e raccapriccio insieme, all'interno di una colerica e affascinante Venezia. Lo scrittore di fama mondiale Gustav Aschenbach, uomo temprato dal duro lavoro, va incontro ad una inesorabile passione senile che lo conduce alla morte. Morte del poeta e del corpo, morte di quello spirito energico che per tutta la vita si è negato qualsiasi piacere relegandosi nel lavoro sublime che conduce al successo intellettuale. Tra le fosche e misteriose calli veneziane, dove sempre più forte è l'odore della morte, l'uomo afflitto insegue la chimera del bellissimo ragazzino di cui si è innamorato. Una poesia dalla prima all'ultima pagina, ma anche una riflessione spietata sull'uomo che si illude di essere al di sopra di ogni pulsione umana. Ho preferito La morte a Venezia ai Buddenbrook. Da leggere e tenere vicino per sempre.

review.text: 2025: რავიცი მეგობრებო, დიდად ვერც ხელმეორედ წაკითხვამ უშველა ამ ნაწარმოებს. არც იმის ბრალია, რომ: უი, დედა! როგორ გაბედა პედოფილიის აპოლოგია თომას მანმა-მეთქი და არც პირიქით. ჰომოსექსუალობის გამო ხომ საერთოდ არ დავაკლებდი ქულებს.... უბრალოდ "ვაიბს" ვერ ვუჭერ სათანადოდ. 2025 (ისევ): მესამე და სამართალი? არა. არ ვიცი სიყრმის ბრალია, თუ გარემოების, მაგრამ ამ კაცის ამბავი სრულიად არ მადარდებს. კაცის, რომელიც არის დაღლილი, გადაპრანჭული გარეგნულად და გონებრივად და ამბის მოყოლა სულ არ ეხალისება, ერთი სული აქვს ბერძნული მითოლოგიის და ხელოვნების ცოდნა გაგიფლექსოს. კაცის, რომელიც "აპრავებს" თავის გადაწყვეტილებებს ბედისწერით, მაგრამ არაფერს აშავებს. ხოდა თუ არაფერს აშავებს ღარას "აპრავებს"? ეს არის ამბავი "სიყვარულის, რომელიც არაფრით არ გეკუთვნის" ხომ? მე ვერ დავარქმევდი სიყვარულს, რაც არ უნდა ბევრ მითოსურ გმირს შეადაროს აშეიმბახმა ტაძიო. შებყრობა და ობსესია კიბატონო. სიყვარული არა.

review.text: الموت في البندقية رواية قصيرة لتوماس مان سردها رشيق و مكثف ، سريع بشكلٍ لا يتنافى مع العذوبة الروائي الشهير الخمسيني الذي يذهب ليروّح عن ذاته في البندقية ينصدم بمشاعر مختلطة جديدة كلية عليه كان في السابق يزدري من يعبر عنها بشكل صريح ، مشاعر قد تتلاشى أمامها القيم لكنه يحاول فصل اعجابه بالفتى البولوني تادزيو كتمثال جميل في شكله الصوري عن أي رغبات فعلية قد تجرفه لما لا يتماشى وسمعته التي كدّ لبنائها ، و يؤذي روحه . كيف يترفع عن الانحطاط و روحه تتناهبها الشيطانية من ملائكيتها ، فتركن تلك الروح تلقائيًا لشيطانها مما يشوهها بشكلٍ حتمي و يزهقها تدريجيا الجدير بالذكر أن الموت هو البندقية القدرية كلية القدرة على الجسد بينما تبقى الروح مترفعة أو منحطة بعيدًا عن أي مرمى يسيطر عليه الموت ، فموت الأرواح يتعلق بنواياها تلك حكاية غوستاف اشنباخ و الفتى البولوني
review.text_raw:
الموت في البندقية

رواية قصيرة لتوماس مان

سردها رشيق و مكثف ، سريع بشكلٍ لا يتنافى مع العذوبة

الروائي الشهير الخمسيني الذي يذهب ليروّح عن ذاته في البندقية ينصدم بمشاعر مختلطة جديدة كلية عليه كان في السابق يزدري من يعبر عنها بشكل صريح ، مشاعر قد تتلاشى أمامها القيم لكنه يحاول فصل اعجابه بالفتى البولوني تادزيو كتمثال جميل في شكله الصوري عن أي رغبات فعلية قد تجرفه لما لا يتماشى وسمعته التي كدّ لبنائها ، و يؤذي روحه .

كيف يترفع عن الانحطاط و روحه تتناهبها الشيطانية من ملائكيتها ، فتركن تلك الروح تلقائيًا لشيطانها مما يشوهها بشكلٍ حتمي و يزهقها تدريجيا
الجدير بالذكر أن الموت هو البندقية القدرية كلية القدرة على الجسد بينما تبقى الروح مترفعة أو منحطة بعيدًا عن أي مرمى يسيطر عليه الموت ، فموت الأرواح يتعلق بنواياها


تلك حكاية غوستاف اشنباخ و الفتى البولوني

review.text: نمیدونم به دلیل سبک نگارش نویسنده بود یا ترجمه ی خیلی خیلی ضعیف که کتاب بی نهایت خسته کننده بود و خیلی طول کشید. و به نظرم اومد نویسنده حرفی برای گفتن نداره،.در تمام طول کتاب خارج از بحث اصلی اینقدر از این شاخه به اون شاخه پریده بود و موضوعات رو اینقدر طول داده بود که بعضی جاها جمله ها معنی خودشون رو از دست داده بودن. شخصیت اصلی داستان خیلی ضعیف پرداخته شده بود و پایان داستان با اینکه بهترین بخش کتاب بود به نظرم ضعیف اومد. داستان عشقی که نویسنده تعریف میکرد ومفاهیم هنری ای که میخواست به این عشق وصل کنه به نظرم خیلی مصنوعی ،زورکی یا ساختگی اومد. به نظرم نویسنده خواسته بود حرف جدیدی در مورد عشق و مفاهیمی که تو مغز نویسنده ان بزنه اما نتونسته بود این کارو تمام و کمال و عمیق انجام بده. شایدهم همه ی این اشکالات از ترجمه بوده.ولی اینو قطعا میدونم که باید برگردم و دوباره بخونم کتاب رو.

review.text: Con este libro, un clásico eternamente pendiente, damos por comenzado nuestro particular club de lectura ♥ "Los sentimientos y observaciones del hombre solitario son al mismo tiempo más confusos y más intensos que los de las gentes sociables; sus pensamientos son más graves, más extraños y siempre tienen un matiz de tristeza. Imágenes y sensaciones que se esfumarían fácilmente con una mirada, con una risa, un cambio de opiniones, se aferran fuertemente en el ánimo del solitario, se ahondan en el silencio y se convierten en acontecimientos, aventuras, sentimientos importantes. La soledad engendra lo original, lo atrevido, y lo extraordinariamente bello; la poesía. Pero engendra también lo desagradable, lo inoportuno, absurdo e inadecuado."

review.text: من العنوان تعرف يقيناً نهاية الرواية، وهذا ملخص، إن شئت أكمله وإن لم تشأ فالرواية تفتح يديها لك لتقرأها: آنشباخ الألماني الشاعر يخرج من وسطه ليقع في حب مدينة البندقية، من سكان مختلفي الجنسيات، لطبيعة وغيرها، ينمدج فيهم متأثراً، وبعدها تنتهي إجازته فينوي العودة، إلا أن مشكلة صغيرة في الفندق بإجراءات المغادرة تدفعه للبقاء ساعة أكثر، هذه الساعة يغير فيها ما نوى فيبقى، آنشباخ الواقع في حب المدينة، ترفضه المدينة بدورها، المحبوبة المتعجرفة، وذلك بوباء يصيب البندقية، فيخرج معظم أهلها المحليين ويبقى الأجانب، والذين لا يعلمون بالموضوع لأن الجرائد المترجمة لا تبث الخبر، على عكس الجرائد المحلية، آنشباخ العاشق للمدينة يعلم بالخبر قبل إصابته بالوباء إلا أنه يبقى على حبه، ويموت

آنشباخ الألماني الشاعر يخرج من وسطه ليقع في حب مدينة البندقية، من سكان مختلفي الجنسيات، لطبيعة وغيرها، ينمدج فيهم متأثراً، وبعدها تنتهي إجازته فينوي العودة، إلا أن مشكلة صغيرة في الفندق بإجراءات المغادرة تدفعه للبقاء ساعة أكثر، هذه الساعة يغير فيها ما نوى فيبقى، آنشباخ الواقع في حب المدينة، ترفضه المدينة بدورها، المحبوبة المتعجرفة، وذلك بوباء يصيب البندقية، فيخرج معظم أهلها المحليين ويبقى الأجانب، والذين لا يعلمون بالموضوع لأن الجرائد المترجمة لا تبث الخبر، على عكس الجرائد المحلية، آنشباخ العاشق للمدينة يعلم بالخبر قبل إصابته بالوباء إلا أنه يبقى على حبه، ويموت

review.text: I did not love this book...at all. Very weird. The writing is dense & complex, which is not necessarily bad, but Aschenbach is a character that I find rather repulsive. His obsessive nature creeps me out, and it's not just his obsession with Tadzio, but his obsessively dismal outlook, his obsessive need for change, and his obsessive desire to learn what is really happening in Venice. He is such a strange, off-putting individual (and in fact, all the characters are off-putting) that it is difficult to appreciate Mann's nuanced writing as a result.
review.text_raw: I did not love this book...at all. Very weird. The writing is dense & complex, which is not necessarily bad, but Aschenbach is a character that I find rather repulsive. His obsessive nature creeps me out, and it's not just his obsession with Tadzio, but his obsessively dismal outlook, his obsessive need for change, and his obsessive desire to learn what is really happening in Venice. He is such a strange, off-putting individual (and in fact, all the characters are off-putting) that it is difficult to appreciate Mann's nuanced writing as a result.

review.text: The protagonists reasoning for his liking sounds like what Socrates (probably to Alcibiades) would say. The prose however is less clinical than Plato. Nice wordy prose aside, the intense obsession he seems to develop for a young boy was quite disturbing. It must say something about this novella if even after squirming at the implications, the prose makes you think and keep you on the edge. Its a wonderful piece of fiction and requires a definite reread as I am unable to come to terms with the thoughts it has raised. (I will be revisiting this after reading Symposium.)

The protagonists reasoning for his liking sounds like what Socrates (probably to Alcibiades) would say. The prose however is less clinical than Plato. Nice wordy prose aside, the intense obsession he seems to develop for a young boy was quite disturbing. It must say something about this novella if even after squirming at the implications, the prose makes you think and keep you on the edge. Its a wonderful piece of fiction and requires a definite reread as I am unable to come to terms with the thoughts it has raised.

(I will be revisiting this after reading Symposium.)

review.text: Early 20th century, Venice, Italy. Thomas Mann chooses the delicate subject of a famous and successful, but aging author falling in love with a beautiful young boy. The setting is Venice at a time when there is a cholera outbreak which the authorities try to cover up. Our hero is informed of the danger and knows he should leave Venice immediately. But he chooses to stay as long as the object of his desire is staying. Death in Venice is his destiny. Although the book is well written, it is not one of my favorite books of Thomas Mann.

Early 20th century, Venice, Italy.
Thomas Mann chooses the delicate subject of a famous and successful, but aging author falling in love with a beautiful young boy.
The setting is Venice at a time when there is a cholera outbreak which the authorities try to cover up. Our hero is informed of the
danger and knows he should leave Venice immediately. But he chooses to stay as long as the object of his desire is staying.
Death in Venice is his destiny.
Although the book is well written, it is not one of my favorite books of Thomas Mann.

review.text: Nu credeam că o să mă mai impresioneze o operă de beletristică atât de tare-și iată-ma aici. Poate sunt sensibilă la tot ceea ce se învârte în jurul patologicului, sau poate pur și simplu cartea a fost bine scrisă-ideea e că am rămas mască. Mă cam saturasem de beletristică recent, având în vedere cantitatea enorma care îmi este administrata (mai mult sau mai puțin dorit) de către facultate. 5/5 pentru Mann, patologicul și intertextualitatea sa.

review.text: I feel unsure what I think of this, the writing itself was good but I do not like the love this old man has for a 14 year old boy. It is entirely wrong and I hate the old man. He’s arrogant and I feel repulsed by his obsession with the boy. Every part I loved of the writing felt ruined by the disgust I felt for the blatant pedophilia. If the same story had featured people of a similar age, I would have found it beautiful, I think.
review.text_raw:
I feel unsure what I think of this, the writing itself was good but I do not like the love this old man has for a 14 year old boy. It is entirely wrong and I hate the old man. He’s arrogant and I feel repulsed by his obsession with the boy.
Every part I loved of the writing felt ruined by the disgust I felt for the blatant pedophilia. If the same story had featured people of a similar age, I would have found it beautiful, I think.

review.text: A pesar de ser corto, es un libro muy denso por el exceso de descripciones. En la primera mitad no encontré nada que fuera de mi interés, luego aparecen algunos elementos que despiertan curiosidad en el lector (un joven con una belleza inusual y vestigios de que algo malo acontece en Venecia). La trama avanza de manera lenta (principalmente por las descripciones semi poéticas constantes) y no sentí ese apremio que disfrutamos los que leemos de querer saber que va a pasar.

review.text: I really don't get all the hoopla associated with this book. I saw it as a pervy old guy stalking a teenager. You can try to psychoanalyze the story as being about the beauty of youth and the sorrow of the aged or dissect it by comparing it to various Greek tragedies, but it's still a dirty old man story to me.

review.text: Gustav von Aschenbach has achieved the status and recognition accorded only to the greats of literature. He has worked hard for it, self-disciplined since youth, and is now in the midst of creation of a hefty work which will, he is sure, add to his renown. One day while out walking, he sees a red-haired traveller, and this sparks in him the desire to travel too. He goes first to Pola, but finds both the weather and the society disappointing, so decides to head to Venice. There, in the Hotel des Bains, he sees for the first time a young Polish boy, whose name he learns is Tadzio, and over the next few weeks his admiration for the boy’s perfect godlike beauty grows into an obsession that will lead him into a kind of madness… The book is filled with references and allusions to the philosophers and mythologies of Ancient Greece (much of which sailed straight over my head, but it didn’t matter). At first his admiration is purely artistic, one creator of beautiful things recognising the superb work of another, this perfect boy created by the gods. Slowly, however, the sensual creeps in, and the desire for recognition and even love. Gradually Aschenbach changes from passive admirer to active stalker, spending hours watching the boy on the beach or following him on his excursions around the city. Meantime there are rumours that a pestilence is spreading through the city, though the city fathers are doing everything to hide it from the lucrative foreign tourists. Aschenbach becomes aware of the danger but cannot bear to leave while Tadzio is still there. The story of an ageing man’s desire for a very young boy could so easily have degenerated into a sordid and unpleasant tale, but it’s not really about that – it’s about the impact of this sudden obsession on Aschenbach. It is the story of a man who has lived through his art coming face to face with feelings he has never allowed to surface before, may never even have been aware of. It shows us how his repressions and inhibitions are gradually broken down until he loses touch with the dignity and sense of self-worth that has been his hallmark. At first desperate not to reveal his attraction, eventually all caution is lost and even though he is aware that Tadzio’s family have taken to keeping the boy away from him, he no longer cares. He will risk any indignity for a glance or – oh! - a smile! At first happy with his staid appearance as a distinguished elderly gentleman, soon he is thinking about what to wear and how to dress his hair like a young debutante going to her first ball. There is a subtle vein of humour and mockery throughout that for me stops the story from becoming a full-scale tragedy. Apparently it is loosely based on a fascination Mann himself once developed for a beautiful young boy in a hotel, so that in some respects it feels like self-mockery. Mann uses imagery from mythology brilliantly to show how Aschenbach is hiding from reality, while Aschenbach uses the words of the Ancient Greeks to justify his actions to himself. Aschenbach can convince himself of the lofty purity of his admiration until his dreams become infused with imagery straight from the orgies of Pan and his disciples, and then, forced to recognise the base physicality within his obsession, he is too weak to pull back, although he is also too weak to do more than gaze longingly at this child who has taken possession of all his thoughts. I felt throughout that, despite his obvious sexual attraction to Tadzio, in reality Aschenbach thought of him as a beautiful work of art to be looked at but not touched, and that saved this from sinking into Lolita territory for me. There is also an unsettling air of menace in parts, externally from the miasma rising from the stagnant canals and the growing fears of the pestilence, and internally, from the breaking down of Aschenbach’s character and dignity as he becomes ever less able to control his obsession. There are moments of grotesqueness that almost take the book towards horror – recurring appearances of red-haired men who seem more menacing each time, an odd episode of a group of players whose performance turns into something out of a carnivalesque nightmare, that dream sequence, full of Pan imagery and chaos. As it moves towards that death in the title, the final impression is of the mind of an artist driven to delirium by a beauty beyond anything of his own creating and destroyed by the sudden surfacing of desires repressed and unsuspected for a lifetime. I loved this one. www.fictionfanblog.wordpress.com

Gustav von Aschenbach has achieved the status and recognition accorded only to the greats of literature. He has worked hard for it, self-disciplined since youth, and is now in the midst of creation of a hefty work which will, he is sure, add to his renown. One day while out walking, he sees a red-haired traveller, and this sparks in him the desire to travel too. There, in the Hotel des Bains, he sees for the first time a young Polish boy, whose name he learns is Tadzio, and over the next few weeks his admiration for the boy’s perfect godlike beauty grows into an obsession that will lead him into a kind of madness…

The book is filled with references and allusions to the philosophers and mythologies of Ancient Greece (much of which sailed straight over my head, but it didn’t matter). At first his admiration is purely artistic, one creator of beautiful things recognising the superb work of another, this perfect boy created by the gods. Slowly, however, the sensual creeps in, and the desire for recognition and even love. Meantime there are rumours that a pestilence is spreading through the city, though the city fathers are doing everything to hide it from the lucrative foreign tourists. The story of an ageing man’s desire for a very young boy could so easily have degenerated into a sordid and unpleasant tale, but it’s not really about that – it’s about the impact of this sudden obsession on Aschenbach. It is the story of a man who has lived through his art coming face to face with feelings he has never allowed to surface before, may never even have been aware of. He will risk any indignity for a glance or – oh! - a smile! At first happy with his staid appearance as a distinguished elderly gentleman, soon he is thinking about what to wear and how to dress his hair like a young debutante going to her first ball.

There is a subtle vein of humour and mockery throughout that for me stops the story from becoming a full-scale tragedy. Mann uses imagery from mythology brilliantly to show how Aschenbach is hiding from reality, while Aschenbach uses the words of the Ancient Greeks to justify his actions to himself. I felt throughout that, despite his obvious sexual attraction to Tadzio, in reality Aschenbach thought of him as a beautiful work of art to be looked at but not touched, and that saved this from sinking into Lolita territory for me.

There is also an unsettling air of menace in parts, externally from the miasma rising from the stagnant canals and the growing fears of the pestilence, and internally, from the breaking down of Aschenbach’s character and dignity as he becomes ever less able to control his obsession. There are moments of grotesqueness that almost take the book towards horror – recurring appearances of red-haired men who seem more menacing each time, an odd episode of a group of players whose performance turns into something out of a carnivalesque nightmare, that dream sequence, full of Pan imagery and chaos. As it moves towards that death in the title, the final impression is of the mind of an artist driven to delirium by a beauty beyond anything of his own creating and destroyed by the sudden surfacing of desires repressed and unsuspected for a lifetime. I loved this one.

www.fictionfanblog.wordpress.com

review.text: Relato de belleza etérea y personificada en Venecia con grandes descripciones y miradas al introspectivo de nuestro protagonista (eje seductor) y Tadzio (eje seducido), con grandes lecciones de vida y reflexiones acerca del arte, belleza, el espíritu racional, las vacaciones y los espacios ínfimos y eternos en el tiempo. El relato juega con todo este simbolismo expresado en descripciones con precisión estética y sensible, con exactitud y hermosura Todas las descripciones y el léxico empleado por Aschenbach nos transporta a las tierras y lagunas pantanosas misteriosas y empedradas de la región del Véneto. El protagonista juega a medio camino como un mero espectador omnisciente del relato, observando con delicadeza tras un espejo el espíritu y el atractivo encanto de la figura de Tadzio, aprendida en océanos, en similes precedidos a la ternura y perfección de dioses (griegos) Por otro lado, todo Tadzio es el dicho deseado detrás del espejo y se nos presenta como uno divinidad a palabras del profesor, lo que dota al relato de una bellísima misticidad en la que se combinan arte y belleza en su mismo puro sentido y en la que objeto mayor y menor son tanto observados como observado Debemos comprender el peso que tiene la gran figura de Tadzio en el profesor, ya que este dota a Aschenbach de sentido a su vida y tanto a su preceso como posterioridad vital. El profesor no podría seguir viviendo de un modo metafórico y metafísico sin la presencia de esta divinidad humana y sencilla tan grande a sus ojos en su entorno tanto idílico como apoteósico 10/10
review.text_raw:
Relato de belleza etérea y personificada en Venecia con grandes descripciones y miradas al introspectivo de nuestro protagonista (eje seductor) y Tadzio (eje seducido), con grandes lecciones de vida y reflexiones acerca del arte, belleza, el espíritu racional, las vacaciones y los espacios ínfimos y eternos en el tiempo. El relato juega con todo este simbolismo expresado en descripciones con precisión estética y sensible, con exactitud y hermosura

Todas las descripciones y el léxico empleado por Aschenbach nos transporta a las tierras y lagunas pantanosas misteriosas y empedradas de la región del Véneto. El protagonista juega a medio camino como un mero espectador omnisciente del relato, observando con delicadeza tras un espejo el espíritu y el atractivo encanto de la figura de Tadzio, aprendida en océanos, en similes precedidos a la ternura y perfección de dioses (griegos)

Por otro lado, todo Tadzio es el dicho deseado detrás del espejo y se nos presenta como uno divinidad a palabras del profesor, lo que dota al relato de una bellísima misticidad en la que se combinan arte y belleza en su mismo puro sentido y en la que objeto mayor y menor son tanto observados como observado

Debemos comprender el peso que tiene la gran figura de Tadzio en el profesor, ya que este dota a Aschenbach de sentido a su vida y tanto a su preceso como posterioridad vital. El profesor no podría seguir viviendo de un modo metafórico y metafísico sin la presencia de esta divinidad humana y sencilla tan grande a sus ojos en su entorno tanto idílico como apoteósico

10/10

review.text: The novel poses one of those insoluble problems that arise from the problematic nature of our nature. Since I could not express it better, I bring here the words that Vargas Llosa collected in the prologue to my edition referring to instincts: “although their presence always entails a risk for the individual and a threat of dissolution and violence for society, their total exile impoverishes life, depriving it of that exaltation and intoxication – the party and the adventure – that are also a necessity of being.” At the head of these “dangerous” instincts are those that have to do with sexuality, especially when the object of desire is morally unacceptable. These problems had to be faced by Gustav Aschenbach, a famous man of letters, endowed with talent and, no less important, with an iron will, severe discipline and great ambition. His health problems isolated him from the world very early on, preventing him from enjoying the carefree abandon of youth. Perhaps for this reason, his favorite word was always resist, a motto that marked his character and his work. The heroes of his books embodied the figure of Saint Sebastian: to remain firm despite affliction and torment, despite misery, abandonment and physical weakness, despite vice, passion and a thousand other impediments. The heroism of his characters was that of weakness, the heroism of those who managed to overcome their vices and hardships.

review.text: "The observations and encounters of a man of solitude and few words are at once more nebulous and more intense than those of a gregarious man, his thoughts more ponderable, more bizarre and never without a hint of sadness." The novella features Gustav von Aschenbach, a famous author in his early 50s, who visits Venice and becomes increasingly obsessed by the sight of a boy in a family of Polish tourists—Tadzio. On the surface, the book may appear polarizing because of its plot, but as we dive deeper into the beauty of the words and allusions, come to realise that there is more to it. The book explores plenty of themes including, but not limited to, the tragedy of an artist, obsession with beauty and death, and poses many philosophical questions asking what love and beauty truly are, and how to define them. The flaw of the protagonist is evident, which is explicitly acknowledged and wonderfully portrayed. The book references Socrates' and Plato's works like Symposium and Phaedrus, respectively, but having not read those works, I'm afraid much of it failed to allude me. All these, on top of the beautiful prose, gives the book an extra edge, making it mesmerizing and poignant at the same time.
review.text_raw:
"The observations and encounters of a man of solitude and few words are at once more nebulous and more intense than those of a gregarious man, his thoughts more ponderable, more bizarre and never without a hint of sadness."

The novella features Gustav von Aschenbach, a famous author in his early 50s, who visits Venice and becomes increasingly obsessed by the sight of a boy in a family of Polish tourists—Tadzio.

On the surface, the book may appear polarizing because of its plot, but as we dive deeper into the beauty of the words and allusions, come to realise that there is more to it. The book explores plenty of themes including, but not limited to, the tragedy of an artist, obsession with beauty and death, and poses many philosophical questions asking what love and beauty truly are, and how to define them. The flaw of the protagonist is evident, which is explicitly acknowledged and wonderfully portrayed.

The book references Socrates' and Plato's works like Symposium and Phaedrus, respectively, but having not read those works, I'm afraid much of it failed to allude me.

All these, on top of the beautiful prose, gives the book an extra edge, making it mesmerizing and poignant at the same time.

review.text: Đôi khi mình không hiểu mục đích viết cuốn này của tác giả là gì. Ngôn ngữ khá là dài dòng quanh co, nói nhiều vào khía cạnh nghệ thuật và bàn luận về cái đẹp, thẩm mĩ. Một ông giáo sư quý tộc đột nhiên có tình cảm với một cậu bé con Ba Lan đẹp trai mà ông mới nhìn lần đầu tiên, thậm chí họ còn không có sự tiếp xúc tương tác nào với nhau. Họ không nói cùng một ngôn ngữ, nhưng ngôn ngữ duy nhất mà ông có thể hiểu được từ cậu bé đó là vẻ đẹp đáng yêu tuyệt trần của cậu - chính là cái mĩ học, cái đẹp trên hết thảy vượt qua cuộc sống phàm tục đáng chán này. Nhờ có cậu mà ông giáo sư cảm thấy muốn được sống, để hàng ngày được chiêm ngưỡng, được nhìn ngắm cậu cho thoả mối tình đơn phương này. Cuốn sách tập trung vào cảm xúc nhân vật, cảm xúc của tình yêu cái đẹp, tôn vinh cảm cảm thụ mĩ học, văn học, nghệ thuật chứ không có cốt truyện rõ ràng hay sự phát triển tâm lý nhân vật. Tác giả miên man chìm đắm trong suy nghĩ về cái đẹp, cảm xúc của tình yêu, của khát vọng, của đắm say, mong ngóng (thông qua ông giáo sư) hơn là xây dựng một mạch truyện chặt chẽ. Kết truyện cũng giống như chính tên của truyện thôi, một cái kết khá viên mãn và hạnh phúc đối với ông già.

review.text: „Pomyleniec nie znał i nie chciał już nic innego, tylko ścigać nieprzerwanie przedmiot, który go zapalał, marzyć o nim, gdy był nieobecny, i zwyczajem kochającym darzyć sam jego cień tkliwymi słowami. Samotność, obczyzna i szczęście późnego i głębokiego odurzenia ośmielały i namawiały go do wybaczenia sobie najdziwniejszych rzeczy bez obawy i rumieńca, co też się stało, gdy późnym wieczorem, wróciwszy z Wenecji, zatrzymał się na pierwszym piętrze hotelu przy drzwiach pięknego chłopca, oparł czoło w całkowitym upojeniu o zawiasy drzwi i nie mógł się długo stamtąd oderwać narażając się na to, że może być przydybany i pochwycony w tak obłąkanym położeniu.“

review.text: Khá hay. Tadzio hiện lên đúng như một thiếu niên trong thần thoại Hy Lạp, các kiểu thiếu niên mà Zeus vẫn lén lút xuống ngủ cùng. Trong đầu mình khi hình dung Tadzio thì cứ tự động nhớ lại những bức tượng ở bảo tàng nghệ thuật ở Lille, nhất là bức Hermaphrodite, kể cũng hơi kỳ vì giới tính không giống nhau nhưng vẻ đẹp mềm mại ngây thơ lại quyến rũ thì chắc là tương đồng. Cả hành trình sa ngã của vị học giả mà trước đó có phần khắc kỷ cũng là một chủ đề rất hay và được thể hiện rất thành công.

review.text: Mann'ın eşsiz anlatımıyla yazılmış bir eseri daha. Behçet Necatigil tarafından Almanca'dan çevrilmiş. Hikayede, Mann, Aschenbach'ın yaşadığı trajedileri ve çelişkileri kusursuz bir anlatımla sürükleyici bir şekilde aktarmış. Uzun bir öykü sayılabilecek eserde okurken bir sonraki sayfayı sabırsızlıkla bekledim. Naçizane tavsiyemdir.

review.text: Una lectura corta pero intensa. La historia de una obsesión en edad madura. Una obsesión que hace que el protagonista se de cuenta del paso del tiempo como nunca gantes lo ha hecho. Una obsesión que hace que el protagonista se pregunte si ha hecho bien en huir de si mismo toda la vida para ser aceptado por la sociedad. Una obsesión rodeada de muerte. Muy recomendable. 📖❤️

review.text: Gros gros coup de coeur. C’est littéralement le principe du balancier dionysiaque-apollinien de la création condensé en moins de 150 pages. Du pur génie. Si vous avez lu La Naissance de la tragédie de Nietzsche vous DEVEZ lire La Mort à Venise !!!!

Si vous avez lu La Naissance de la tragédie de Nietzsche vous DEVEZ lire La Mort à Venise !!!!

review.text: Begonnen in de Duitse versie, maar helaas toch iets te pittig. Kon het verhaal wel volgen, maar miste te veel en daarmee de verdieping. Ben blij dat ik heb besloten naar het NL te switchen. Het is immers prettig als je het begrijpt als je iets leest. Aschenbach had de Poolse fam moeten waarschuwen. En Tadzio is veel te jong voor hem, zelfs om naar te kijken. Ik kreeg daarom Kan Je Niet Maken van Prins S. en de Geit maar niet uit mijn hoofd tijdens het lezen. En nog altijd dreunt deze plaat lekker door. Twee tips dus: De Dood In Venetië lezen en luisteren naar die plaat.

review.text: SMRT U VENECIJI-TOMAS MAN ✒️"Mi bismo najradije porekli taj ponor i stekli dostojanstvo, ali kako se god okrenuli, on nas privlači. Stoga se odričemo, recimo,: razorne spoznaje, jer spoznaja, Fedre, nema dostojanstva ni strogosti; ona zna, razumije, oprašta, bez oslonca i oblika; spoznaja je naklonjena ponoru, ona je sama ponor. Nju dakle odlučno odbacujemo i stoga težimo jedino za ljepotom, što će reći za jednostavnošću, veličinom i novom strogošću, za drugom bezazlenošću i formom. Ali forma i bezazlenost, Fedre, vode nas do opijenosti i požude, vode plemenita čovjeka možda i do jezovitih opakih osjećaja, koje njegova lijepa strogost odbacuje kao nešto sramotno, vode ga do ponora, one ga također vode do ponora. Nas pjesnike, velim ti, vode onamo jer se mi ne možemo vinuti u visine, mi se možemo samo izopačiti. A sad ja, Fedre, idem, a ti ostani ovdje; i tek kad me ne budeš više vidio, otiđi i ti." Spremna za Klasiciranje @bookatorium 🧘‍♀️🤓 #7sensesofabook #tomasman #smrtuveneciji

review.text: Primer de tot, la traducció al català de Joan Fontcuberta és meravellosa… En segon lloc, no havia llegit res de Thomas Mann i he de confesar que no sabia què esperar-me… però és una obra que no m’ha deixat indiferent. Una lloança a la bellesa i també una advertència sobre la follia de l’enamorament envers la bellesa sensible… La bellesa envoltada de mort, la mort que enalteix la bellesa, la bellesa i la mort portades a extrems per la follia.

review.text: Insuportavelmente bem escrito, mas vagamente entediante, este quase monólogo do velho artista alemão que se perde de amor platonico por um belo jovem polaco. Nem a rara coincidência de Veneza estar assolada por epidemia vinda da Ásia devolvem interesse a esta cloaca. Melhor voltar a Fedro.

review.text: La muerte en Venecia - Thomas Mann (3/5) “Because man loves and honors man as long as he is not able to judge him, and desire is a product of lacking knowledge.” Sinopsis del servidor: Gustav Von Aschenbach (50) alcanza el mayor éxito como escritor y pasa por una crisis existencial donde pierde el sentido e inspiración para generar nuevas ideas, toma la resolución de tomar un viaje que renueve la emoción, de la cuál piensa, nunca ha gozado. Decide como destino una isla de moda, pero el mal tiempo lo hace abandonarla e ir en otra dirección, a la idealizada y artística Venecia. Aquí pasa todo y a la vez casi nada. Gustav se encuentra con algo que lo vislumbra, algo que despierta su "sentido estético", innumerables cosas debe de haber en Venecia que pudieran despertar este sentido, sin embargo, el origen de éste en nuestro protagonista fue un adolescente polaco de 14 años, que dentro de muy poco tiempo pasará a ser su objeto de más profunda obsesión. Que me pareció?: Es un libro ligero, cuenta con 123 páginas (Edición Debols!llo) y se puede leer de manera fluida, lleva un ritmo bastante lento puesto que en la primera mitad del libro no pasa casi nada, si el libro fuese más extenso probablemente lo habría abandonado. Aschenbach pasa mucho tiempo absorto en su mente, encontré algunos pensamientos valiosos e interesantes dentro de sus divagaciones. La forma en la que el autor describe la fisionomía de los personajes, me pareció genial, uno se puede crear una imagen mental muy detallada con semejantes descripciones, lo mismo va para los entornos y situaciones, aunque en cierto punto, sobre todo en la parte estética, siento que abusa un poco de los adornos que utiliza. Algo digno de mención es la relación que establece el autor entre la belleza y el envejecimiento, plasmada de una manera casi grotesca en aquellos que pretenden ser algo que claramente ya no son. Mi principal descontento con este libro es su trama. Cuando comencé a leerlo no sabía de qué trataba, solo comencé y conforme avanzaba seguía sin saberlo, de pronto ya me encontraba en la página 60 y apenas se empezaba a esclarecer un poco el rumbo que tomaría la obra. Mencioné que el autor hace hincapié en las descripciones, cuando estas son sobre Tadzio, el joven polaco de 14 años, no pude evitar sentirme incomodo, ya que fue en los momentos donde sentí que más se abusó de los elementos descriptivos. En segundo plano tenemos la ciudad de Venecia, azotada por una súbita epidemia de cólera, la cual permanece oculta ante los ojos pero no ante la nariz. Las callejas inundadas por un nauseabundo olor a desinfectante espantan a los extranjeros, dejando a nuestro enamorado permanentemente “solitario”. La narrativa del libro oscila entre estos dos planos, salta de uno a otro y solo en momentos breves se estaciona en ellos, cuando lo hace, la trama avanza y se torna más interesante, como por ejemplo, cuando toma la decisión de irse de Venecia y cuando está a punto de hacerlo una profunda nostalgia lo invade y lo obliga a volver, puesto que sabe que si se va, lo hará para siempre. Esta escena para mi es de las más valiosas en la novela, pero se ve interrumpida por la reintroducción de Tadzio, quién opaca la reciente conclusión de Gustav. Por estas situaciones es que siento que el libro es un poco atropellado y nunca logra establecerse un hilo argumental fluido. "La razón es y sólo debe ser esclava de las pasiones y no puede aspirar a ninguna otra función que la de servir y obedecerlas". David Hume Al comienzo de la novela vemos a un Aschenbach sin pasiones, meditabundo y que se guía completamente por su razonamiento de naturaleza estoica y rudimentaria. Cada página que pasa vemos su razonamiento en declive y su obsesión ante el objeto de deseo en aumento, creando un enorme conflicto en su ser, la pasión empieza a tomar las riendas de su actuar, dejándole de importar lo mal visto que estén las acciones que realice y muy pronto la repercusión que tendrá su estadía en su salud. Recomendaría este libro? Quizá mis comentarios no hayan parecido los más alentadores, es un libro que a primeras no me pareció bueno, de hecho pensé que el libro estaba más cerca de ser un 2 que un 3 pero conforme escribía esta reseña y profundizaba en la lectura empecé a digerirlo y mi pensamiento cambio, no sería el primer libro que vendría a mi mente si alguien me solicita una recomendación, pero si pienso que es un buen libro y puede gustar a alguien más. Si tienes curiosidad sobre Mann (como yo) te lo recomendaría, ya que considero que es un libro muy ligero, corto y diferente, quizá el más conocido del autor. Si te interesa la estética, el libro está lleno de metáforas y alusiones a la belleza que encontraras fascinantes. Pd: si próximamente viajaras a Venecia probablemente deberías postergar la lectura de este libro.

Aschenbach pasa mucho tiempo absorto en su mente, encontré algunos pensamientos valiosos e interesantes dentro de sus divagaciones. La forma en la que el autor describe la fisionomía de los personajes, me pareció genial, uno se puede crear una imagen mental muy detallada con semejantes descripciones, lo mismo va para los entornos y situaciones, aunque en cierto punto, sobre todo en la parte estética, siento que abusa un poco de los adornos que utiliza. Algo digno de mención es la relación que establece el autor entre la belleza y el envejecimiento, plasmada de una manera casi grotesca en aquellos que pretenden ser algo que claramente ya no son.
Mi principal descontento con este libro es su trama. Cuando comencé a leerlo no sabía de qué trataba, solo comencé y conforme avanzaba seguía sin saberlo, de pronto ya me encontraba en la página 60 y apenas se empezaba a esclarecer un poco el rumbo que tomaría la obra. Mencioné que el autor hace hincapié en las descripciones, cuando estas son sobre Tadzio, el joven polaco de 14 años, no pude evitar sentirme incomodo, ya que fue en los momentos donde sentí que más se abusó de los elementos descriptivos.
En segundo plano tenemos la ciudad de Venecia, azotada por una súbita epidemia de cólera, la cual permanece oculta ante los ojos pero no ante la nariz. Las callejas inundadas por un nauseabundo olor a desinfectante espantan a los extranjeros, dejando a nuestro enamorado permanentemente “solitario”.
La narrativa del libro oscila entre estos dos planos, salta de uno a otro y solo en momentos breves se estaciona en ellos, cuando lo hace, la trama avanza y se torna más interesante, como por ejemplo, cuando toma la decisión de irse de Venecia y cuando está a punto de hacerlo una profunda nostalgia lo invade y lo obliga a volver, puesto que sabe que si se va, lo hará para siempre. Esta escena para mi es de las más valiosas en la novela, pero se ve interrumpida por la reintroducción de Tadzio, quién opaca la reciente conclusión de Gustav. Por estas situaciones es que siento que el libro es un poco atropellado y nunca logra establecerse un hilo argumental fluido.

"La razón es y sólo debe ser esclava de las pasiones y no puede aspirar a ninguna otra función que la de servir y obedecerlas". David Hume

Al comienzo de la novela vemos a un Aschenbach sin pasiones, meditabundo y que se guía completamente por su razonamiento de naturaleza estoica y rudimentaria. Cada página que pasa vemos su razonamiento en declive y su obsesión ante el objeto de deseo en aumento, creando un enorme conflicto en su ser, la pasión empieza a tomar las riendas de su actuar, dejándole de importar lo mal visto que estén las acciones que realice y muy pronto la repercusión que tendrá su estadía en su salud.

Recomendaría este libro? Quizá mis comentarios no hayan parecido los más alentadores, es un libro que a primeras no me pareció bueno, de hecho pensé que el libro estaba más cerca de ser un 2 que un 3 pero conforme escribía esta reseña y profundizaba en la lectura empecé a digerirlo y mi pensamiento cambio, no sería el primer libro que vendría a mi mente si alguien me solicita una recomendación, pero si pienso que es un buen libro y puede gustar a alguien más. Si tienes curiosidad sobre Mann (como yo) te lo recomendaría, ya que considero que es un libro muy ligero, corto y diferente, quizá el más conocido del autor. Si te interesa la estética, el libro está lleno de metáforas y alusiones a la belleza que encontraras fascinantes.

Pd: si próximamente viajaras a Venecia probablemente deberías postergar la lectura de este libro.

review.text: Che dire, mi aspettavo di molto ma molto meglio. La cosa migliore del libro e' immaginarsi Dirk Bogarde, la Mangano e l'attore che faceva Tadzio nel film di Visconti. che secondo me (perdonate l'eresia) ha reso il libro meglio di Mann.

review.text: Mann is clearly an artist with words, which he doesn't like to spare. But, sometimes there are too many of them. The endless sentences know how to discourage me from seeing the bigger picture and the meaning of the sentence itself, so I have to re-read them sometimes. Apart from those technical writing anomalies, there is great symbolism in it where he ties the mythical analogies with the inner struggles of the opposites that take place throughout the novella. Sinking progressively in his immorality, Aschenbach (the main protagonist) eats the "forbidden fruit" at the end and trespasses along the River Styx to the underworld. This was Venice, the flattering and suspect beauty--this city, half fairy tale and half tourist trap, in whose insalubrious air the arts once rankly and voluptuously blossomed, where composers have been inspired to lulling tones of somniferous eroticism." It is this atmosphere into which Aschenbach languidly slips.

review.text: The real boy was not 14. He was 10. The real fantasy, then, does not even have the romanticizing sanction of classical pederasty, but was the daydream of what the average person might imagine to have been a common pedophile, a prowler at the periphery of playgrounds, the type of person one speaks of putting not in but under the jail rather than awarding the Nobel Prize. Yet here we are, rereading perhaps the 20th century's greatest novella, Thomas Mann's masterpiece of 1912. Gustav von Aschenbach is a middle-aged German author of great renown, one who renounced his youthful Romanticism to become an artist of Classical restraint and moral greatness, one taught in schoolbooks and granted an honorary title. The aristocratic "von" was a gift of the state; he is of bourgeois origin, the son of a German civil servant and a Bohemian woman whose own father was a music conductor. As in all of Mann's works—as in Mann's own life—this tension between the stolid North/West and the fervid South/East will structure the tale. Aschenbach, long a widower, exhausted from his literary labors, takes a stroll through Munich one day. Upon seeing first a Byzantine church with "mystical" inscriptions above its door—the novella's first hint of "the east"—and then, at the gate of a graveyard, a red-headed stranger, "clearly not of Bavarian stock," his red hair likely signifying Jewish heritage, the weary author begins to yearn for escape to a Southern or Eastern place (but "not all the way to the tigers") to liberate him self from his respectable and somewhat duplicitous martyrdom to literary form. Why duplicitous? And does form not have two faces? Is it not moral and amoral at the same time—moral insofar as form is the product and expression of discipline, but amoral and indeed immoral insofar as it harbors within itself by nature a certain moral indifference and indeed is essentially bent on forcing the moral realm to stoop under its proud and absolute scepter? Much as his own art might be a moral imposture, its marmoreal solidity no less an expression of the will-to-power than some more overtly rebellious performance would be, he still thinks a trip might introduce a lightness or liveliness into a literary style that conspicuously lacks it. To Venice, "this sunken queen of cities," he goes. Like Mann's later German tourists to Italy in the anti-fascist parable Mario and the Magician , Aschenbach finds the warm Catholic South a stew of mountebanks and swindlers. Even en route, shipboard, he is forced to endure the company of an old man in youthful drag, his cheeks rouged and a wig on his head; then, when he arrives, he is nearly robbed by an unlicensed gondolier. Suffocating from the heat, he tries to leave almost as soon as he arrives—he'd long been sickly and had fallen ill in the city once before—but then chides himself for his timidity and takes advantage of the accident of misdirected luggage to stay. And the city does offer him one attraction: a beautiful 14-year-old Polish boy named Tadzio, an ephebe walled off from Aschenbach's stricken attention by his wealthy mother, a governess, and a company of sisters. "It was a face reminiscent of Greek statues from the noblest period of antiquity," the imperious narrator, a narrator as Classical in form and proportion and irony as Aschenbach's own are implied to be, "It was the face of Eros…" How better to escape the prison of his Northern soul than in an imaginative dalliance with the Eastern boy on the Southern lagoon, a warm welling-up of aristocratic Greek love in the fevered heart of the German bourgeois? The sight of this lively adolescent figure, seductive and chaste, lovely as a tender young god, emerging from the depths of the sky and the sea with dripping locks and escaping the clutches of the elements—it all gave rise to mythic images. Like Ulysses and The Waste Land , like the poetry of Yeats and the plays of O'Neill, Death in Venice has an apparently realistic or naturalistic surface secretly subtended by myth. In Mann's case, at least three antecedent texts are running like code beneath the surface to generate the novella's superstructure. First is Plato's Phaedrus , with its coruscating dialogue on the ethics of pederasty and the divine frenzy to which every true poet is subject, a divinity heralded on earth by the beautiful boy. Second is Nietzsche's Birth of Tragedy and its definition of the ideal drama as one in which the roiling, tearing, burning, brutal flux of life, typified by the Eastern god Dionysus, is arrested in cool, still images by the Greek artist pledged to Apollo. Third is the Bacchae of Euripides, source of the novella's plot and of its finest and most famous passage, too long to quote here, Aschenbach's apocalyptic dream of a fatal Dionysian revel, a party to end the world. For this is a masque of death. Venice, that squalid city with nothing left to sell but itself, that rouged corpse liquefying into the marsh from which it emerged, is too protective of its precious tourist trade to inform the public that "the Asiatic cholera" has entered the city. The pestilence originated in the warm swamps of the Ganges delta, rising on the foul-smelling air of that lushly uninhabitable primeval world, that wilderness of islands avoided by humankind where tigers lurk in bamboo thickets. Aschenbach hadn't gone to the tigers, so the tigers have come to him. Like Dionysus himself, the plague rises out of the east to conquer a German burgher besotted with a Greek Apollo, in a parodic reversal of the 19th-century progressive self-confidence with which Hegel proclaimed the westward march of liberty from Athens to the Atlantic seaboard. Aschenach has been informed about the coming plague by plain-dealing German newspapers and an honest Englishman, but how can he leave off looking at Tadzio? The boy has inflamed his soul: "His intellect was in labor…Strangely fertile intellect between a mind and a body!" For the Greek philosophers, the beardless youth, feminine without the inconveniently fecund animality of female flesh, allows the male intellectual to propagate his sublime thoughts in spirit alone. In the modern world, this won't do. The Jewish and Christian testaments outlaw Greek love and place in its stead an ideal of natural law that enshrines the family; the Enlightenment, for all its revolt against religion, reinforces the heteronormative standard with its ideal of the nation whose warm heart was the domestic hearth, a secular paradigm fulfilled only in the present century with its democratizing extension to same-sex couples. Aschenbach's lonely desire—are we really to imagine Tadzio as his first boy?—is out of time, out of place, even as its the source of the art for which his place and time do him such honor: It is surely for the best that the world knows only the lovely work and not also its origins, not the conditions under which it came into being; for knowledge of the origins from which flows the artist's inspiration would surely often confuse the world, repel it, and thus vitiate the effects of excellence. He stays in Venice to keep gazing at the ephebe, even as his doom draws nearer—not only the doom of the cholera, but his own moral squalor. Sold on hair dye and rouge by a canny barber ("anyway, we're only as old as we feel in our hearts and minds," he says like a spokesman in a contemporary commercial), Aschenbach, resembling Euripides's cross-dressing Pentheus, has become the grotesque elderly man masquerading as a youth he'd seen on the ship, has become just another Venetian fraud, a dressed-up decadent on a death march. He stalks the child through the labyrinthine city—a child, we are told, who's grown wary of the old man's appetitive gaze—and finally expires on the beach from the plague as he watches Tadzio wade into the lagoon. But not before a final revelation. Feverish and delirious, he imagines himself as Socrates and pronounces a moral no less stern than what the philosopher says in the Phaedrus when he instructs his young charge that sex, even the pederastic variety, is a category mistake, an inadequate substitution of rutting bodies for what ought to be coupling souls: "For you must know that we poets cannot walk the path of beauty without Eros joining our company and even making himself our leader… Do you see, then, that we poets can be neither wise nor honorable, that we necessarily go astray, that we necessarily remain dissolute adventurers of emotion? The masterly demeanor of our style is a lie and a folly, our fame and our honor a sham, the confidence accorded us by our public utterly ridiculous, the education of the populace and of the young by means of art a risky enterprise that ought not to be allowed. For how can a person succeed in educating others who has an inborn, irremediable, and natural affinity for the abyss?" There is, then, nothing of which we might accuse Mann. When it came to his life, and when it came to the boys in his life, he only looked; he never touched. And when it came to his art, and the boys in his art, he told the truth, or most of it. While Aschenbach's Platonic epiphany suggests it's better not to make any art at all, Mann made it anyway, but encased its fiery, soul- and society-destroying Eros in a heavy carapace of irony—form as quarantine, allowing us to observe the workings of the disease at a dispassionate distance without catching it ourselves. This, too, is honest; Mann is no rouge-bearing barber; he is at one with 20th-century aesthetics' persistent and various reminders—everything from urinals to metanovels—that art is not life. For all this, and for an eloquence imperishable in every translation I've tried, Death in Venice more than earns its laurels. Does Mann's sole dishonesty, the little white lie with which I began—that the boy was not 14 (questionable enough) but just 10, and looked it—mean anything? Does the move from pederast to pedophile abrade our appreciation? Aschenbach reflects: There is inborn in every artistic disposition an indulgent and treacherous tendency to accept injustice when it produces beauty and to respond with complicity and even admiration when the aristocrats of this world get preferential treatment. Sure—I've read Nietzsche too—but may we not draw the line somewhere ? For one thing, this novella is treated casually in criticism as a work about homosexuality, but then we don't speak of Lolita —Dolores Haze was 12—as a work about heterosexual desire per se. The 20th century, that century in which sex was liberated, may have been the century of pedophilia, gay, straight, and in-between, from Freud's putative evasion of his patients' testimony to Foucault's rumored pleasures among the crypts of Tunisia. Gide, Wilde, Isherwood, Ginsberg, Burroughs—all liked their boys young. The movie stars, the rock stars, and the rap stars of the century, from Jerry Lee Lewis to Jimmy Page to R. Kelly, preferred their girls the same way. John Money, the very man who gave us the terms "gender role," "gender identity," and "sexual orientation," invented another two-word phrase that failed to catch on but perhaps informed the others: "affectional pedophilia," which he "would not call…pathological in any way." Well, what can we expect of men? Yet in the 1970s we find a woman—albeit one who'd clearly read Mann—at work on an innovation in erotica, the boys' love genre, in the brilliant graphic novel The Heart of Thomas . The last two decades' digitally-enabled contagion of such quasi-pornography about boys for girls has lately begun to arise in the admittedly controversial testimony of "detransitioners" , while it has arguably also slipped into the mainstream of young-adult publishing, with its panoply of progressive gay-boy love stories that often seem to be read, just as they often were written, by adult women. As Toni Morrison suggests in her final novel, God Help the Child —but as Mann intimates no less in Death in Venice —it may be that no hands are clean. And it may be that when we pull one thread, the whole fabric comes undone. In another of his early novellas about the travails of the artist, Tonio Kröger , Mann could be said to reverse the meaning of Death in Venice . There he takes the artist's perversion for granted and argues instead that his deepest longing is for the normal world, the adult life, the calm of companionate marriage, the common decency of the good burgher: Always with burning cheeks he had stood in his dark corner and suffered for you, you blond, you living, you happy ones! To besmirch the innocence we both idealize and know we can't have—is this the modern artist's destiny, in our time of democratized eroticism just as in Mann's modernist world of aesthetic aristocracy? How, I've always wondered, could it be any other way? Of course Apollo's pediment is perversion; the statue sits over a sewer; where else? "The artist is the brother of the felon and the madman," the devil tells the composer in Doctor Faustus . Hear, hear, I thought when I read that, and thanks, mein Herr, for the bitter truth. But when I remember that the boy was 10—which fact I only learned the other day—I ask myself whether we should have been so cavalier about the gods of the hearth.

As Toni Morrison suggests in her final novel, God Help the Child—but as Mann intimates no less in Death in Venice—it may be that no hands are clean. And it may be that when we pull one thread, the whole fabric comes undone.

In another of his early novellas about the travails of the artist, Tonio Kröger, Mann could be said to reverse the meaning of Death in Venice. There he takes the artist's perversion for granted and argues instead that his deepest longing is for the normal world, the adult life, the calm of companionate marriage, the common decency of the good burgher:
Always with burning cheeks he had stood in his dark corner and suffered for you, you blond, you living, you happy ones!
To besmirch the innocence we both idealize and know we can't have—is this the modern artist's destiny, in our time of democratized eroticism just as in Mann's modernist world of aesthetic aristocracy? How, I've always wondered, could it be any other way? Of course Apollo's pediment is perversion; the statue sits over a sewer; where else? "The artist is the brother of the felon and the madman," the devil tells the composer in Doctor Faustus. Hear, hear, I thought when I read that, and thanks, mein Herr, for the bitter truth. But when I remember that the boy was 10—which fact I only learned the other day—I ask myself whether we should have been so cavalier about the gods of the hearth.

review.text: 4/5 2019’un son ayının son gününde, tek oturuşta okuyup bitirdiğim çıtır bir kitap oluyor kendisi. Gerçi çıtır dediğime bakmayın yazarın betimlemeleri biraz uzun olunca ilk başlarda sayfalar öyle su gibi akıp gitmedi. 😅 Ama “yarım mı bıraksam acaba” da dedirtmedi. Her neyse kitap genel olarak hep aynı tempoda gidiyor. Bir tek son sahnesi okur için vurucu olabilir. Ama o da bir anda olup bitiyor. Pek bir şey anlaşılmıyor yani. Kısacası elinizde sürünmeyeceğini düşündüğüm güzel bir kitap. Keyifli okumalar.

review.text: 100/5 ⭐ "Lo que buscaba era un mundo exótico, que no tuviera relación alguna con el ambiente habitual, pero que no estuviese muy alejado. " 🛶 De entrada me veo en la obligación de decir que NO puedo recomendar éste libro a todo el mundo porque a muchos no les va a gustar. 🛶Desde el comienzo la prosa es BELLÍSIMA, por momentos lírica, por momentos muy enrevesado. Y en otros las descripciones son tan minuciosas y detalladas que parece que el paisaje se desliza ante los ojos del lector. 🛶Es mi primer encuentro con éste autor y creo que es mi primer Premio Nobel que leo. Thomas Mann lo ganó en 1929. 🛶 Un libro que habla de un hombre que viaja en soledad; Publicado en 1912, creo que fue un libro "provocador". Catalogado como una obra de ficción, supuestamente no es tan así, ya que el autor dice que todo lo escrito en ésta novela no fue inventado. Tampoco FUE un libro censurado (pensemos la época en que fue publicado). 🛶 “La muerte en Venecia” por medio de cinco capítulos, no es otra cosa que la historia de un hombre, su autor, consagrado de nombre Aschenbach, viudo con hijos, escritor de éxito que está atravesando como un bloqueo de escritor, incluso una crisis existencial que le impide crear nuevas obras. Ha tocado fondo en lo profesional y en su vida toda. Por ello, de manera impulsiva decide viajar a Venecia para recuperar la inspiración y volver a escribir. Es en el hotel en donde se aloja, donde coincide con un niño polaco de catorce años de edad de gran belleza que está junto a sus hermanas, institutriz y su mamá e inmediatamente este joven será objeto de obsesión de Aschenbach; Y a partir de aquí caerá poco a poco en el infierno. Éste niño representa todos los valores como juventud, belleza, inocencia que le falta a Aschenbach, que es un cincuentón. Mientras tanto, como drama de fondo, una peste se cierne sobre la ciudad. 🛶Como entré a ciegas a éste libro, tuve que investigar sobre su autor y descubrí que Thomas Mann nunca pudo aceptar su homosexualidad (estaba casado y tuvo seis hijos) De hecho, ésta obsesión quedó plasmada en ésta obra; Y es muy explícita en éste libro. Una vez más otro autor me confirma que mucho de lo que ellos vuelcan en sus libros, no es más que su inconsciente haciéndose consciente por medio de la prosa. 🛶Creo que Thomas Mann busca explorar la pasión a tal punto de caer en el ridículo y enseñarselo al lector. 🛶 Una novela corta que aborda muchos temas; el personaje inicia la novela marcado por la razón, el autodominio, la autodisciplina, para ir luego cayendo poco a poco en una pasión que lo llevará a la búsqueda permanente del placer hasta hacer el ridículo. 🛶 “La Muerte en Venecia” NO SE CENTRA en la atracción homoerótica que vive el protagonista hacia éste niño, sino que todo el drama surge de esa pasión casi dolorosa que lo llevará a perder su reconocimiento que tiene hasta caer en el más completo ridículo. Las referencias homosexuales que hay en el libro son sobre la admiración hacia la belleza, hacia la juventud, lo que me recordó un poco a “El retrato de Dorian Grey”de Oscar Wilde. 🛶La forma en que está narrado “La Muerte en Venecia” en lo personal me resultó poética pero debo decir que es algo denso, tiene muchos simbolismos y muchas referencias culturales. La historia es sencilla pero profunda ya que el narrador se mete en la cabeza de Aschenbach. 🛶El hecho de estar ambientado en Venecia y que el personaje esté atravesando un conflicto, no es para nada romántico; todo lo contrario, sentí la soledad del personaje que se manifiesta en su vejez, una soledad que no eligió, un amor platónico y la melancolía. 🛶 La muerte en Venecia es una novela que refiere a la soledad, los fantasmas que hacen a una persona cuando pasa mucho tiempo a solas consigo mismo; reflexiones así hay muchas. Y el ridículo en las páginas finales que somete el autor al protagonista es… TREMENDO. ¿Para qué llegar a eso, para qué anhelar la juventud y la belleza si…la muerte acecha?

review.text: I don't think I believe in the voluptuousness of doom. Some of the Sturm und Drang around the artist's dignity was interesting, and I did come to feel that Aschenbach is a tragic figure in that he has, in ways he's never recognized before, wasted his life. But I didn't get on with his projection of his psyche onto myth, the elevated sterility and slumber inducing bore of it. Higher hopes for the next Mann. I am a philistine.
review.text_raw: I don't think I believe in the voluptuousness of doom. Some of the Sturm und Drang around the artist's dignity was interesting, and I did come to feel that Aschenbach is a tragic figure in that he has, in ways he's never recognized before, wasted his life. But I didn't get on with his projection of his psyche onto myth, the elevated sterility and slumber inducing bore of it. Higher hopes for the next Mann. I am a philistine.

review.text: „𝐴𝑢𝑐𝘩 𝑝𝑒𝑟𝑠𝑜̈𝑛𝑙𝑖𝑐𝘩 𝑔𝑒𝑛𝑜𝑚𝑚𝑒𝑛 𝑖𝑠𝑡 𝑗𝑎 𝑑𝑖𝑒 𝐾𝑢𝑛𝑠𝑡 𝑒𝑖𝑛 𝑒𝑟𝘩𝑜̈𝘩𝑡𝑒𝑠 𝐿𝑒𝑏𝑒𝑛. 𝑆𝑖𝑒 𝑏𝑒𝑔𝑙𝑢̈𝑐𝑘𝑡 𝑡𝑖𝑒𝑓𝑒𝑟, 𝑠𝑖𝑒 𝑣𝑒𝑟𝑧𝑒𝘩𝑟𝑡 𝑟𝑎𝑠𝑐𝘩𝑒𝑟.“ 💫 Dieser Auszug aus Thomas Manns Novelle „Der Tod in Venedig“ veranschaulicht sehr gut, worum es darin geht. Kunst und Dekadenz stehen hier in einem Kontext, schließen sich nicht voneinander aus und können somit als ambivalent verstanden werden. Einerseits beglückt Kunst bzw. Luxus, andererseits verzehrt sie und bringt den Tod. Theodor W. Adorno beispielsweise benutzt die Begriffe Luxus und Kunst synonym und so könne man Dekadenz und Tod in diesem Kontext ebenfalls synonym verwenden. „Der Tod in Venedig“ ist dafür jedenfalls ein passendes Beispiel. Veröffentlicht wurde die Novelle im Jahre 1913, nachdem es 1911 erschienen ist. Wie so oft in Thomas Manns Werken, zeigt sich in dieser Novelle das Verhältnis zwischen Kunst, Geist und Leben. Seine Gestaltung bleibt dabei künstlerisch und ästhetisch, während er sich motivisch u.a. auf die Venedig-Literatur des Fin de Siècle bezieht. Er schreibt sehr strukturiert und mit vielen verschachtelten, aber sehr bildhaft beschriebenen Sätzen. Auch im Hinblick auf die Gattung lassen sich teilweise typische Merkmale, wie zum Beispiel die Kürze des Textes, aber auch untypische Merkmale, wie beispielsweise der komplexe Aufbau sowie die vorhandenen Elemente des klassischen Dramas, finden. Der Tod in Venedig besteht aus fünf Kapiteln und lehnt sich damit an das klassizistische Modell der fünfaktigen Tragödie an. Durch die literarische Umsetzung von Manns Reiseerlebnissen bleiben auch biographische Züge nicht aus, die sich nicht zuletzt auch in der Novelle „Der Tod in Venedig“ finden lassen. Seine 1911 getätigte Reise durch Venedig hat wohl im Wesentlichen dazu beigetragen. Dies sind einige Aspekte der Novelle, die durchaus interessant sind. Für mich war besonders der Bezug zur Dekadenz sehr interessant, weswegen mir die sogenannten „Todesboten“ beim Lesen direkt aufgefallen sind. „Der Tod in Venedig“ lässt viele Deutungsperspektiven zu und ist wohl nach jedem erneuten Lesen eine Offenbarung.

„𝐴𝑢𝑐𝘩  𝑝𝑒𝑟𝑠𝑜̈𝑛𝑙𝑖𝑐𝘩 𝑔𝑒𝑛𝑜𝑚𝑚𝑒𝑛 𝑖𝑠𝑡 𝑗𝑎 𝑑𝑖𝑒 𝐾𝑢𝑛𝑠𝑡 𝑒𝑖𝑛 𝑒𝑟𝘩𝑜̈𝘩𝑡𝑒𝑠 𝐿𝑒𝑏𝑒𝑛. 𝑆𝑖𝑒 𝑏𝑒𝑔𝑙𝑢̈𝑐𝑘𝑡 𝑡𝑖𝑒𝑓𝑒𝑟, 𝑠𝑖𝑒 𝑣𝑒𝑟𝑧𝑒𝘩𝑟𝑡 𝑟𝑎𝑠𝑐𝘩𝑒𝑟.“ 💫

Dieser Auszug aus Thomas Manns Novelle „Der Tod in Venedig“ veranschaulicht sehr gut, worum es darin geht. Seine Gestaltung bleibt dabei künstlerisch und ästhetisch, während er sich motivisch u.a. auf die Venedig-Literatur des Fin de Siècle bezieht. Er schreibt sehr strukturiert und mit vielen verschachtelten, aber sehr bildhaft beschriebenen Sätzen. Auch im Hinblick auf die Gattung lassen sich teilweise typische Merkmale, wie zum Beispiel die Kürze des Textes, aber auch untypische Merkmale, wie beispielsweise der komplexe Aufbau sowie die vorhandenen Elemente des klassischen Dramas, finden. review.text: Βαθύς αναστεναγμός. Πασίγνωστο έργο, πασίγνωστου Νομπελίστα. Τι μπορεί να πάει στραβά; Επειδή είναι πολλά, παρακαλώ διαβάστε με υπομονή πώς διαβάζω τα «μεγάλα», όχι τόσο σύγχρονα βιβλία και επιστρέψτε εδώ, ευχαριστώ. Πρακτικά όλα αυτά ισχύουν και για το «Θάνατος στη Βενετία». Εντελώς προβλέψιμα, του κειμένου προηγούνται 24 σελίδες που προσπάθησαν να με πείσουν ότι πρόκειται περί διαχρονικού αριστουργήματος. Οι λόγοι που απέτυχαν, επίσης πολλοί: Πρώτον, δεν διαβάζω τέτοιου είδους εισαγωγές – διθυράμβους πριν σχηματίσω δική μου γνώμη. Δεύτερον, ακόμα κι αν την είχα διαβάσει πριν το ίδιο το έργο, το μόνο που θα είχε καταφέρει θα ήταν να μου το σποϊλεριάσει μέχρι κεραίας. Και, τρίτον, το μυθιστόρημα δείχνει την ηλικία του, τόσο που, για μένα τουλάχιστον, δεν το σώζει καμία έλα-να-σου-εξηγήσω-τι-γνώμη-πρέπει-να-έχεις εισαγωγή. Αφού επιτέλους ξεκινήσει, το βιβλίο αναλώνει το πρώτο του τέταρτο, δηλαδή 30 ολόκληρες σελίδες, με σκέψεις του πρωταγωνιστή, κατά τις οποίες σελίδες δεν γίνεται τίποτα (μα τίποτα) εκτός από το να μάθουμε πόσο φοβερός και τρομερός συγγραφέας είναι (ο πρωταγωνιστής, όχι ο Μανν, ευτυχώς), καθώς και να διδαχτούμε διάφορες απόψεις, μερικές από τις οποίες, συγνώμη, αλλά μου φαίνονται επιεικώς αυθαίρετες και αστήριχτες. Ένα παράδειγμα από πολλά που μου θυμίζει το περίφημο σχόλιο του Χριστιανόπουλου για τους διανοούμενους (αν δεν το έχετε διαβάσει, κάντε το, είναι απολαυστικότατο): Και μήπως η μορφή δεν έχει δυο όψεις; Δεν είναι ηθική και μαζί ανήθικη; Ηθική σαν επίτευγμα και μορφοποίηση της πνευματικής καλλιέργειας, ανήθικη, πάλι, και από μόνη της άρνηση της ηθικής, αφού από φυσικού της περικλείει μια ηθική αδιαφορία, όταν μάλιστα έχει την τάση να υποτάσσει το ηθικό κάτω από το περήφανο και παντοδύναμο σκήπτρο της; Δηλαδή πάμε πάλι να το καταλάβουμε. Βάζω δικές μου παρενθέσεις για να το καταλάβω με το μικρό μου το μυαλό. Επίσης, πολύ σημαντικό, στο κείμενο δεν έχει "εξηγηθεί" ή "οριστεί" πουθενά πιο πριν η έννοια της μορφής. Ούτε καν έχει αναφερθεί. Αυτή είναι η πρώτη φορά που τη συναντάμε. Πάμε, λοιπόν: Και μήπως η μορφή δεν έχει δυο όψεις; Δεν είναι ηθική και μαζί ανήθικη; (Η μορφή.) Ηθική σαν επίτευγμα και μορφοποίηση της πνευματικής καλλιέργειας (Η μορφή αυτό. Δηλαδή η πνευματική καλλιέργεια είναι εξ ορισμού και πάντα ηθική.), ανήθικη, πάλι, και από μόνη της άρνηση της ηθικής (Δεν φτάνει που είναι ανήθικη, είναι και άρνηση της ηθικής. Η μορφή.), αφού από φυσικού της περικλείει μια ηθική αδιαφορία (Η μορφή. Δηλαδή ό,τι από φυσικού του περικλείει μια ηθική αδιαφορία, π.χ. μια πέτρα, είναι ανήθικο. Δηλαδή μια πέτρα είναι ανήθικη.), όταν μάλιστα έχει την τάση να υποτάσσει το ηθικό κάτω από το περήφανο και παντοδύναμο σκήπτρο της; (Η μορφή όλ' αυτά.) Ε, όχι, ρε Τόμας. Από πουθενά δεν προκύπτουν αυτά, ούτε τραβηγμένα απ' τα μαλλιά. Και δεν είναι το μόνο χωρίο. Υπάρχουν και άλλα τέτοια τσιτάτα. Καμιά φορά δουλεύουν καλά, όχι όμως πάντα. Το υπόλοιπο βιβλίο σέρνεται σε μια τετριμμένη υπόθεση και μια προβλέψιμη εξέλιξη. Στα συν η αναμφισβήτητη άνεση με τη γλώσσα, μια αφήγηση που προάγει πραγματικά αβίαστα την ιστορία που θέλει να πει. Δυστυχώς, η ιστορία, αλλά και όλα όσα υποτίθεται ότι θα έπρεπε να εισπράξω εγώ ως αναγνώστης, δεν με άγγιξαν.
